topics.title: Basic Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is *Data*?

In the formal context of Computer Science and the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, *Data* is defined as a **raw fact, figure, symbol, or character** that by itself carries no specific meaning to the receiver. Data is the fundamental input upon which a computer program operates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Data* is a collection of raw, unprocessed facts, figures, symbols, or characters that must be processed to generate meaningful information.

### 1.2 Hierarchical Vocabulary of Data

The KTU syllabus emphasizes a precise vocabulary hierarchy. The following are the **canonical building blocks** you must master:

| Vocabulary Term | Formal Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|-----------------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Data** | Raw, unprocessed facts | Ungraded wheat grain |
| **Data Item** | A single unit of named data | A single grain of wheat |
| **Data Element** | Synonym for data item; a single value | One grain |
| **Group Item** | A data item that contains sub-items | A handful of grains |
| **Data Object** | A collection of related data items | A sack containing many handfuls |
| **Entity** | A real-world object described by data | The farmer himself |
| **Attribute** | A property/field describing an entity | Farmer's height, weight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *Data Item* can be either a **Group Item** (composite, like a student record) or an **Elementary Item** (atomic, like a single integer `roll_no = 25`). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 1.3 What is a *Data Type*?

A **Data Type** is a named classification of data which determines:
1. The **type of values** the variable can hold.
2. The **range of values** it can take.
3. The **set of operations** that can be performed on it.

**Example:** The `int` data type in C takes values in the range $[-2^{31}, 2^{31}-1]$ and supports operations like `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`.

### 1.4 What is a *Data Structure*?

A **Data Structure** is a specialized **mathematical model** used to organize, manage, and store data in memory such that it can be accessed and used **efficiently**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Pattern):** A data structure is a particular way of storing and organizing data in a computer so that it can be used efficiently. It defines the *logical relationship* between data items, the *storage representation* in memory, and the *set of operations* permitted on it.

**Conceptual Analogy (The Library Analogy):**
Imagine a library. If books are thrown into a pile, finding one is impossible. But if they are arranged on shelves (by author, by subject, by Dewey Decimal), finding a book becomes efficient. That organized arrangement is your **Data Structure**.

### 1.5 What is an *Abstract Data Type (ADT)*?

An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a **mathematical model** for data types where:
- A data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a *user*.
- The **implementation details** are deliberately *hidden* (encapsulated).

> [!NOTE]
> ADT = **What** operations are performed, NOT **How** they are implemented.

**Analogy:** When you drive a car, you only need to know the *interface* — steering wheel, brake, accelerator. You don't need to know *how* the engine's combustion happens internally. The car is your **ADT**.

### 1.6 What is an *Algorithm*?

An **Algorithm** is a **finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous, executable instructions** that, when applied to a valid input, produces the corresponding output and terminates in a finite amount of time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Essential Characteristics of an Algorithm (Finiteness, Definiteness, Input, Output, Effectiveness)** — these are **HIGH-YIELD** KTU questions worth memorizing verbatim.

### 1.7 Conceptual Map of Basic Definitions

```
DATA (raw facts)
   ↓ processed into
DATA STRUCTURE (organized storage in memory)
   ↓ abstracted as
ADT (logical description + operations, implementation hidden)
   ↓ manipulated by
ALGORITHM (step-by-step procedure to solve a problem)
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Data Structures

The KTU syllabus mandates a layered understanding of data structure taxonomy:

**A. Primitive vs Non-Primitive**

- **Primitive Data Structures:** Basic data types built into the language. *Examples: `int`, `float`, `char`, `pointer`.*
- **Non-Primitive Data Structures:** Derived from primitive types. Further divided into:
  - **Linear Data Structures:** Data is arranged in a *sequential* linear order. *Examples: Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue.*
  - **Non-Linear Data Structures:** Data is arranged in a *hierarchical* or *network* order. *Examples: Trees, Graphs.*

**B. Static vs Dynamic**

- **Static Data Structures:** Size and structure are *fixed at compile-time*. Memory is allocated **once**. *Example: Array.*
- **Dynamic Data Structures:** Size can *grow or shrink* at runtime. Memory is allocated **on-demand**. *Example: Linked List.*

**C. Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous**

- **Homogeneous:** All elements are of the *same data type*. *Example: `int arr[10]`.*
- **Heterogeneous:** Elements are of *different data types*. *Example: A `struct Student` containing `int roll`, `char name[50]`, `float cgpa`.*

### 2.2 The Two Pillars of an Algorithm

Every algorithm has two pillars — **Time Complexity** and **Space Complexity**.

| Complexity Type | What it Measures | KTU Formula |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Time Complexity** $T(n)$ | Computational time as a function of input size $n$ | $T(n) = \text{compile time + run time}$ (we model run time) |
| **Space Complexity** $S(n)$ | Memory consumed as a function of input size $n$ | $S(n) = C + S_P(n)$ where $C$ is fixed part, $S_P$ is variable part |

### 2.3 Asymptotic Notations — The KTU Formula Sheet

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** expects fluency in three asymptotic notations used to express the *order of growth* of an algorithm's complexity:

| Notation | Formal Definition (Set Notation) | Intuitive Meaning |
|----------|--------------------------------|-------------------|
| **Big-O** $O(g(n))$ | $f(n) = O(g(n))$ if $\exists$ constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$ | **Upper Bound** — worst-case guarantee |
| **Big-Theta** $\Theta(g(n))$ | $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if $\exists$ constants $c_1, c_2 > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \le c_1 \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$ | **Tight Bound** — exact order of growth |
| **Big-Omega** $\Omega(g(n))$ | $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ if $\exists$ constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$ | **Lower Bound** — best-case guarantee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Trap:** Students often confuse the notations. Use the mnemonic: **O** → cap (upper), **Ω (Omega)** → floor (lower), **Θ (Theta)** → equals (tight). For *every algorithm*, $O \ge \Omega$.

### 2.4 Common Complexity Classes (Ranked Best → Worst)

$$\boxed{ O(1) \;\prec\; O(\log n) \;\prec\; O(n) \;\prec\; O(n \log n) \;\prec\; O(n^2) \;\prec\; O(n^3) \;\prec\; O(2^n) \;\prec\; O(n!) }$$

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $\prec$ means **"grows slower than"** (i.e., is asymptotically smaller). $O(1)$ is the fastest, $O(n!)$ is the slowest.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industry Application |
|---------|---------------------|
| Array-based structures | Image pixel buffers, signal processing in MATLAB |
| Linked List | OS process scheduling, music playlist (Spotify, Apple Music) |
| Stack | Browser back-button, Undo/Redo in MS Word, function call stack |
| Queue | CPU scheduling, printer job management, call center routing |
| Tree | Database indexing (B-Trees in MySQL), file systems |
| Graph | Google Maps, social network (Facebook graph), packet routing |
| Hash Table | Caching (Redis), DNS resolution, compiler symbol tables |

### 2.6 ADT vs Data Structure — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | ADT | Data Structure |
|---------|-----|----------------|
| **What is it?** | Logical/mathematical model | Concrete implementation in memory |
| **Focus** | *What* operations (interface) | *How* operations are performed |
| **Implementation** | Hidden (encapsulated) | Exposed (actual code) |
| **Example** | Stack ADT defines `push()`, `pop()`, `peek()` | Array-Stack or Linked-Stack is the implementation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One ADT → Many Data Structures.** A Stack ADT can be implemented using an *array* OR a *linked list*. Both satisfy the same interface but differ in performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time Complexity of a Simple Loop

Consider the standard KTU starter snippet:

```c
int sum = 0;
for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    sum = sum + i;
}
```

**Step 1 — Count the basic operations:**

The statement `sum = sum + i` involves three fundamental operations: one addition, one assignment, and one variable fetch. We treat this as **one unit of work**, say $T_0$ (constant time).

**Step 2 — Determine how many times the loop runs:**

The loop variable $i$ starts at $1$ and increments by $1$ until it reaches $n$. Therefore, the number of iterations is exactly $n$.

**Step 3 — Write the total time function $T(n)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= (\text{initialization cost}) + (\text{cost per iteration}) \times (\text{number of iterations}) \\
     &= T_0 + n \cdot T_0 \\
     &= T_0 (1 + n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply asymptotic analysis (drop constants and lower-order terms):**

In Big-O analysis, we discard multiplicative constants and lower-order terms because as $n \to \infty$, they become insignificant. Hence:

$$
T(n) = T_0(1 + n) \implies \boxed{T(n) = O(n)}
$$

This makes intuitive sense: the loop executes exactly $n$ times, so the runtime grows **linearly** with the input size.

### 3.2 Derivation: Time Complexity of a Nested Loop

Consider:

```c
for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
        printf("Hello");
    }
}
```

**Step 1 — Inner loop cost:**

The inner loop runs $n$ times, and each iteration performs a constant-cost `printf`. So the inner loop's cost is $T_0 \cdot n = O(n)$.

**Step 2 — Outer loop multiplier:**

The outer loop also runs $n$ times, and for *each* of its iterations, the inner loop runs in $O(n)$ time.

**Step 3 — Total time function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} T_0 \\
     &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( n \cdot T_0 \right) \\
     &= n \cdot n \cdot T_0 \\
     &= T_0 \cdot n^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic bound:**

$$
T(n) = T_0 \cdot n^2 \implies \boxed{T(n) = O(n^2)}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is a **quadratic-time** algorithm — typical of naive matrix operations like Bubble Sort or Selection Sort.

### 3.3 Derivation: Logarithmic Complexity via Repeated Halving

Consider the algorithm:

```c
int i = n;
while (i > 1) {
    i = i / 2;
}
```

**Step 1 — Track the value of $i$ after each iteration:**

After iteration $k$, the value of $i$ is $\frac{n}{2^k}$.

**Step 2 — Determine when the loop terminates:**

The loop terminates when $i \le 1$, i.e., when $\frac{n}{2^k} \le 1$.

**Step 3 — Solve the inequality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n}{2^k} &\le 1 \\
n &\le 2^k \\
\log_2 n &\le k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Hence the loop runs $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ times:**

$$
\boxed{T(n) = O(\log_2 n)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly the principle behind **Binary Search**, where the search space is halved in each iteration, yielding logarithmic complexity.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Performance Comparison

Let $n = 10^6$ operations. Examine how time grows for each complexity class:

| Complexity $T(n)$ | $n = 10$ | $n = 100$ | $n = 10^3$ | $n = 10^6$ | Verdict |
|-------------------|----------|-----------|------------|------------|---------|
| $O(1)$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Excellent |
| $O(\log n)$ | ~3.3 | ~6.6 | ~10 | ~20 | Excellent |
| $O(n)$ | 10 | 100 | 1000 | $10^6$ | Good |
| $O(n \log n)$ | ~33 | ~664 | ~9966 | ~$2 \cdot 10^7$ | Acceptable |
| $O(n^2)$ | 100 | $10^4$ | $10^6$ | $10^{12}$ | Slow |
| $O(2^n)$ | 1024 | $1.27 \cdot 10^{30}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Intractable |
| $O(n!)$ | $3.6 \cdot 10^6$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Impossible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Algorithms with $O(2^n)$ or $O(n!)$ complexity are called **intractable** — they become infeasible even for moderately sized inputs. The **P vs NP** problem in theoretical CS revolves around such complexity classes.

### 3.5 Worked Example: Verifying $f(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 10 \in O(n^2)$

**Step 1:** Identify the dominant term. The dominant term in $f(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 10$ is $3n^2$.

**Step 2:** We want to find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $f(n) \le c \cdot n^2$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

**Step 3:** By the Big-O definition, we set $g(n) = n^2$. Try $c = 4$ and $n_0 = 10$.

**Step 4:** Verify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3n^2 + 5n + 10 &\le 4n^2 \\
5n + 10 &\le n^2 \\
n^2 - 5n - 10 &\ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n \ge 10$, this inequality holds true.

**Step 5:** Therefore, by formal definition:

$$
\boxed{3n^2 + 5n + 10 = O(n^2)}
$$

This proof technique — **finding concrete witnesses** $c$ and $n_0$ — is the **gold standard** for KTU 14-mark answers on asymptotic analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Data Structures (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["DATA STRUCTURE"]
    rootA --> nodeB["PRIMITIVE DS"]
    rootA --> nodeC["NON-PRIMITIVE DS"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Integer"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Float"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Character"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Pointer"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["LINEAR DS"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["NON-LINEAR DS"]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a["Static Linear: Array"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b["Dynamic Linear: Linked List"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1c["Restricted Linear: Stack and Queue"]

    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a["Trees: Binary, BST, AVL, B-Tree"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b["Graphs: Directed, Undirected, Weighted"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c["Hash Table"]

    style rootA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style nodeC1 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff
    style nodeC2 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 The ADT Abstraction Layer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    userA["USER / APPLICATION LAYER"]
    adtB["ABSTRACT DATA TYPE LAYER"]
    implC["IMPLEMENTATION LAYER"]
    physD["PHYSICAL MEMORY LAYER"]

    userA -->|"calls operations like push pop"| adtB
    adtB -->|"defines interface and semantics"| userA
    adtB -->|"delegates to"| implC
    implC -->|"uses array OR linked list"| physD

    style userA fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723
    style adtB fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000
    style implC fill:#f4b084,stroke:#833c0c
    style physD fill:#b4c7e7,stroke:#1f3864
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *User Layer* only sees the ADT interface. It does not know whether the underlying implementation uses an array or a linked list. This is the **principle of encapsulation** that makes software modular.

### 4.3 Algorithm Execution Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA(["START: Receive Input"])
    initB["Initialize data structures and counters"]
    loopC{"Condition Check"}
    procD["Execute Processing Step"]
    incE["Update state and increment"]
    termF{"Termination Condition Met?"}
    outputG[/"Generate Output"/]
    stopH(["END"])

    startA --> initB
    initB --> loopC
    loopC -->|"True"| procD
    procD --> incE
    incE --> termF
    termF -->|"No"| loopC
    termF -->|"Yes"| outputG
    loopC -->|"False"| outputG
    outputG --> stopH

    style startA fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style stopH fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style procD fill:#ffc000
    style outputG fill:#9dc3e6
```

### 4.4 Asymptotic Notation Visualization (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    setA["Set of All Functions f of n"]
    setA --> supA["UPPER BOUND: Big-O"]
    setA --> tightA["TIGHT BOUND: Big-Theta"]
    setA --> lowA["LOWER BOUND: Big-Omega"]

    supA --> noteA["O g of n: f of n grows no faster than g of n"]
    tightA --> noteB["Theta g of n: f of n grows exactly as g of n"]
    lowA --> noteC["Omega g of n: f of n grows no slower than g of n"]

    noteA -.->|"envelopes from above"| gbox["TARGET FUNCTION g of n"]
    tightA -.->|"envelopes tightly"| gbox
    noteC -.->|"envelopes from below"| gbox

    style setA fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style supA fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff
    style tightA fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style lowA fill:#7030a0,color:#fff
    style gbox fill:#000000,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define the following terms: (i) Data Structure, (ii) Abstract Data Type, (iii) Algorithm. List any five characteristics of an algorithm.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Data Structure:** A data structure is a specialized format for organizing, managing, and storing data in memory so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. It defines the logical relationship between data items, their storage representation, and the operations permitted on them.

**(ii) Abstract Data Type (ADT):** An ADT is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its behavior (semantics) from the user's perspective. It specifies *what* operations can be performed but hides *how* they are implemented internally.

**(iii) Algorithm:** An algorithm is a finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous instructions that, when executed with valid input, produces the desired output and terminates in a finite amount of time.

**Five Characteristics of an Algorithm:**
1. **Finiteness** — Must terminate after a finite number of steps.
2. **Definiteness** — Each step must be precisely defined and unambiguous.
3. **Input** — Zero or more well-defined inputs.
4. **Output** — At least one well-defined output that is functionally related to the input.
5. **Effectiveness** — Each operation must be basic enough to be carried out exactly and in finite time.

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks add a sixth characteristic: **Generality** — the algorithm should work for a broad set of inputs, not just a single specific case.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper 2]**
*Distinguish between an Abstract Data Type (ADT) and a Data Structure with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected by Examiners):**

| Parameter | Abstract Data Type | Data Structure |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | A theoretical/mathematical model that defines data by its behavior | A concrete implementation that realizes the ADT in memory |
| **Focus** | *What* operations (logical view) | *How* operations are implemented (physical view) |
| **Implementation** | Hidden from the user | Exposed in code |
| **Dependency** | Independent of programming language | Dependent on language and memory |
| **Example** | Stack ADT (defines `push`, `pop`, `peek`) | Array-based Stack, Linked-List based Stack |

**Illustration:** A *Stack ADT* specifies that `push(x)` adds element `x` to the top and `pop()` removes the top element. The actual implementation can use either a static array (Array-Stack) or dynamic nodes (Linked-Stack). Both satisfy the same ADT interface but differ in memory behavior.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Past Year Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the three asymptotic notations: Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega. Provide the formal set-theoretic definition of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Prove formally that $f(n) = 5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7n + 12$ is $O(n^3)$ by finding explicit constants $c$ and $n_0$. *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer for Q3(A)(a):

**Step 1 — Big-O (Upper Bound):**
$$
f(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists \; c > 0 \text{ and } n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \; \forall \; n \ge n_0
$$
**Meaning:** $f(n)$ grows no faster than $g(n)$.

**Step 2 — Big-Theta (Tight Bound):**
$$
f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff \exists \; c_1, c_2 > 0 \text{ and } n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le c_1 \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n) \; \forall \; n \ge n_0
$$
**Meaning:** $f(n)$ grows exactly at the same rate as $g(n)$ (sandwiched between two scaled copies of $g$).

**Step 3 — Big-Omega (Lower Bound):**
$$
f(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \iff \exists \; c > 0 \text{ and } n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \; \forall \; n \ge n_0
$$
**Meaning:** $f(n)$ grows no slower than $g(n)$.

**Step 4 — Relationship:** For any function $f(n)$: $\Omega(g(n)) \cap O(g(n)) = \Theta(g(n))$.

### Model Answer for Q3(A)(b):

**Step 1 — Statement:** We claim $f(n) = 5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7n + 12 \in O(n^3)$.

**Step 2 — Setup:** Set $g(n) = n^3$. We must find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that:

$$
5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7n + 12 \le c \cdot n^3 \quad \forall \; n \ge n_0
$$

**Step 3 — Propose candidate $c$:**

Try $c = 8$. Then we need:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7n + 12 &\le 8n^3 \\
2n^2 + 7n + 12 &\le 3n^3 \\
3n^3 - 2n^2 - 7n - 12 &\ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the inequality for large $n$:**

For $n \ge 4$, the term $3n^3$ dominates $2n^2 + 7n + 12$. Let us check $n = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} &= 3(64) - 2(16) - 7(4) - 12 \\
           &= 192 - 32 - 28 - 12 \\
           &= 120 \ge 0 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

Choosing $c = 8$ and $n_0 = 4$, the inequality $f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ holds for all $n \ge 4$.

$$
\boxed{5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7n + 12 = O(n^3)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the *value* of $c$ and $n_0$ is **not unique**. Any $(c, n_0)$ pair that satisfies the definition is acceptable. Examiners award full marks for any correct witness.

---

**Q3 (B). [Alternative Choice for KTU Internal Option]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of data structures in detail with a hierarchical diagram and suitable examples for each category. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define Space Complexity. Derive the space complexity of an algorithm that uses an array of size $n$, two scalar variables, and a recursion depth of $\log n$. *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer for Q3(B)(a):

**Step 1 — Top-Level Classification:**

Data structures are classified into two main categories:

1. **Primitive Data Structures:** These are the basic data types provided directly by the programming language.
   - *Examples:* `int`, `float`, `double`, `char`, `pointer` in C.

2. **Non-Primitive Data Structures:** These are derived from primitive types and provide more sophisticated organization.

**Step 2 — Sub-classification of Non-Primitive:**

Non-primitive structures are further divided as follows:

- **Linear Data Structures:** Elements are arranged in a sequential, linear order. Each element has a unique predecessor and successor (except endpoints).
  - *Examples:* Array, Linked List (Singly, Doubly, Circular), Stack, Queue.

- **Non-Linear Data Structures:** Elements are arranged in a hierarchical or networked fashion with non-sequential relationships.
  - *Examples:* Trees (Binary Tree, BST, AVL Tree, B-Tree), Graphs (Directed, Undirected), Heap.

**Step 3 — Static vs Dynamic (Cross-Cutting Classification):**

- **Static Structures:** Size fixed at compile-time. *Example:* Array.
- **Dynamic Structures:** Size varies at run-time via memory allocation. *Example:* Linked List, Tree.

**Step 4 — Diagram Representation:**

```
                ┌──► Integer, Float, Char (Primitive)
                │
DATA STRUCTURE ─┤
                │                                  ┌──► Array
                │              ┌──► LINEAR ────────┤
                └──► NON-PRIM ─┤                   └──► Linked List, Stack, Queue
                                │
                                │                   ┌──► Tree (Binary, BST)
                                └──► NON-LINEAR ───┤
                                                    └──► Graph, Heap
```

**Step 5 — Summary Table:**

| Type | Examples | Memory Behavior |
|------|----------|-----------------|
| Primitive | `int`, `char` | Fixed |
| Linear Static | Array | Contiguous, fixed |
| Linear Dynamic | Linked List | Non-contiguous, growable |
| Non-Linear | Tree, Graph | Hierarchical/Networked |

### Model Answer for Q3(B)(b):

**Step 1 — Definition:**

Space Complexity $S(n)$ of an algorithm is the total amount of memory it requires as a function of the input size $n$. It is given by:

$$
S(n) = C + S_P(n)
$$

where $C$ is the **fixed part** (memory for instructions, constants, simple variables) and $S_P(n)$ is the **variable part** (memory for dynamically allocated data structures, function call stack, recursion).

**Step 2 — Identify the components for our algorithm:**

- **Array of size $n$:** Contributes $n \cdot k$ memory units (where $k$ is the size of each element, typically 4 bytes for `int`).
- **Two scalar variables:** Contributes a constant $c_1$ (typically $2 \cdot 4 = 8$ bytes).
- **Recursion depth of $\log n$:** Each recursive call stores return address, parameters, and local variables on the call stack. If each call uses constant memory $c_2$, total stack memory is $c_2 \cdot \log n$.

**Step 3 — Compose the total space complexity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(n) &= (\text{Array memory}) + (\text{Scalar memory}) + (\text{Recursion stack memory}) \\
     &= n \cdot k + c_1 + c_2 \cdot \log n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic simplification:**

The dominant term is $n \cdot k$. Applying Big-O analysis (drop constants and lower-order terms):

$$
\boxed{S(n) = O(n)}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Although the recursion contributes only $O(\log n)$ space, the *array* dominates. If the algorithm had **no array** but only recursion depth $\log n$, the space complexity would drop to $O(\log n)$ — characteristic of efficient divide-and-conquer algorithms like merge sort's auxiliary stack.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ADT with Data Structure:** Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** if you use these terms interchangeably. Remember: ADT = *what*, Data Structure = *how*.
> 2. **Forgetting the constants in Big-O proofs:** You must explicitly state the chosen values of $c$ and $n_0$. Writing only "$f(n) = O(n^2)$" without proof fetches **partial credit only**.
> 3. **Writing `O`, `Θ`, `Ω` incorrectly:** Use proper Greek symbols or the spelled-out names. Scribbled or miswritten notation may cost style marks.
> 4. **Confusing time complexity with wall-clock time:** Time complexity is an *analytical measure*, not actual seconds. Never say "$O(n)$ means $n$ seconds."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Data** is the raw input; a **Data Item** is a single unit; a **Data Object** is a collection of related items.
- [x] A **Data Type** defines a set of values + operations; a **Data Structure** adds *organization* to that.
- [x] A **Data Structure** = logical organization + storage representation + permitted operations.
- [x] **ADT** = logical description; **Data Structure** = physical implementation. *One ADT → Many Data Structures.*
- [x] Primitive structures: `int`, `float`, `char`, `pointer`. Non-primitive: linear (Array, LL, Stack, Queue) and non-linear (Tree, Graph).
- [x] Static vs Dynamic: Array is static (fixed size); Linked List is dynamic (variable size).
- [x] **Algorithm Characteristics (memorize order):** Finiteness, Definiteness, Input, Output, Effectiveness.
- [x] **Asymptotic Notations:** $O$ = upper bound (worst case); $\Omega$ = lower bound (best case); $\Theta$ = tight bound (exact).
- [x] For Big-O proof: explicitly *find* constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ — do not just assert the bound.
- [x] **Complexity Ranking:** $O(1) \prec O(\log n) \prec O(n) \prec O(n \log n) \prec O(n^2) \prec O(2^n) \prec O(n!)$.
- [x] **Space Complexity Formula:** $S(n) = C + S_P(n)$ — fixed part + variable part.
- [x] Every algorithm has a **time complexity** AND a **space complexity** — KTU questions often ask for both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classifications of Data Structures: ADT and CDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classifications of Data Structures: ADT and CDT # 📚 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Data Structure? In computer science, a **data structure** is a systematic way of organizing, managing, and storing data so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. KTU 2024 defines a data structure as a **named storage location that can hold data of a specific type along with the operations that can be performed on that data**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to *classify* data structures into primitive/non-primitive, linear/non-linear, and static/dynamic categories. They must also clearly distinguish between an **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** and a **Concrete Data Type (CDT)**. ## 1.2 The Big Picture — Conceptual Analogy 🏗️ Imagine you are building a **house**. Before laying bricks, an architect first draws a **blueprint** that says: - There will be rooms, doors, windows, and a roof. - People should be able to enter, exit, and move between rooms. The **blueprint is the ADT** — it tells you *what* the house should do, not *how* to build it. The **actual construction (bricks, cement, steel)** is the **Concrete Data Type (CDT)** — it defines *how* the house is actually built using real materials. | House Analogy | Computer Science Equivalent | |---------------|------------------------------| | Blueprint / Design | **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** | | Real construction (bricks, cement) | **Concrete Data Type (CDT)** | | "A house must have a door" | ADT operation: `push()`, `pop()` | | "The door is made of wood" | CDT: Array or Linked List implementation | ## 1.3 Formal Definition of ADT (Abstract Data Type) > [!IMPORTANT] > **ADT Definition:** An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a **user**, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations. An ADT does **NOT** specify *how* the data is stored in memory or *how* the operations are implemented. It only specifies: 1. **The data** being stored. 2. **The operations** that can be performed on that data. 3. **The properties / axioms** that these operations must satisfy. **Examples of ADTs:** Stack, Queue, List, Tree, Graph, Dictionary, Set. ## 1.4 Formal Definition of CDT (Concrete Data Type) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CDT Definition:** A **Concrete Data Type (CDT)**, also called a **Data Structure**, is the actual **implementation** of an ADT in a specific programming language. It specifies *how* data is organized in memory (the storage representation) and *how* the operations are actually performed (the algorithm). A CDT is **bound to a specific implementation**. The same ADT can have multiple CDTs. **Example:** The **Stack ADT** can be implemented as: - **Array-based Stack** (CDT #1) — uses a contiguous memory block. - **Linked List based Stack** (CDT #2) — uses nodes connected via pointers. # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Data Structures Data structures are classified along several dimensions. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following classification tree: ### A. Primitive vs Non-Primitive Data Structures **Primitive Data Structures** are the basic data types built into a programming language. They can hold only a **single value** and are operated upon directly by the hardware/machine-level instructions. | Primitive Type | Typical Size | Purpose | |----------------|-------------|---------| | `int` | **4 bytes** | Integer arithmetic | | `float` | **4 bytes** | Single-precision decimal | | `double` | **8 bytes** | Double-precision decimal | | `char` | **1 byte** | Single character | **Non-Primitive Data Structures** are derived from primitive types. They can hold **multiple values** (either of same or different types) and include structures like arrays, linked lists, trees, etc. ### B. Linear vs Non-Linear Data Structures - **Linear DS:** Data elements are arranged in a **sequential / linear order**. Each element has a unique predecessor and successor (except the first and last). Examples: Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue. - **Non-Linear DS:** Data elements are arranged in a **hierarchical or networked** manner. Examples: Tree, Graph, Heap. ### C. Static vs Dynamic Data Structures - **Static DS:** Size is **fixed at compile-time**. Memory is allocated on the **stack**. Example: Array of size 100. - **Dynamic DS:** Size can **grow or shrink at runtime**. Memory is allocated on the **heap** using `malloc()` / `new`. Example: Linked List, Dynamic Array (`vector`). ### D. Homogeneous vs Non-Homogeneous - **Homogeneous:** All elements are of the **same data type**. Example: `int arr[10]`. - **Non-Homogeneous:** Elements can be of **different data types**. Example: `struct Student { int roll; char name[20]; float cgpa; }`. ## 2.2 The ADT — Three Formal Components An ADT can be formally specified using three components: 1. **Type Declaration** (data being stored) 2. **Operations** (functions/methods that can be invoked) 3. **Axioms / Pre-conditions / Post-conditions** (rules governing behavior) ### Example: STACK ADT Formal Specification **Type Declaration:** - A Stack is a linear collection of elements where insertion and deletion happen at one end called the **top**. **Operations:** - `push(x)`: Inserts element x at the top. - `pop()`: Removes and returns the top element. - `peek() / top()`: Returns the top element without removing it. - `isEmpty()`: Returns true if the stack has no elements. - `isFull()`: Returns true if the stack is full (in case of bounded implementation). **Axioms (LIFO property):** - The element removed by `pop()` is always the element most recently inserted by `push()`. - If `n` elements are pushed and then `n` elements are popped, the stack returns to its original state. ## 2.3 The CDT — Storage and Algorithm Layer A CDT must provide: 1. **Storage Representation:** A specific memory layout (array, linked nodes, etc.). 2. **Algorithm for each ADT operation:** A step-by-step procedure in code. **Example — Stack as Array (CDT):** - Storage: A 1-D array `stack[MAX]` plus an integer variable `top` initialized to `-1`. - `push(x)`: If `top < MAX-1`, then `top = top + 1; stack[top] = x`. - `pop()`: If `top >= 0`, then `x = stack[top]; top = top - 1; return x`. **Example — Stack as Linked List (CDT):** - Storage: A pointer `head` to the topmost node. Each node has `data` and `next`. - `push(x)`: Create new node with data x; set its `next` to `head`; update `head`. - `pop()`: Save `head->data`; move `head = head->next`; free old node. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > The following table is the **minimum required** set of relationships for ADT/CDT-related 3-mark and 14-mark questions. | Concept | Property / Formula | Symbol | Unit / Notes | |---------|-------------------|--------|--------------| | Array Memory Address | Base address of `A[i]` | `&A[i] = Base + (i × size)` | bytes | | 2D Array Address (Row-Major) | Address of `A[i][j]` | `Base + [(i × n) + j] × size` | bytes, $n$ = columns | | Linked List Traversal | Time to access $k$-th node | $T(k) = O(k)$ | steps | | Array Access | Time to access any element | $T = O(1)$ | constant | | Stack operations | `push` / `pop` | $O(1)$ | both top of stack | | Queue operations | `enqueue` / `dequeue` | $O(1)$ | with circular array | ## 2.5 Why Two Layers? — The Engineering Utility | Benefit | Explanation | |---------|-------------| | **Modularity** | Programmer can use a Stack without knowing its internals. | | **Reusability** | The same ADT code can be re-implemented using different CDTs. | | **Maintainability** | Changing the implementation (e.g., array to linked list) doesn't affect the user. | | **Encapsulation** | Data hiding principle of OOP is built on the ADT/CDT distinction. | | **Algorithm Analysis** | Algorithm design can be discussed in ADT terms, then bound to specific CDT complexities. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Connection:** In **Module 2 (Stacks and Queues)** and **Module 3 (Linked Lists)**, every problem will first define the ADT, then ask you to implement it as a CDT using arrays and/or linked lists. This is the core pattern! # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Array Address Calculation (1-D) This is the most common derivation question on ADT/CDT topics in KTU exams. **Problem:** Given a 1-D integer array `A` with base address $\text{Base} = 1000$, each element occupies $4$ bytes. Calculate the address of `A[7]`. **Step 1 — Identify the formula.** For a 1-D array, the address of the $i$-th element is given by: $$\text{Addr}(A[i]) = \text{Base} + (i \times \text{size of element})$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the known values.** We have: - $\text{Base} = 1000$ - $i = 7$ - $\text{size of element} = 4$ bytes (since it is an integer) **Step 3 — Compute the offset (number of bytes to skip).** $$i \times \text{size} = 7 \times 4 = 28 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4 — Add to base address.** $$\text{Addr}(A[7]) = 1000 + 28 = 1028$$ **Final Answer:** `A[7]` is stored at memory address **1028 bytes**. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: 2-D Array Address Calculation (Row-Major) **Problem:** A 2-D integer array `A[10][20]` is stored in row-major order with base address $\text{Base} = 2000$ and each element is $4$ bytes. Find the address of `A[4][6]`. **Step 1 — Recall the row-major formula.** For an array declared as `A[m][n]` (m rows, n columns), the address of `A[i][j]` is: $$\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base} + [(i \times n) + j] \times \text{size}$$ **Step 2 — List the parameters.** - $\text{Base} = 2000$ - $m = 10$ (rows) - $n = 20$ (columns) - $i = 4$ - $j = 6$ - $\text{size} = 4$ bytes **Step 3 — Compute the row-offset.** $$i \times n = 4 \times 20 = 80$$ **Step 4 — Add the column-index.** $$(i \times n) + j = 80 + 6 = 86$$ **Step 5 — Compute the total byte-offset.** $$86 \times 4 = 344 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the final address.** $$\text{Addr}(A[4][6]) = 2000 + 344 = 2344$$ **Final Answer:** `A[4][6]` is stored at memory address **2344 bytes**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ADT vs CDT Comparison This matrix is the **most frequently asked** comparison in KTU Module 1 university exams. Memorize it. | Parameter | Abstract Data Type (ADT) | Concrete Data Type (CDT) | |-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------| | **Level of abstraction** | High-level (logical) | Low-level (physical) | | **Focus** | **What** the data does | **How** the data is stored | | **Also called** | Mathematical model | Data Structure / Implementation | | **Specifying tool** | Axioms, pre/post-conditions | Algorithms, memory layouts | | **Programming language** | Language-independent | Language-dependent (C, C++, Java) | | **Visibility of internals** | Hidden from user (encapsulated) | Exposed in implementation | | **Example: Stack** | "LIFO collection with push, pop" | "Array with `top = -1` and indexed access" | | **Example: List** | "Ordered sequence with insert, delete" | "Singly Linked List using `struct Node`" | | **Changes** | Stable, rarely modified | Can be re-implemented without changing ADT | | **Used by** | Application programmer | System programmer / Designer | --- ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Number of Elements in a 1-D Array **Problem:** A 1-D array `A` starts at address 5000 and ends at address 5048. If each element is 4 bytes, how many elements are there? **Step 1 — Total memory occupied.** $$\text{Total memory} = \text{End Address} - \text{Start Address} + \text{size of last element}$$ $$= 5048 - 5000 + 4 = 52 \text{ bytes}$$ *(Note: The +1 is because the last address itself is occupied by an element.)* **Step 2 — Divide by size of one element.** $$N = \frac{52}{4} = 13 \text{ elements}$$ **Final Answer:** The array holds **13 elements**, with valid indices $0$ to $12$. --- ## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation: ADT Operation Count **Problem:** A queue has 5 elements initially. We perform 3 `enqueue(x)` and 2 `dequeue()` operations. How many elements are in the queue? **Step 1 — Initial state.** $$N_0 = 5$$ **Step 2 — After 3 enqueues.** $$N_1 = N_0 + 3 = 5 + 3 = 8$$ **Step 3 — After 2 dequeues.** $$N_2 = N_1 - 2 = 8 - 2 = 6$$ **Final Answer:** The queue has **6 elements**. # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Data Structures ```mermaid graph TD root["DATA STRUCTURES"] root --> primitive["PRIMITIVE"] root --> nonprim["NON-PRIMITIVE"] primitive --> p1["int"] primitive --> p2["float"] primitive --> p3["char"] primitive --> p4["double"] primitive --> p5["pointer"] nonprim --> lin["LINEAR"] nonprim --> nlin["NON-LINEAR"] lin --> l1["Array"] lin --> l2["Linked List"] lin --> l3["Stack"] lin --> l4["Queue"] nlin --> nl1["Tree"] nlin --> nl2["Graph"] nlin --> nl3["Heap"] nlin --> nl4["Trie"] style root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style primitive fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nonprim fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style lin fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nlin fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 ADT vs CDT — Layered Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph USR ["USER LAYER - APPLICATION"] APP["Main Program / Client Code
Stack s
s.push 10
s.push 20
s.pop"] end subgraph ADTL ["ADT LAYER - LOGICAL CONTRACT"] SPEC["STACK ADT SPECIFICATION
Data: Linear LIFO collection
Operations:
push x
pop returns x
peek returns x
isEmpty returns bool
Axiom: LIFO Property"] end subgraph CDTL ["CDT LAYER - PHYSICAL IMPLEMENTATION"] CDTA["CDT 1: Array-Based Stack
stack MAX 100
top equals -1
push: stack top plus 1 equals x
pop: x equals stack top--"] CDTL2["CDT 2: Linked List Stack
struct Node: data, next ptr
head points to top
push: new node next equals head
pop: move head to head next"] end APP -->|uses interface of| SPEC SPEC -.->|can be implemented by| CDTA SPEC -.->|can be implemented by| CDTL2 style USR fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#000 style ADTL fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#000 style CDTL fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000 style APP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style SPEC fill:#ffe082,stroke:#000 style CDTA fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#000 style CDTL2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Flow Diagram — ADT Design Methodology ```mermaid graph LR A["Step 1: Identify the Problem
What data and behavior?"] --> B["Step 2: Define ADT
Declare type and operations"] B --> C["Step 3: Specify Axioms
What properties must hold?"] C --> D["Step 4: Choose CDT
Array or Linked List?"] D --> E["Step 5: Implement CDT
Write algorithms in C"] E --> F["Step 6: Test and Verify
Does CDT satisfy ADT axioms?"] F -->|No| E F -->|Yes| G["Step 7: Deploy
Use ADT in application"] style A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000 style B fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#000 style C fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#000 style D fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#000 style E fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#000 style F fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Memory Layout — Array vs Linked List (CDT View) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ARR ["CDT 1 - ARRAY STACK IN MEMORY"] A0["Addr 1000: 5"] A1["Addr 1004: 12"] A2["Addr 1008: 7"] A3["Addr 1012: 22"] A0 -->|next address| A1 A1 -->|next address| A2 A2 -->|next address| A3 end subgraph LL ["CDT 2 - LINKED LIST STACK IN MEMORY"] L0["Node at 2000: data=5, next=2010"] L1["Node at 2010: data=12, next=2020"] L2["Node at 2020: data=7, next=NULL"] HEAD["HEAD pointer at 100: 2000"] HEAD -->|points to| L0 L0 -->|next ptr| L1 L1 -->|next ptr| L2 end style ARR fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#000 style LL fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 ``` # 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** **Differentiate between Abstract Data Type (ADT) and Concrete Data Type (CDT). Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Sl. No. | Abstract Data Type (ADT) | Concrete Data Type (CDT) | |---------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | ADT is a **logical / mathematical model** that specifies *what* operations can be performed on data, not *how* they are implemented. | CDT is the **actual implementation** of the ADT in a specific programming language, specifying *how* the data is stored and operations are performed. | | 2 | It is described using **axioms, pre-conditions, and post-conditions**. | It is described using **memory layouts and algorithms**. | | 3 | **Example:** The **Stack ADT** is defined by the LIFO property with operations push, pop, peek, isEmpty. | **Example:** A **Stack implemented using an array** with a `top` variable is a CDT. | *(Award 1 mark each for the difference explanation and 1 mark for examples.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** **Classify data structures into primitive and non-primitive types. Give two examples for each category.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Primitive Data Structures:** These are the basic, built-in data types that hold a single value and are directly supported by the programming language. - **Example 1:** `int` — used to store integer values (typically 4 bytes). - **Example 2:** `float` — used to store single-precision decimal values (typically 4 bytes). **Non-Primitive Data Structures:** These are derived from primitive types and can hold multiple values, organized in a specific way. - **Example 1:** **Array** — a linear collection of homogeneous elements stored in contiguous memory. - **Example 2:** **Linked List** — a linear collection of nodes connected via pointers. *(Award 1.5 marks for primitive + examples, 1.5 marks for non-primitive + examples.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** **(a)** Explain the concept of Abstract Data Type (ADT). With a neat diagram, describe the **Stack ADT** including its type, operations, and axioms. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail how the **Stack ADT** can be implemented as a Concrete Data Type (CDT) using (i) an Array and (ii) a Linked List. Compare the two CDTs. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Definition of ADT (2 marks):** An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a **user**. It specifies: - The **data** being stored. - The **operations** that can be performed on the data. - The **axioms / properties** that the operations must satisfy. An ADT hides the implementation details and is described independently of any programming language. **Stack ADT Specification (5 marks):** **Type:** A Stack is a linear collection of elements where all insertions and deletions occur at one end, called the **top**. It follows the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** discipline. **Operations:** | Operation | Description | |-----------|-------------| | `push(x)` | Inserts element x at the top of the stack. | | `pop()` | Removes and returns the top element of the stack. | | `peek()` | Returns (but does not remove) the top element. | | `isEmpty()` | Returns `true` if the stack contains no elements. | | `isFull()` | Returns `true` if the stack is full (bounded case). | | `size()` | Returns the number of elements currently in the stack. | **Axioms (Pre-conditions / Post-conditions):** 1. The element removed by `pop()` is always the most recently inserted element by `push(x)`. 2. After a `pop()`, the new top element is the one that was inserted just before the removed one. 3. `isEmpty()` returns `true` iff no `push()` has been called more times than `pop()`. **Diagram (in words):** Show a vertical box with arrows representing `push` going IN at the top and `pop` coming OUT from the top, illustrating LIFO order. --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **(i) Array-Based Stack CDT (3 marks):** **Storage Representation:** - A 1-D array `stack[MAX]` of size `MAX`. - An integer variable `top` initialized to `-1` to indicate an empty stack. **Algorithms:** | Operation | Algorithm | |-----------|-----------| | `push(x)` | If `top < MAX-1`, then `top = top + 1; stack[top] = x;` else print "Stack Overflow". | | `pop()` | If `top >= 0`, then `x = stack[top]; top = top - 1; return x;` else print "Stack Underflow". | | `peek()` | If `top >= 0`, return `stack[top]`; else error. | | `isEmpty()` | Return `top == -1`. | | `isFull()` | Return `top == MAX-1`. | **(ii) Linked List Based Stack CDT (2 marks):** **Storage Representation:** - A `struct Node { int data; struct Node* next; }`. - A pointer `head` to the topmost node (initially `NULL`). **Algorithms:** | Operation | Algorithm | |-----------|-----------| | `push(x)` | Create new node `n`; set `n->data = x; n->next = head; head = n;` | | `pop()` | If `head != NULL`, save `x = head->data; head = head->next; free(old); return x;` | **Comparison (2 marks):** | Parameter | Array-Based Stack | Linked List Based Stack | |-----------|-------------------|--------------------------| | Memory | Fixed size, may waste memory | Dynamic, grows as needed | | Size Limit | Must declare `MAX` upfront | No predefined limit | | Cache Performance | Better (contiguous memory) | Poorer (scattered memory) | | Overflow | Possible (fixed size) | Not possible (until heap exhausted) | | Memory per element | Just the data | Data + pointer (extra overhead) | | Access time | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ (only at head) | --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP] — Internal Choice** **(a)** What is a Data Structure? Classify data structures along **three different dimensions** and explain each with one example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1-D integer array `A` has base address 5000 and stores 10 elements. Each element occupies 4 bytes. Calculate the address of `A[3]` and `A[8]`. If the same array is interpreted as a 2-D array `A[2][5]` stored in row-major order, calculate the address of `A[1][3]`. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Definition (1 mark):** A **data structure** is a named storage location along with a specific way of organizing data so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. It defines the storage format and the operations that can be performed on the data. **Classification along three dimensions (6 marks, 2 each):** **Dimension 1: Primitive vs Non-Primitive (2 marks):** - **Primitive Data Structures:** Built-in types in a programming language that hold a single value. Examples: `int`, `float`, `char`. - **Non-Primitive Data Structures:** Derived from primitive types, can hold multiple values. Examples: Array, Stack, Tree. **Dimension 2: Linear vs Non-Linear (2 marks):** - **Linear DS:** Elements are arranged sequentially; each element has a unique predecessor and successor. Example: **Linked List** — each node points to the next in a single sequence. - **Non-Linear DS:** Elements are arranged in a hierarchical or networked manner. Example: **Tree** — a root node with multiple child nodes forming a hierarchy. **Dimension 3: Static vs Dynamic (2 marks):** - **Static DS:** Size is fixed at compile-time; memory allocated on the stack. Example: **Array** of size 50 — cannot be resized during program execution. - **Dynamic DS:** Size can grow or shrink at runtime; memory allocated on the heap. Example: **Linked List** — new nodes can be added or removed at any time. --- ### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Given:** - Base address $\text{Base} = 5000$ - Number of elements $N = 10$ - Size of each element $= 4$ bytes **(1) Address of `A[3]` (1-D array):** Using the 1-D array address formula: $$\text{Addr}(A[i]) = \text{Base} + (i \times \text{size})$$ For $i = 3$: $$\text{Addr}(A[3]) = 5000 + (3 \times 4) = 5000 + 12 = 5012$$ **Address of `A[3]` = 5012 bytes.** **(2) Address of `A[8]` (1-D array):** For $i = 8$: $$\text{Addr}(A[8]) = 5000 + (8 \times 4) = 5000 + 32 = 5032$$ **Address of `A[8]` = 5032 bytes.** **(3) Address of `A[1][3]` in 2-D array `A[2][5]` (Row-Major): (4 marks)** For a 2-D array `A[m][n]` in row-major order: $$\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base} + [(i \times n) + j] \times \text{size}$$ Given: - $m = 2$ rows, $n = 5$ columns - $i = 1$, $j = 3$ - $\text{size} = 4$ bytes **Step 1:** Compute $i \times n$: $$i \times n = 1 \times 5 = 5$$ **Step 2:** Add the column index $j$: $$(i \times n) + j = 5 + 3 = 8$$ **Step 3:** Compute the byte-offset: $$8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the final address: $$\text{Addr}(A[1][3]) = 5000 + 32 = 5032$$ **Address of `A[1][3]` = 5032 bytes.** > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Most common mistake (1-mark loss):** Using `n` (columns) in place of `m` (rows) when calculating `i × n`. Always remember: in row-major, the **row stride** equals the **number of columns**. > - **Index confusion:** Some students confuse `A[i][j]` with `A[j][i]`. Carefully read the declaration `A[2][5]` — there are 2 rows and 5 columns. > - **Off-by-one error:** Do not add 1 to the index. The address of `A[0]` is the base address itself. > - **Confusing ADT and CDT:** In 14-mark questions, students often describe the Stack ADT using array implementation, mixing up the two layers. **Always first describe the ADT (what), then the CDT (how).** --- ## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:** - ✅ **Data Structure** = Storage format + Operations on that data. - ✅ **ADT** = Logical / Mathematical model → defines *what* operations exist. - ✅ **CDT** = Physical implementation → defines *how* the operations work in code. - ✅ **Same ADT can have multiple CDTs** (e.g., Stack → Array or Linked List). - ✅ **Primitive DS:** int, float, char, double, pointer (single value, built-in). - ✅ **Non-Primitive DS:** Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue, Tree, Graph. - ✅ **Linear DS:** Sequential arrangement (Array, List, Stack, Queue). - ✅ **Non-Linear DS:** Hierarchical/Networked (Tree, Graph, Heap). - ✅ **Static DS:** Fixed size at compile time (Array). - ✅ **Dynamic DS:** Resizable at runtime (Linked List, Dynamic Array). - ✅ **Homogeneous:** All elements same type; **Non-Homogeneous:** Mixed types. - ✅ **Stack ADT** = LIFO discipline with push, pop, peek, isEmpty, isFull, size. - ✅ **Queue ADT** = FIFO discipline with enqueue, dequeue, front, rear, isEmpty. - ✅ **1-D Array Address Formula:** $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = \text{Base} + (i \times \text{size})$. - ✅ **2-D Array Address Formula (Row-Major):** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base} + [(i \times n) + j] \times \text{size}$. - ✅ **2-D Array Address Formula (Column-Major):** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base} + [(j \times m) + i] \times \text{size}$. - ✅ **ADT specification** = Type + Operations + Axioms. - ✅ **Encapsulation, Modularity, Reusability** are the 4 key benefits of the ADT/CDT separation. > [!NOTE] > **📌 Exam Strategy Tip:** For every 14-mark question in Modules 2-5 that involves a data structure, **start with the ADT specification (type, operations, axioms)** before jumping into the CDT implementation. Examiners award 3-4 marks specifically for the ADT description.

topics.title: Linear and Non-Linear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear and Non-Linear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Structure Classification — Linear vs Non-Linear**
>
> A **data structure** is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data in memory so it can be accessed and worked with efficiently. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies data structures into two foundational categories based on the **arrangement of elements** and the **relationship between them**.

### Linear Data Structure
A **linear data structure** is one in which elements are arranged in a **sequential, one-dimensional order**, where each element is connected to its previous and next element (except the first and last). Traversal happens in a **single straight run** (level-by-level, side-by-side).

### Non-Linear Data Structure
A **non-linear data structure** is one in which elements are arranged in a **hierarchical** or **network (graph) relationship**, where one element may be connected to **multiple predecessors or successors**. Traversal may follow **multiple paths**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear = A Train.** Imagine a long passenger train. You board Coach 1, then Coach 2, then Coach 3, in that exact order. Each coach has exactly one coach in front and one behind. You **cannot reach Coach 5 without passing through Coach 2, 3, and 4**. The arrangement is strictly **one-after-another (1-D)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Linear = A Family Tree.** Take your own family tree. Your grandparents have two children (your parents and your uncle). Each of those has multiple children. One person (your grandfather) is connected to **many** descendants, and there are **multiple ways** to move through the tree (downward, sideways among siblings, upward). This **multi-level, many-to-many** linkage is the essence of non-linearity.

---

## 1.3 Standard Examples (Must-Memorize List for KTU)

| **Linear Data Structures** | **Non-Linear Data Structures** |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Array** (1-D, 2-D) | **Tree** (Binary, BST, AVL, B-Tree) |
| **Linked List** (Singly, Doubly, Circular) | **Graph** (Directed, Undirected, Weighted) |
| **Stack** (LIFO) | **Heap** (Min-Heap, Max-Heap) |
| **Queue** (FIFO, Deque, Priority Queue) | **Trie**, **Segment Tree**, **Hash Table** (chained) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A *Hash Table* is treated as a **non-linear** structure in KTU 2024 Scheme because chaining creates a multi-level layout, even though indexing is computed in O(1). Mention this in answers to show conceptual depth.

---

## 1.4 Why This Classification Matters

- It dictates the **time and space complexity** of operations (search, insert, delete, traverse).
- It guides the choice of **traversal algorithm** (single pass vs. recursive/multi-branch).
- It determines **memory layout** — *contiguous (linear)* vs. *scattered/linked (non-linear)*.
- It influences **real-world applications** — arrays for matrices, trees for databases/AI, graphs for networks/maps.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Linear Data Structures

A linear data structure obeys the **strict ordering property**: if element $e_i$ precedes $e_j$, then $e_j$ cannot be reached without traversing all elements between them.

**Core Properties:**
- **Single-level:** Only one "level" of data. No nesting.
- **Sequential memory or pointer-based chain:** Either contiguous (Array) or linked (Linked List).
- **Single traversal path:** Begin $\rightarrow$ End is unique.
- **Static or Dynamic size:** Arrays are *static*, Linked Lists/Queues can be *dynamic*.

**Operations supported uniformly across linear structures:**
- `Traversal` — Visit every element once.
- `Insertion` — Add an element at a specified position.
- `Deletion` — Remove an element.
- `Search` — Locate an element.
- `Sort` / `Merge` — Reorder or combine.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Non-Linear Data Structures

Non-linear data structures do **not** enforce a single traversal order. An element may have **multiple successors** (tree) or **both predecessors and successors** (graph).

**Core Properties:**
- **Multi-level / Hierarchical:** Tree has parent–child levels; Graph is a flat network.
- **Non-contiguous memory:** Nodes scattered, connected via pointers/references.
- **Multiple traversal paths:** Many ways to walk through the structure.
- **Recursive algorithms dominate:** In-order, Pre-order, Post-order, BFS, DFS.

**Specialized Operations:**
- Tree: *height, depth, ancestors, descendants, subtree extraction*.
- Graph: *shortest path, connectivity, cycle detection, topological sort, MST*.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Module 1 Examinations**

| **Concept** | **Formula / Notation** | **Used For** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Address of 1-D Array element | $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i \times w$ | Random access in array |
| Address of 2-D Array (row-major) | $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \times n + j) \times w$ | Random access in matrix |
| Address of 2-D Array (column-major) | $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (j \times m + i) \times w$ | Fortran-style matrices |
| Array index range | $0 \le i \le n-1$ | Valid index bound |
| Linked list traversal cost | $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ extra space | Sequential search |
| Binary tree node count vs height | Min nodes $= h+1$, Max nodes $= 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Tree property questions |
| Full binary tree node relation | $n = 2i + 1$, where $i$ = internal nodes | KTU theory derivations |
| Graph edge count (tree) | $E = V - 1$ | Tree-as-special-graph proof |
| BFS / DFS complexity | $O(V + E)$ | Graph traversal questions |
| Big-O standard complexities | $O(1)$, $O(\log n)$, $O(n)$, $O(n \log n)$, $O(n^2)$ | Operation cost analysis |

Where $B$ = base address, $w$ = word size (bytes per element), $n$ = columns, $m$ = rows, $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $h$ = height.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Engineering Utility

| **Real-World Application** | **Structure Used** | **Category** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Student record management | Array / Linked List | Linear |
| CPU process scheduling | Queue (FCFS, Round Robin) | Linear |
| Function call management | Stack | Linear |
| Undo/Redo in editors | Stack | Linear |
| Company org chart | Tree | Non-Linear |
| Google Maps shortest route | Graph (weighted) | Non-Linear |
| Database indexing | B-Tree / B+ Tree | Non-Linear |
| AI decision making | Tree (game tree) | Non-Linear |
| Social network (Facebook) | Graph | Non-Linear |
| Priority-based task handling | Heap | Non-Linear |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Linear structures power the **CPU and memory layer** (cache arrays, stacks, queues). Non-linear structures power the **software intelligence layer** (databases, AI, networks). KTU exam questions frequently test this *application mapping*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Address Calculation in a 1-D Array

**Problem Setup:** An array `A` of integers (4 bytes each) has base address $B = 1000$. Find the address of `A[5]`.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
B = 1000, \quad w = 4 \text{ bytes}, \quad i = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard KTU formula.**

$$
\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i \times w
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{Addr}(A[5]) = 1000 + 5 \times 4
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the multiplication.**

$$
5 \times 4 = 20
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final address.**

$$
\text{Addr}(A[5]) = 1000 + 20 = 1020
$$

**Result:** The integer `A[5]` is stored at memory location **1020**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Address Calculation in a 2-D Array (Row-Major)

**Problem Setup:** A 2-D array `A[10][20]` of integers (2 bytes each) has base address $B = 2000$. Find `A[3][5]` using row-major order.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
B = 2000, \quad w = 2, \quad m = 10 \text{ rows}, \quad n = 20 \text{ columns}, \quad i = 3, \quad j = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the row-major formula.**

$$
\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \times n + j) \times w
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the indices.**

$$
\text{Addr}(A[3][5]) = 2000 + (3 \times 20 + 5) \times 2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the inner bracket.**

$$
3 \times 20 = 60, \quad 60 + 5 = 65
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by word size.**

$$
65 \times 2 = 130
$$

**Step 6 — Add to base address.**

$$
\text{Addr}(A[3][5]) = 2000 + 130 = 2130
$$

**Result:** The element `A[3][5]` is located at memory address **2130**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Number of Nodes in a Binary Tree

**Theorem:** A binary tree of height $h$ has at most $2^{h+1} - 1$ nodes.

**Step 1 — Set up the recurrence.** Let $N(h)$ be the maximum number of nodes at height $h$. At each level, a binary tree has at most **2 × (nodes of previous level)**.

$$
N(0) = 1, \quad N(h) = 2 \times N(h-1)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the recurrence by expansion.**

$$
N(1) = 2 \times 1 = 2
$$

$$
N(2) = 2 \times 2 = 4
$$

$$
N(3) = 2 \times 4 = 8
$$

**Step 3 — Generalize the pattern.**

$$
N(h) = 2^{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum across all levels (0 to $h$).**

$$
\text{Total Nodes} = \sum_{k=0}^{h} 2^{k} = 2^{h+1} - 1
$$

This is a **geometric series sum** with first term $a = 1$, ratio $r = 2$, and $h+1$ terms.

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$
\boxed{N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Core 7-Parameter Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is a **direct KTU Part-B favorite**. Memorize for 14-mark questions.

| **Parameter** | **Linear Data Structure** | **Non-Linear Data Structure** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Arrangement of elements** | Sequential, one after another | Hierarchical or networked |
| **Levels of data** | Single level | Multiple levels |
| **Traversal** | Single sequential pass (Begin $\to$ End) | Multiple paths (recursive, BFS, DFS) |
| **Memory utilization** | May waste memory (arrays) or use pointers | Efficient for sparse/irregular data |
| **Implementation complexity** | Easy to implement | Complex (recursion, pointers) |
| **Examples** | Array, Stack, Queue, Linked List | Tree, Graph, Heap, Trie |
| **Time complexity of search** | $O(n)$ for unsorted; $O(\log n)$ for sorted array | $O(\log n)$ BST; $O(V+E)$ graph BFS/DFS |
| **Application domain** | CPU scheduling, memory, simple lists | AI, databases, networks, OS |

---

## 3.5 Operations Complexity Cheat Sheet

| **Data Structure** | **Access** | **Search** | **Insertion** | **Deletion** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Array** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Linked List** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ at head | $O(1)$ at head |
| **Stack** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Queue** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Binary Search Tree** | $O(\log n)$ avg, $O(n)$ worst | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(\log n)$ avg |
| **Hash Table** | N/A | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg |
| **Graph (Adjacency List)** | N/A | $O(V+E)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(E)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Linear Data Structure (Array + Linked List)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["A0 1000"] --> A1["A1 1004"] --> A2["A2 1008"] --> A3["A3 1012"] --> A4["A4 1016"] --> AN["An-1 NULL"]
    style A0 fill:#cfe2ff
    style A1 fill:#cfe2ff
    style A2 fill:#cfe2ff
    style A3 fill:#cfe2ff
    style A4 fill:#cfe2ff
    style AN fill:#f8d7da
```

**Interpretation:** Each node points to exactly **one** next node — the defining property of a linear structure. Traversal must follow the chain sequentially.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Non-Linear Data Structure (Binary Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR["ROOT 50"] --> nodeL["LEFT 30"]
    nodeR --> nodeRt["RIGHT 70"]
    nodeL --> nodeLL["L-LEFT 20"]
    nodeL --> nodeLR["L-RIGHT 40"]
    nodeRt --> nodeRL["R-LEFT 60"]
    nodeRt --> nodeRR["R-RIGHT 80"]
    style nodeR fill:#fff3cd
    style nodeL fill:#d1e7dd
    style nodeRt fill:#d1e7dd
    style nodeLL fill:#f8d7da
    style nodeLR fill:#f8d7da
    style nodeRL fill:#f8d7da
    style nodeRR fill:#f8d7da
```

**Interpretation:** The **root (50)** has **two** children. Each child has its own sub-children. Multiple traversal paths exist — In-order, Pre-order, Post-order, Level-order. This is the hallmark of non-linearity.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Graph (Non-Linear Network)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV1["V1 AHMEDABAD"] --- nodeV2["V2 MUMBAI"]
    nodeV2 --- nodeV3["V3 PUNE"]
    nodeV2 --- nodeV4["V4 DELHI"]
    nodeV3 --- nodeV4["V4 DELHI"]
    nodeV4 --- nodeV5["V5 KOLKATA"]
    nodeV1 --- nodeV4["V4 DELHI"]
    style nodeV1 fill:#cfe2ff
    style nodeV2 fill:#fff3cd
    style nodeV3 fill:#d1e7dd
    style nodeV4 fill:#f8d7da
    style nodeV5 fill:#e2d9f3
```

**Interpretation:** A general graph where **V2 (Mumbai)** connects to three other cities. There is no parent-child hierarchy — this is **peer-to-peer networking**, the second flavor of non-linearity.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph blockLinear["LINEAR DATA STRUCTURES"]
        blockA["ARRAY Static Contiguous"]
        blockB["LINKED LIST Dynamic Pointer Chain"]
        blockC["STACK LIFO Order"]
        blockD["QUEUE FIFO Order"]
    end
    subgraph blockNonLinear["NON-LINEAR DATA STRUCTURES"]
        blockE["TREE Hierarchical Parent-Child"]
        blockF["GRAPH Network of Nodes"]
        blockG["HEAP Priority Tree Structure"]
    end
    blockA --> blockC
    blockB --> blockD
    blockE --> blockG
    blockF --> blockE
    style blockLinear fill:#e7f1ff
    style blockNonLinear fill:#fff4e6
```

**Interpretation:** Shows the architectural grouping used in the **KTU 2024 syllabus**. Linear structures form the **low-level memory tier**; non-linear structures form the **high-level application tier**.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Traversal Path Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph linearPath["LINEAR PATH"]
        dir1["START"] --> dir2["STEP 1"] --> dir3["STEP 2"] --> dir4["STEP 3"] --> dir5["END"]
    end
    subgraph nonLinearPath["NON-LINEAR PATHS"]
        dirA["START ROOT"] --> dirB["LEFT BRANCH"]
        dirA --> dirC["RIGHT BRANCH"]
        dirB --> dirD["LEAF 1"]
        dirB --> dirE["LEAF 2"]
        dirC --> dirF["LEAF 3"]
    end
    style linearPath fill:#d1e7dd
    style nonLinearPath fill:#f8d7da
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** When asked to *explain the difference between linear and non-linear traversal*, draw this exact dual-path diagram. It is worth **2-3 bonus marks** in KTU evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Differentiate between linear and non-linear data structures. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis** | **Linear** | **Non-Linear** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Elements arranged sequentially | Elements arranged hierarchically |
| Levels | Single level | Multiple levels |
| Traversal | Single run Begin to End | Multiple paths |
| Examples | Array, Stack, Queue, Linked List | Tree, Graph, Heap |
| Memory | Mostly contiguous | Pointer-based scattered |

**Examples (1 mark):**
- Linear: Array, Stack
- Non-Linear: Binary Tree, Graph

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - July 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**List the time complexity of search operation in (i) Array, (ii) Binary Search Tree, and (iii) Hash Table.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Array (unsorted):** $O(n)$ — linear scan required.
2. **Binary Search Tree (balanced):** $O(\log n)$ — height-based search.
3. **Hash Table:** $O(1)$ average, $O(n)$ worst case — direct indexing via hash function.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write $O(\log n)$ for Hash Table. This is **incorrect**. Hash Table gives $O(1)$ *average* and $O(n)$ *worst* case. **Lose 1 mark** for missing the word "average."

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Module Internal Choice** is mandatory. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from the same module. Each question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1, Set A]`

#### Part (a) — Explain linear data structures in detail with examples and applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A linear data structure arranges data elements in a sequential order, where each element is connected to its previous and next element. Traversal occurs in a single straight run from the first to the last element.

**2. Types of Linear Data Structures (4 marks):**

**(i) Array** — Collection of homogeneous elements stored in **contiguous memory locations**. Access via index. Supports random access in $O(1)$.
- *Example:* `int marks[50];` to store 50 student marks.
- *Application:* Mathematical matrices, lookup tables.

**(ii) Linked List** — Collection of nodes connected via pointers. Each node has `data` and `next`. Dynamic size.
- *Example:* Music playlist where each song points to the next.
- *Application:* Dynamic memory allocation, symbol tables in compilers.

**(iii) Stack** — Follows **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** principle. Two main operations: `push` and `pop`.
- *Example:* Stack of plates in a cafeteria.
- *Application:* Function call stack, undo operation, parenthesis matching.

**(iv) Queue** — Follows **FIFO (First In, First Out)** principle. Operations: `enqueue` and `dequeue`.
- *Example:* People standing in a ticket counter line.
- *Application:* CPU process scheduling, printer spooling, BFS traversal.

**3. Common Operations (1 mark):**
Traversal, Insertion, Deletion, Searching, Sorting, Merging.

**4. Merits and Demerits (1 mark):**
- *Merit:* Easy to implement, efficient memory use for static data.
- *Demerit:* Insertion/deletion in array is costly ($O(n)$); linked list has no random access.

---

#### Part (b) — Explain non-linear data structures in detail with examples and applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A non-linear data structure arranges elements in a **hierarchical** or **network relationship**, where one element may be linked to multiple predecessors and successors. Traversal can occur in multiple ways.

**2. Types of Non-Linear Data Structures (4 marks):**

**(i) Tree** — A hierarchical structure with a **root** node and **sub-trees** of children. A binary tree has at most two children per node.
- *Example:* File system directory structure, organizational hierarchy.
- *Application:* Database indexing (B-Tree), expression evaluation, decision-making in AI.

**(ii) Graph** — A set of **vertices (V)** connected by **edges (E)**. Can be *directed* or *undirected*, *weighted* or *unweighted*.
- *Example:* Social network (Facebook friends), road map of cities.
- *Application:* Google Maps shortest path, network routing, social network analysis.

**(iii) Heap** — A **complete binary tree** that satisfies the heap property (min-heap or max-heap).
- *Example:* Priority queue implementation.
- *Application:* Priority-based task scheduling, Dijkstra's algorithm.

**3. Traversal Methods (1 mark):**
- **Tree:** In-order, Pre-order, Post-order, Level-order.
- **Graph:** BFS (Breadth-First Search), DFS (Depth-First Search).

**4. Merits and Demerits (1 mark):**
- *Merit:* Efficient for hierarchical/network data; reflects real-world relationships.
- *Demerit:* Complex implementation; recursive algorithms needed; higher memory for pointers.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1, Set B]`

#### Part (a) — Compare linear and non-linear data structures on the basis of any seven parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Tabular Form — 7 marks, 1 mark per correct parameter):**

| **Parameter** | **Linear Data Structure** | **Non-Linear Data Structure** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Arrangement | Sequential, one after another | Hierarchical or networked |
| 2. Levels of organization | Single level | Multiple levels (tree) or flat network (graph) |
| 3. Traversal method | Single sequential run | Multiple paths (BFS, DFS, recursion) |
| 4. Memory layout | Contiguous (array) or pointer-chain | Non-contiguous, pointer-based |
| 5. Implementation complexity | Simple | Complex (recursion-heavy) |
| 6. Examples | Array, Stack, Queue, Linked List | Tree, Graph, Heap |
| 7. Applications | CPU scheduling, memory mgmt, buffers | Databases, AI, networks, OS file systems |
| 8. Search complexity | $O(n)$ unsorted array | $O(\log n)$ BST avg, $O(V+E)$ graph |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical):**
> 1. **No tabular form → -2 marks.** KTU examiners award full marks only for a clean comparison table.
> 2. **Fewer than 7 parameters → Lose 1 mark per missing row.**
> 3. **Mentioning only definitions without examples → Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **Writing `O(1)` for linear search → -1 mark.** It is $O(n)$, not constant time.

---

#### Part (b) — Write a C program to perform in-order traversal of a binary tree and insertion at the rear end of a singly linked list. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) In-Order Traversal of Binary Tree (3.5 marks):**

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *left;
    struct Node *right;
};

void inorder(struct Node *root) {
    if (root != NULL) {
        inorder(root->left);          // Visit left subtree
        printf("%d ", root->data);    // Visit root
        inorder(root->right);         // Visit right subtree
    }
}
```

**Output Order:** Left $\to$ Root $\to$ Right.

**Part (ii) Insertion at Rear of Singly Linked List (3.5 marks):**

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};

void insertRear(struct Node **head, int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    if (*head == NULL) {
        *head = newNode;
        return;
    }
    struct Node *temp = *head;
    while (temp->next != NULL)
        temp = temp->next;
    temp->next = newNode;
}
```

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — must traverse the list to find the last node.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Coding Pitfalls (Lose 1 mark each):**
> 1. Forgetting the **base case** `if (root != NULL)` in in-order traversal → infinite recursion.
> 2. Not handling the **empty list** case in `insertRear` → segmentation fault.
> 3. Forgetting to use `**head` (double pointer) for head modification in linked list insertion.
> 4. Missing `malloc.h` / `stdlib.h` header — KTU evaluators deduct **0.5 mark** for incomplete code.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING (Module 1)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in "Linear vs Non-Linear" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Static vs Dynamic:** Writing "Array is dynamic" is **wrong**. Arrays are **static**; Linked Lists are **dynamic**.
> 2. **Calling Hash Table linear:** Hash Table is **non-linear** in KTU 2024 classification due to chaining.
> 3. **Forgetting examples:** Every definition **must** have at least **2 examples** for full marks.
> 4. **Missing traversal methods:** Tree = In/Pre/Post-order; Graph = BFS/DFS. **Both** must be mentioned.
