> 5. **Ignoring KTU 2024 NEP mapping:** Questions now test **Course Outcomes (CO1: Apply)** — so pure definitions without application/algorithm get **partial credit only**.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize This List**

- ☐ **Linear DS** = Sequential, single-level, one-to-one linkage, single traversal path.
- ☐ **Non-Linear DS** = Hierarchical or networked, multi-level, many-to-many linkage, multiple traversal paths.
- ☐ **Linear examples:** Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue. (Mnemonic: **A-L-S-Q**)
- ☐ **Non-Linear examples:** Tree, Graph, Heap. (Mnemonic: **T-G-H**)
- ☐ **Address of 1-D array:** $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i \times w$.
- ☐ **Address of 2-D row-major:** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \times n + j) \times w$.
- ☐ **Address of 2-D column-major:** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (j \times m + i) \times w$.
- ☐ **Binary tree max nodes** at height $h$: $N_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1$.
- ☐ **Binary tree min nodes** at height $h$: $N_{\min} = h + 1$.
- ☐ **Tree traversals:** In-order (LNR), Pre-order (NLR), Post-order (LRN), Level-order (BFS).
- ☐ **Graph traversals:** BFS uses **Queue**, DFS uses **Stack/Recursion**. Both have $O(V + E)$ complexity.
- ☐ **Stack** = LIFO, **Queue** = FIFO, **Deque** = both ends.
- ☐ **Static** data structure = fixed size at compile time (Array). **Dynamic** = resizable at runtime (Linked List).
- ☐ **Hash Table** is classified as **non-linear** under KTU 2024.
- ☐ **Trees** have $E = V - 1$ (if connected and acyclic). **Graphs** can have any number of edges.
- ☐ **Real-world mapping:** Train = Linear, Family Tree = Non-Linear, Social Network = Non-Linear Graph, CPU Queue = Linear.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 Scheme exam pattern:** 3-mark short answers + 14-mark with sub-parts (7+7), module-internal choice mandatory.
- ☐ **Always include:** Definition + Examples + Operations + Application + Time Complexity for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic, Operations on Data Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic, Operations on Data Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Data Structure** is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data in memory so that it can be accessed and manipulated efficiently according to the operations performed upon it. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, data structures are classified along two orthogonal axes: **storage allocation strategy** (Static vs. Dynamic) and **operational capability** (Linear vs. Non-Linear, Homogeneous vs. Heterogeneous).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recall-ready):**
> "A data structure is a named storage location that can be used to store and organize data. And, a data structure that does not change in size during program execution is called a **static data structure**, while one that grows or shrinks in size during program execution is called a **dynamic data structure**."

### 1.1.1 Static Data Structures

A **Static Data Structure** is one whose size, shape, and memory layout are **fixed at compile time**. The memory is allocated on the **stack segment** (for local arrays) or in the **data segment** (for global/static arrays). Once allocated, the size cannot be altered at runtime.

| Aspect | Property |
|---|---|
| Size determination | **Compile-time** |
| Memory region | **Stack / Data segment** |
| Resizability | **No** (fixed) |
| Memory utilization | Often **wasteful** (over-allocation) |
| Allocation cost | **O(1)** (single instruction) |
| Examples | Arrays (C `int arr[50];`), Records/Structs |

### 1.1.2 Dynamic Data Structures

A **Dynamic Data Structure** is one whose size, shape, and memory occupancy can **change at runtime**. Memory is allocated on the **heap segment** using routines such as `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `realloc()` in C, or `new`/`delete` in C++. Elements are linked using **pointers** or rely on **resizable blocks** (e.g., amortized doubling in dynamic arrays).

| Aspect | Property |
|---|---|
| Size determination | **Run-time** |
| Memory region | **Heap** |
| Resizability | **Yes** (growable/shrinkable) |
| Memory utilization | **Efficient** (pay-as-you-use) |
| Allocation cost | **O(1) amortized** but with cache misses |
| Examples | Linked Lists, Stacks (linked), Trees, Graphs, Dynamic Arrays (`vector`, `ArrayList`) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ The Restaurant Table Analogy**
>
> **Static Data Structure** = A **fixed set of dining tables** in a restaurant. The owner decides at construction time (compile-time) that there will be exactly **20 tables**. If only 5 customers arrive, **15 tables sit idle** (wasted memory). If 25 customers arrive, **5 customers must be turned away** (cannot resize).
>
> **Dynamic Data Structure** = A **smart restaurant** that calls a furniture vendor whenever more seating is needed. When customers leave, tables are returned to the vendor (memory is freed). At any moment, the seating exactly matches the demand — no waste, no shortage. The only "cost" is the phone calls (allocation overhead).

## 1.3 KTU Highlight — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static ≠ Simple ; Dynamic ≠ Complex.**
> The choice is governed by **problem constraints**, not by difficulty. KTU questions frequently test whether you can justify *why* a particular structure is preferred (e.g., "Why use a static array instead of a linked list for matrix multiplication?").

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static vs Dynamic — A Comparative Deep-Dive

### 2.1.1 Memory Layout (Textual Schematic)

```
STATIC STRUCTURE (e.g., int A[100])       DYNAMIC STRUCTURE (e.g., linked list)
+-----------------------+                [Head] -> [Node A] -> [Node B] -> [Node C] -> NULL
|  Stack / Data Segment |                
|  Address: 0x1000      |                Heap fragments:
|  Size: 100 * 4 = 400B |                [Node A @ 0x7A00] -> 0x7A04
|  Contiguous, fixed    |                [Node B @ 0xB300] -> 0xB304
+-----------------------+                [Node C @ 0x5500] -> 0x5504
                                         (Non-contiguous, growable)
```

### 2.1.2 Operational Differences — Why and How

* **Why static is faster for indexed access:** Because the CPU can compute the address of the *i-th* element in **one multiplication + one addition**, exploiting **spatial locality** and **cache line prefetching**. No pointer dereferencing needed.
* **Why dynamic is more flexible:** Because elements can be inserted/erased at *any* logical position without shifting — only pointer rewiring is required (O(1) for singly linked list insertion at head).
* **How static wastes memory:** If the declared maximum (e.g., 1000) is never reached, the unused slots are still reserved — this is called **internal fragmentation** of the array itself.
* **How dynamic can fragment memory:** Frequent allocations and deallocations scatter nodes across the heap, producing **external fragmentation**, which slows down large contiguous allocations.

## 2.2 Operations on Data Structures

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists **eight canonical operations**. Every data structure can be evaluated by how efficiently it implements them.

1. **Traversal** — Visit every element exactly once.
2. **Search** — Locate an element satisfying a given predicate.
3. **Insertion** — Add a new element at a specified position.
4. **Deletion** — Remove an existing element.
5. **Sorting** — Rearrange elements into a specified order.
6. **Merging** — Combine two structures of the same type into one.
7. **Splitting** — Partition a structure into two or more sub-structures.
8. **Update / Access** — Read or modify the value at a given index/key.

### 2.2.1 Detailed Operational Semantics

* **Traversal**: Must be **systematic** (no skipping, no repetition). For linear structures → sequential; for trees → DFS/BFS; for graphs → DFS/BFS with visited-flag.
* **Search**: Two principal strategies — **Linear Search** (O(n), works on unsorted data) and **Binary Search** (O(log n), requires sorted data, used in static arrays).
* **Insertion**:
  * In a **static array**: requires shifting O(n − k) elements to make room → worst-case **O(n)**.
  * In a **dynamic linked list**: requires only pointer rewiring → **O(1)** at head, **O(k)** at position k.
* **Deletion**:
  * In a **static array**: requires shifting O(n − k) elements to close the gap → **O(n)**.
  * In a **dynamic linked list**: requires locating predecessor → **O(1)** if pointer to node is given, else **O(k)**.
* **Sorting**: In-place vs. not-in-place; stable vs. unstable; comparison-based vs. non-comparison-based.
* **Merging**: Used heavily in merge-sort, mergeable heaps, disjoint-set union operations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Static Array | Dynamic Linked List | Dynamic Array (Vector) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Access by index | **O(1)** | **O(n)** (must traverse) | **O(1)** |
| 2 | Search (linear) | **O(n)** | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |
| 3 | Search (binary, sorted) | **O(log n)** | Not applicable | **O(log n)** |
| 4 | Insertion at front | **O(n)** | **O(1)** | **O(n)** amortized **O(1)** |
| 5 | Insertion at end | **O(1)** | **O(n)** (traverse) | **O(1)** amortized |
| 6 | Insertion at middle | **O(n)** | **O(1)** if node given | **O(n)** |
| 7 | Deletion at front | **O(n)** | **O(1)** | **O(n)** |
| 8 | Deletion at end | **O(1)** | **O(n)** | **O(1)** amortized |
| 9 | Memory overhead | None (raw bytes) | **2× or 3×** (next/prev ptrs) | Capacity \geq size, **\leq 2×** slack |
| 10 | Cache locality | **Excellent** (contiguous) | **Poor** (scattered) | **Excellent** |
| 11 | Resizeable | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes** (auto-doubling) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When a question says "Justify the choice of data structure," you must mention **at least one operation** and **one memory/cache consideration**. A one-line answer will be penalized.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Static arrays** are used in embedded systems (firmware) where memory is fixed, in kernel boot code, and in numerical kernels (BLAS) for cache efficiency.
* **Dynamic linked structures** power OS process lists, file-system FAT/B-trees, browser DOM trees, and garbage-collected heaps.
* **Hybrid dynamic arrays** (vectors) back Python lists, Java `ArrayList`, C++ `std::vector` — combining static-array speed with dynamic resizability via **amortized doubling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Address Calculation in a Static 1-D Array

Let `A` be a static array declared as `T A[N]` where each element of type `T` occupies `w` bytes. The base address (address of `A[0]`) is denoted as `Base(A)`. We wish to derive the address of `A[i]` for any valid index `i`.

**Step 1 — Identify the offset.**
The element `A[i]` lies `i` positions after `A[0]`. Each position occupies `w` bytes. Therefore, the byte-offset of `A[i]` relative to `A[0]` is `i \times w` bytes.

**Step 2 — Add the offset to the base address.**
The physical memory address of `A[i]` is therefore:

$$
\text{Addr}(A[i]) \;=\; \text{Base}(A) \;+\; i \cdot w
$$

**Step 3 — Bound-check the formula.**
The valid index range is `0 \leq i < N`. The total memory consumed is `N \cdot w` bytes.

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration.**
Suppose `Base(A) = 1000`, `w = 4` bytes (an `int`), `N = 10`. Compute the address of `A[3]`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addr}(A[3]) &= 1000 + 3 \cdot 4 \\
&= 1000 + 12 \\
&= 1012
\end{aligned}
$$

The total memory footprint is `10 \times 4 = 40` bytes, occupying addresses `1000` through `1039`.

## 3.2 Derivation — Cost of Insertion at Position *k* in a Static Array

Consider an array `A` of current size `n`. We insert a new element `x` at index `k` (0-based). The procedure is:

1. Shift every element from index `k` to `n-1` one position to the right (to make room).
2. Place `x` at index `k`.
3. Update the logical size to `n+1`.

**Step 1 — Count the elements to be shifted.**
Indices `k, k+1, \dots, n-1` are shifted. The number of shifts is:

$$
\text{shifts} \;=\; n \;-\; k
$$

**Step 2 — Express the time-complexity.**
The shift operation is a constant-time memory copy. So total work is proportional to `n − k`.

$$
T_{\text{insert}}(k) \;=\; \Theta(n - k)
$$

**Step 3 — Derive best, average, and worst cases.**

* **Best case** — insert at the end: `k = n` → `T = \Theta(n - n) = \Theta(0) = O(1)`.
* **Worst case** — insert at the beginning: `k = 0` → `T = \Theta(n - 0) = \Theta(n)`.
* **Average case** — assume uniform distribution of `k` over `{0, 1, \dots, n}`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{k=0}^{n} (n - k) \\
&= \frac{1}{n+1} \left[ (n) + (n-1) + \dots + 0 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n+1} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{n}{2} \\
&= \Theta(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

So even on average, insertion into a static array is **linear time**.

## 3.3 Derivation — Cost of Insertion at Head in a Singly Linked List (Dynamic)

A singly linked list node contains `[data \vert next]`. Insertion at the head requires only:

1. Allocate a new node `p` and store `x` in `p->data`.
2. Set `p->next = head`.
3. Update `head = p`.

All three steps involve **a constant number of pointer assignments**, regardless of the current list size:

$$
T_{\text{insert\_head}} \;=\; O(1)
$$

This is the **defining advantage** of dynamic linked structures.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Structure | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Size known, fixed, small | **Static Array** | Lowest overhead, best cache. |
| Size known, fixed, large, cache-sensitive | **Static Array** | Sequential access is fastest. |
| Size unknown, frequent inserts/deletes | **Linked List** | O(1) insert/delete at known positions. |
| Size variable, indexed access needed | **Dynamic Array (Vector)** | O(1) access + amortized O(1) append. |
| Need binary search | **Sorted Static Array** | O(log n) search; linked list cannot binary search efficiently. |
| Frequent insertions at both ends | **Doubly Linked List / Deque** | O(1) at both ends. |
| Tree-shaped data | **Dynamic Tree** | Static arrays cannot represent trees without wasting space. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Static Array Memory Layout (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A100["Base Address 0x1000 - A0"] --> A104["0x1004 - A1"]
    A104 --> A108["0x1008 - A2"]
    A108 --> A112["0x100C - A3"]
    A112 --> A116["0x1010 - A4"]
    A116 --> A120["0x1014 - A5"]
    A120 --> A124["0x1018 - A6"]
    A124 --> A128["0x101C - A7"]
    A128 --> A132["0x1020 - A8"]
    A132 --> A136["0x1024 - A9"]
    A136 --> A140["0x1028 - END OF ARRAY"]

    style A100 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A140 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Dynamic Linked List Memory Layout (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD["HEAD pointer - stack 0x2000"] --> N1["Node1 - heap 0x7A00 data=10 next"]
    N1 --> N2["Node2 - heap 0xB300 data=20 next"]
    N2 --> N3["Node3 - heap 0x5500 data=30 next"]
    N3 --> NULL["NULL - 0x0000"]

    style HEAD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style NULL fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hybrid Dynamic Array (e.g., `std::vector`)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USER["User Code - vectorint v"] --> LOGICAL["Logical Interface - size, operator, push_back"]
    LOGICAL --> MEMMGR["Memory Manager"]

    MEMMGR --> CAPCHK{"Capacity == Size"}
    CAPCHK -- YES --> REALLOC["Allocate new block of 2x capacity - copy old data - free old block"]
    CAPCHK -- NO  --> INPLACE["Write at v size - increment size"]

    REALLOC --> HEAP1["Heap block - new capacity = 16"]
    INPLACE --> HEAP2["Heap block - existing capacity = 16"]

    HEAP1 -. amortized O(1) doubling .-> HEAP2

    style USER fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style LOGICAL fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MEMMGR fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CAPCHK fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style REALLOC fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style INPLACE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Operation Taxonomy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OPS["Operations on Data Structures"] --> T1["Traversal"]
    OPS --> T2["Search"]
    OPS --> T3["Insertion"]
    OPS --> T4["Deletion"]
    OPS --> T5["Sorting"]
    OPS --> T6["Merging"]
    OPS --> T7["Splitting"]
    OPS --> T8["Update - Access"]

    T1 --> T1A["Linear Scan - O n"]
    T1 --> T1B["DFS - BFS - O V+E"]

    T2 --> T2A["Linear Search - O n"]
    T2 --> T2B["Binary Search - O log n"]
    T2 --> T2C["Hash Search - O 1 avg"]

    T3 --> T3A["At head - O 1 list"]
    T3 --> T3B["At tail - O 1 amortized vector"]
    T3 --> T3C["At middle - O n array"]

    T5 --> T5A["Comparison - n log n"]
    T5 --> T5B["Non-comparison - n + k"]

    style OPS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T7 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T8 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: Differentiate between static and dynamic data structures. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Static Data Structure | Dynamic Data Structure |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Fixed at compile time | Can grow/shrink at run time |
| Memory allocation | Stack / data segment | Heap |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Memory utilization | Often wastes reserved space | Efficient, pay-as-you-use |
| Example | Array of 100 integers | Singly Linked List |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Static is preferred when size is known and fixed (e.g., matrix of fixed dimensions). Dynamic is preferred when size is unpredictable (e.g., browser history, undo stack).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: List any four primitive operations performed on data structures and briefly explain any two.**

**Model Answer (Enumeration):**
The four primitive operations are:
1. **Traversal** — visiting each element exactly once.
2. **Insertion** — adding a new element.
3. **Deletion** — removing an existing element.
4. **Searching** — locating an element that satisfies a given condition.

**Explanation (any two, 1 mark each):**
* **Traversal** ensures every element is processed, a prerequisite for operations like printing, summing, or counting.
* **Insertion** in a static array requires shifting elements to maintain contiguity (O(n) worst-case), whereas in a linked list it requires only pointer rewiring (O(1)).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions offer an **internal choice** — answer *either* OR. Both questions below are independent 14-mark problems with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of static and dynamic data structures with suitable examples. Discuss the memory allocation strategy for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a static array of size *n*, derive the time complexity of inserting an element at position *k*. Show the best, worst, and average cases. Compare this with insertion in a singly linked list. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
A static data structure has its size fixed at compile-time; memory is allocated in the stack or data segment. A dynamic data structure can change its size at runtime; memory is allocated on the heap using pointers.

**Examples (2 marks):**
* **Static** — A C array `int marks[60];` reserves 60 integer slots permanently.
* **Dynamic** — A singly linked list of student records, where each node is allocated as students are added.

**Memory allocation strategy (3 marks):**

* **Static allocation:** The compiler emits instructions that reserve a contiguous block of `N × w` bytes at load time. No runtime allocator is involved. Cost is O(1) at program start, but the reserved memory cannot be returned to the OS until the program terminates.
* **Dynamic allocation:** At runtime, the program invokes `malloc(n)` which traverses the heap's free list, finds a block of at least `n` bytes, marks it as used, and returns its address. Deallocation via `free()` returns the block. Cost is O(1) amortized but with possible fragmentation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation for static array (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.2, the time complexity is `T(k) = Θ(n − k)`. The best case is `O(1)` at the tail, worst case is `Θ(n)` at the head, and the average case is `Θ(n/2) = Θ(n)`.

**Comparison with linked list (3 marks):**
In a singly linked list, insertion at the head costs `O(1)` — three pointer assignments: allocate node, set `next` to current head, update head. This is independent of the list size `n`.

| Case | Static Array | Singly Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Insert at head | O(n) | **O(1)** |
| Insert at tail | O(1) | O(n) |
| Insert at middle | O(n) | O(1) if node pointer known |

**Conclusion (mandatory for full marks):** Neither structure dominates universally — the choice depends on the dominant operation pattern.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** What is meant by an operation on a data structure? Explain the traversal and search operations in detail with example complexities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A static array of 10 integers is stored starting at address 2000. Each integer occupies 4 bytes. Calculate the address of `A[4]` and the total memory occupied. Explain with the address-calculation formula. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (1 mark):**
An operation on a data structure is a well-defined algorithmic action that manipulates the stored data — e.g., adding, removing, locating, or rearranging elements.

**Traversal (3 marks):**
Traversal is the systematic visit of every element exactly once.
* In a **static array**, traversal is sequential: `for (i = 0; i < n; i++) visit(A[i]);` — time complexity **O(n)**, space complexity **O(1)**.
* In a **binary tree**, traversal is recursive: in-order, pre-order, post-order, or level-order — all **O(n)** in time, but with **O(h)** recursion stack where `h` is the tree height.

**Search (3 marks):**
Search locates an element satisfying a predicate.
* **Linear search** in an unsorted array — examines each element in turn: **O(n)** time.
* **Binary search** in a sorted array — halves the search space each step: **O(log n)** time.
* **Hash-based search** — uses a hash function to jump directly to a bucket: **O(1)** average, **O(n)** worst-case.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** `Base(A) = 2000`, `w = 4 bytes`, `N = 10`, index `i = 4`.

**Step 1 — Apply the address formula (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addr}(A[i]) &= \text{Base}(A) + i \cdot w \\
\text{Addr}(A[4]) &= 2000 + 4 \cdot 4 \\
&= 2000 + 16 \\
&= 2016
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total memory (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= N \cdot w \\
&= 10 \cdot 4 \\
&= 40 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The array occupies addresses `2000` through `2039`.

**Step 3 — Explain (2 marks):**
The formula works because static arrays are **contiguous**: each element is placed exactly `w` bytes after the previous one. The CPU therefore needs only one multiplication and one addition to reach `A[i]`, giving `O(1)` random access.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "static" with "global".** A variable can be global **and** dynamic (e.g., `int *p = malloc(1000);`). The KTU board expects you to distinguish *storage class* from *allocation strategy*.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention worst-case AND average-case** time complexities. A common 7-mark question asks "analyze the complexity of insertion" — answering only the worst case earns at most 4/7.
> 3. **Writing "linked list is better than array"** without justification. Both boards and modern OS engineers want a *trade-off analysis*. Always state which operations dominate.
> 4. **Ignoring cache behavior.** Mentioning L1/L2 cache locality in a 14-mark answer for static arrays is a hallmark of a top-scoring answer.
> 5. **Mixing up address-of-element vs. address-of-byte.** In a 4-byte integer array, `A[2]` is at `Base + 2 × 4 = Base + 8`, **not** `Base + 2`. Many students skip the multiplier and lose 2 marks immediately.
> 6. **Skipping the formula derivation** in address-calculation questions. Even if the answer is right, the board deducts 1–2 marks if the derivation steps are absent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**
>
> * Static data structures → size **fixed at compile time**, allocated on **stack/data segment** (e.g., C arrays).
> * Dynamic data structures → size **variable at run time**, allocated on **heap** via `malloc/new` (e.g., linked lists, trees).
> * Static pros: **O(1) random access**, cache-friendly, simple.
> * Static cons: **wastes memory** if underused, **cannot grow**.
> * Dynamic pros: **growable/shrinkable**, efficient memory use.
> * Dynamic cons: **pointer overhead**, no random access, possible fragmentation.
> * **Eight canonical operations**: traversal, search, insertion, deletion, sorting, merging, splitting, update/access.
> * Static array insertion at position *k* costs **Θ(n − k)**; worst case **O(n)** at head; best case **O(1)** at tail.
> * Linked list insertion at head costs **O(1)**; at tail costs **O(n)** without a tail pointer.
> * Address formula for static array: `Addr(A[i]) = Base(A) + i × w`.
> * **Always mention** both time *and* space complexity, worst *and* average case.
> * **Justify** your choice of data structure with operational AND memory reasons.
> * Hybrid dynamic arrays (vectors) combine **O(1) access** with **amortized O(1) append** via capacity doubling.
> * **Cache locality** is the silent tie-breaker in performance-critical engineering applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asymptotic Notations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Asymptotic Notations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Asymptotic Notations?

In the formal language of algorithm analysis (KTU 2024 Scheme — *PERAT751*), **Asymptotic Notations** are the mathematical language used to classify the **runtime** or **space consumption** of an algorithm as the input size `n` grows *toward infinity*. Because we cannot physically measure "infinite" input on a finite machine, we use these notations to describe the **limiting behavior** (i.e., the *asymptote*) of a complexity function $f(n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Asymptotic notations are mathematical symbols that represent the order of growth of functions, describing how the running time (or space) of an algorithm approaches a limiting value as the input size `n` becomes arbitrarily large.*

---

## 1.2 The Three Primary Notations

The KTU syllabus (Module 1) emphasizes three core notations, plus two "little" variants for tighter bounds.

### (a) Big-Oh — $O$ — *Upper Bound* (Worst-Case Ceiling)
A function $f(n)$ is $O(g(n))$ if $f(n)$ grows **no faster than** $g(n)$ beyond some point $n_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Think of $O$ as a **speed limit on a highway**. Just because the sign says "80 km/h" doesn't mean every car drives at 80. Some go 60, some go 40. Big-Oh is that *upper speed cap* — your algorithm will *never cost more* than $g(n)$ operations (asymptotically).

### (b) Big-Omega — $\Omega$ — *Lower Bound* (Best-Case Floor)
A function $f(n)$ is $\Omega(g(n))$ if $f(n)$ grows **no slower than** $g(n)$ beyond some point $n_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** $\Omega$ is the **minimum salary guarantee** in a job contract. The algorithm will earn *at least* $g(n)$ in computational cost — it cannot be cheaper than this in the worst-asymptotic sense.

### (c) Big-Theta — $\Theta$ — *Tight Bound* (Exact Order)
A function $f(n)$ is $\Theta(g(n))$ if $f(n)$ grows **at the same rate as** $g(n)$ (i.e., it is sandwiched between $c_1 \cdot g(n)$ and $c_2 \cdot g(n)$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** $\Theta$ is the **exact tailored suit**. The algorithm is *neither oversized nor undersized* — it fits $g(n)$ perfectly from both above and below.

---

## 1.3 The Two "Little" Variants

| Notation | Meaning | Use Case in KTU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $o(g(n))$ | **Strict** upper bound (always less than) | Proving an algorithm is *strictly* better |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | **Strict** lower bound (always greater than) | Rare; used in adversarial lower bounds |

---

## 1.4 Best, Worst, and Average Case Analysis

For any input of size $n$, algorithms behave differently depending on the arrangement of data:

* **Best Case ($T_{\text{best}}(n)$):** The minimum cost — usually the *most favorable* input (e.g., already-sorted array for insertion sort).
* **Worst Case ($T_{\text{worst}}(n)$):** The maximum cost — the *most adversarial* input. **This is the one KTU examiners focus on.**
* **Average Case ($T_{\text{avg}}(n)$):** Expected cost over all possible inputs, weighted by probability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** When a question says *"analyze the time complexity"*, the default expectation is the **Worst Case**, expressed as an asymptotic bound. Best/Average cases are only asked when the question explicitly demands them.

---

## 1.5 Why Do We Use Asymptotic Notations?

The KTU 2024 OBE framework stresses that asymptotic analysis **abstracts away**:
* **Hardware differences** (CPU speed, RAM, cache).
* **Constant factors** (e.g., a $5n$ loop is considered the *same order* as a $10n$ loop).
* **Low-order terms** (e.g., $n^2 + 100n + 500 \rightarrow \Theta(n^2)$).

This allows **machine-independent comparison** of algorithms, which is the cornerstone of Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Mathematical Definitions (KTU Board Standard)

Let $f(n)$ and $g(n)$ be positive functions for sufficiently large $n$.

### Big-Oh (Upper Bound)
$$
f(n) = O(g(n))
$$

$$
\iff \exists \, c > 0 \;\text{and}\; n_0 \ge 0 \;\text{such that}\; 0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

### Big-Omega (Lower Bound)
$$
f(n) = \Omega(g(n))
$$

$$
\iff \exists \, c > 0 \;\text{and}\; n_0 \ge 0 \;\text{such that}\; 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

### Big-Theta (Tight Bound)
$$
f(n) = \Theta(g(n))
$$

$$
\iff \exists \, c_1, c_2 > 0 \;\text{and}\; n_0 \ge 0 \;\text{such that}\; c_1 \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n) \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

### Little-Oh (Strict Upper Bound)
$$
f(n) = o(g(n))
$$

$$
\iff \forall \, c > 0,\; \exists \, n_0 \ge 0 \;\text{such that}\; 0 \le f(n) < c \cdot g(n) \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

> Equivalently: $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0$.

### Little-Omega (Strict Lower Bound)
$$
f(n) = \omega(g(n))
$$

$$
\iff \forall \, c > 0,\; \exists \, n_0 \ge 0 \;\text{such that}\; 0 \le c \cdot g(n) < f(n) \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

> Equivalently: $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master cheat-sheet** you should memorize for the ESE exam. It is derived from KTU past papers (2019–2024 Scheme).

### Table 2.1 — Master Notation Cheat-Sheet

| Notation | Formal Quantifier | Meaning | Limit Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $O(g(n))$ | $\exists\, c, n_0$ | $f \le c \cdot g$ | $\limsup \frac{f}{g} < \infty$ |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | $\exists\, c, n_0$ | $f \ge c \cdot g$ | $\liminf \frac{f}{g} > 0$ |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | $\exists\, c_1, c_2, n_0$ | $c_1 g \le f \le c_2 g$ | $0 < \lim \frac{f}{g} < \infty$ |
| $o(g(n))$ | $\forall\, c$ | $f < c \cdot g$ | $\lim \frac{f}{g} = 0$ |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | $\forall\, c$ | $f > c \cdot g$ | $\lim \frac{f}{g} = \infty$ |

### Table 2.2 — Standard Function Growth Hierarchy

| Function | Notation Name | Common Algorithm Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $1$ | Constant | Array indexing |
| $\log n$ | Logarithmic | Binary Search |
| $n$ | Linear | Linear Search |
| $n \log n$ | Linearithmic | Merge Sort, Quick Sort (avg) |
| $n^2$ | Quadratic | Bubble, Selection, Insertion Sort |
| $n^3$ | Cubic | Naive Matrix Multiply |
| $2^n$ | Exponential | Recursive Fibonacci |
| $n!$ | Factorial | Brute-force TSP |

> **Growth rule:** $1 \prec \log n \prec n \prec n \log n \prec n^2 \prec n^3 \prec 2^n \prec n!$

### Table 2.3 — Useful Algebraic Limits (for $o$, $\omega$ proofs)

| Expression | Limit Value | Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{\log n}{n}$ | $0$ | $\log n = o(n)$ |
| $\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{n}{n^2}$ | $0$ | $n = o(n^2)$ |
| $\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{n^k}{2^n}$ | $0$ | $n^k = o(2^n)$ |
| $\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{2^n}{n!}$ | $0$ | $2^n = o(n!)$ |
| $\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{n!}{n^n}$ | $0$ | $n! = o(n^n)$ |

---

## 2.3 Properties Used by KTU Examiners

* **Transitivity:** If $f = O(g)$ and $g = O(h)$, then $f = O(h)$. (Holds for all 5 notations.)
* **Reflexivity:** $f = O(f)$, $f = \Omega(f)$, $f = \Theta(f)$.
* **Symmetry:** $f = O(g) \iff g = \Omega(f)$.
* **Transpose Symmetry:** $f = o(g) \iff g = \omega(f)$.
* **Constant Drop:** $O(c \cdot f(n)) = O(f(n))$ for any constant $c > 0$.
* **Lower-order Drop:** $O(f(n) + g(n)) = O(\max(f(n), g(n)))$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, asymptotic analysis is the **screening tool** for system design:
* A database query planner uses $O(n \log n)$ vs $O(n^2)$ comparisons to choose between merge-join and nested-loop join.
* A routing engine in networking rejects any algorithm worse than $O(E \log V)$ for large graphs (Dijkstra's bound).
* Embedded systems engineers insist on $\Theta(1)$ or $O(\log n)$ guarantees to meet **hard real-time deadlines** (e.g., airbag controllers, pacemakers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example A — Proving $3n + 2 = O(n)$

We must show: there exist constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 \ge 0$ such that $3n + 2 \le c \cdot n$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** Choose any convenient constant, e.g., $c = 4$.

We must show that $3n + 2 \le 4n$ for sufficiently large $n$.

**Step 2.** Rearrange the inequality:
$$
3n + 2 \le 4n
$$

$$
2 \le 4n - 3n
$$

$$
2 \le n
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore, the inequality $3n + 2 \le 4n$ holds whenever $n \ge 2$. So we choose $n_0 = 2$.

**Step 4.** Conclude formally:
$$
\exists\, c = 4,\; n_0 = 2 \;\text{such that}\; 3n + 2 \le 4n \;\;\forall\, n \ge 2
$$

$$
\therefore \quad 3n + 2 = O(n). \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — Proving $3n + 2 = \Omega(n)$

We must show: $\exists\, c > 0,\; n_0$ such that $3n + 2 \ge c \cdot n$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

**Step 1.** Choose $c = 3$.

**Step 2.** For all $n \ge 1$:
$$
3n + 2 \ge 3n + 0 \ge 3n
$$

**Step 3.** The inequality holds for any $n_0 \ge 1$, with $c = 3$.

**Step 4.** Combine results: Since $3n + 2 = O(n)$ **and** $3n + 2 = \Omega(n)$, by definition:
$$
3n + 2 = \Theta(n). \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C — Using the Limit Method for $n^2$ vs $n \log n$

Determine whether $n \log n = O(n^2)$, $\Omega(n^2)$, or $\Theta(n^2)$.

**Step 1.** Compute the limit:
$$
L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \log n}{n^2}
$$

**Step 2.** Simplify:
$$
L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\log n}{n}
$$

**Step 3.** Apply L'Hôpital's Rule (treating log as natural log):
$$
L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\frac{1}{n}}{1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} = 0
$$

**Step 4.** Interpret:
* Since $L = 0$, we have $n \log n = o(n^2)$.
* Since $L = 0 < \infty$, we *also* have $n \log n = O(n^2)$.
* Since $L \ne \infty$, we do **NOT** have $n \log n = \Omega(n^2)$.

**Conclusion:** $n \log n$ grows strictly slower than $n^2$, so $n \log n = O(n^2)$ but not $\Theta(n^2)$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example D — Proving $n^2 \ne O(n)$ (Negation Proof)

We want to prove $n^2$ is *not* $O(n)$.

**Step 1.** Assume the contrary: $n^2 = O(n)$. Then $\exists\, c, n_0$ such that:
$$
n^2 \le c \cdot n \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

**Step 2.** Divide both sides by $n > 0$:
$$
n \le c \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

**Step 3.** But $n$ grows unboundedly, so no single constant $c$ can satisfy $n \le c$ for *all* $n \ge n_0$.

**Step 4.** **Contradiction.** Therefore, the assumption is false:
$$
n^2 \ne O(n). \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Complexity Functions at $n = 10^6$

| Function | Value at $n = 10^6$ | Practical Verdict (on 10$^9$ ops/sec machine) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\log_2 n$ | $\approx 20$ | Instantaneous |
| $n$ | $10^6$ | $\approx 0.001$ s |
| $n \log n$ | $\approx 2 \cdot 10^7$ | $\approx 0.02$ s |
| $n^2$ | $10^{12}$ | $\approx 1000$ s ($\sim 17$ min) |
| $n^3$ | $10^{18}$ | $\approx 31.7$ years |
| $2^n$ | $2^{10^6}$ | Astronomically large — impossible |

> **Take-away:** Crossing from $n^2$ to $n^3$ moves the problem from "minutes" to "decades" — illustrating why asymptotic analysis is not academic, but **mission-critical**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Notation Hierarchy & Equivalences

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Function f(n)"] --> node2["Upper Bound Family"]
    node1 --> node3["Tight Bound"]
    node1 --> node4["Lower Bound Family"]

    node2 --> nodeA1["O(g) -- Big-Oh -- Non-strict upper"]
    node2 --> nodeA2["o(g) -- Little-oh -- Strict upper"]

    node3 --> nodeB1["THETA(g) -- Big-Theta -- Exact Order"]

    node4 --> nodeC1["OMEGA(g) -- Big-Omega -- Non-strict lower"]
    node4 --> nodeC2["omega(g) -- Little-omega -- Strict lower"]

    nodeA1 -. "exists c, n0" .-> nodeB1
    nodeB1 -. "exists c, n0" .-> nodeC1
    nodeA2 -. "for all c > 0" .-> nodeA1
    nodeC1 -. "for all c > 0" .-> nodeC2

    style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeB1 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> * $\Theta$ sits **between** $O$ and $\Omega$.
> * $o$ is a *stricter* version of $O$; $\omega$ is a *stricter* version of $\Omega$.
> * All five notations are *upper-level concepts* that describe **the same function** $f(n)$ from different perspectives.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Growth-Rate Hierarchy of Complexity Classes

```mermaid
graph LR
    level1["O(1) -- Constant"] --> level2["O(log n) -- Log"]
    level2 --> level3["O(n) -- Linear"]
    level3 --> level4["O(n log n) -- Linearithmic"]
    level4 --> level5["O(n^2) -- Quadratic"]
    level5 --> level6["O(n^3) -- Cubic"]
    level6 --> level7["O(2^n) -- Exponential"]
    level7 --> level8["O(n!) -- Factorial"]

    level1 -. "FASTEST" .-> level8
    level8 -. "SLOWEST" .-> level1

    style level1 fill:#00FF00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style level2 fill:#7FFF00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style level3 fill:#FFFF00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style level4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style level5 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style level6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    level7 fill:#FF0000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style level8 fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF

    style level8 stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
```

> **Each arrow means "faster growth".** Moving one step right means the function eventually outgrows everything to its left. This is the **asymptotic ladder**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Best / Worst / Average Case Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input["Input of size n"] --> arr["All possible arrangements"]

    arr --> best["Best Case T_best n"]
    arr --> avg["Average Case T_avg n"]
    arr --> worst["Worst Case T_worst n"]

    best --> Asymp1["OMEGA g n -- Lower bound"]
    avg --> Asymp2["THETA or O -- Typical"]
    worst --> Asymp3["O g n -- Upper bound"]

    style input fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style arr fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style best fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style avg fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style worst fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Asymp1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Asymp2 fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Asymp3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Engineering meaning:** The **best case** is rarely useful (an adversarial user can always break it); the **average case** is hard to compute (requires probability distribution over inputs); the **worst case** is the **guaranteed promise** — hence KTU's emphasis on $O(\cdot)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Big-Oh notation. Prove that $5n^2 + 3n + 10 = O(n^2)$.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
$f(n) = O(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that $0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

**Proof (2 marks):** We must find $c$ and $n_0$ such that $5n^2 + 3n + 10 \le c \cdot n^2$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

For $n \ge 1$, observe:
* $3n \le 3n^2$ (since $n \le n^2$)
* $10 \le 10n^2$ (since $1 \le n^2$)

Adding:
$$
5n^2 + 3n + 10 \le 5n^2 + 3n^2 + 10n^2 = 18n^2
$$

Choosing $c = 18$ and $n_0 = 1$ satisfies the definition. Hence $5n^2 + 3n + 10 = O(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Best Case, Worst Case, and Average Case time complexity with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Best Case (1 mark):** Minimum running time over all inputs of size $n$. *Example:* In Linear Search, the target element is at the first position — cost = $1 = \Omega(1)$.
* **Worst Case (1 mark):** Maximum running time over all inputs of size $n$. *Example:* In Linear Search, the target is absent — cost = $n = O(n)$.
* **Average Case (1 mark):** Expected running time, averaged over all possible inputs weighted by probability. *Example:* Linear Search average $= (n+1)/2 = \Theta(n)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the different asymptotic notations used to represent the time complexity of an algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that $10n^3 + 4n + 7 = O(n^3)$ and $10n^3 + 4n + 7 \ne O(n^2)$. Justify using the formal definition of Big-Oh. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — Asymptotic Notations Diagram & Explanation

**Step 1 — Conceptual Introduction (2 marks):**
Asymptotic notations describe how the running time $T(n)$ of an algorithm behaves as $n \to \infty$, abstracting away hardware and constant factors. The five notations are $O, \Omega, \Theta, o, \omega$.

**Step 2 — Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeF["f(n)"] --> nodeO["O(g) -- Upper Bound -- f ≤ c·g"]
    nodeF --> nodeTheta["THETA(g) -- Tight -- c1·g ≤ f ≤ c2·g"]
    nodeF --> nodeOmega["OMEGA(g) -- Lower Bound -- f ≥ c·g"]

    nodeO -. "exists c, n0" .-> nodeTheta
    nodeTheta -. "exists c, n0" .-> nodeOmega

    nodeO --> nodeLittleO["o(g) -- strict f < c·g for ALL c"]
    nodeOmega --> nodeLittleOmega["omega(g) -- strict f > c·g for ALL c"]

    style nodeF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeO fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeTheta fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeOmega fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeLittleO fill:#4682B4,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
    style nodeLittleOmega fill:#A0522D,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
```

**Step 3 — Detailed Explanation (2 marks):**
* $O(g)$: $f$ is *at most* a constant multiple of $g$ for large $n$ — represents the **worst-case ceiling**.
* $\Omega(g)$: $f$ is *at least* a constant multiple of $g$ for large $n$ — represents the **best-case floor**.
* $\Theta(g)$: $f$ is *sandwiched* between two constant multiples of $g$ — represents the **tight, exact order**.
* $o(g)$ and $\omega(g)$: stricter variants useful in theoretical proofs.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — Two Proofs

**Part 1: Prove $10n^3 + 4n + 7 = O(n^3)$ (3 marks)**

By definition, we need $\exists\, c > 0,\, n_0 \ge 0$ such that:
$$
10n^3 + 4n + 7 \le c \cdot n^3 \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

Choose $c = 21$ and $n_0 = 1$. For all $n \ge 1$:
* $4n \le 4n^3$ (since $n \le n^3$)
* $7 \le 7n^3$ (since $1 \le n^3$)

Therefore:
$$
10n^3 + 4n + 7 \le 10n^3 + 4n^3 + 7n^3 = 21n^3
$$

Thus, with $c = 21$ and $n_0 = 1$, the inequality holds. $\therefore 10n^3 + 4n + 7 = O(n^3)$. $\blacksquare$

**Part 2: Prove $10n^3 + 4n + 7 \ne O(n^2)$ (4 marks)**

Assume the contrary: $10n^3 + 4n + 7 = O(n^2)$.

Then $\exists\, c > 0,\, n_0$ such that:
$$
10n^3 + 4n + 7 \le c \cdot n^2 \;\;\forall\, n \ge n_0
$$

Divide both sides by $n^2$ (valid since $n > 0$):
$$
10n + \frac{4}{n} + \frac{7}{n^2} \le c
$$

As $n \to \infty$, the LHS $\to \infty$. Hence, for *any* fixed $c$, there exists a sufficiently large $n$ that **violates** the inequality. This is a **contradiction**.

Therefore, our assumption is false:
$$
10n^3 + 4n + 7 \ne O(n^2). \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A/b):** Many students write *"because $n^3 > n^2$, so $n^3 \ne O(n^2)$"* and lose **3 marks**. The KTU Board strictly requires the **formal contradiction argument** using the definition — show that dividing by $n^2$ leads to $10n \le c$, which is impossible for unbounded $n$. Always name your $c$ and $n_0$ explicitly in the positive proof; always invoke *contradiction* in the negative proof.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define $O$, $\Omega$, and $\Theta$ notations formally. State and prove the relationship $\Theta(g(n)) = O(g(n)) \cap \Omega(g(n))$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Arrange the following functions in increasing order of growth rate using asymptotic notation: $n^2,\; n \log n,\; \log n,\; n!,\; 2^n,\; n,\; 1,\; n^3$. Justify each step using limit tests. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Formal Definitions + Intersection Theorem

**Step 1 — Formal Definitions (3 marks):**

Let $f(n), g(n): \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}^+$.

* $f(n) = O(g(n))$ iff $\exists\, c > 0,\, n_0$ such that $f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.
* $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ iff $\exists\, c > 0,\, n_0$ such that $f(n) \ge c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.
* $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ iff $\exists\, c_1, c_2 > 0,\, n_0$ such that $c_1 \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

**Step 2 — Theorem (1 mark):**
$$
\Theta(g(n)) = O(g(n)) \cap \Omega(g(n))
$$

**Step 3 — Proof ($\supseteq$ direction, 1.5 marks):**
Assume $f \in \Theta(g)$. Then $\exists\, c_1, c_2, n_0$ with:
$$
c_1 g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 g(n)
$$

The left inequality implies $f \in \Omega(g)$, the right implies $f \in O(g)$. So $f \in O(g) \cap \Omega(g)$.

**Step 4 — Proof ($\subseteq$ direction, 1.5 marks):**
Assume $f \in O(g)$ **and** $f \in \Omega(g)$. Then there exist $c_2, n_1$ for the first and $c_1, n_2$ for the second. Let $n_0 = \max(n_1, n_2)$. Then for all $n \ge n_0$:
$$
c_1 g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 g(n)
$$

So $f \in \Theta(g)$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Ordering Functions via Limits

We apply $\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{f_i}{f_j}$ comparisons. If the limit is $0$, then $f_i = o(f_j)$.

**Step 1.** Start with the smallest functions. We know $\log n = o(n)$. Hence:
$$
1 < \log n < n < n \log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n!
$$

**Step 2 — Justify $\log n < n$ (1 mark):**
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{\log n}{n} \stackrel{\text{L'H}}{=} \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1/n}{1} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Justify $n < n \log n$ (1 mark):** Divide by $n$:
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{n}{n \log n} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1}{\log n} = 0
$$

So $n = o(n \log n)$, meaning $n < n \log n$.

**Step 4 — Justify $n \log n < n^2$ (1 mark):**
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{n \log n}{n^2} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{\log n}{n} = 0
$$

So $n \log n = o(n^2)$.

**Step 5 — Justify $n^2 < n^3$ (1 mark):**
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{n^2}{n^3} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1}{n} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Justify $n^3 < 2^n$ (1 mark):**
By L'Hôpital, $\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{n^3}{2^n} = 0$ (differentiating gives $\dfrac{3n^2}{2^n \ln 2} \to 0$). So $n^3 = o(2^n)$.

**Step 7 — Justify $2^n < n!$ (1 mark):** For $n \ge 4$, $n!$ has at least $n/2$ factors each $\ge 2$, so $n! \ge 2^{n/2}$, giving $\lim \frac{2^n}{n!} = 0$.

**Final Ordered Hierarchy (1 mark):**
$$
1 \;\prec\; \log n \;\prec\; n \;\prec\; n \log n \;\prec\; n^2 \;\prec\; n^3 \;\prec\; 2^n \;\prec\; n!
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B/b):** Students often write the ordering **without limit computations** and lose up to **5 marks**. The Board expects **every adjacent pair** to be justified with either the formal definition or a limit test. Also, do not skip the step showing *why* $n^3 \prec 2^n$ — use L'Hôpital explicitly on $\frac{n^3}{2^n}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

* $\mathbf{O(g)}$: **Upper** bound (worst-case ceiling). Use: $\exists c, n_0$.
* $\mathbf{\Omega(g)}$: **Lower** bound (best-case floor). Use: $\exists c, n_0$.
* $\mathbf{\Theta(g)}$: **Tight** bound (exact order). Use: $\exists c_1, c_2, n_0$.
* $\mathbf{o(g)}$: **Strict upper** bound — $\forall c > 0$, $f < c \cdot g$. Equivalently, limit is $0$.
* $\mathbf{\omega(g)}$: **Strict lower** bound — $\forall c > 0$, $f > c \cdot g$. Equivalently, limit is $\infty$.
* **$\Theta$ theorem:** $\Theta(g) = O(g) \cap \Omega(g)$.
* **Symmetry:** $f = O(g) \iff g = \Omega(f)$.
* **Constant drop rule:** $O(5n) = O(n)$ — constants and lower-order terms are **dropped** in asymptotic analysis.
* **Max-rule:** $O(f + g) = O(\max(f, g))$.
* **Growth ladder (in increasing order):**
  $$
  1 \;\prec\; \log n \;\prec\; n \;\prec\; n \log n \;\prec\; n^2 \;\prec\; n^3 \;\prec\; 2^n \;\prec\; n!
  $$
* **Limit rule of thumb:** If $\lim \frac{f}{g} = L$:
  * $L = 0 \Rightarrow f = o(g)$ (and $f = O(g)$).
  * $0 < L < \infty \Rightarrow f = \Theta(g)$.
  * $L = \infty \Rightarrow f = \omega(g)$ (and $f = \Omega(g)$).
* **Worst case is the default** when a question says "analyze the algorithm" without specifying the case.
* **Always specify $c$ and $n_0$** explicitly when proving a positive $O$ or $\Omega$ statement.
* **For negations** ($f \ne O(g)$), use **proof by contradiction**: divide, show unbounded growth, conclude.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Notion of Recursive Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Notion of Recursive Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A *recursive algorithm* is a computational procedure that solves a problem by **calling itself** on one or more smaller instances of the same problem, until a **base case** (a trivially solvable smallest instance) is reached.

A recursive algorithm must always contain **two essential structural components**:
1. **Base Case (Termination Condition):** The stopping criterion that returns a direct, non-recursive answer.
2. **Recursive Case (Reduction Step):** The branch in which the algorithm invokes itself with arguments that are *strictly smaller* (in some well-founded order) than the original input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Russian Matryoshka Doll:**
> Imagine a set of nested wooden dolls. To "solve" the outermost doll, you open it and find a *smaller doll of the same kind* inside. You repeat the same action on the smaller doll. Eventually you reach the **smallest solid doll** that cannot be opened — this is the *base case*. Now you "unwind" your way back out. A recursive algorithm behaves identically: it drills down to a base case, then unwinds, often accumulating a final answer as it returns.

**Simple Mathematical Anchor — Factorial:**
The factorial $n!$ is the canonical textbook example of a recursive definition:
$$n! = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \text{ (base case)} \\ n \times (n-1)! & \text{if } n > 0 \text{ (recursive case)} \end{cases}$$
Notice how the right-hand side of the recursive case contains the **same function** $!$ applied to a *strictly smaller* argument $(n-1)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU PERAT751 Module 1, the *notion* of recursion is studied before its use in specific data structures. The board expects you to (i) state the definition, (ii) identify base and recursive cases, (iii) trace the call stack, and (iv) derive the recurrence relation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Recursive Algorithm

Every recursive algorithm has a well-defined structure. The following logic steps describe how the machine actually executes a recursive call:

- **Step 1 — Argument Validation:** The function checks the current arguments against the base case condition.
- **Step 2 — Base-Case Return:** If the condition is met, a constant-time value is returned. The current stack frame is *popped* immediately.
- **Step 3 — Recursive Decomposition:** Otherwise, the problem is reduced into one or more sub-problems whose inputs are strictly smaller in some well-founded measure.
- **Step 4 — Self-Invocation:** The function calls itself for each sub-problem. Each call creates a new **stack frame** on the program call stack.
- **Step 5 — Composition:** The results returned by the sub-calls are combined (e.g., summed, multiplied) using the combining operator (e.g., $+$, $\times$, $\min$).
- **Step 6 — Return:** The composed value is returned to the caller, and the current stack frame is destroyed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why recursion works mathematically (Well-Founded Induction):**
> A recursive algorithm is justified by the principle of *mathematical induction*. Because every recursive call strictly decreases a non-negative integer parameter (e.g., $n \to n-1$, or size of array), the chain of calls must terminate. This guarantee is the **Well-Founded Ordering Principle**.

### 2.2 Classification of Recursion

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Direct Recursion** | Function calls itself directly | `fact(n)` calls `fact(n-1)` |
| **Indirect Recursion** | Function A calls B, B calls A | `f1 → f2 → f1` (mutual recursion) |
| **Tail Recursion** | Recursive call is the *last* operation | `gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b)` |
| **Non-Tail Recursion** | Recursive call is followed by work | `fact(n) = n * fact(n-1)` (multiplication after) |
| **Single (Linear) Recursion** | Exactly one recursive call per invocation | `fact`, `linear search` |
| **Multiple (Tree) Recursion** | Two or more recursive calls per invocation | `Fibonacci`, `Hanoi` |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Recurrence Cheat Sheet

The **time complexity** $T(n)$ of a recursive algorithm is expressed as a *recurrence relation* — an equation defining $T(n)$ in terms of $T$ of smaller arguments. Below is the high-yield formula table:

| Algorithm Pattern | Recurrence Relation | Solution (Asymptotic) | Space (Call Stack) |
|-------------------|---------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Linear recursion (e.g., factorial, sum) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Binary recursion (e.g., naive Fibonacci) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c$ | $\Theta(\phi^n) \approx \Theta(2^n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Halving recursion (e.g., binary search) | $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| Divide & Conquer (e.g., merge sort) | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| Tower of Hanoi | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(2^n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Power (naive) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Power (fast exponentiation) | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** In the above, the constant $c > 0$ denotes the cost of the *non-recursive* work done in a single call (comparisons, arithmetic, etc.). The base case is typically $T(0) = T(1) = d$ for some constant $d \geq 0$.

### 2.4 Why and How Recursion Is Used in Engineering

- **Inherently recursive problems** (tree/graph traversals, BFS/DFS, divide-and-conquer, backtracking) are most naturally expressed recursively.
- **Compiler / OS design:** Recursive descent parsers use mutual recursion; operating systems implement process control using stack-based call frames.
- **Algorithmic problem-solving:** Tower of Hanoi, fractals, dynamic programming memoization all rely on the recursive paradigm.
- **Real-time cost:** Recursion carries a hidden *space cost* equal to the maximum call-stack depth. For very deep recursions (e.g., $n > 10^4$), iterative reformulation is often mandated in safety-critical systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time Complexity of Recursive Factorial

We define a function `fact(n)` that returns $n!$. Let $T(n)$ denote the number of elementary operations performed by `fact(n)`.

**Recurrence Formulation:**

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} d & \text{if } n \leq 1 \quad (\text{base case}) \\ T(n-1) + c & \text{if } n > 1 \quad (\text{recursive case}) \end{cases}$$

where $c$ is the cost of the multiplication $n \times \text{result}$ and the comparison, and $d$ is the cost of returning $1$.

**Step-by-Step Unrolling (Telescoping Method):**

We substitute the recursive case into itself repeatedly, each time decreasing the argument by $1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + c \\
&= \bigl[T(n-2) + c\bigr] + c \\
&= T(n-2) + 2c \\
&= \bigl[T(n-3) + c\bigr] + 2c \\
&= T(n-3) + 3c \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(n-k) + kc
\end{aligned}
$$

After $k$ unrollings, the argument has decreased by $k$. We choose $k = n - 1$ so that the argument becomes $1$ (the base case):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\bigl(n-(n-1)\bigr) + (n-1)c \\
&= T(1) + (n-1)c \\
&= d + (n-1)c \\
&= cn - c + d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic Conclusion:**

$$T(n) = cn - c + d = \Theta(n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The dominant term is $cn$. The lower-order constants $-c$ and $+d$ are absorbed by the $\Theta$ notation. Hence, *recursive factorial is linear in time*. Its **space** complexity is also $\Theta(n)$ because $n$ stack frames coexist on the call stack at the moment the base case is reached.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time Complexity of Recursive Fibonacci (Naive)

The naive Fibonacci algorithm `fib(n)` returns:
- $0$ if $n = 0$
- $1$ if $n = 1$
- $\text{fib}(n-1) + \text{fib}(n-2)$ otherwise.

**Recurrence Formulation:**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c, \quad T(0) = T(1) = d$$

Because the larger of the two sub-terms dominates, we can *lower-bound* it:

$$T(n) \geq 2\,T(n-2) + c \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(n) = \Omega(2^{n/2}) = \Omega\bigl(\phi^{n}\bigr)$$

where $\phi = \tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ is the **golden ratio**.

**Tighter Upper Bound via Recursion Tree:**

Each level $i$ of the recursion tree has at most $2^i$ nodes, each contributing cost $c$. The depth is $n$. Summing a geometric series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &\leq \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{i}\,c \\
&= c\bigl(2^{n} - 1\bigr) \\
&= \Theta(2^{n})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The naive recursive Fibonacci is **exponential** in time, $T(n) = \Theta(2^n)$, even though it computes only $n+1$ distinct values. This is the canonical KTU example of *why* memoization / dynamic programming is necessary.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recursion-Tree Counting for $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ (Tower of Hanoi)

For the classical Tower of Hanoi with $n$ disks:

$$T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

**Telescoping Unroll:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n-1) + 1 \\
&= 2\bigl[2T(n-2) + 1\bigr] + 1 \\
&= 2^{2}T(n-2) + 2 + 1 \\
&= 2^{3}T(n-3) + 4 + 2 + 1 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= 2^{k}T(n-k) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Choose $k = n-1$ so that $n-k = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2^{n-1}T(1) + \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} 2^{i} \\
&= 2^{n-1}\cdot 1 + \bigl(2^{n-1} - 1\bigr) \\
&= 2^{n} - 1 \\
&= \Theta(2^{n})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous result: **Tower of Hanoi requires $2^n - 1$ moves**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Iteration vs Recursion

| Property | Iteration | Recursion |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Control mechanism | Loops (`for`, `while`) | Self-calls |
| Memory usage | $\Theta(1)$ extra | $\Theta(\text{depth})$ stack |
| Speed | Generally faster (no call overhead) | Slower (call/return cost) |
| Code clarity for trees/graphs | Verbose | Concise and natural |
| Risk | Loop bugs (off-by-one) | Stack overflow on deep inputs |
| Termination | Loop condition | Base case |
| Tail-call optimisable? | N/A | Yes (if compiler supports) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Recursion Tree for Naive `fib(5)`

The following Mermaid block renders the complete call-expansion tree of the naive recursive Fibonacci algorithm for $n = 5$. Each node represents a single function invocation, and the two children of every internal node represent the two recursive sub-calls `fib(n-1)` and `fib(n-2)`.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeF5["FIB 5"]
    nodeF4["FIB 4"]
    nodeF3a["FIB 3"]
    nodeF2a["FIB 2"]
    nodeF1a["FIB 1"]
    nodeF0a["FIB 0"]
    nodeF3b["FIB 3"]
    nodeF2b["FIB 2"]
    nodeF1b["FIB 1"]
    nodeF0b["FIB 0"]
    nodeF1c["FIB 1"]
    nodeF0c["FIB 0"]

    nodeF5 --> nodeF4
    nodeF5 --> nodeF3a
    nodeF4 --> nodeF2a
    nodeF4 --> nodeF1a
    nodeF3a --> nodeF0a
    nodeF3a --> nodeF1a
    nodeF2a --> nodeF0a
    nodeF2a --> nodeF1a
    nodeF3b --> nodeF1b
    nodeF3b --> nodeF2b
    nodeF2b --> nodeF0b
    nodeF2b --> nodeF1b

    leaf1["return 1"] --- nodeF0a
    leaf2["return 0"] --- nodeF0a
    leaf3["return 1"] --- nodeF1a
    leaf4["return 1"] --- nodeF1b
    leaf5["return 0"] --- nodeF0b
    leaf6["return 1"] --- nodeF1c
    leaf7["return 0"] --- nodeF0c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Observe the repeated sub-calls — `FIB 3` is computed *twice*, `FIB 2` is computed *three times*. This redundant recomputation is precisely why the naive algorithm is $\Theta(2^n)$ and why memoization (caching) reduces it to $\Theta(n)$.

---

### 4.2 Call-Stack Lifecycle for `fact(4)`

```mermaid
graph TD
    stackStart["Call Stack BEFORE invocation"] --> push1["PUSH fact 4"]
    push1 --> push2["PUSH fact 3"]
    push2 --> push3["PUSH fact 2"]
    push3 --> push4["PUSH fact 1"]
    push4 --> baseHit["BASE CASE HIT  n le 1  return 1"]
    baseHit --> pop1["POP fact 1  return 1"]
    pop1 --> mul2["fact 2  compute 2 times 1 = 2"]
    mul2 --> pop2["POP fact 2  return 2"]
    pop2 --> mul3["fact 3  compute 3 times 2 = 6"]
    mul3 --> pop3["POP fact 3  return 6"]
    pop3 --> mul4["fact 4  compute 4 times 6 = 24"]
    mul4 --> pop4["POP fact 4  return 24"]
    pop4 --> final["FINAL ANSWER 24"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Stack frame analysis:** The maximum depth of the call stack equals the value of $n$. Each frame stores the local variables and the *return address*. For very large $n$ (say, $n > 10^5$), this causes a *stack overflow* — the classic reason iterative reformulation is required in production code.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Recursive Call

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT n"] --> B["GUARD  Is n base case"]
    B -->|YES| C["RETURN base value"]
    B -->|NO| D["DECOMPOSE  Build subproblem of smaller size"]
    D --> E["INVOKE RECURSIVE CALL on subproblem"]
    E --> F["RECEIVE sub result"]
    F --> G["COMBINE using operator"]
    G --> H["RETURN combined result"]
    C --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This block diagram maps the *control flow* of any recursive routine, independent of the problem domain. KTU examiners often award marks for *diagrammatic representation* of the recursive decomposition.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a *recursive algorithm*. State and explain its two essential components with the help of the factorial function.

**Model Answer:**

A *recursive algorithm* is a function that solves a problem by **calling itself** with a strictly smaller or simpler instance of the same problem until a trivially solvable case is reached.

The two essential components are:

1. **Base Case (Termination Condition):** A condition under which the recursion stops, returning a direct value. In the factorial function, this is the case $n = 0$ (or $n = 1$), returning the value $1$.

2. **Recursive Case (Reduction Step):** The branch in which the function expresses the answer in terms of a call to itself with a smaller argument. For factorial, this is the relation $n! = n \times (n-1)!$, where the right-hand side invokes the function on the smaller value $n-1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a well-defined base case, the recursion will never terminate and will eventually exhaust the call stack (stack overflow).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Distinguish between *direct recursion* and *indirect recursion*. Give one example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Direct Recursion | Indirect Recursion |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | A function calls **itself** directly. | A function calls another function which in turn calls the first. |
| Call chain length | 1 (self → self) | $\geq 2$ (A → B → A or longer) |
| Example | `fact(n)` calls `fact(n-1)` | `isEven(n)` calls `isOdd(n-1)`; `isOdd(n)` calls `isEven(n-1)` |
| Common use | Simple linear/tree recursion | Mutual recursion, recursive-descent parsers |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of recursion in detail. Draw the recursion tree for `fib(4)` and identify the number of repeated sub-calls.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Write the recursive pseudocode for computing the *sum of the first n natural numbers*. Derive its time and space complexity using a recurrence relation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Recursion**

Recursion is a programming paradigm in which a function solves a problem by calling a smaller instance of itself. Each call creates a new **stack frame** on the call stack containing the local variables and the return address. Execution unwinds once the base case is hit.

**Recursion Tree for `fib(4)`:**

```
                fib(4)
               /      \
          fib(3)      fib(2)
          /    \      /    \
      fib(2) fib(1) fib(1) fib(0)
      /   \
  fib(1) fib(0)
```

- Total calls: $9$
- Distinct sub-calls: $4$ (namely `fib(0)`, `fib(1)`, `fib(2)`, `fib(3)`)
- Repeated sub-calls: $9 - 4 = 5$ (namely `fib(1)`×3, `fib(2)`×2, `fib(0)`×2 — see below)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (KTU Common Mistake):** Many students only count direct invocations and miss that `fib(1)` is invoked **three** times in the tree above. The valuation key explicitly deducts 1 mark for every miscounted node.

---

**(b) Recursive Pseudocode for Sum of First $n$ Natural Numbers**

```
function sum(n):
    if n == 0:
        return 0
    else:
        return n + sum(n-1)
```

**Recurrence Relation:**

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} d & \text{if } n = 0 \\ T(n-1) + c & \text{if } n > 0 \end{cases}$$

**Derivation (Telescoping):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + c \\
&= T(n-2) + 2c \\
&= \;\cdots \\
&= T(0) + nc \\
&= d + nc \\
&= \Theta(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Space Complexity:** The call stack holds $n$ frames at peak depth. Each frame stores a constant amount of data. Therefore, $S(n) = \Theta(n)$.

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = \Theta(n)$ time, $S(n) = \Theta(n)$ space.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a *recurrence relation*? Set up and solve the recurrence for the *Tower of Hanoi* problem and prove that it requires $2^n - 1$ moves.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare *tail recursion* and *non-tail recursion*. Rewrite the factorial function to make it tail-recursive and explain why tail recursion is preferred in functional languages.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Recurrence Relation and Tower of Hanoi**

A *recurrence relation* is an equation that defines a function $T(n)$ in terms of $T$ of one or more smaller argument values. It is the natural mathematical model of recursive algorithms because each recursive call contributes one $T$-term and the work done in the current call contributes the remaining term.

**Tower of Hanoi Setup:**
To move $n$ disks from source peg `S` to destination peg `D` using auxiliary peg `A`:
1. Move $n-1$ disks from `S` to `A` (cost $T(n-1)$).
2. Move the largest disk from `S` to `D` (cost $1$).
3. Move $n-1$ disks from `A` to `D` (cost $T(n-1)$).

**Recurrence:**

$$T(n) = 2\,T(n-1) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

**Closed-Form Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2\,T(n-1) + 1 \\
&= 2\bigl[2\,T(n-2) + 1\bigr] + 1 = 2^{2}T(n-2) + 2 + 1 \\
&= 2\bigl[2\,T(n-3) + 1\bigr] + 3 = 2^{3}T(n-3) + 4 + 2 + 1 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= 2^{k}T(n-k) + \bigl(2^{k} - 1\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $k = n-1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2^{n-1}\,T(1) + \bigl(2^{n-1} - 1\bigr) \\
&= 2^{n-1}\cdot 1 + 2^{n-1} - 1 \\
&= 2^{n} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the number of moves is exactly $2^{n} - 1$, which is $\Theta(2^{n})$.

---

**(b) Tail Recursion vs Non-Tail Recursion**

| Property | Tail Recursion | Non-Tail Recursion |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|
| Definition | Recursive call is the **last** operation in the function. | Additional computation follows the recursive call. |
| Stack frames needed | Can be optimised to $\Theta(1)$ by tail-call elimination. | Requires $\Theta(n)$ stack frames. |
| Convertibility | Can be transformed to a loop. | Often requires an explicit stack. |
| Example | `factTail(n, acc)` with accumulator | Standard `fact(n) = n * fact(n-1)` |

**Tail-Recursive Factorial (with Accumulator):**

```
function factTail(n, accumulator):
    if n == 0:
        return accumulator
    else:
        return factTail(n - 1, n * accumulator)
```

Initial call: `factTail(n, 1)`.

**Why Functional Languages Prefer It:** Languages like Haskell, OCaml, and Scheme guarantee **tail-call elimination (TCE)**: the compiler reuses the current stack frame for the recursive call, reducing space from $\Theta(n)$ to $\Theta(1)$. Imperative languages like C and Java generally do **not** perform TCE, so the iterative version remains preferable in those contexts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Failing to mention the *base case* explicitly → **2-mark deduction**.
> - Confusing *time* and *space* complexity in the derivation → **1-mark deduction**.
> - Not stating that $T(n) = \Theta(2^n)$ for naive Fibonacci **because of repeated sub-calls** (not the recurrence itself) → **2-mark deduction**.
> - Using the vertical pipe `|n|` instead of `\vert n\vert` in LaTeX tables will not be penalised by the examiner, but is a syntactic warning. Use $\vert n\vert$ for absolute value.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Recursion ≠ Iteration.** Recursion uses the call stack; iteration uses loop counters. Both can express repetition, but recursion is natural for problems with self-similar substructure.
- **Two components are non-negotiable:** a **base case** and a **recursive case** with *strictly smaller* arguments.
- **Time complexity** is captured by a **recurrence relation** $T(n)$. Solve it using the *telescoping (unrolling) method* or *Master Theorem*.
- **Space complexity** of a recursive algorithm is **at least** the maximum depth of the recursion tree (call-stack height), even though each frame stores $O(1)$ data.
- **Memoization** converts exponential recursions (e.g., Fibonacci) into polynomial ones by caching already-computed sub-results.
- **Tail recursion** is the key bridge to iterative code: a tail-recursive function can be mechanically rewritten as a `while` loop.
- **Golden ratio $\phi \approx 1.618$** appears in the closed form of Fibonacci and in the tight complexity bound $T(n) = \Theta(\phi^{n})$.
- **Stack overflow** is the most common practical failure mode — guard against it by adding depth checks or by converting to iteration when $n$ is large.
- **KTU 2024 Mark Weighting (typical):** Definition = 2 marks, Components = 2 marks, Recurrence setup = 3 marks, Derivation = 4 marks, Tree/Stack diagram = 2 marks, Conclusion = 1 mark.
- **Common recurrence identities to memorise:**
  - $T(n) = T(n-1) + c \;\Rightarrow\; T(n) = \Theta(n)$
  - $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; T(n) = 2^{n} - 1 = \Theta(2^{n})$
  - $T(n) = T(n/2) + c \;\Rightarrow\; T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$
  - $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn \;\Rightarrow\; T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recurrence Relations, Representation of Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recurrence Relations, Representation of Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Recurrence Relations — Formal Definition

A **Recurrence Relation** is a mathematical equation or inequality that defines a sequence, function, or algorithm's running time in terms of **its own previous values**, combined with one or more **base cases (initial conditions)** that terminate the recursion. In the context of Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA), recurrence relations are the *backbone* of algorithmic complexity analysis because most divide-and-conquer, recursive, and tree-based algorithms (such as Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen's Matrix Multiplication, etc.) are naturally expressed recursively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Standard):**
> A recurrence relation is an equation that expresses each element of a sequence as a linear or non-linear function of the preceding elements, together with the initial values (base conditions) that uniquely determine the sequence.

Mathematically, a generic divide-and-conquer recurrence takes the canonical form:

$$
T(n) = 
\begin{cases}
a \cdot T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right) + f(n), & n > 1 \\
\Theta(1), & n \le n_0
\end{cases}
$$

where:
- $a$ = number of **sub-problems** generated in the recursive step
- $n/b$ = **size** of each sub-problem
- $f(n)$ = **cost** of dividing the problem and combining the results

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a recurrence like a **Russian Nesting Doll (Matryoshka)**. The outermost doll (size $n$) contains $a$ smaller dolls, each of size $n/b$. The cost of opening the outer doll is $f(n)$. To know the *total* cost, you must recursively open every nested doll until you hit the smallest "atom" (the base case $\Theta(1)$). The recurrence is the *set of instructions* describing how the cost of one level depends on the cost of the levels below it.

**Another analogy — Payroll Calculation:**
Imagine a company where a manager's salary is computed as: $1.5 \times$ (the sum of salaries of two sub-managers) + a fixed bonus. This is a **recurrence** — your salary depends on salaries "below" you, until you reach the leaf employees (base case).

### 1.3 Array Representation — Formal Definition

An **Array** is a contiguous block of memory locations that store homogeneous data elements, accessible in **constant time $O(1)$** via an index. Since memory in a computer is one-dimensional (linear), higher-dimensional arrays must be **mapped (linearized)** onto this 1-D memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Standard Mapping Schemes for 2-D Arrays:**
> 1. **Row-Major Order (RMO):** Elements are stored row-by-row (used in C, C++, Python, Java).
> 2. **Column-Major Order (CMO):** Elements are stored column-by-column (used in Fortran, MATLAB, R).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 1 emphasizes the derivation of the **address calculation formula** for both 1-D and 2-D arrays, along with the solution of recurrence relations using at least three methods (Substitution, Iteration/Recursion Tree, and Master Theorem).

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in DSA

- **Recurrence relations** are used to analyze the **time complexity** of every recursive algorithm you will study: Binary Search, Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Tower of Hanoi, Fibonacci generation, BFS/DFS tree traversals, etc.
- **Array representation** is foundational because *every* other data structure (Stack, Queue, Hash Table, Heap, Matrix, Graph adjacency matrix) is implemented using arrays underneath.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Misconception:** A recurrence relation is *not* just a recursive function. It is a **mathematical closed-form** describing the cost. Solving a recurrence means finding a **closed-form (explicit) expression** in $\Theta$, $O$, or $\Omega$ notation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Recurrence Relations

Recurrence relations are classified based on **homogeneity**, **linearity**, and **order**:

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Linear Homogeneous** | RHS is a linear combination of previous terms; constant coefficient | $T(n) = 4T(n-1) - 4T(n-2)$ |
| **Linear Non-Homogeneous** | RHS contains an extra function $f(n)$ | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ |
| **Non-Linear** | Contains products / powers of $T(\cdot)$ | $T(n) = T(n/2)^2 + n$ |
| **First Order** | Depends only on the immediately previous term | $T(n) = 2T(n-1)$ |
| **k-th Order** | Depends on the $k$ previous terms | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2)$ (Fibonacci) |
