### 2.2 Standard Methods to Solve Recurrence Relations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Methods You MUST Know for KTU 2024:**

1. **Substitution Method (Guess & Verify)** — Guess a bound and prove by mathematical induction.
2. **Iteration (Recursion Tree) Method** — Repeatedly unfold the recurrence and sum the per-level costs.
3. **Master Theorem** — A direct cookbook method for recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.

### 2.3 Master Theorem (Detailed Statement)

Let $a \ge 1$ and $b > 1$ be constants, and let $f(n)$ be a non-negative function. Define $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ on the integers, where $n/b$ is interpreted as $\lceil n/b \rceil$ or $\lfloor n/b \rfloor$. Then $T(n)$ has the following asymptotic bounds:

> [!NOTE]
> **Let $n^{\log_b a}$ be the critical exponent. Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$:**

- **Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then
$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})
$$

- **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \ge 0$, then
$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)
$$

- **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ **AND** $a \cdot f(n/b) \le c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ (regularity condition), then
$$
T(n) = \Theta(f(n))
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Exam Hall)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Generic Divide & Conquer | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | $a \ge 1$, $b > 1$ |
| 2 | Master Theorem Critical Exponent | $n^{\log_b a}$ | Compare with $f(n)$ |
| 3 | 1-D Array Address | $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = \text{Base}(A) + (i - \text{LB}) \times w$ | $w$ = word size in bytes |
| 4 | 2-D Array Address (RMO) | $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base}(A) + [(i - r_1)(c_2 - c_1 + 1) + (j - c_1)] \times w$ | Row-Major |
| 5 | 2-D Array Address (CMO) | $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base}(A) + [(j - c_1)(r_2 - r_1 + 1) + (i - r_1)] \times w$ | Column-Major |
| 6 | Number of Elements (2-D) | $n = (r_2 - r_1 + 1) \times (c_2 - c_1 + 1)$ | Rows × Cols |
| 7 | Fibonacci Recurrence | $F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)$, $F(0)=F(1)=1$ | Binet's formula: $F(n) = \frac{\phi^n - \psi^n}{\sqrt{5}}$ |
| 8 | Geometric Sum | $\sum_{i=0}^{k} a^i = \frac{a^{k+1} - 1}{a - 1}$ | Used in iteration method |
| 9 | Logarithmic Depth | $T_{\text{depth}} = \lceil \log_b n \rceil$ | Number of recursion levels |
| 10 | Sum of Logarithms | $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_b n} \log_b(n/b^i) = O(\log^2 n)$ | Recursion tree leaf work |

### 2.5 Engineering Applications

- **Compiler Design:** Recurrences model the cost of parsing algorithms (CYK, LL(k), LR(k)).
- **Database Systems:** B-Tree search cost: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(1)$.
- **Computer Graphics:** Image processing kernels use 2-D array address calculations.
- **Machine Learning:** Strassen's algorithm reduces $7 \times 7$ matrix multiply to $7$ sub-multiplications: $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2) \Rightarrow \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})$.
- **Networks:** Routing algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford) have recurrences solvable via Master Theorem.
- **Embedded Systems:** Array representation in contiguous memory is critical for cache locality and real-time guarantees.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Locality Tip:** Row-Major Order is **cache-friendly** in C/C++ because adjacent memory addresses map to adjacent columns in the same row — a property exploited heavily in HPC and GPU programming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1: Solving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ using the **Iteration Method**

This is the canonical **Merge Sort** recurrence. Let us solve it step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Unfold the recurrence once:**
$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T(n/2) = 2T(n/4) + n/2$:**
$$
T(n) = 2\left[2T(n/4) + n/2\right] + n = 4T(n/4) + n + n
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $T(n/4) = 2T(n/8) + n/4$:**
$$
T(n) = 4\left[2T(n/8) + n/4\right] + 2n = 8T(n/8) + n + 2n
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the pattern after $i$ iterations:**
$$
T(n) = 2^i \cdot T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2^i}\right) + i \cdot n
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the depth of recursion.** The recursion bottoms out when $n/2^i = 1$, i.e., $i = \log_2 n$.

**Step 6 — Substitute $i = \log_2 n$ into the pattern:**
$$
T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + (\log_2 n) \cdot n
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify $2^{\log_2 n} = n$ and let $T(1) = c$:**
$$
T(n) = n \cdot c + n \log_2 n
$$

**Step 8 — Drop constants and lower-order terms in Big-Theta:**
$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using Master Theorem:** $a = 2$, $b = 2$, so $n^{\log_b a} = n^1 = n$. We have $f(n) = n = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, which is **Case 2** with $k = 0$. Thus $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$. ✅ Matches.

---

### 3.2 Example 2: Solving $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ using the **Substitution Method**

**Step 1 — Apply Master Theorem first (faster).** Compute $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$.

**Step 2 — Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^{0.792}$.** Since $n^2$ grows polynomially faster than $n^{0.792}$, we are in **Case 3** provided the regularity condition holds.

**Step 3 — Check regularity condition:**
$$
a \cdot f(n/b) = 3 \cdot (n/4)^2 = \dfrac{3n^2}{16} \le c \cdot n^2
$$
Choose $c = 3/16$. Since $3/16 < 1$, the regularity condition is satisfied. ✅

**Step 4 — Conclude:** $T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n^2)$.

**Step 5 — Verification by substitution method:** Guess $T(n) \le c n^2$.

Assume $T(n/4) \le c(n/4)^2 = c n^2 / 16$. Then:
$$
T(n) = 3 \cdot \dfrac{c n^2}{16} + n^2 = \dfrac{3c}{16} n^2 + n^2
$$

We need this to be $\le c n^2$. So:
$$
\dfrac{3c}{16} n^2 + n^2 \le c n^2
$$

Rearranging:
$$
n^2 \le c n^2 - \dfrac{3c}{16} n^2 = \dfrac{13c}{16} n^2
$$

This gives $c \ge 16/13$. Choosing $c = 2$ works for sufficiently large $n$ (we also need to handle small $n$ as the base case). Therefore $T(n) = O(n^2)$, and combined with the obvious $\Omega(n^2)$ lower bound from the cost of the $f(n)$ term alone, we get:
$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^2)
$$

---

### 3.3 Example 3: Address Calculation for 2-D Array (Row-Major Order)

**Problem:** A 2-D array $A$ is declared as $A[10 \ldots 35, 15 \ldots 28]$. Each element occupies **4 bytes**. The base address is **1000**. Find the address of $A[25][20]$ using **Row-Major Order**.

**Step 1 — Identify the bounds:**
- $r_1 = 10$, $r_2 = 35$ → Number of rows $= 35 - 10 + 1 = 26$
- $c_1 = 15$, $c_2 = 28$ → Number of cols $= 28 - 15 + 1 = 14$
- $w = 4$ bytes
- $\text{Base}(A) = 1000$

**Step 2 — Write the RMO formula:**
$$
\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base}(A) + \left[(i - r_1)(c_2 - c_1 + 1) + (j - c_1)\right] \cdot w
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $i = 25$, $j = 20$:**
$$
\text{Addr}(A[25][20]) = 1000 + \left[(25 - 10)(28 - 15 + 1) + (20 - 15)\right] \cdot 4
$$

**Step 4 — Compute inside the brackets:**
$$
(15)(14) + 5 = 210 + 5 = 215
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $w$ and add to base:**
$$
1000 + 215 \times 4 = 1000 + 860 = 1860
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{Addr}(A[25][20]) = \mathbf{1860}$.

---

### 3.4 Example 4: Address Calculation for 2-D Array (Column-Major Order)

**Same problem but with Column-Major Order.**

**Step 1 — Write the CMO formula:**
$$
\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = \text{Base}(A) + \left[(j - c_1)(r_2 - r_1 + 1) + (i - r_1)\right] \cdot w
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $i = 25$, $j = 20$:**
$$
\text{Addr}(A[25][20]) = 1000 + \left[(20 - 15)(35 - 10 + 1) + (25 - 10)\right] \cdot 4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute inside the brackets:**
$$
(5)(26) + 15 = 130 + 15 = 145
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $w$ and add to base:**
$$
1000 + 145 \times 4 = 1000 + 580 = 1580
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{Addr}(A[25][20]) = \mathbf{1580}$ (CMO).

---

### 3.5 Example 5: Solving $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ (Linear, Non-Homogeneous)

**Step 1 — Unfold iteratively:**
$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + n
$$
$$
T(n) = T(n-2) + (n-1) + n
$$
$$
T(n) = T(n-3) + (n-2) + (n-1) + n
$$

**Step 2 — After $i$ iterations:**
$$
T(n) = T(n-i) + \sum_{k=0}^{i-1}(n - k)
$$

**Step 3 — Set $n - i = 1$, i.e., $i = n - 1$:**
$$
T(n) = T(1) + \sum_{k=0}^{n-2}(n - k) = T(1) + \sum_{m=2}^{n} m
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the sum using $\sum_{m=1}^{n} m = n(n+1)/2$:**
$$
\sum_{m=2}^{n} m = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1
$$

**Step 5 — Final closed form (with $T(1) = c$):**
$$
T(n) = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} + c - 1 = \Theta(n^2)
$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Master Theorem Quick Reference

| $f(n)$ vs $n^{\log_b a}$ | Case | Result $T(n) = $ | Example |
|---------------------------|------|------------------|---------|
| $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$ → $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ | 2 | $\Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$ | $T(n) = T(2n/3) + 1$ → $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ | 3 | $\Theta(f(n))$ | $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n \log n$ → $\Theta(n \log n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort)

This Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture of the recursion tree** showing how Merge Sort's cost is distributed across recursion levels.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode["Level 0 : Problem Size n | Cost = n"]
    lvl1L["Level 1 Left : Size n/2 | Cost = n/2"]
    lvl1R["Level 1 Right : Size n/2 | Cost = n/2"]
    lvl2LL["Level 2 LL : Size n/4 | Cost = n/4"]
    lvl2LR["Level 2 LR : Size n/4 | Cost = n/4"]
    lvl2RL["Level 2 RL : Size n/4 | Cost = n/4"]
    lvl2RR["Level 2 RR : Size n/4 | Cost = n/4"]
    leaves["Level 3 Leaves : 8 sub-problems of size n/8 | Cost = 8 x n/8 = n"]

    rootNode --> lvl1L
    rootNode --> lvl1R
    lvl1L --> lvl2LL
    lvl1L --> lvl2LR
    lvl1R --> lvl2RL
    lvl1R --> lvl2RR
    lvl2LL --> leaves
    lvl2LR --> leaves
    lvl2RL --> leaves
    lvl2RR --> leaves
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - At **Level 0** the cost is $n$.
> - At **Level 1** there are 2 sub-problems each costing $n/2$ → total = $n$.
> - At **Level 2** there are 4 sub-problems each costing $n/4$ → total = $n$.
> - This pattern repeats: at every level, the total cost is exactly $n$.
> - There are $\log_2 n + 1$ levels, so total cost = $n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

### 4.2 Memory Layout Diagram for a 2-D Array (Row-Major vs Column-Major)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RMO["Row-Major Order Storage in Memory C Style"]
        R00["A00"]
        R01["A01"]
        R02["A02"]
        R10["A10"]
        R11["A11"]
        R12["A12"]
        R20["A20"]
        R21["A21"]
        R22["A22"]
    end
    R00 --> R01 --> R02 --> R10 --> R11 --> R12 --> R20 --> R21 --> R22

    subgraph CMO["Column-Major Order Storage in Memory Fortran Style"]
        C00["A00"]
        C10["A10"]
        C20["A20"]
        C01["A01"]
        C11["A11"]
        C21["A21"]
        C02["A02"]
        C12["A12"]
        C22["A22"]
    end
    C00 --> C10 --> C20 --> C01 --> C11 --> C21 --> C02 --> C12 --> C22
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In Row-Major Order, traversing the array with a pointer walks across a **row** at a time. In Column-Major Order, the pointer walks down a **column** at a time. The two layouts result in different addresses for the same element (as shown in Examples 3 & 4 above: 1860 vs 1580).

---

### 4.3 Master Theorem Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["Given T(n) = aT(n/b) plus f(n)"]
    compute["Compute n raised to log base b of a"]
    comp1["Is f(n) = O of n to log_b a minus epsilon ?"]
    comp2["Is f(n) = Theta of n to log_b a times log to the k of n ?"]
    comp3["Is f n = Omega of n to log_b a plus epsilon AND regularity ?"]
    case1["Case 1: T(n) = Theta of n to log_b a"]
    case2["Case 2: T(n) = Theta of n to log_b a times log to the k plus 1 of n"]
    case3["Case 3: T(n) = Theta of f(n)"]
    fallback["Master Theorem does not apply, use Akra-Bazzi or Substitution Method"]

    start --> compute
    compute --> comp1
    comp1 -- YES --> case1
    comp1 -- NO --> comp2
    comp2 -- YES --> case2
    comp2 -- NO --> comp3
    comp3 -- YES --> case3
    comp3 -- NO --> fallback
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Array Address Calculation

```mermaid
graph TD
    input["Input: Base address, indices i and j, bounds r1 r2 c1 c2, word size w"]
    parse["Parse bounds and compute number of rows N_r and columns N_c"]
    choose["Select mapping scheme: Row-Major or Column-Major"]
    computeRMO["RMO: offset = i minus r1 times N_c plus j minus c1"]
    computeCMO["CMO: offset = j minus c1 times N_r plus i minus r1"]
    multiply["Multiply offset by word size w"]
    addBase["Final Address = Base address plus offset times w"]
    output["Output: Computed Memory Address"]

    input --> parse
    parse --> choose
    choose -- RMO --> computeRMO
    choose -- CMO --> computeCMO
    computeRMO --> multiply
    computeCMO --> multiply
    multiply --> addBase
    addBase --> output
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a recurrence relation. What are the necessary components for solving a recurrence relation? Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **recurrence relation** is a mathematical equation that defines each term of a sequence in terms of the preceding terms. The two necessary components are:
1. **Recursive equation (Recurrence step):** e.g., $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$
2. **Base case (Initial condition):** e.g., $T(1) = 1$ or $T(0) = c$

**Example:** The Fibonacci sequence is defined by $F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)$ with $F(0) = 0$ and $F(1) = 1$.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark components + 1 mark example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between Row-Major and Column-Major storage of a 2-D array. Which one is used in C language?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Row-Major Order (RMO) | Column-Major Order (CMO) |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Storage pattern | Row-by-row | Column-by-column |
| Used in | C, C++, Java, Python | Fortran, MATLAB, R |
| Address formula | $(i - r_1)(c_2 - c_1 + 1) + (j - c_1)$ | $(j - c_1)(r_2 - r_1 + 1) + (i - r_1)$ |
| Cache locality | Friendly for row-wise access | Friendly for column-wise access |

**C language uses Row-Major Order.** *(1 mark table, 1 mark for key difference, 1 mark C language answer.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ using the Master Theorem. Show that the regularity condition is satisfied. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $f(n) = n^2$

**Step 2 — Compute the critical exponent:**
$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_4 3} = n^{0.7924\ldots}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_4 3}$:**
$$
f(n) = n^2 \gg n^{0.7924}
$$
So $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_4 3 + \epsilon})$ with $\epsilon \approx 1.2 > 0$. This points to **Case 3**.

**Step 4 — Verify the regularity condition:**
We need $a \cdot f(n/b) \le c \cdot f(n)$ for some constant $c < 1$.
$$
3 \cdot f(n/4) = 3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{n}{4}\right)^2 = 3 \cdot \dfrac{n^2}{16} = \dfrac{3}{16} n^2
$$
We need $\dfrac{3}{16} n^2 \le c \cdot n^2$, i.e., $c \ge 3/16$. Choosing $c = 1/2$ (which is $< 1$) **satisfies** the regularity condition. ✅

**Step 5 — Conclude by Case 3 of Master Theorem:**
$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n^2)}
$$

*(Mark distribution: Step 1 → 1 mark, Step 2 → 1 mark, Step 3 → 2 marks, Step 4 → 2 marks, Step 5 → 1 mark.)*

---

**(b) A 2-D array $A$ is declared as $A[5 \ldots 25, -10 \ldots 15]$. Each element occupies 2 bytes. The base address is 400. Find the address of $A[15][5]$ using (i) Row-Major Order, and (ii) Column-Major Order. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the bounds and counts:**
- $r_1 = 5$, $r_2 = 25$ → Number of rows $= 25 - 5 + 1 = 21$
- $c_1 = -10$, $c_2 = 15$ → Number of cols $= 15 - (-10) + 1 = 26$
- $w = 2$ bytes, $\text{Base} = 400$

**Step 2 — Compute RMO address:**
$$
\text{Addr}(A[15][5])_{\text{RMO}} = 400 + \left[(15 - 5)(26) + (5 - (-10))\right] \times 2
$$
$$
= 400 + \left[(10)(26) + 15\right] \times 2 = 400 + (260 + 15) \times 2 = 400 + 550 = \mathbf{950}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute CMO address:**
$$
\text{Addr}(A[15][5])_{\text{CMO}} = 400 + \left[(5 - (-10))(21) + (15 - 5)\right] \times 2
$$
$$
= 400 + \left[(15)(21) + 10\right] \times 2 = 400 + (315 + 10) \times 2 = 400 + 650 = \mathbf{1050}
$$

*(Mark distribution: Step 1 → 1 mark, Step 2 → 3 marks, Step 3 → 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + n^3$ asymptotically using the Master Theorem. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $a = 8$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^3$

**Step 2 — Compute the critical exponent:**
$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 8} = n^3
$$

**Step 3 — Compare $f(n)$ with $n^3$:**
$$
f(n) = n^3 = \Theta(n^3) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^0 n)
$$
This is **Case 2** of the Master Theorem with $k = 0$.

**Step 4 — Apply Case 2:**
$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n^3 \log n)
$$

**Final Answer:** $T(n) = \Theta(n^3 \log n)$.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for params, 1 mark for critical exponent, 2 marks for comparison, 1 mark for case identification, 2 marks for final result.)*

---

**(b) Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ using the iteration method and determine the asymptotic complexity. Verify your result using the Master Theorem. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration Method:**

**Step 1 — Unfold the recurrence:**
$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n
$$
$$
T(n) = 2\left[2T(n/4) + \frac{n}{2} \log(n/2)\right] + n \log n
$$
$$
T(n) = 4T(n/4) + n \log(n/2) + n \log n
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the pattern after $i$ iterations:**
$$
T(n) = 2^i T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2^i}\right) + n \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} \log\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2^k}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — At depth $i = \log_2 n$, $T(1) = c$:**
$$
T(n) = n \cdot c + n \sum_{k=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} \log\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2^k}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify $\log(n/2^k) = \log n - k$:**
$$
n \sum_{k=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} (\log n - k) = n \left[ (\log_2 n)(\log n) - \sum_{k=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} k \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} k = m(m-1)/2 \approx m^2/2$ where $m = \log_2 n$:**
$$
= n \left[ \log_2 n \cdot \log n - \dfrac{(\log_2 n)(\log_2 n - 1)}{2} \right] = n \left[ \log^2 n - \dfrac{\log^2 n}{2} \right] \approx n \log^2 n
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude:**
$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)}
$$

**Verification using Master Theorem:** $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $n^{\log_b a} = n^1 = n$. We have $f(n) = n \log n = \Theta(n \log n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^1 n)$ with $k = 1$. This is **Case 2** with $k = 1$:
$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n) \quad \checkmark
$$

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for unfolding, 2 marks for pattern, 2 marks for sum evaluation, 1 mark final result, 1 mark Master Theorem verification.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Base Cases:** Many students write the recurrence but forget to specify $T(0)$ or $T(1)$. Without base cases, a recurrence is **incomplete** and you will lose **1 to 2 marks** depending on the examiner.
>
> 2. **Confusing RMO and CMO formulas:** In the heat of the exam, students often swap $i$ and $j$ in the address formula. **Mnemonic:** In Row-Major, the *row index comes first* (inside the multiplication for stride).
>
> 3. **Wrong Index Difference:** A very common error is writing $(r_2 - r_1)$ instead of $(r_2 - r_1 + 1)$ for the number of rows. The "+1" is **mandatory** because the bounds are *inclusive*.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Regularity Condition in Case 3 of Master Theorem:** Many students correctly identify Case 3 but forget to verify $a f(n/b) \le c f(n)$. This costs **1 full mark** in most KTU valuations.
>
> 5. **Using Master Theorem on non-standard forms:** Master Theorem **does not apply** when $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ or $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$. The student must recognize these and use **Substitution** or **Akra-Bazzi** instead.
>
> 6. **Off-by-One Errors in Recursion Depth:** In $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, the depth is $\lfloor \log_b n \rfloor$, not $\log_b n$. Examiners deduct marks for non-integer log bases in the final answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Recurrence Relations & Array Representation**

- **Recurrence Relation:** Equation defining a term using its predecessors. Two parts: **Recurrence** + **Base Case**.
- **Three solution methods:** Substitution (Guess & Induct), Iteration/Recursion Tree (Unfold & Sum), Master Theorem (Cookbook).
- **Master Theorem form:** $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ → critical exponent is $n^{\log_b a}$.
- **Case 1:** $f(n)$ polynomially smaller → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- **Case 2:** $f(n) \approx n^{\log_b a}$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
- **Case 3:** $f(n)$ polynomially larger + regularity → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
- **Geometric Sum:** $\sum_{i=0}^{k} r^i = (r^{k+1} - 1)/(r-1)$ — use when per-level cost forms a geometric series.
- **Logarithmic depth:** Most divide-and-conquer recurrences have depth $O(\log n)$.
- **1-D Array Address:** $\text{Base} + (i - LB) \times w$.
- **2-D RMO Address:** $\text{Base} + [(i - r_1)(c_2 - c_1 + 1) + (j - c_1)] \times w$.
- **2-D CMO Address:** $\text{Base} + [(j - c_1)(r_2 - r_1 + 1) + (i - r_1)] \times w$.
- **Bounds are inclusive:** $r_2 - r_1 + 1$, never $r_2 - r_1$.
- **C/C++/Java → RMO**, Fortran/MATLAB/R → CMO.
- **Tower of Hanoi:** $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1 \Rightarrow T(n) = 2^n - 1$.
- **Binary Search:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$.
- **Merge Sort:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.
- **Strassen:** $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{2.807})$.
- **Karatsuba:** $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + \Theta(n) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.585})$.
- **Linear Recurrence:** $T(n) = T(n-1) + n \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$.
- **Number of elements in 2-D:** Rows $\times$ Columns, not the difference of bounds.
- **Always include the units** (bytes/words) in the final address.
- **Recursion Tree total cost** = (sum of per-level costs) + (leaf cost). For balanced trees, leaves often dominate or match the internal cost.
- **Cache friendliness:** RMO is preferred in modern hardware due to spatial locality.

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Recurrence Relations & Representation of Arrays*
*Prepared in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, B.Tech PERAT751 — Data Structures & Algorithms.*

topics.title: Applications of Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> An **Array** is a linear, homogeneous, contiguous, and index-addressable data structure that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the **same data type**, accessed in **O(1)** constant time using a zero-based (or one-based) integer index. The base address `BA` of an element `A[i]` in a one-dimensional array is computed as:
> `Address(A[i]) = BA + (i × size_of_element)`

**Applications of Arrays** refer to the systematic use of this contiguous memory layout to solve well-defined computational problems where:
- The number of elements is **known in advance** (or grows predictably).
- Random access by index is required.
- Memory locality and cache efficiency are critical.
- The operations are predominantly **search, traverse, insert, delete, or transform**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Post-Office P.O. Box" Analogy:**
> Imagine a post office with a long wall of **200 numbered lockers** (locker #000 to #199). Every locker is the same size, holds exactly one parcel, and the lockers are arranged side-by-side in one continuous row.
> - To find locker #057, the postmaster doesn't search — she simply walks to position **57**. This is **O(1) random access**.
> - If parcel #070 is added, the parcels after it must **shift right** by one — this is the **cost of insertion in an array**.
> - If parcel #120 is removed, all parcels after it must **shift left** to keep the row continuous.
> - The **Mail Sorting Room** stores only the lockers that actually have parcels — this is exactly how a **Sparse Matrix** is represented in a smaller, denser array.

**Elementary Visualization:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Index 0"]  --> B["Index 1"]  --> C["Index 2"]  --> D["Index 3"]  --> E["Index 4"]
    A -.- F["Value: 10"]
    B -.- G["Value: 20"]
    C -.- H["Value: 30"]
    D -.- I["Value: 40"]
    E -.- J["Value: 50"]
```

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Emphasis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Complexity Constants for Array Operations (must be memorized):**
> - **Access by index:** **O(1)**
> - **Traversal:** **O(n)**
> - **Linear Search:** **O(n)**
> - **Binary Search (sorted):** **O(log n)**
> - **Insertion at end:** **O(1)**
> - **Insertion at beginning/middle:** **O(n)**
> - **Deletion:** **O(n)**
> - **Space (n elements):** **O(n)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Major Array Applications

### A. Polynomial Representation using Arrays
A polynomial of degree `n` is given by:
`P(x) = a_0 + a_1·x + a_2·x^2 + ... + a_n·x^n`

It is stored using a structure containing two parallel arrays (or an array of structures):
- `coeff[i]` → coefficient of the term with power `i`
- `degree`  → highest non-zero power (avoids wasting space on zero coefficients)

**Algorithm: ADD_TWO_POLYNOMIALS(P1, P2)**
1. Let `i = 0, j = 0, k = 0`.
2. While `i ≤ P1.degree` AND `j ≤ P2.degree`:
   - If `i == j`: `R.coeff[k] = P1.coeff[i] + P2.coeff[j]`; increment `i, j, k`.
   - Else if `i > j`: copy `P1.coeff[i]` to `R`; increment `i, k`.
   - Else: copy `P2.coeff[j]` to `R`; increment `j, k`.
3. Copy any remaining terms from the longer polynomial.
4. Return `R`.

**Time Complexity: O(n + m)** where n and m are the degrees of the two polynomials.

### B. Sparse Matrix Representation
A matrix is **sparse** if the number of zero entries is much greater than the number of non-zero entries. It is stored compactly as a **triplet** `(row, column, value)` to save **O(rows × cols)** space down to **O(k)** where k = number of non-zero entries.

**Sparse Density Ratio:**
`Density = (Number of Non-Zero Entries / Total Entries) × 100%`

If `Density ≤ 30%`, the matrix is officially classified as **sparse** by KTU convention.

### C. Stack Implementation using Array
A **Stack** is a **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** data structure. Implemented using a single 1-D array and a `top` index (initially `-1`).
- `PUSH(x)` → `top = top + 1; arr[top] = x`
- `POP()`   → `x = arr[top]; top = top - 1`
- `PEEK()`  → return `arr[top]`
- **Overflow** occurs when `top == MAX - 1`.
- **Underflow** occurs when `top == -1`.

### D. Queue Implementation using Array
A **Queue** is a **FIFO (First In, First Out)** data structure. Implemented using a 1-D array, `front` (initially `0`), `rear` (initially `-1`), and `size`.
- **Naive Queue** (shifts array on every dequeue): O(n) per dequeue.
- **Circular Queue** (rear wraps via modulo): **O(1) per enqueue/dequeue**.
  - `rear = (rear + 1) % MAX`
  - `front = (front + 1) % MAX`
  - **Full condition:** `(rear + 1) % MAX == front`
  - **Empty condition:** `front == -1` OR `front == rear + 1` (depending on convention).

### E. String Storage and Operations
Strings are stored as **null-terminated (`'\0'`) character arrays**. Core operations:
- `strlen(s)` → O(n)
- `strcpy(dest, src)` → O(n)
- `strcmp(s1, s2)` → returns 0 if equal (lexicographic compare) → O(n)
- `strcat(dest, src)` → O(n+m)
- `strrev(s)` → reverse in place using two-pointer swap → O(n)

### F. In-Place Array Transformations
- **Reverse** the array: two-pointer swap, **O(n) time, O(1) space**.
- **Rotation** by `k`: using reversal algorithm `reverse(0, n-1) → reverse(0, k-1) → reverse(k, n-1)`, **O(n) time, O(1) space**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Complexity (Time) | Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| Address of A[i] (1-D) | `BA + i × size` | **O(1)** | — |
| Address of A[i][j] (Row-Major) | `BA + (i × n + j) × size` | **O(1)** | — |
| Address of A[i][j] (Col-Major) | `BA + (j × m + i) × size` | **O(1)** | — |
| Polynomial Addition | Loop once over both | **O(n + m)** | **O(n + m)** |
| Polynomial Multiplication | Nested term-by-term loop | **O(n × m)** | **O(n + m)** |
| Sparse Triplet Storage | (row, col, val) per non-zero | **O(k)** | **O(k)** |
| Sparse Transpose | Swap row ↔ col in triplet | **O(k + r)** | **O(k)** |
| Stack PUSH / POP | `top` index move | **O(1)** | **O(n)** |
| Circular Queue ENQ / DEQ | Modulo arithmetic | **O(1)** | **O(n)** |
| String Length / Compare | Single linear pass | **O(n)** | **O(1)** |
| Array Reverse | Two-pointer | **O(n)** | **O(1)** |
| Array Rotation by k | Triple reversal | **O(n)** | **O(1)** |
| Linear Search | Sequential scan | **O(n)** | **O(1)** |
| Binary Search | Halve interval | **O(log n)** | **O(1)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Arrays underpin **digital signal processing (DSP)** buffers, **image pixel matrices**, **CPU cache lines**, **lookup tables (LUTs)** in embedded firmware, **hash table buckets**, and **device drivers' ring buffers**. Sparse matrices are fundamental to **graph algorithms (Adjacency form)**, **finite element analysis (FEA)**, and **Google's PageRank** computations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Address Calculation of A[i][j] in Row-Major Order

A 2-D array `A[m][n]` is stored linearly in memory. In **Row-Major Order**, the elements of row 0 are stored first, then row 1, then row 2, and so on.

**Goal:** Find the linear memory offset (in bytes) of element `A[i][j]` from the base address `BA`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Number of complete rows before row } i = i \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Each complete row contains } n \text{ elements} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Elements in those } i \text{ complete rows} = i \times n \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Offset within the current row before column } j = j \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Total linear offset (in elements)} = i \times n + j \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{Multiply by element size } w \text{ (bytes per element)} \\
\text{Step 7: } & \text{Final address} = BA + (i \times n + j) \times w
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Exam Tip:** The same derivation in **Column-Major Order** swaps the roles of `m` and `n`:
> $$\text{Address}(A[i][j])_{\text{col-major}} = BA + (j \times m + i) \times w$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Polynomial Addition

Given:
- `P1(x) = 5 + 0·x + 10·x^2 + 6·x^3` (degree 3, length 4)
- `P2(x) = 1 + 2·x + 3·x^3` (degree 3, length 4)

Find `R(x) = P1(x) + P2(x)`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Initialize } i=0, j=0, k=0 \\
\text{Step 2: } & i=0, j=0: \text{ powers equal.} \\
                & R.\text{coeff}[0] = 5 + 1 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i=1, j=1, k=1 \\
\text{Step 3: } & i=1, j=1: \text{ powers equal.} \\
                & R.\text{coeff}[1] = 0 + 2 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i=2, j=2, k=2 \\
\text{Step 4: } & i=2, j=2: \text{ powers equal.} \\
                & R.\text{coeff}[2] = 10 + 0 = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i=3, j=3, k=3 \\
\text{Step 5: } & i=3, j=3: \text{ powers equal.} \\
                & R.\text{coeff}[3] = 6 + 3 = 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i=4, j=4, k=4 \\
\text{Step 6: } & i > P1.\text{degree}; \text{ copy remaining } P2 \text{ terms (none).} \\
\text{Result: } & R(x) = 6 + 2x + 10x^2 + 9x^3
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Circular Queue Trace

Given: `MAX = 5`, perform sequence: `ENQ(10), ENQ(20), ENQ(30), ENQ(40), DEQ, DEQ, ENQ(50), ENQ(60)`. Show the state after each step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial: } & \text{front} = 0, \text{rear} = -1, \text{size} = 0 \\
\text{ENQ(10): } & \text{rear} = ({-1}+1)\bmod 5 = 0; \text{arr}[0] = 10; \text{size} = 1 \\
\text{ENQ(20): } & \text{rear} = (0+1)\bmod 5 = 1; \text{arr}[1] = 20; \text{size} = 2 \\
\text{ENQ(30): } & \text{rear} = 2; \text{arr}[2] = 30; \text{size} = 3 \\
\text{ENQ(40): } & \text{rear} = 3; \text{arr}[3] = 40; \text{size} = 4 \\
\text{DEQ: }     & \text{return arr[0]=10; front} = (0+1)\bmod 5 = 1; \text{size} = 3 \\
\text{DEQ: }     & \text{return arr[1]=20; front} = 2; \text{size} = 2 \\
\text{ENQ(50): } & \text{rear} = (3+1)\bmod 5 = 4; \text{arr}[4] = 50; \text{size} = 3 \\
\text{ENQ(60): } & \text{rear} = (4+1)\bmod 5 = 0; \text{arr}[0] = 60; \text{size} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final state:** `front = 2`, `rear = 0`, queue contents (in logical order): **30, 40, 50, 60**.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Sparse Matrix Triplet Storage

Original 4×5 matrix `M`:

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 3 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 & 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 6 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Triplet Representation (extra header row stores dimensions):**

| Row Index | Column Index | Value |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 4 | 5 | 6 |
| 0 | 2 | 3 |
| 0 | 4 | 4 |
| 1 | 2 | 5 |
| 1 | 3 | 7 |
| 3 | 1 | 2 |
| 3 | 2 | 6 |

> **Space saved:** Original = 4 × 5 = **20 cells**. Triplet = 7 rows × 3 cols = **21 cells** (more in this case — sparse only pays off when k ≪ m×n).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of a Stack Implemented on an Array

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU["CPU / User Program"]
    PUSH["PUSH Operation"]
    POP["POP Operation"]
    PEEK["PEEK Operation"]
    CHECK["Overflow / Underflow Checker"]
    TOP["TOP Index (initially -1)"]
    ARR["Contiguous Array A of size MAX"]

    CPU --> PUSH
    CPU --> POP
    CPU --> PEEK
    PUSH --> CHECK
    POP  --> CHECK
    CHECK -- "top == MAX-1" --> OF["OVERFLOW: Stack Full"]
    CHECK -- "top == -1"     --> UF["UNDERFLOW: Stack Empty"]
    CHECK -- "Safe"          --> TOP
    TOP -- "arr top = x"     --> ARR
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Polynomial Addition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Start ADD P1 and P2"]
    INIT["Initialize i=0, j=0, k=0"]
    LOOP{"i <= d1 AND j <= d2?"}
    EQ{"i == j?"}
    ADDC["R.coeff k = P1.coeff i + P2.coeff j; i++, j++, k++"]
    IG["Copy P1.coeff i to R; i++, k++"]
    JL["Copy P2.coeff j to R; j++, k++"]
    REM1["Copy remaining P1 terms to R"]
    REM2["Copy remaining P2 terms to R"]
    END["Return polynomial R"]

    START --> INIT --> LOOP
    LOOP -- "True"  --> EQ
    LOOP -- "False" --> REM1 --> REM2 --> END
    EQ  -- "Yes" --> ADDC --> LOOP
    EQ  -- "No, i greater" --> IG  --> LOOP
    EQ  -- "No, j greater" --> JL  --> LOOP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Circular Queue Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENQ["ENQUEUE x"]
    DEQ["DEQUEUE"]
    RCOND{"(rear + 1) mod MAX == front?"}
    FCOND{"front == -1?"}
    WR["rear = rear + 1 mod MAX; arr rear = x; size++"]
    RD["x = arr front; front = front + 1 mod MAX; size--"]
    OFL["QUEUE FULL Error"]
    EFL["QUEUE EMPTY Error"]

    ENQ --> RCOND
    RCOND -- "Yes" --> OFL
    RCOND -- "No"  --> WR
    DEQ --> FCOND
    FCOND -- "Yes" --> EFL
    FCOND -- "No"  --> RD
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Sparse Matrix Triplet Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["Header Row: rows, cols, num_nonzero"]
    T1["Triplet 1: row, col, val"]
    T2["Triplet 2: row, col, val"]
    T3["..."]
    TN["Triplet k: row, col, val"]

    H  --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> TN
    T1 -.- NOTE1["Stored densely in 2D array: k+1 rows, 3 cols"]
    H  -.- NOTE2["Drastically reduces memory if sparsity greater than 70%"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a sparse matrix. Explain its triplet representation with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A matrix in which the number of zero entries is significantly larger than the number of non-zero entries is called a **sparse matrix**. A matrix with density ≤ **30%** is generally considered sparse.
- **Triplet Form (1 mark):** It is stored as a 2-D array of size `(k+1) × 3`, where `k` is the number of non-zero entries. The first row stores the dimensions `(rows, cols, k)`. Each subsequent row stores `(row_index, col_index, value)` of one non-zero element.
- **Example (1 mark):** For matrix
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$
the triplet is:
`[[3, 3, 3], [0, 1, 5], [1, 0, 3], [2, 2, 4]]`

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between a stack and a queue. State the overflow and underflow conditions when implemented using an array.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Stack (LIFO)** — Last In, First Out. Insertion and deletion occur at the **same end** called the `top`. Example: undo operations, recursion.
- **Queue (FIFO)** — First In, First Out. Insertion happens at `rear` and deletion at `front`. Example: CPU scheduling, printer spooler.
- **Stack overflow:** `top == MAX - 1` (no space left). **Stack underflow:** `top == -1` (no element to pop).
- **Circular queue overflow:** `(rear + 1) % MAX == front`. **Underflow:** `front == -1`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students are expected to answer **EITHER (a) OR (b)** within the choice pair.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q(a)** Explain polynomial addition and subtraction using arrays. Write the algorithm and illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A polynomial of degree `n` is stored as an array of `n+1` coefficients such that `P[i]` is the coefficient of `x^i`. To save space, only the highest non-zero degree is tracked.

**Algorithm: ADD(P1, P2) → R (3 marks):**
```
1. Start
2. i = 0, j = 0, k = 0
3. while i ≤ P1.degree AND j ≤ P2.degree:
       if i == j:
           R[k] = P1[i] + P2[j]
           i = i + 1, j = j + 1
       else if i > j:
           R[k] = P1[i]
           i = i + 1
       else:
           R[k] = P2[j]
           j = j + 1
       k = k + 1
4. while i ≤ P1.degree:  R[k++] = P1[i++]
5. while j ≤ P2.degree:  R[k++] = P2[j++]
6. R.degree = k - 1
7. Return R
```

**Subtraction** is identical except the term `R[k] = P1[i] - P2[j]` replaces the addition.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**
- `P1 = 3 + 5x + 2x^3` (degree 3)
- `P2 = 1 + 4x + 6x^2` (degree 2)
- Trace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i=0,j=0: &\quad R[0] = 3+1 = 4,\ k=1 \\
i=1,j=1: &\quad R[1] = 5+4 = 9,\ k=2 \\
i=2,j=2: &\quad R[2] = 0+6 = 6,\ k=3 \\
i=3,j=3: &\quad P2 \text{ exhausted. Copy } P1[3]=2. \\
R(x) &= 4 + 9x + 6x^2 + 2x^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Complexity:** Time = **O(n + m)**, Space = **O(n + m)**.

**Q(b)** Implement a stack using an array. Write the PUSH, POP, and PEEP algorithms. Explain how multiple stacks can be efficiently stored in a single array. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1-D Array Stack Implementation (3 marks):**

```
PUSH(arr, top, MAX, x):
    if top == MAX - 1:
        print("STACK OVERFLOW"); return
    top = top + 1
    arr[top] = x
    return top

POP(arr, top):
    if top == -1:
        print("STACK UNDERFLOW"); return
    x = arr[top]
    top = top - 1
    return x, top

PEEP(arr, top, i):   // i-th element from top
    if top - i + 1 < 0:
        print("Stack has fewer than i elements"); return
    return arr[top - i + 1]
```

**Multiple Stacks in One Array (4 marks):**
- **Naïve approach:** divide the array into `k` equal slots — wastes space.
- **Efficient approach (Memory-Sharing / Buddy System):** Keep `k` stacks in one array of size `n`. Maintain two arrays:
  - `bounds[i]` → upper limit of stack `i` (where it can grow up to).
  - `top[i]`    → current top index of stack `i`.
- Initially: `top[1] = 0`, `bounds[k] = n`, and for `i > 1`: `top[i] = bounds[i-1] = bounds[i] - 1`. Boundaries are equally spaced.
- When a stack overflows but another stack has free space, perform a **shift operation**: move the lower stack's elements up by one position to create a slot. Detection of overflow: `top[i] == bounds[i]`.
- **Time complexity:** PUSH = **O(1)** amortized; overflow shift = **O(n)** in the worst case but rare.
- **Space efficiency:** Achieves **O(n)** total space utilization.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q(a)** Explain the circular queue implementation using an array. Write the ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE algorithms. What are the advantages of a circular queue over a linear queue? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (1 mark):** A circular queue treats the array indices as **modular** — after index `MAX-1`, the next index wraps to `0`. This eliminates the "wasted space" problem of a linear queue.

**Algorithm (3 marks):**
```
ENQUEUE(arr, front, rear, MAX, size, x):
    if size == MAX:
        print("QUEUE FULL"); return
    if front == -1: front = 0
    rear = (rear + 1) % MAX
    arr[rear] = x
    size = size + 1

DEQUEUE(arr, front, rear, MAX, size):
    if size == 0:
        print("QUEUE EMPTY"); return
    x = arr[front]
    if front == rear:
        front = -1; rear = -1
    else:
        front = (front + 1) % MAX
    size = size - 1
    return x
```

**Overflow condition:** `size == MAX` or equivalently `(rear + 1) % MAX == front`.  
**Underflow condition:** `size == 0` or `front == -1`.

**Worked Trace (2 marks):** `MAX = 5`, sequence `ENQ(10), ENQ(20), ENQ(30), ENQ(40), DEQ, DEQ, ENQ(50), ENQ(60)`.
- After 4 ENQ: `front=0, rear=3`.  
- After 2 DEQ: `front=2, rear=3`.  
- After `ENQ(50)`: `rear=4`.  
- After `ENQ(60)`: `rear=(4+1)%5=0` (wrap-around successful).  
- Final logical order: **30, 40, 50, 60**.

**Advantages over Linear Queue (1 mark):**
- **No memory wastage** — slots freed by DEQ are immediately reusable.
- **O(1)** time for both ENQ and DEQ, regardless of queue size.
- **Better cache locality** due to contiguous storage.
- Used in **OS process scheduling, CPU scheduling (Round-Robin), disk scheduling, and ring buffers** in device drivers.

**Q(b)** Discuss the various applications of arrays in computer science with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Polynomial Representation and Manipulation (2 marks):**
- Arrays store coefficients; operations like addition, multiplication, and evaluation use simple index arithmetic.
- Example: Horner's method for `P(x) = a_n·x^n + ... + a_0` evaluates in **O(n)** using nested multiplications.

**2. Sparse Matrix Storage (2 marks):**
- Used in scientific computing, graph adjacency, and image processing.
- Triplet form reduces memory from **O(m×n)** to **O(k)**.
- Real-world: Google PageRank, finite element meshes, social network graphs.

**3. Implementation of Linear Data Structures (1 mark):**
- Stacks (LIFO), Queues (FIFO), Deques, and priority queues are routinely implemented using static or dynamic arrays.

**4. String Storage and Manipulation (1 mark):**
- Character arrays with `'\0'` terminator.
- Pattern matching (Naïve `O(nm)`, KMP `O(n+m)`), palindrome check, anagram detection.

**5. Lookup Tables and Direct-Address Tables (1 mark):**
- Constant-time direct access — used in compiler symbol tables, switch-case jump tables, and compiler opcode dispatching.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the header row** in sparse matrix triplet → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Using `front = -1` and `rear = -1` instead of a `size` counter** for circular queue overflow detection → full/empty condition confusion costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing row-major and column-major** address calculation in 2-D arrays — re-derive before writing.
> 4. **Writing `O(n²)` for polynomial multiplication** when it should be **O(n × m)**.
> 5. **Not distinguishing stack overflow (`top == MAX-1`) from underflow (`top == -1`)** — examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **Skipping the modular wraparound step** in circular queue — always show `rear = (rear + 1) % MAX` explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> - ✅ **Array** = contiguous, homogeneous, random-access structure; **O(1)** indexing.
> - ✅ **Address formula** (row-major): `BA + (i × n + j) × w`.
> - ✅ **Polynomial storage** uses parallel coefficient array; operations are **O(n + m)**.
> - ✅ **Sparse matrix** density threshold = **30%**; triplet form `(row, col, val)` cuts memory to **O(k)**.
> - ✅ **Stack** = LIFO; `PUSH/POP/PEEP` all **O(1)**; uses `top` index starting at `-1`.
> - ✅ **Linear queue** wastes space on DEQ; **Circular queue** uses modulo `% MAX` to reuse slots.
> - ✅ **Circular queue overflow:** `(rear + 1) % MAX == front`; **underflow:** `front == -1`.
> - ✅ **Multiple stacks in one array** use `bounds[]` and `top[]` arrays for memory sharing.
> - ✅ **String operations** (strlen, strcmp, strcat, strrev) are all **O(n)**.
> - ✅ **Array rotation by k** uses triple-reversal trick in **O(n) time, O(1) space**.
> - ✅ Always declare **time AND space complexity** in KTU answers.
> - ✅ Draw a **memory diagram** with arrows for any stack/queue/sparse matrix question — visuals fetch extra marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sparse Matrix and Its Representation, Stack Definitions and Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sparse Matrix and Its Representation, Stack Definitions and Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sparse Matrix — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sparse Matrix (KTU 2024 Definition):** A matrix in which the number of **zero elements** is significantly greater than the number of **non-zero elements** is termed a *sparse matrix*.

Mathematically, a matrix $A$ of order $m \times n$ is considered **sparse** if:

$$\text{Sparsity} = \frac{\text{Number of Zero Elements}}{\text{Total Number of Elements}} \geq 0.5 \quad \text{(or commonly } \geq 0.75\text{)}$$

Equivalently, a matrix is sparse if the count of **non-zero elements** $nnz \ll m \times n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Rule (commonly tested in KTU):** If $nnz \leq \frac{m \times n}{3}$, the matrix is treated as sparse. Otherwise, it is stored as a dense matrix.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant classroom seating chart of 1000 seats** for a college fest, but only **45 students actually turned up** while the rest are empty. Would you build a chart tracking every empty seat? **No!** You would simply make a list: *"Seat B-12: Arun, Seat C-07: Meera, …"*. That compact list is exactly how a **sparse matrix representation** works — we ignore zeros and remember only what matters.

### 1.3 Stack — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *Stack* is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** principle, where insertion and deletion of elements occur at only one end, called the **TOP** of the stack.

Formally, a stack is an **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** characterized by the ordered tuple:

$$\text{Stack} = (D, S, \text{TOP}, \text{Operations})$$

Where:
- $D$ = Data elements
- $S$ = The ordered storage container
- **TOP** = A pointer/index marking the most recently inserted element
- **Operations** = $\{$ `push`, `pop`, `peek`, `isEmpty`, `isFull` $\}$

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition for Stack

Think of a **pringles chips can** 🍽️ — you can only push chips in from the top, and you can only pull out the chip that was placed **last** (the top one). The first chip placed at the bottom stays there until all others above it are removed. That's LIFO in physical form.

Another classic analogy: a stack of **books on a desk** 📚, or the **Back button** in your web browser (the most recently visited page is the first one you return to).

### 1.5 KTU Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Module 1 Takeaways:**
> - Sparse matrix → memory-efficient storage (uses **array triplet** or **linked list**)
> - Stack → fundamental LIFO ADT; basis for recursion, expression evaluation, and DFS
> - Both topics are **CO1 (Apply)** mapped — focus on implementation and algorithmic analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Sparse Matrices Need Special Representation

A standard $m \times n$ matrix in memory uses $m \times n$ storage cells. If most cells contain zeros, this is **memory-inefficient**. For example, storing a $1000 \times 1000$ matrix with only 2000 non-zero entries wastes $999,000$ cells.

The core insight: **only store the (row, column, value) of non-zero entries**.

---

### 2.2 Representation 1 — Array (Triplet / COO Format)

In this scheme, we maintain a **2D array** of size $3 \times (nnz + 1)$:

- **Row 0** stores metadata: `rows`, `cols`, `nnz`
- **Rows 1 to nnz** store: `row_index`, `col_index`, `value`

**Memory required:**

$$\text{Memory}_{\text{triplet}} = 3 \times (nnz + 1) \times \text{sizeof(data)}$$

**Memory saved vs dense:**

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{m \times n - 3(nnz + 1)}{m \times n} \times 100\%$$

### 2.3 Representation 2 — Linked List Representation

Each non-zero element is a node containing three fields: `row`, `col`, `value`, and a `next` pointer.

**Memory per node:**

$$\text{Memory}_{\text{node}} = \text{sizeof}(row) + \text{sizeof}(col) + \text{sizeof}(value) + \text{sizeof}(pointer)$$

### 2.4 Stack — Detailed Operation Semantics

Let `S[0..MAX-1]` be an array-based stack and `TOP` be an integer pointer initialized to `-1`.

| Operation | Precondition | Postcondition | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `push(S, x)` | `TOP < MAX - 1` (Stack NOT FULL) | `TOP = TOP + 1; S[TOP] = x` | Inserts `x` at the top |
| `pop(S)` | `TOP ≥ 0` (Stack NOT EMPTY) | `TOP = TOP - 1`; returns `S[TOP+1]` | Removes and returns top element |
| `peek(S)` | `TOP ≥ 0` | No state change; returns `S[TOP]` | Views top without removing |
| `isEmpty(S)` | — | Returns `TRUE` if `TOP = -1` | Empty check |
| `isFull(S)` | — | Returns `TRUE` if `TOP = MAX - 1` | Full check |

### 2.5 Stack Overflow and Underflow Conditions

> [!WARNING]
> - **Stack Overflow:** Attempting to `push` when `TOP = MAX - 1` → memory exceeded
> - **Stack Underflow:** Attempting to `pop` or `peek` when `TOP = -1` → empty stack violation

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the consolidated high-yield formula table for Module 1 (Sparse Matrix & Stack).

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sparse Matrix Sparsity | $\frac{z}{m \times n}$ where $z$ = zero count | Ratio (dimensionless) |
| 2 | Triplet Memory | $3 \times (nnz + 1)$ | Cells |
| 3 | Linked-list Memory | $(nnz) \times (\text{int} + \text{int} + \text{double} + \text{ptr})$ | Bytes |
| 4 | Stack push (array) | `S[++TOP] = x` | Operation |
| 5 | Stack pop (array) | `x = S[TOP--]` | Operation |
| 6 | Stack Empty | `TOP = -1` | Boolean state |
| 7 | Stack Full | `TOP = MAX - 1` | Boolean state |
| 8 | Total stack capacity | $\text{MAX}$ | Elements |
| 9 | Time complexity, push/pop/peek | $O(1)$ | Time |
| 10 | Time complexity, sparse-matrix addition via triplet | $O(m \times n + nnz_A \times nnz_B)$ | Time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Sparse matrices** are pivotal in **graph algorithms** (adjacency matrices), **machine learning** (one-hot encoded vectors), **scientific computing** (finite element analysis), and **search engines** (inverted index).
> - **Stacks** power **function call management** in compilers, **expression evaluation**, **backtracking algorithms**, **browser history**, **undo/redo operations**, and **memory management**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Triplet Representation of a Sparse Matrix

Consider the following $4 \times 5$ sparse matrix:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 3 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 7 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Count the non-zero elements:**

$$
nnz = 5 \quad (\text{the values } 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 = 6 \text{ non-zeros})
$$

Let me recount: row 1 → 2 nonzero, row 2 → 1 nonzero, row 3 → 1 nonzero, row 4 → 2 nonzero. So $nnz = 6$.

**Step 2 — Construct the triplet array:**

Row 0 holds metadata, rows 1 to 6 hold triplets.

$$
\text{Triplet} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 0 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 & 5 \\ 2 & 3 & 6 \\ 3 & 0 & 7 \\ 3 & 4 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Validate memory savings:**

$$
\text{Dense memory} = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ cells}
$$

$$
\text{Triplet memory} = 3 \times (6 + 1) = 21 \text{ cells}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For this small example, triplet actually uses **1 more cell** — sparse representation truly shines only when $nnz \ll \frac{m \times n}{3}$. For very large matrices (e.g., $10000 \times 10000$ with $nnz = 5000$), the savings are massive.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Addition of Two Sparse Matrices Using Triplets

Given two sparse matrices $A$ and $B$ in triplet form, derive $C = A + B$.

**Algorithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Initialize } i_A = 1, i_B = 1, i_C = 1 \\
&\text{while } (i_A \leq nnz_A \text{ AND } i_B \leq nnz_B) \text{ do:} \\
&\quad \text{if } (\text{row}_A < \text{row}_B) \text{ OR } (\text{row}_A = \text{row}_B \text{ AND } \text{col}_A < \text{col}_B): \\
&\qquad \text{copy } (A.row, A.col, A.val) \text{ to } C[i_C++]; \quad i_A++ \\
&\quad \text{else if } (\text{row}_A > \text{row}_B) \text{ OR } (\text{row}_A = \text{row}_B \text{ AND } \text{col}_A > \text{col}_B): \\
&\qquad \text{copy } (B.row, B.col, B.val) \text{ to } C[i_C++]; \quad i_B++ \\
&\quad \text{else (row}_A = \text{row}_B \text{ AND col}_A = \text{col}_B\text{):} \\
&\qquad \text{sum} = A.val + B.val \\
&\qquad \text{if } \text{sum} \neq 0: \text{ store } (A.row, A.col, \text{sum}) \text{ in } C[i_C++] \\
&\qquad i_A++; \quad i_B++ \\
&\text{Append remaining rows of } A \text{ or } B \text{ to } C.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= O(nnz_A + nnz_B) \quad \text{(each triplet is visited at most twice)} \\
&\ll O(m \times n) \quad \text{(dense addition)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Stack Operations Trace

Consider an empty stack with `MAX = 5`, `TOP = -1`. Perform the sequence: `push(10), push(20), pop(), push(30), peek(), push(40), push(50)`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Initial:} \quad S = [\;], \quad \text{TOP} = -1 \\
\\
&\textbf{push(10)}: \quad \text{TOP} < 4 \Rightarrow \text{TOP} = 0, \quad S[0] = 10 \\
&\quad S = [10, \;, \;, \;, \;], \quad \text{TOP} = 0 \\
\\
&\textbf{push(20)}: \quad \text{TOP} < 4 \Rightarrow \text{TOP} = 1, \quad S[1] = 20 \\
&\quad S = [10, 20, \;, \;, \;], \quad \text{TOP} = 1 \\
\\
&\textbf{pop()}: \quad \text{TOP} \geq 0 \Rightarrow \text{returns } S[1] = 20, \quad \text{TOP} = 0 \\
&\quad S = [10, \;, \;, \;, \;], \quad \text{TOP} = 0 \\
\\
&\textbf{push(30)}: \quad \text{TOP} < 4 \Rightarrow \text{TOP} = 1, \quad S[1] = 30 \\
&\quad S = [10, 30, \;, \;, \;], \quad \text{TOP} = 1 \\
\\
&\textbf{peek()}: \quad \text{TOP} \geq 0 \Rightarrow \text{returns } S[1] = 30, \quad \text{TOP remains } 1 \\
\\
&\textbf{push(40)}: \quad \text{TOP} < 4 \Rightarrow \text{TOP} = 2, \quad S[2] = 40 \\
&\quad S = [10, 30, 40, \;, \;], \quad \text{TOP} = 2 \\
\\
&\textbf{push(50)}: \quad \text{TOP} < 4 \Rightarrow \text{TOP} = 3, \quad S[3] = 50 \\
&\quad S = [10, 30, 40, 50, \;], \quad \text{TOP} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 C Language Implementation — Array-Based Stack

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 100

int stack[MAX];
int TOP = -1;

void push(int x) {
    if (TOP == MAX - 1) {
        printf("Stack Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    stack[++TOP] = x;
}

int pop() {
    if (TOP == -1) {
        printf("Stack Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return stack[TOP--];
}

int peek() {
    if (TOP == -1) {
        printf("Stack is Empty\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return stack[TOP];
}

int isEmpty() { return TOP == -1; }
int isFull()  { return TOP == MAX - 1; }
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Time complexity of every stack operation above = $O(1)$** (constant time). This is the chief reason stacks are used in performance-critical systems.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Representation Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triplet vs Linked-list Sparse Representation — Head-to-Head**

| Criterion | Triplet (Array) | Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Memory (asymptotic) | $O(nnz)$ | $O(nnz)$ |
| Random access | $O(1)$ by index | $O(n)$ traversal |
| Insertion of new element | $O(nnz)$ — requires shift | $O(1)$ at head |
| Deletion | $O(nnz)$ | $O(1)$ if pointer given |
| Cache locality | Excellent (contiguous) | Poor (scattered) |
| Ease of transpose computation | Moderate | Moderate |
| KTU recommendation | Most commonly tested | Less frequently asked |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sparse Matrix Triplet Representation — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Original Sparse Matrix A m x n"] --> B["Scan all m x n cells"]
    B --> C{"Is element non-zero?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["Record row col value"]
    C -->|"No"| E["Skip element"]
    D --> F["Triplet Array 3 x nnz+1"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Row 0: Metadata rows cols nnz"]
    F --> H["Row 1 to nnz: Triplets"]
    G --> I["Final Compact Representation"]
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Sparse Matrix Addition Algorithm — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Start: Read Triplet A and Triplet B"] --> Q{"iA nnzA AND iB nnzB"}
    Q -->|"Yes"| R{"Compare rowA colA vs rowB colB"}
    R -->|"A position smaller"| S["Copy A entry to result C"]
    R -->|"B position smaller"| T["Copy B entry to result C"]
    R -->|"Same position"| U["sum valA plus valB"]
    U --> V{"sum equals 0?"}
    V -->|"No"| W["Store sum in result C"]
    V -->|"Yes"| X["Skip both entries"]
    S --> Y["Increment pointer iA or iB"]
    T --> Y
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
    Y --> Q
    Q -->|"No"| Z["Append remaining entries"]
    Z --> AA["Output sparse result matrix C"]
```

### 4.3 Stack ADT — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stack ADT"] --> B["Data: Array S of MAX size"]
    A --> C["Pointer: TOP"]
    B --> D["S 0"]
    B --> E["S 1"]
    B --> F["S TOP"]
    B --> G["S MAX minus 1"]
    C --> H["Tracks current top element index"]
    A --> I["Operations"]
    I --> I1["push x"]
    I --> I2["pop"]
    I --> I3["peek"]
    I --> I4["isEmpty"]
    I --> I5["isFull"]
    I1 --> J1["Insert x at S TOP then TOP plus plus"]
    I2 --> J2["Return S TOP then TOP minus minus"]
    I3 --> J3["Return S TOP without modification"]
    I4 --> J4["Return TRUE if TOP equals minus 1"]
    I5 --> J5["Return TRUE if TOP equals MAX minus 1"]
```

### 4.4 Stack State Transitions — Push and Pop

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> OneElement: push x
    OneElement --> TwoElement: push y
    TwoElement --> OneElement: pop
    OneElement --> Empty: pop
    TwoElement --> ThreeElement: push z
    ThreeElement --> Full: push w
    Full --> FullError: push attempted
    Empty --> EmptyError: pop attempted
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the state diagram above, **Empty** and **Full** are terminal bound states where push/pop respectively trigger overflow/underflow errors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a sparse matrix. State the condition under which a matrix is considered sparse.

**Model Answer:**

A matrix is called **sparse** when the number of zero elements is significantly larger than the number of non-zero elements. Formally, if the matrix has order $m \times n$ and contains $nnz$ non-zero entries, it is sparse when:

$$nnz \ll m \times n$$

Equivalently, using a common rule: a matrix is sparse when the ratio of zeros to total elements exceeds 0.5 (or 50%). Such matrices are stored compactly using triplet or linked-list representations to save memory. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is a stack? Explain the LIFO principle with one real-world example.

**Model Answer:**

A **stack** is a linear data structure that permits insertion and deletion of elements at only one end, called the **TOP**. It obeys the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** principle — the element inserted most recently is the first one to be removed.

**Real-world example:** A stack of plates in a cafeteria — only the topmost plate can be picked up or a new plate can be added on top. The last plate placed is removed first. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **triplet representation** of a sparse matrix. Convert the following $4 \times 4$ sparse matrix into its triplet form:
>
> $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 & 0 & 7 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Write the algorithm (in C-style pseudocode) to add two sparse matrices stored in triplet form. State its time complexity.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Triplet Representation Explanation:**

In triplet representation, we store only the **non-zero elements** along with their position (row and column indices) in a compact 2D array of size $3 \times (nnz + 1)$. The first row holds metadata: total rows, total columns, and total non-zero count. The remaining rows store `(row, column, value)` of each non-zero entry.

**Step 1 — Identify non-zero entries in $M$:**

- $(0, 2) \to 5$
- $(1, 0) \to 3$
- $(1, 3) \to 7$
- $(2, 1) \to 2$
- $(3, 3) \to 4$

**Step 2 — Build the triplet array:**

$$
\text{Triplet}(M) = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 2 & 5 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 7 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The first row $(4, 4, 5)$ denotes 4 rows, 4 columns, 5 non-zero elements. The next five rows are the triplets in row-major order. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Algorithm — Sparse Matrix Addition (Triplet Form):**

```c
void addSparse(int A[][3], int B[][3], int C[][3]) {
    int i = 1, j = 1, k = 1;
    int t1 = A[0][2];   // nnz of A
    int t2 = B[0][2];   // nnz of B

    // Set metadata of C
    C[0][0] = A[0][0];
    C[0][1] = A[0][1];

    while (i <= t1 && j <= t2) {
        if (A[i][0] < B[j][0] || 
            (A[i][0] == B[j][0] && A[i][1] < B[j][1])) {
            C[k][0] = A[i][0];
            C[k][1] = A[i][1];
            C[k][2] = A[i][2];
            i++; k++;
        }
        else if (A[i][0] > B[j][0] || 
                 (A[i][0] == B[j][0] && A[i][1] > B[j][1])) {
            C[k][0] = B[j][0];
            C[k][1] = B[j][1];
            C[k][2] = B[j][2];
            j++; k++;
        }
        else {  // Same row and column
            int sum = A[i][2] + B[j][2];
            if (sum != 0) {
                C[k][0] = A[i][0];
                C[k][1] = A[i][1];
                C[k][2] = sum;
                k++;
            }
            i++; j++;
        }
    }

    // Append remaining elements
    while (i <= t1) { C[k][0]=A[i][0]; C[k][1]=A[i][1]; C[k][2]=A[i][2]; i++; k++; }
    while (j <= t2) { C[k][0]=B[j][0]; C[k][1]=B[j][1]; C[k][2]=B[j][2]; j++; k++; }

    C[0][2] = k - 1;   // nnz of C
}
```

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

Each triplet is visited at most once → $O(nnz_A + nnz_B)$.

Since $nnz_A + nnz_B \ll m \times n$, this is far superior to dense addition which costs $O(m \times n)$. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Define the **Stack ADT**. Write C-style functions for the operations `push`, `pop`, `peek`, `isEmpty`, and `isFull` using an array of size `MAX`. Explain overflow and underflow conditions.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Simulate the following sequence of stack operations on an initially empty stack of `MAX = 6`. Display the stack state and `TOP` value after each step:
>
> `push(5), push(12), pop(), push(7), push(20), push(9), pop(), pop(), push(15)`

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Stack ADT Definition:**

A **Stack** is a linear Abstract Data Type (ADT) that allows insertion (`push`) and deletion (`pop`) at only one end, the **TOP**. It obeys **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** semantics.

**C Implementation:**

```c
#define MAX 100
int S[MAX];
int TOP = -1;

void push(int x) {
    if (isFull()) {
        printf("Stack Overflow!\n");
        return;
    }
    S[++TOP] = x;
}

int pop() {
    if (isEmpty()) {
        printf("Stack Underflow!\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return S[TOP--];
}

int peek() {
    if (isEmpty()) return -1;
    return S[TOP];
}

int isEmpty() { return TOP == -1; }
int isFull()  { return TOP == MAX - 1; }
```

**Overflow:** Occurs when `push` is attempted with `TOP = MAX - 1`. No space remains for new elements.

**Underflow:** Occurs when `pop` or `peek` is attempted with `TOP = -1`. The stack is empty, so no element can be removed or viewed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step-by-step trace with `MAX = 6`, initial `TOP = -1`:**

| Step | Operation | Condition | TOP after | Stack State (left to right, bottom to top) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `push(5)` | TOP=−1 < 5 ✅ | TOP = 0 | `[5]` |
| 2 | `push(12)` | TOP=0 < 5 ✅ | TOP = 1 | `[5, 12]` |
| 3 | `pop()` | TOP=1 ≥ 0 ✅ returns 12 | TOP = 0 | `[5]` |
| 4 | `push(7)` | TOP=0 < 5 ✅ | TOP = 1 | `[5, 7]` |
| 5 | `push(20)` | TOP=1 < 5 ✅ | TOP = 2 | `[5, 7, 20]` |
| 6 | `push(9)` | TOP=2 < 5 ✅ | TOP = 3 | `[5, 7, 20, 9]` |
| 7 | `pop()` | TOP=3 ≥ 0 ✅ returns 9 | TOP = 2 | `[5, 7, 20]` |
| 8 | `pop()` | TOP=2 ≥ 0 ✅ returns 20 | TOP = 1 | `[5, 7]` |
| 9 | `push(15)` | TOP=1 < 5 ✅ | TOP = 2 | `[5, 7, 15]` |

**Final state:** `TOP = 2`, Stack = `[5, 7, 15]`, no overflow/underflow encountered. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sparse matrix triplet construction**: Students frequently forget to include the **metadata row** (rows, cols, nnz) at index 0. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Triplets must be stored in row-major order** (ascending row, then ascending column within the same row). Random ordering costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **In stack trace questions**, students often forget to mention the **TOP value** explicitly after each operation. Always state both `TOP` and the array content.
> 4. **Overflow/Underflow** is a favorite 2-mark sub-question — do **not** skip these definitions.
> 5. For **sparse matrix addition**, students forget the edge case when `A[i][2] + B[i][2] = 0` → must be **excluded** from the result. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 (Sparse Matrix + Stack):**

- ✅ A **sparse matrix** has mostly zero entries; defined by $nnz \ll m \times n$.
- ✅ **Triplet representation** stores `(row, col, value)` of non-zero elements only, with a metadata header row.
- ✅ Triplet memory = $3 \times (nnz + 1)$ cells.
- ✅ **Linked-list representation** uses dynamic nodes; better for frequent insertion/deletion but worse for cache.
- ✅ Sparse matrices are added efficiently in $O(nnz_A + nnz_B)$ via a merge-like scan.
- ✅ A **stack** is a **LIFO** linear ADT with one access point: **TOP**.
- ✅ Core operations: `push`, `pop`, `peek`, `isEmpty`, `isFull` — all run in $O(1)$.
- ✅ **Overflow** = `push` when `TOP = MAX − 1`. **Underflow** = `pop/peek` when `TOP = −1`.
- ✅ Stack is the foundation of **recursion, expression evaluation, DFS, backtracking, and compiler call-stack management**.
- ✅ Always check the **sparsity threshold** before choosing a special representation.
- ✅ For KTU exams, **trace tables** for stack operations and **stepwise triplet conversions** are high-yield questions.
- ✅ Practice writing C code for **both array-based and linked-list** stack implementations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations on Stacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operations on Stacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Stack** is a linear, ordered data structure that obeys the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, wherein insertion and deletion of elements are permitted only at one end, formally known as the **TOP** of the stack. The end opposite to the TOP is sealed (fixed), and is referred to as the **BOTTOM** of the stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> A stack is an abstract data type (ADT) defined by the operations `Push`, `Pop`, `Peek`, `isEmpty`, and `isFull`. It is mathematically equivalent to a restricted form of a singly-linked list or a bounded array.

The two most fundamental operations performed on a stack are:

- **Push** — adds an element to the TOP of the stack.
- **Pop** — removes and returns the element at the TOP of the stack.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a vertical stack of plates in a college canteen:

- You can only **place a new plate on top** of the existing pile (this is `Push`).
- You can only **take the top-most plate** (this is `Pop`).
- You cannot directly access a plate buried in the middle without removing the ones above it first.

This is exactly the LIFO discipline. The plate you kept **last** is the one you take **first** — hence **Last-In-First-Out**.

A second analogy is the **browser's "Back" button**: every URL you visit is pushed onto a stack; pressing "Back" pops the most recently visited URL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **TOP** — index of the most recently inserted element.
> - **Overflow** — attempt to `Push` into a full stack (fixed-size array).
> - **Underflow** — attempt to `Pop` from an empty stack.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (as per KTU Model Question Paper Conventions)

| Metric | Standard Value / Notation |
|---|---|
| Time complexity of Push/Pop | **O(1)** |
| Space complexity (array of n) | **O(n)** |
| Maximum capacity (array) | **MAX** or **N** |
| Initial value of TOP | **−1** (array) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primitive Operations on a Stack — Structured Breakdown

A stack ADT supports **five primitive operations**. Below is each operation's pre-condition, post-condition, and logical purpose.

### 2.1.1 `Push(S, x)` — Insertion
- **Pre-condition:** Stack is not full.
- **Post-condition:** `TOP` increments by 1; element `x` is stored at the new TOP.
- **Why:** Inserts an element so that it becomes the next candidate for removal.

### 2.1.2 `Pop(S)` — Deletion
- **Pre-condition:** Stack is not empty.
- **Post-condition:** Returns the element at TOP; `TOP` decrements by 1.
- **Why:** Removes the most recently inserted element, preserving LIFO.

### 2.1.3 `Peek(S)` / `Top(S)` — Inspection
- **Pre-condition:** Stack is not empty.
- **Post-condition:** Returns the value at TOP **without** modifying the stack.
- **Why:** Lets the caller inspect the next-to-be-popped element safely.

### 2.1.4 `isEmpty(S)`
- **Returns:** `TRUE` if `TOP == −1`, else `FALSE`.
- **Why:** Prevents underflow before invoking Pop/Peek.

### 2.1.5 `isFull(S)`
- **Returns:** `TRUE` if `TOP == MAX − 1`, else `FALSE`.
- **Why:** Prevents overflow before invoking Push (array-based only).

## 2.2 Two Storage Strategies

KTU frequently asks students to contrast these. Both store the LIFO logical structure but differ in memory behavior.

| Aspect | Array-Based Stack | Linked-List-Based Stack |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Static, fixed at compile time | Dynamic, grows at runtime |
| Overflow | Possible (bounded) | Practically impossible (until heap exhausts) |
| TOP representation | Integer index | Pointer to top node |
| Extra memory | None | Pointer per node |
| Access time | O(1) with arithmetic | O(1) via pointer chase |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it appears verbatim in nearly every KTU module test for Module 1.

| Operation | Array-Based Equation | Condition Check | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Push | `TOP ← TOP + 1` ; `S[TOP] ← x` | `if (TOP < MAX − 1)` | **O(1)** |
| Pop | `x ← S[TOP]` ; `TOP ← TOP − 1` | `if (TOP ≥ 0)` | **O(1)** |
| Peek | `x ← S[TOP]` | `if (TOP ≥ 0)` | **O(1)** |
| isEmpty | returns `TOP == −1` | — | **O(1)** |
| isFull | returns `TOP == MAX − 1` | — | **O(1)** |
| Size of stack | `n = TOP + 1` | — | **O(1)** |

For linked-list implementation, the formulas become:

$$\text{Length of stack} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 \quad \text{(counted by traversal if no size field maintained)}$$

The pointer invariants are:

$$\text{TOP} \to \text{next} = \text{old TOP (before Push)}$$

$$\text{old TOP} \to \text{next} = \text{NULL (after Pop)}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stacks are not a textbook abstraction — they power:

1. **Function call management** — every C/Java program uses the system's *call stack* to store return addresses, local variables, and frame pointers.
2. **Expression evaluation & conversion** — infix to postfix/prefix, parenthesis balancing (compilers).
3. **Undo/Redo mechanisms** — text editors (VS Code, MS Word).
4. **Backtracking algorithms** — Depth-First Search (DFS), N-Queens, Sudoku solvers.
5. **Memory management** — the JVM, .NET CLR use stacks for evaluation of bytecode.
6. **Browser navigation history** — every commercial browser.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Array-Based Implementation — Exhaustive Algorithms

We model a stack `S` as an integer array of maximum capacity `MAX`, plus an integer `TOP` initialized to `−1`.

### 3.1.1 Algorithm: `Push(S, x)`

**Step 1 — Begin.**

**Step 2 — Check for overflow.**

$$\text{if } \text{TOP} \geq \text{MAX} - 1$$

then print `"Stack Overflow"` and **GOTO Step 5**.

**Step 3 — Increment TOP.**

$$\text{TOP} \leftarrow \text{TOP} + 1$$

**Step 4 — Insert element.**

$$S[\text{TOP}] \leftarrow x$$

**Step 5 — Return (success or failure indicator).**

### 3.1.2 Algorithm: `Pop(S)`

**Step 1 — Begin.**

**Step 2 — Check for underflow.**

$$\text{if } \text{TOP} < 0$$

then print `"Stack Underflow"` and **return −1** (sentinel value).

**Step 3 — Read the top element.**

$$x \leftarrow S[\text{TOP}]$$

**Step 4 — Decrement TOP.**

$$\text{TOP} \leftarrow \text{TOP} - 1$$

**Step 5 — Return x.**

### 3.1.3 Algorithm: `Peek(S)`

**Step 1 — Begin.**

**Step 2 — Check for underflow.**

$$\text{if } \text{TOP} < 0$$

then print `"Stack is Empty"` and **return −1**.

**Step 3 — Return the top element.**

$$\text{return } S[\text{TOP}]$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Trace (KTU Board Style)

Let `MAX = 5`, `TOP = −1`. Perform the following sequence: `Push(10), Push(20), Push(30), Pop, Push(40), Peek`.

**After Push(10):**
`TOP = 0`, `S[0] = 10`.

**After Push(20):**
`TOP = 1`, `S[1] = 20`.

**After Push(30):**
`TOP = 2`, `S[2] = 30`.

**After Pop:**
Returned value = `S[2] = 30`; `TOP` becomes `1`.

**After Push(40):**
`TOP = 2`, `S[2] = 40`.

**After Peek:**
Returns `S[2] = 40`; `TOP` remains `2`.

Final stack contents (bottom to top): `10, 20, 40`.

## 3.2 C Implementation (Array-Based)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 100

int stack[MAX];
int TOP = -1;

void push(int x) {
    if (TOP >= MAX - 1) {
        printf("Stack Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    TOP = TOP + 1;
    stack[TOP] = x;
    printf("%d pushed\n", x);
}

int pop() {
    if (TOP < 0) {
        printf("Stack Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    int x = stack[TOP];
    TOP = TOP - 1;
    return x;
}

int peek() {
    if (TOP < 0) {
        printf("Stack is Empty\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return stack[TOP];
}

int isEmpty() {
    return (TOP == -1);
}

int isFull() {
    return (TOP == MAX - 1);
}
```

## 3.3 Linked-List-Based Implementation

Here, the stack is a chain of nodes where the **head** plays the role of the **TOP**.

### 3.3.1 Node Definition

Each node stores a value and a pointer to the next node:

$$\text{Node} = \langle \text{data} : \text{int},\ \text{next} : \text{Node}^*\rangle$$

### 3.3.2 Algorithm: `Push(Head, x)` (Linked List)

**Step 1 — Allocate a new node.**

$$\text{newNode} \leftarrow \text{malloc(sizeof(Node))}$$

**Step 2 — Check allocation.**

$$\text{if (newNode} = \text{NULL)} \rightarrow \text{print "Memory Overflow"}$$

**Step 3 — Set fields of new node.**

$$\text{newNode} \to \text{data} \leftarrow x$$

$$\text{newNode} \to \text{next} \leftarrow \text{Head}$$

**Step 4 — Update Head.**

$$\text{Head} \leftarrow \text{newNode}$$

### 3.3.3 Algorithm: `Pop(Head)` (Linked List)

**Step 1 — Check underflow.**

$$\text{if (Head} = \text{NULL)} \rightarrow \text{print "Stack Underflow"}$$

**Step 2 — Read top value.**

$$x \leftarrow \text{Head} \to \text{data}$$

**Step 3 — Move Head forward.**

$$\text{temp} \leftarrow \text{Head}$$

$$\text{Head} \leftarrow \text{Head} \to \text{next}$$

$$\text{free(temp)}$$

**Step 4 — Return x.**

### 3.3.4 C Implementation (Linked List)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* next;
};

struct Node* top = NULL;

void push(int x) {
    struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Memory Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    newNode->data = x;
    newNode->next = top;
    top = newNode;
}

int pop() {
    if (top == NULL) {
        printf("Stack Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    int x = top->data;
    struct Node* temp = top;
    top = top->next;
    free(temp);
    return x;
}

int peek() {
    if (top == NULL) {
        printf("Stack is Empty\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return top->data;
}
```

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Push Operation)

| Property | Array-Based | Linked-List-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-check | `TOP == MAX − 1` | `malloc == NULL` |
| Memory cost | None extra | One pointer per node |
| Amortized cost | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Worst-case cost | **O(1)** | **O(n)** (if allocator scans free list) |
| Predictability | Highly predictable | Depends on heap state |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stack as a Bounded Array — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TOP Index Pointer starts at -1"] --> B{"isFull check: TOP == MAX-1 ?"}
    B -- YES --> C["Overflow: Push rejected"]
    B -- NO --> D["Increment TOP by 1"]
    D --> E["Write element into S at TOP"]
    E --> F["Return success status"]

    G["Stack Memory Array S of size MAX"] --> E
```
