```mermaid
    F --> H{"isEmpty check: TOP == -1 ?"}
    H -- YES --> I["Underflow: Pop rejected"]
    H -- NO --> J["Read S at TOP"]
    J --> K["Decrement TOP by 1"]
    K --> L["Return popped value to caller"]
```

## 4.2 Stack as a Linked List — Node Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0["BOTTOM Node | data = 10 | next -> N1"]
    N1["Middle Node | data = 20 | next -> N2"]
    N2["TOP Node | data = 30 | next -> NULL"]
    HEAD["Head Pointer points to N2"]

    HEAD -.-> N2
    N0 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
```

## 4.3 Push Operation — State Transition

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> HasOne: Push 10, TOP becomes 0
    HasOne --> HasTwo: Push 20, TOP becomes 1
    HasTwo --> HasThree: Push 30, TOP becomes 2
    HasThree --> HasTwo: Pop, TOP becomes 1, returns 30
    HasTwo --> HasTwoPlusOne: Push 40, TOP becomes 2
```

## 4.4 Pop Operation — Underflow Guard Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Caller invokes Pop"] --> P1{"TOP is less than 0?"}
    P1 -- YES --> P2["Print Stack Underflow"]
    P2 --> P3["Return sentinel -1"]
    P1 -- NO --> P4["Read element at TOP"]
    P4 --> P5["Decrement TOP by 1"]
    P5 --> P6["Return popped value"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module Test 1]`

**Define a stack. List its primitive operations and explain any two in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

A stack is a linear data structure that follows the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle. Insertion and deletion occur only at one end called the **TOP**.

Primitive operations:

1. `Push(S, x)` — inserts `x` at TOP.
2. `Pop(S)` — removes and returns TOP element.
3. `Peek(S)` — returns TOP element without deletion.
4. `isEmpty(S)` — checks whether stack is empty.
5. `isFull(S)` — checks whether stack is full.

**Push:** Increments TOP by 1, then stores `x` at `S[TOP]`. Fails if `TOP == MAX − 1` (overflow).

**Pop:** Returns `S[TOP]`, then decrements TOP by 1. Fails if `TOP == −1` (underflow).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Internal Sessional 2]`

**Differentiate between array-based and linked-list-based implementations of a stack. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Array-Based | Linked-List-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Memory allocation | Static (fixed size MAX) | Dynamic (grows at runtime) |
| Overflow | Possible if TOP reaches MAX − 1 | Practically no overflow (until heap exhausts) |
| Memory overhead | No extra pointer | One pointer per node |
| Access time | Direct via index | Via pointer chase |
| Cache performance | Better (contiguous) | Worse (scattered) |

- **Advantage of array-based:** Faster, cache-friendly, simpler code.
- **Advantage of linked-list-based:** No predefined size limit, no wasted memory.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Choice 1)

**(a)** Write the algorithms for `Push` and `Pop` operations on a stack implemented using an array. Explain overflow and underflow conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Trace the following operations on an empty stack of capacity 6. Show the contents of the stack and the value of TOP after every step. **(7 Marks)**

Operations: `Push(15), Push(25), Pop, Push(35), Push(45), Pop, Pop, Push(55), Peek`

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Algorithm `Push(S, x)`:**

```text
Step 1: START
Step 2: IF TOP == MAX - 1
            PRINT "Stack Overflow"
            RETURN
        END IF
Step 3: TOP ← TOP + 1
Step 4: S[TOP] ← x
Step 5: PRINT "Pushed", x
Step 6: RETURN
```

**Algorithm `Pop(S)`:**

```text
Step 1: START
Step 2: IF TOP < 0
            PRINT "Stack Underflow"
            RETURN -1
        END IF
Step 3: x ← S[TOP]
Step 4: TOP ← TOP - 1
Step 5: RETURN x
```

**Overflow condition:** When `TOP == MAX − 1`, no more elements can be inserted. Example: For `MAX = 5`, overflow happens when TOP equals 4 and another Push is attempted.

**Underflow condition:** When `TOP == −1`, the stack contains no elements. Popping in this state returns an error.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

Initial state: `TOP = −1`, stack is empty.

| Step | Operation | Stack Contents (bottom → top) | TOP | Returned Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Push(15) | 15 | 0 | — |
| 2 | Push(25) | 15, 25 | 1 | — |
| 3 | Pop | 15 | 0 | 25 |
| 4 | Push(35) | 15, 35 | 1 | — |
| 5 | Push(45) | 15, 35, 45 | 2 | — |
| 6 | Pop | 15, 35 | 1 | 45 |
| 7 | Pop | 15 | 0 | 35 |
| 8 | Push(55) | 15, 55 | 1 | — |
| 9 | Peek | 15, 55 | 1 | 55 |

Final state: Stack = `[15, 55]`, `TOP = 1`, last `Peek` returned **55**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Choice 2)

**(a)** Implement the `Push` and `Pop` operations of a stack using a singly linked list. Write the algorithm and explain how the TOP pointer is updated. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the time and space complexities of array-based and linked-list-based stack implementations for `n` elements. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Node Structure:**

$$\text{struct Node} = \{ \text{int data},\ \text{struct Node* next} \}$$

**Algorithm `Push(Head, x)`:**

```text
Step 1: START
Step 2: newNode ← malloc(sizeof(Node))
Step 3: IF newNode == NULL
            PRINT "Memory Overflow"
            RETURN
        END IF
Step 4: newNode.data  ← x
Step 5: newNode.next  ← Head
Step 6: Head          ← newNode
Step 7: RETURN
```

**Algorithm `Pop(Head)`:**

```text
Step 1: START
Step 2: IF Head == NULL
            PRINT "Stack Underflow"
            RETURN -1
        END IF
Step 3: x         ← Head.data
Step 4: temp      ← Head
Step 5: Head      ← Head.next
Step 6: free(temp)
Step 7: RETURN x
```

**Explanation of TOP pointer update:**

- During **Push**, a new node is created. Its `next` is set to the **current Head** (the previous TOP). Then `Head` is moved to the new node, making it the new TOP. The new node therefore becomes the most accessible element.
- During **Pop**, the value stored in the current `Head` is retrieved. `Head` is advanced to `Head.next`, effectively discarding the old TOP. The freed node no longer belongs to the stack.

The **head of the linked list is the TOP of the stack** in this representation. This allows both Push and Pop to complete in **O(1)** time, because no traversal is required.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Time Complexity Comparison:**

| Operation | Array-Based | Linked-List-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Push | **O(1)** | **O(1)** amortized |
| Pop | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Peek | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Search | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |

**Justification:**

- In an array-based stack, accessing `S[TOP]` uses direct index arithmetic, which takes constant time regardless of stack size.
- In a linked-list-based stack, `top->data` is accessed by following a single pointer, also a constant-time operation.
- Push in linked-list involves `malloc`, which on average is O(1) but in the worst case can scan the free list — hence amortized O(1).

**Space Complexity Comparison:**

- **Array-based:** Total memory = `n × sizeof(element)`. No overhead for pointers. Space = **O(n)**.
- **Linked-list-based:** Total memory = `n × (sizeof(element) + sizeof(pointer))`. Extra pointer overhead per node. Space = **O(n)** but with a larger constant factor.

**Conclusion:** Array-based is more space-efficient and cache-friendly; linked-list-based is more flexible in size.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Initial value of TOP:** Students often write `TOP = 0`. The correct value is `TOP = −1`, indicating an empty stack.
> 2. **Overflow condition:** The check is `TOP == MAX − 1`, **not** `TOP == MAX`. Writing the latter is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Underflow condition:** Must explicitly check `TOP < 0` (or `TOP == −1`) **before** accessing `S[TOP]`.
> 4. **Linked-list Push:** Many students forget to update `newNode.next` to the old Head, breaking the chain.
> 5. **Linked-list Pop:** Forgetting `free(temp)` causes a memory leak. KTU examiners deduct marks for this.
> 6. **Trace questions:** Always tabulate TOP after every operation. Skipping steps loses marks even if the final state is correct.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- A **stack** is a **LIFO** linear data structure.
- The end where insertion and deletion occur is called the **TOP**.
- The opposite end (sealed) is the **BOTTOM**.
- **Five primitive operations:** `Push`, `Pop`, `Peek`, `isEmpty`, `isFull`.
- Array-based stack: `TOP` initialized to **−1**; maximum capacity = `MAX`.
- **Overflow** = `TOP == MAX − 1` (Push fails).
- **Underflow** = `TOP == −1` (Pop fails).
- All primitive stack operations run in **O(1)** time.
- Linked-list-based stack uses the **head pointer** as the **TOP**.
- Linked-list Push: create new node → set `next` to old Head → update Head.
- Linked-list Pop: read Head data → advance Head → free old node.
- Stacks underpin function calls, recursion, expression evaluation, DFS, undo/redo.
- For a stack of `n` elements, both implementations use **O(n)** space; linked-list has extra pointer overhead.
- In **Peek**, the stack is **not modified**; in **Pop**, the stack **is modified**.
- A common KTU mistake: confusing **stack underflow** with **stack overflow** in trace problems — read the operation carefully.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Stacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of Stacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Stack** is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** principle, where insertion and deletion of elements occur only at one end, called the **TOP** of the stack. The two fundamental operations on a stack are:

- **PUSH(S, x)** — Inserts element `x` at the top of stack `S`. Time complexity: **O(1)**.
- **POP(S)** — Removes and returns the top element from stack `S`. Time complexity: **O(1)**.

An **Application of a Stack** refers to any computational problem where the natural order of processing is *reversed* relative to the order of arrival, or where temporary storage with LIFO semantics is required to track context, state, or pending operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PERAT751 — Module 1):**
> The official syllabus mandates coverage of the following stack applications:
> 1. **Infix to Postfix / Prefix conversion** (with parenthesis handling)
> 2. **Evaluation of Postfix / Prefix expressions**
> 3. **Balancing of symbols / parenthesis matching**
> 4. **Recursion simulation via function call stack**
> 5. **Backtracking algorithms** (e.g., N-Queens, Rat-in-a-Maze)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stack of Trays

Imagine the spring-loaded tray dispenser in a college canteen:

- **PUSH** = When the canteen worker places a fresh tray on top of the stack.
- **POP** = When a student picks the *top-most* tray (the one most recently placed).
- You **cannot** pull a tray from the middle or bottom — only the top.

The **last tray placed** is the **first one taken**. This is LIFO. Now think of your browser's "Back" button — the last page you visited is the first one you return to. Stacks make this trivially easy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Stacks work for Expression Conversion:**
> In an arithmetic expression, an operator must wait until *both* of its operands are processed before it can be applied. Since the *most recent pending operator* should always be evaluated first (due to precedence), a LIFO structure (stack) is the perfect match.

## 1.3 Operator Precedence Reference

For KTU board exams, the following precedence hierarchy is *standard* and must be memorized:

| Level | Operators | Associativity |
|:-----:|:---------:|:-------------:|
| 1 (Highest) | `^` (Exponentiation) | Right-to-Left |
| 2 | `*`, `/`, `%` | Left-to-Right |
| 3 | `+`, `-` | Left-to-Left |
| 4 (Lowest) | `(`, `)` | N/A (delimiters) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle: Why Stacks Match Expression Processing

When scanning an infix expression `A + B * C`, the rules dictate that `*` must be evaluated before `+`, even though `+` appears first. A stack-based algorithm uses this principle:

1. **Operands** (A, B, C) are *output immediately* (they are ready to be used).
2. **Operators** are *deferred* — pushed onto the stack.
3. An incoming operator of **higher or equal precedence** triggers a **POP** of the existing operator first (so that higher precedence gets output earlier and thus evaluates first).
4. **Left parenthesis** `(` is pushed as a "barrier" — it stops premature popping.
5. **Right parenthesis** `)` triggers popping until a matching `(` is found.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every algorithm parameter you must know for the KTU board exam:

| Algorithm | Input | Output | Stack Role | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|:---------:|:-----:|:------:|:----------:|:---------------:|:----------------:|
| Infix → Postfix | Infix expression | Postfix expression | Holds operators + `(` | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |
| Infix → Prefix | Infix expression | Prefix expression | Holds operators + `(` | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |
| Postfix Evaluation | Postfix expression | Numeric result | Holds operands | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |
| Prefix Evaluation | Prefix expression | Numeric result | Holds operands | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |
| Parenthesis Matching | Symbol string | Balanced / Not Balanced | Holds opening symbols | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |
| Function Call Stack | Recursive call | Return value | Holds activation records | **O(depth)** | **O(depth)** |
| Backtracking (N-Queens) | Board size `n` | All solutions | Holds current path | **O(n²)** | **O(n)** |

## 2.3 Infix → Postfix Algorithm (Shunting-Yard by Dijkstra)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Legend (used throughout KTU solutions):**
> - `icp` = **incoming precedence** (of the scanned token)
> - `isp` = **in-stack precedence** (of the operator at stack TOP)
> - `≻` means "pops the existing operator before pushing the new one"

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Initialize an empty operator stack `S`.
2. Scan the infix expression token by token from **left to right**.
3. If token is an **operand** → append it directly to the output.
4. If token is `(` → **PUSH** it onto `S`.
5. If token is `)` → **POP** and output all operators until `(` is encountered. Discard the `(`.
6. If token is an **operator** `op1`:
   - While `S` is non-empty AND `isp(TOP of S) ≻ icp(op1)`: **POP** and output TOP.
   - **PUSH** `op1` onto `S`.
7. After full scan → **POP and output** all remaining operators in `S`.

## 2.4 Postfix Evaluation Algorithm

1. Initialize an empty operand stack `S`.
2. Scan the postfix expression left to right.
3. If token is an **operand** → **PUSH** it onto `S`.
4. If token is an **operator** `op`:
   - **POP** operand2 from `S`.
   - **POP** operand1 from `S`.
   - Compute `result = operand1 op operand2`.
   - **PUSH** result onto `S`.
5. Final value on top of `S` is the answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall — Operand Order:**
> When popping for a binary operator, the **first popped** value is the **RIGHT operand** (`operand2`) and the **second popped** is the **LEFT operand** (`operand1`). Reversing this yields an incorrect result, especially for non-commutative operators like `-`, `/`, `^`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design (Lexical & Syntax Analysis):** Every production-grade compiler (GCC, Clang, Javac) uses a stack-based architecture for parsing expressions and generating machine code.
- **Operating Systems:** The call stack manages function invocations, local variable allocation, and return addresses. Stack overflow is a runtime fault caused by runaway recursion.
- **Embedded Systems & RTOS:** Interrupt nesting uses a hardware stack to save context.
- **Web Browsers:** The "Back" button is a stack of visited URLs.
- **Text Editors:** Undo/Redo functionality is implemented with two parallel stacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1: Convert Infix → Postfix

**Infix Expression:** `A + B * C - (D / E + F)`

### Token-by-Token Trace

We define the precedence table used:

$$
\text{isp}('(') = 0, \quad \text{icp}('(') = 5 \quad (\text{sentinel high to prevent popping itself})
$$

$$
\text{isp}('+') = \text{isp}('-') = 1, \quad \text{icp}('+') = \text{icp}('-') = 1
$$

$$
\text{isp}('*') = \text{isp}('/') = 2, \quad \text{icp}('*') = \text{icp}('/') = 2
$$

$$
\text{isp}('\textasciicircum') = 4, \quad \text{icp}('\textasciicircum') = 3 \quad (\text{right-assoc.})
$$

| Step | Token Read | Action | Stack (bottom→top) | Output |
|:----:|:----------:|:-------|:-------------------|:-------|
| 1 | `A` | Operand → output | empty | `A` |
| 2 | `+` | Stack empty → PUSH | `+` | `A` |
| 3 | `B` | Operand → output | `+` | `A B` |
| 4 | `*` | isp(`+`) = 1 < icp(`*`) = 2 → PUSH | `+ *` | `A B` |
| 5 | `C` | Operand → output | `+ *` | `A B C` |
| 6 | `-` | isp(`*`) = 2 ≻ icp(`-`) = 1 → POP `*`; isp(`+`) = 1 = icp(`-`) → POP `+`; PUSH `-` | `-` | `A B C * +` |
| 7 | `(` | PUSH (sentinel barrier) | `- (` | `A B C * +` |
| 8 | `D` | Operand → output | `- (` | `A B C * + D` |
| 9 | `/` | isp(`(`) = 0 < icp(`/`) = 2 → PUSH | `- ( /` | `A B C * + D` |
| 10 | `E` | Operand → output | `- ( /` | `A B C * + D E` |
| 11 | `+` | isp(`/`) = 2 ≻ icp(`+`) = 1 → POP `/`; PUSH `+` | `- ( +` | `A B C * + D E /` |
| 12 | `F` | Operand → output | `- ( +` | `A B C * + D E / F` |
| 13 | `)` | POP until `(` → POP `+`, POP `(` and discard | `-` | `A B C * + D E / F +` |
| 14 | End | POP remaining → POP `-` | empty | `A B C * + D E / F + -` |

**Final Postfix Expression:**

$$
\boxed{A\,B\,C\,*\,+\,D\,E\,/\,F\,+\,-}
$$

## 3.2 Example 2: Evaluate the Resulting Postfix Expression

Take the postfix: `A B C * + D E / F + -` with values `A=2, B=3, C=4, D=8, E=2, F=5`.

Substituting:

$$
2 \; 3 \; 4 \; * \; + \; 8 \; 2 \; / \; 5 \; + \; -
$$

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (bottom→top) |
|:----:|:-----:|:-------|:-------------------|
| 1 | `2` | PUSH | `2` |
| 2 | `3` | PUSH | `2, 3` |
| 3 | `4` | PUSH | `2, 3, 4` |
| 4 | `*` | POP 4 (op2), POP 3 (op1) → `3*4 = 12`; PUSH | `2, 12` |
| 5 | `+` | POP 12 (op2), POP 2 (op1) → `2+12 = 14`; PUSH | `14` |
| 6 | `8` | PUSH | `14, 8` |
| 7 | `2` | PUSH | `14, 8, 2` |
| 8 | `/` | POP 2 (op2), POP 8 (op1) → `8/2 = 4`; PUSH | `14, 4` |
| 9 | `5` | PUSH | `14, 4, 5` |
| 10 | `+` | POP 5 (op2), POP 4 (op1) → `4+5 = 9`; PUSH | `14, 9` |
| 11 | `-` | POP 9 (op2), POP 14 (op1) → `14-9 = 5`; PUSH | `5` |

**Final Evaluated Result:**

$$
\boxed{5}
$$

## 3.3 Example 3: Parenthesis Matching

**Input Symbol String:** `{ ( a + b ) * [ ( c - d ) ] }`

| Step | Symbol | Stack (top at right) | Status |
|:----:|:------:|:---------------------|:------:|
| 1 | `{` | `{` | OK |
| 2 | `(` | `{(` | OK |
| 3 | `a` | `{(` | OK (operand ignored) |
| 4 | `+` | `{(` | OK (operand ignored) |
| 5 | `b` | `{(` | OK |
| 6 | `)` | `{` | OK — matched with `(` |
| 7 | `*` | `{` | OK |
| 8 | `[` | `{[` | OK |
| 9 | `(` | `{[(` | OK |
| 10 | `c` | `{[(` | OK |
| 11 | `-` | `{[(` | OK |
| 12 | `d` | `{[(` | OK |
| 13 | `)` | `{[` | OK — matched with `(` |
| 14 | `]` | `{` | OK — matched with `[` |
| 15 | `}` | empty | OK — matched with `{` |

At End of String → **Stack is empty → BALANCED** ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matching Rule:** When popping for `}`, the top of stack must be `{`. If top is `(` or `[`, it is a **MISMATCH** — the string is unbalanced.

## 3.4 Example 4: Function Call Stack — Recursion Trace

Consider the recursive computation of factorial `n! = n × (n−1)!`, with `0! = 1`.

For `factorial(3)`, the call stack evolves as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Call 1: } & \text{PUSH } \langle\text{fact}(3),\; \text{retAddr}=main,\; n=3\rangle \\
\text{Call 2: } & \text{PUSH } \langle\text{fact}(2),\; \text{retAddr}=fact3,\; n=2\rangle \\
\text{Call 3: } & \text{PUSH } \langle\text{fact}(1),\; \text{retAddr}=fact2,\; n=1\rangle \\
\text{Base: } & \text{Return 1 → POP Call 3} \\
\text{Resolve: } & \text{fact}(1) \text{ returns } 1 \times 1 = 1 \rightarrow \text{POP Call 2} \\
\text{Resolve: } & \text{fact}(2) \text{ returns } 2 \times 1 = 2 \rightarrow \text{POP Call 1} \\
\text{Resolve: } & \text{fact}(3) \text{ returns } 3 \times 2 = 6 \rightarrow \text{POP main frame} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stack Depth:** `n + 1 = 4` activation records → **Space Complexity = O(n)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Infix → Postfix Conversion Engine

The following block diagram maps the data flow of the Shunting-Yard algorithm, showing how each token type is routed through the engine:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Infix Expression] --> B{Token Available?}
    B -- No --> Z[End: Pop All Stack to Output]
    Z --> Z1[Return Final Postfix String]
    B -- Yes --> C[Read Next Token T]
    C --> D{T is Operand?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Append T to Output]
    E --> B
    D -- No --> F{T is Operator?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Compare icp of T vs isp of Stack TOP]
    G --> H{isp TOP >= icp T?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Pop TOP and Append to Output]
    I --> G
    H -- No --> J[Push T onto Stack]
    J --> B
    F -- No --> K{T is Open Paren?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Push T onto Stack]
    L --> B
    K -- No --> M{T is Close Paren?}
    M -- Yes --> N{Pop until Open Paren?}
    N -- Pop --> O[Append Popped to Output]
    O --> N
    N -- Found Open --> P[Discard Open Paren]
    P --> B
```

## 4.2 Postfix Evaluation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Read Postfix String] --> S2{Token Left?}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Final Result is at Stack TOP]
    S3 --> S3a[Return Value]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Read Token T]
    S4 --> S5{T is Operand?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Push T onto Operand Stack]
    S6 --> S2
    S5 -- No --> S7[Pop Operand2 from Stack]
    S7 --> S8[Pop Operand1 from Stack]
    S8 --> S9[Compute val = Operand1 op Operand2]
    S9 --> S10[Push val onto Stack]
    S10 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Parenthesis Matching State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Initialize Empty Stack] --> P2[Read Symbol s]
    P2 --> P3{s is Opening?}
    P3 -- Yes Yes --> P4[Push s onto Stack]
    P4 --> P5{More Symbols?}
    P3 -- No --> P6{s is Closing?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Peek Stack TOP]
    P7 --> P8{Stack Empty?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[ERROR: Unbalanced]
    P8 -- No --> P10{TOP matches s?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Pop TOP]
    P11 --> P5
    P10 -- No --> P12[ERROR: Mismatch]
    P6 -- No --> P5
    P5 -- Yes --> P2
    P5 -- No --> P13[End: Stack Empty?]
    P13 -- Yes --> P14[SUCCESS: Balanced]
    P13 -- No --> P15[ERROR: Unclosed Symbols]
```

## 4.4 Backtracking Pattern (Generic Depth-First State Space Search)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BT1[Start: Push Initial State] --> BT2[Peek TOP State]
    BT2 --> BT3{Goal Reached?}
    BT3 -- Yes --> BT4[Record Solution]
    BT4 --> BT7
    BT3 -- No --> BT5{More Choices?}
    BT5 -- Yes --> BT6[Push Next Valid Choice]
    BT6 --> BT2
    BT5 -- No --> BT7[Pop TOP State - Backtrack]
    BT7 --> BT8{Stack Empty?}
    BT8 -- No --> BT2
    BT8 -- Yes --> BT9[End: All Solutions Found]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a stack. List any two applications of stacks.**

**Model Answer:**
A stack is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** principle, where insertions (PUSH) and deletions (POP) occur only at one end called the **TOP**.

**Two applications:**
1. **Conversion of infix expressions to postfix/prefix** — the stack temporarily holds operators until their operands are processed.
2. **Function call management in recursion** — the runtime call stack stores activation records (return address, local variables, parameters) for each pending call.

*(Any two of: parenthesis matching, backtracking, undo/redo, memory management, string reversal, browser history.)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**What is the time complexity of converting an infix expression of length `n` to postfix? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**
The time complexity is **O(n)**.

**Justification:** Each token of the infix expression is read exactly once. For each token, the algorithm performs at most one PUSH and one POP. Both PUSH and POP are O(1) operations on a stack implemented with an array or linked list. Hence, total operations = `n × O(1) = O(n)`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Convert the following infix expression to postfix using a stack. Show every step clearly with the stack content and the output at each stage.

$$
\boxed{(A + B) * (C - D) / E}
$$

**(b)** Evaluate the resulting postfix expression for `A=5, B=3, C=10, D=4, E=2`. Show the operand stack at each step.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Infix to Postfix Conversion

Define standard precedences: `*` and `/` have higher precedence than `+` and `-`.

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|:----:|:-----:|:------|:-------|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | — |
| 2 | `A` | `(` | `A` |
| 3 | `+` | `(+` | `A` |
| 4 | `B` | `(+` | `A B` |
| 5 | `)` | empty | `A B +` |
| 6 | `*` | `*` | `A B +` |
| 7 | `(` | `*(` | `A B +` |
| 8 | `C` | `*(` | `A B + C` |
| 9 | `-` | `*(-` | `A B + C` |
| 10 | `D` | `*(-` | `A B + C D` |
| 11 | `)` | `*` | `A B + C D -` |
| 12 | `/` | `/` | `A B + C D - *` |
| 13 | `E` | `/` | `A B + C D - * E` |
| 14 | End | empty | `A B + C D - * E /` |

**Final Postfix:**

$$
\boxed{A\,B\,+\,C\,D\,-\,*\,E\,/}
$$

#### Part (b) — Postfix Evaluation

Substituting: `5 3 + 10 4 - * 2 /`

| Step | Token | Operand2 (first pop) | Operand1 (second pop) | Operation | Result | Stack |
|:----:|:-----:|:--------------------:|:---------------------:|:---------:|:------:|:------|
| 1 | `5` | — | — | PUSH | — | `5` |
| 2 | `3` | — | — | PUSH | — | `5, 3` |
| 3 | `+` | 3 | 5 | 5+3 | 8 | `8` |
| 4 | `10` | — | — | PUSH | — | `8, 10` |
| 5 | `4` | — | — | PUSH | — | `8, 10, 4` |
| 6 | `-` | 4 | 10 | 10−4 | 6 | `8, 6` |
| 7 | `*` | 6 | 8 | 8×6 | 48 | `48` |
| 8 | `2` | — | — | PUSH | — | `48, 2` |
| 9 | `/` | 2 | 48 | 48÷2 | 24 | `24` |

**Final Evaluated Result:**

$$
\boxed{24}
$$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the algorithm for **balancing parentheses** using a stack. Use the input string `{ [ ( A + B ) ] } * ( C - D )` and demonstrate whether it is balanced or not.

**(b)** Describe how stacks are used to implement **recursion**. Illustrate with the recursive computation of the Fibonacci sequence for `n = 4`, showing the call stack evolution and activation records.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Parenthesis Matching Algorithm

**Algorithm:**
1. Create an empty stack `S`.
2. Scan the symbol string from left to right.
3. If current symbol is an **opening bracket** (`{`, `[`, `(`), **PUSH** it onto `S`.
4. If current symbol is a **closing bracket** (`}`, `]`, `)`):
   - If `S` is empty → **unbalanced** (more closing than opening).
   - Else **POP** the top and verify it is the *matching* opening type. If not → **unbalanced**.
5. After full scan, if `S` is empty → **balanced**, else **unbalanced**.

**Trace on Input:** `{ [ ( A + B ) ] } * ( C - D )`

| Step | Symbol | Action | Stack (top right) |
|:----:|:------:|:-------|:------------------|
| 1 | `{` | PUSH | `{` |
| 2 | `[` | PUSH | `{[` |
| 3 | `(` | PUSH | `{[(` |
| 4-7 | `A + B` | Operands/operator → ignore | `{[(` |
| 8 | `)` | POP, match with `(` | `{[` |
| 9 | `]` | POP, match with `[` | `{` |
| 10 | `}` | POP, match with `{` | empty |
| 11 | `*` | Operator → ignore | empty |
| 12 | `(` | PUSH | `(` |
| 13-16 | `C - D` | Ignore | `(` |
| 17 | `)` | POP, match with `(` | empty |

End of string → Stack is empty → **BALANCED** ✅

#### Part (b) — Recursion via Call Stack

**Fibonacci Definition:** `F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)` with base cases `F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1`.

For `n = 4`, the recursive call tree is:

$$
F(4) = F(3) + F(2)
$$

with `F(3) = F(2) + F(1)`, `F(2) = F(1) + F(0)`.

**Call Stack Evolution (Activation Records):**

```
Call 1:  PUSH [F(4), retAddr=main]
Call 2:  PUSH [F(3), retAddr=Call1]      // from F(4) → F(3)
Call 3:  PUSH [F(2), retAddr=Call2]      // from F(3) → F(2)
Call 4:  PUSH [F(1), retAddr=Call3]      // F(2) → F(1), BASE → return 1, POP
Call 5:  PUSH [F(0), retAddr=Call3]      // F(2) → F(0), BASE → return 0, POP
Resolve Call 3: F(2) = 1 + 0 = 1         // POP
Call 6:  PUSH [F(1), retAddr=Call2]      // from F(3) → F(1), BASE → return 1, POP
Resolve Call 2: F(3) = 1 + 1 = 2         // POP
Call 7:  PUSH [F(2), retAddr=Call1]      // from F(4) → F(2)
Call 8:  PUSH [F(1), retAddr=Call7]      // F(2) → F(1), return 1, POP
Call 9:  PUSH [F(0), retAddr=Call7]      // F(2) → F(0), return 0, POP
Resolve Call 7: F(2) = 1 + 0 = 1         // POP
Resolve Call 1: F(4) = 2 + 1 = 3         // POP → return to main
```

**Final Answer:** `F(4) = 3`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Operand Order Reversal (2 marks loss):** In postfix evaluation, students often compute `op2 op op1` instead of `op1 op op2`. Always remember: the **first popped** value is the **right operand**.
> 2. **Missing Parenthesis Handling (1 mark loss):** Forgetting to discard the `(` when `)` is encountered, or treating it as a regular operator in the precedence table.
> 3. **Skipping Trace Steps (2–3 marks loss):** KTU board examiners *demand* an explicit step-by-step table showing stack and output at every token. Writing only the final postfix expression without the trace invites partial marking.
> 4. **Right-to-Left Scan for Prefix (1 mark loss):** When converting infix to **prefix**, the scan must be **right-to-left**, *not* left-to-right. This is the most frequent conceptual error.
> 5. **Associativity of `^`:** Exponentiation is **right-associative**, so `a^b^c = a^(b^c)`, not `(a^b)^c`. The conversion algorithm must reflect this — for `^`, we should NOT pop an existing `^` of equal precedence from the stack.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications of Stacks (Module 1)**

- ✅ A stack is a **LIFO** linear data structure with `PUSH` and `POP` both running in **O(1)**.
- ✅ The **Shunting-Yard algorithm** by **Dijkstra** is the standard for infix → postfix/prefix conversion.
- ✅ **Infix → Postfix** scan direction: **left-to-right**.
- ✅ **Infix → Prefix** scan direction: **right-to-left**; reverse the result at the end.
- ✅ **Precedence hierarchy (high to low):** `(...)` > `^` > `*`, `/`, `%` > `+`, `-`.
- ✅ **Associativity:** `^` is **right-associative**; all others are **left-associative**.
- ✅ Postfix evaluation: first popped value is **operand2** (right), second popped is **operand1** (left).
- ✅ Parenthesis matching uses a stack of **opening symbols**; mismatched types cause failure.
- ✅ **Function call stack** stores activation records: return address, parameters, local variables.
- ✅ **Recursion depth** equals the number of activation records simultaneously on the stack → space complexity **O(depth)**.
- ✅ **Backtracking** (N-Queens, Rat-in-Maze, 8-Puzzle) uses a stack to manage DFS-style exploration; undoing a choice = **POP**.
- ✅ **All stack algorithms** in this module are **O(n)** time and **O(n)** auxiliary space, where `n` is the length of the input.
- ✅ **Memory Management:** OS uses a stack for allocating local variables; `stack overflow` occurs when recursion depth exceeds the reserved stack size.
- ✅ **Undo/Redo:** Implemented as two parallel stacks — `undoStack` and `redoStack`. Performing an action pushes to `undoStack`; undoing pops from `undoStack` and pushes to `redoStack`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polish Expression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polish Expression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polish Notation (Prefix Notation)** is a symbolic logical expression notation in which the **operator symbol precedes** the operands it operates upon. It was introduced by the Polish mathematician **Jan Łukasiewicz** in 1924. The reverse form, where the operator follows its operands, is known as **Reverse Polish Notation (RPN) / Postfix Notation**.

The three equivalent ways of writing an arithmetic expression are:

| Notation | Symbol | Form | Example (A + B) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Infix** | Standard | Operator **between** operands | `A + B` |
| **Prefix (Polish)** | Jan Łukasiewicz | Operator **before** operands | `+ A B` |
| **Postfix (Reverse Polish)** | RPN | Operator **after** operands | `A B +` |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Translator" Analogy** — Think of writing a sentence in **English** (subject-verb-object). Humans read it easily, but a calculator (the computer) gets confused because it needs to know *which words to act on first*. **Polish notation is like rewriting the sentence in a strict, unambiguous "robot grammar"** where the verb (operator) always comes first, telling the machine exactly which two numbers to grab next — no confusion about precedence, no need for parentheses.

## 1.3 Why Computers Need Polish Notation

> [!TIP]
> Infix expressions require the computer to know **operator precedence**, **associativity**, and **parentheses** rules. Polish notation **eliminates the need for brackets and precedence rules entirely**, making expression evaluation both **faster** and **memory-efficient** for stack-based machines (e.g., calculators, JVM bytecode, compiler intermediate code generation).

**Standard precedence & associativity (used during conversion):**

| Token Category | Operators | Precedence (High to Low) | Associativity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Parenthesis | `( )` | **3** (highest structural) | Left to Right |
| Exponentiation | `^` | **2** | Right to Left |
| Multiply / Divide | `* /` | **1** | Left to Right |
| Add / Subtract | `+ -` | **0** (lowest) | Left to Right |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Prefix/Postfix? The Operational Logic

The conversion process is governed by a **stack** that holds operators and opening brackets. The decisions are:

- **Higher or Equal Precedence Encounter** → Pop and output top-of-stack operator (subject to associativity rule for `^`).
- **Lower Precedence Encounter** → Push incoming operator onto stack.
- **Left Parenthesis `(`** → Always push.
- **Right Parenthesis `)`** → Pop and output everything until matching `(` is found.
- **End of Expression** → Pop and output all remaining operators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Rule:** Right associativity is only applied to the exponentiation operator `^`. For `+ - * /`, equal precedence always means **left-to-right** pop behavior.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Infix | Prefix (Polish) | Postfix (Reverse Polish) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Position of Operator | Between operands | Before operands | After operands |
| Parenthesis Required? | Yes (to override precedence) | **No** | **No** |
| Evaluation Direction | Two-pass (scan + apply precedence) | Right-to-Left single pass | Left-to-Right single pass |
| Stack Required for Evaluation | Not natural | Yes | Yes |
| Stack Operation Type | — | Push operands, Pop on operator | Push operands, Pop on operator |
| Result Storage Rule | — | $\text{Push}\,(a \odot b)$ | $\text{Push}\,(a \odot b)$ |
| Invented By | — | **Jan Łukasiewicz (1924)** | **Charles Hamblin (1950s)** |
| KTU Common Use | Human-readable | Compiler front-end (intermediate code) | Stack machines, JVM bytecode |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Polish expressions form the **backbone of compiler design**:
> 1. **Intermediate Code Generation (ICG):** Compilers convert human-written infix to postfix before generating machine code.
> 2. **Stack Machines:** The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and Forth language use postfix internally.
> 3. **HP Calculators:** Historically, HP scientific calculators used RPN as their primary input mode.
> 4. **Expression Evaluation in DBMS:** SQL query optimizers and spreadsheet engines leverage postfix logic.

## 2.4 Core Algorithmic Equations

For stack-based postfix evaluation, the **fundamental relation** applied on encountering an operator $\odot$ is:

$$
\text{Push} \; \Big( \text{Pop}(b) \;\odot\; \text{Pop}(a) \Big)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Order matters!** For **non-commutative** operators like `-, /, ^`, the **first popped** value is the **right operand (b)** and the **second popped** value is the **left operand (a)**. Reversing this order is the #1 error in KTU valuations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Infix to Postfix Conversion

**Problem:** Convert the infix expression $A + B * C - D / E$ to postfix form, then evaluate for $A=5, B=2, C=3, D=8, E=2$.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Trace Table

| Step | Scanned Token | Stack (top→) | Postfix Output | Reasoning |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | `A` | *(empty)* | `A` | Operand → emit directly |
| 2 | `+` | `+` | `A` | Operator → push (stack empty) |
| 3 | `B` | `+` | `A B` | Operand → emit |
| 4 | `*` | `+ *` | `A B` | `*` has **higher** precedence than `+` → push |
| 5 | `C` | `+ *` | `A B C` | Operand → emit |
| 6 | `-` | `-` | `A B C * +` | `+` has **higher/equal** prec. than `-` → pop `+`, then pop `*`, then push `-` |
| 7 | `D` | `-` | `A B C * + D` | Operand → emit |
| 8 | `/` | `- /` | `A B C * + D` | `/` has **higher** precedence than `-` → push |
| 9 | `E` | `- /` | `A B C * + D E` | Operand → emit |
| 10 | *(end)* | *(empty)* | `A B C * + D E / -` | Pop all remaining: ` / `, then `-` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Postfix Expression:** $\;A\,B\,C\,*\,+\,D\,E\,/\,-$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Postfix Evaluation

Given $A=5, B=2, C=3, D=8, E=2$, scan the postfix string `A B C * + D E / -`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Scan } A=5 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{5}] \\
\text{Step 2: Scan } B=2 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{5}, \color{blue}{2}] \\
\text{Step 3: Scan } C=3 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{5}, \color{blue}{2}, \color{blue}{3}] \\
\text{Step 4: Scan } * \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Pop } b=3, a=2,\; a*b = 2 \times 3 = \color{red}{6} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{5}, \color{red}{6}] \\
\text{Step 5: Scan } + \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Pop } b=6, a=5,\; a+b = 5 + 6 = \color{red}{11} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{11}] \\
\text{Step 6: Scan } D=8 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{11}, \color{blue}{8}] \\
\text{Step 7: Scan } E=2 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{11}, \color{blue}{8}, \color{blue}{2}] \\
\text{Step 8: Scan } / \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Pop } b=2, a=8,\; a/b = 8 \div 2 = \color{red}{4} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{11}, \color{red}{4}] \\
\text{Step 9: Scan } - \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Pop } b=4, a=11,\; a-b = 11 - 4 = \color{red}{7} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{7}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Evaluated Result:** $\boxed{7}$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Infix to Prefix Conversion

**Problem:** Convert the infix expression $(A + B) * C$ to prefix form.

### 3.2.1 Standard Algorithm (Right-to-Left Infix Scan)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** For prefix conversion, two methods exist. The most KTU-recommended approach is the **Right-to-Left Infix Scan**, which uses the *same* logic as the infix-to-postfix algorithm but with **reversed operator associativity rules**.

| Step | Scanned Token (R→L) | Stack (top→) | Prefix Output (Reversed) | Reasoning |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | `)` | `)` | *(empty)* | Closing bracket → push |
| 2 | `C` | `)` | `C` | Operand → prepend to output |
| 3 | `*` | `) *` | `C` | Operator → push |
| 4 | `(` | `*` | `C` | Match `)` → pop until `(` |
| 5 | `)` | `)` | `C` | Closing bracket → push |
| 6 | `B` | `)` | `B C` | Operand → prepend to output |
| 7 | `+` | `) +` | `B C` | Operator → push |
| 8 | `(` | `+` | `B C` | Match `)` → pop until `(` |
| 9 | `A` | *(empty)* | `A B C` | Operand → prepend to output |
| 10 | *(end)* | *(empty)* | `+ * A B C` | Pop all & reverse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Prefix Expression:** $\boxed{+ * A B C}$

### 3.2.2 Verification by Evaluation

$$
\begin{aligned}
+ * A B C \quad &\Rightarrow \quad (A \odot B) \odot C \quad \text{where the outermost `+` is applied last in the prefix traversal} \\
\text{For } A=3, B=4, C=5: \quad &\Rightarrow \quad (3 \times 4) + 5 = 12 + 5 = \color{red}{17}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Infix-to-Postfix Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp["INFIX INPUT\nString of Tokens"] --> tk1["Tokenizer\nReads One Token at a Time"]
    tk1 --> dec{"Decision\nBlock"}
    dec -- "Is Operand\nA to Z or 0 to 9" --> outp["OUTPUT\nPostfix String"]
    dec -- "Is Operator\n+ - * / ^" --> prec{"Check\nPrecedence"}
    prec -- "Stack Top has\nHigher or Equal" --> pop1["Pop and\nAppend to Output"]
    prec -- "Stack Empty or\nLower Precedence" --> push1["Push to\nOperator Stack"]
    dec -- "Is Left Paren" --> push2["Push to\nStack"]
    dec -- "Is Right Paren" --> pop2["Pop until\nLeft Paren Found"]
    pop1 --> prec
    push1 --> prec
    push2 --> stk["STACK\nLIFO Memory"]
    pop2 --> stk
    outp --> eval["Postfix\nEvaluator"]
    eval --> res["FINAL\nNUMERIC RESULT"]
```

## 4.2 Architecture: Postfix Evaluation Stack Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pf["POSTFIX\nEXPRESSION"] --> sc["Symbol\nScanner"]
    sc --> chk{"Is\nOperand?"}
    chk -- Yes --> psh["Push Value\nonto Stack"]
    chk -- No  --> opr{"Identify\nOperator"}
    opr --> pp["Pop b\nTop Operand"]
    pp --> pa["Pop a\nSecond Operand"]
    pa --> calc["Compute\na op b"]
    calc --> ppush["Push Result\nonto Stack"]
    psh --> stk["EVALUATION\nSTACK"]
    ppush --> stk
    stk --> fin["Final Result\non Stack Top"]
```

## 4.3 Conversion Trace Visualization (For `A + B * C - D / E`)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Step 1: A"] --> s2["Step 2: + pushed\nStack: +"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: B\nOutput: A B"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 4: * pushed\nStack: + *"]
    s4 --> s5["Step 5: C\nOutput: A B C"]
    s5 --> s6["Step 6: - triggers pop\nPop + and *\nStack: -"]
    s6 --> s7["Step 7: D\nOutput: A B C * + D"]
    s7 --> s8["Step 8: / pushed\nStack: - /"]
    s8 --> s9["Step 9: E\nOutput: A B C * + D E"]
    s9 --> s10["Step 10: End\nPop all\nFinal: A B C * + D E / -"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
> Define **Polish notation** and **Reverse Polish notation**. State **two advantages** of postfix expressions over infix expressions.

**Model Answer:**
- **Polish (Prefix) Notation:** A notation in which the operator is placed *before* its two operands. E.g., `+ A B`.
- **Reverse Polish (Postfix) Notation:** A notation in which the operator is placed *after* its two operands. E.g., `A B +`.
- **Advantages of Postfix:**
    1. **No parentheses required** — the order of evaluation is unambiguous by position.
    2. **No precedence rules required** during evaluation — making it ideal for stack-based single-pass evaluation.
    3. *(Bonus)* Easier to generate machine code for stack-based architectures.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
> List the **relative precedence** (high to low) and **associativity** of the operators: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`, `(`, `)`.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Parentheses `(` `)`** — Highest structural precedence; Left-to-Right.
2. **Exponentiation `^`** — Right-to-Left associativity.
3. **Multiplication `*` and Division `/`** — Left-to-Right associativity.
4. **Addition `+` and Subtraction `-`** — Lowest precedence; Left-to-Right associativity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]*

> **(a)** Convert the infix expression $A + \left(B * C - D / E\right) \uparrow F$ to **postfix** form, showing the stack state at each step. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** Evaluate the postfix expression `5 3 2 * + 8 4 / -` using a stack. Show all intermediate steps. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Pre-processing the input:** $A + (B * C - D / E) \uparrow F$

| Step | Token | Stack | Postfix Output |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `A` | *(empty)* | `A` |
| 2 | `+` | `+` | `A` |
| 3 | `(` | `+ (` | `A` |
| 4 | `B` | `+ (` | `A B` |
| 5 | `*` | `+ ( *` | `A B` |
| 6 | `C` | `+ ( *` | `A B C` |
| 7 | `-` | `+ ( -` | `A B C *` |
| 8 | `D` | `+ ( -` | `A B C * D` |
| 9 | `/` | `+ ( - /` | `A B C * D` |
| 10 | `E` | `+ ( - /` | `A B C * D E` |
| 11 | `)` | `+` | `A B C * D E / -` |
| 12 | `^` | `+ ^` | `A B C * D E / -` |
| 13 | `F` | `+ ^` | `A B C * D E / - F` |
| 14 | *(end)* | *(empty)* | `A B C * D E / - F ^ +` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Postfix:** $\;A\,B\,C\,*\,D\,E\,/\,\,-\,F\,\uparrow\,+\,$

#### Model Solution for (b)

Evaluate postfix `5 3 2 * + 8 4 / -`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scan } 5 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{5}] \\
\text{Scan } 3 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{5}, \color{blue}{3}] \\
\text{Scan } 2 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{5}, \color{blue}{3}, \color{blue}{2}] \\
\text{Scan } * &\rightarrow \text{Pop } b=2,\; a=3,\; 3 \times 2 = \color{red}{6},\; \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{5}, \color{red}{6}] \\
\text{Scan } + &\rightarrow \text{Pop } b=6,\; a=5,\; 5 + 6 = \color{red}{11},\; \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{11}] \\
\text{Scan } 8 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{11}, \color{blue}{8}] \\
\text{Scan } 4 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{11}, \color{blue}{8}, \color{blue}{4}] \\
\text{Scan } / &\rightarrow \text{Pop } b=4,\; a=8,\; 8 \div 4 = \color{red}{2},\; \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{11}, \color{red}{2}] \\
\text{Scan } - &\rightarrow \text{Pop } b=2,\; a=11,\; 11 - 2 = \color{red}{9},\; \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{9}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Evaluated Result:** $\boxed{9}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]*

> **(a)** Convert the infix expression $\left(A + B\right) * \left(C - D\right) / E$ to **prefix** form using a stack. Show the stack evolution. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** Evaluate the prefix expression `- * + 4 5 6 2` step by step. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Method: Right-to-Left Scan of the Infix Expression**

| Step | Token (R→L) | Stack | Prefix Output (Prepended) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `E` | *(empty)* | `E` |
| 2 | `/` | `/` | `E` |
| 3 | `)` | `/ )` | `E` |
| 4 | `D` | `/ )` | `D E` |
| 5 | `-` | `/ ) -` | `D E` |
| 6 | `C` | `/ ) -` | `C D E` |
| 7 | `(` | `/` | `C D E` |
| 8 | `)` | `/ )` | `C D E` |
| 9 | `B` | `/ )` | `B C D E` |
| 10 | `+` | `/ ) +` | `B C D E` |
| 11 | `A` | `/ ) +` | `A B C D E` |
| 12 | `(` | `/` | `A B C D E` |
| 13 | *(end)* | *(empty)* | `/ * + A B - C D E` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Prefix:** $\boxed{/\; *\; +\; A\; B\; -\; C\; D\; E}$

#### Model Solution for (b)

Evaluate prefix `- * + 4 5 6 2` (scan **right to left**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scan } 2 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{2}] \\
\text{Scan } 6 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{2}, \color{blue}{6}] \\
\text{Scan } 5 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{2}, \color{blue}{6}, \color{blue}{5}] \\
\text{Scan } 4 &\rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{2}, \color{blue}{6}, \color{blue}{5}, \color{blue}{4}] \\
\text{Scan } + &\rightarrow \text{Pop } a=4,\; b=5,\; 4+5 = \color{red}{9},\; \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{2}, \color{blue}{6}, \color{red}{9}] \\
\text{Scan } * &\rightarrow \text{Pop } a=9,\; b=6,\; 9 \times 6 = \color{red}{54},\; \text{Stack: } [\color{blue}{2}, \color{red}{54}] \\
\text{Scan } - &\rightarrow \text{Pop } a=54,\; b=2,\; 54 - 2 = \color{red}{52},\; \text{Stack: } [\color{red}{52}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Evaluated Result:** $\boxed{52}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Deduction Traps in Polish Expression Questions:**
> 1. **Operand order reversal in `a op b`:** When evaluating postfix/prefix, the **first popped** element is the **right operand**, not the left. Mixing this up changes every subtraction and division answer.
> 2. **Equal-precedence associativity:** For `+ - * /`, **equal precedence still triggers a pop** (left associativity). For `^`, equal precedence means **do NOT pop** (right associativity). Forgetting this distinction causes 3-mark deductions.
> 3. **Empty stack pop:** Trying to pop when the operator stack is empty (a logic bug) — ensure your algorithm checks `isEmpty()`.
> 4. **Forgot to pop `(` on encountering `)`:** The opening parenthesis is *discarded* once matched; only operators between the pair are emitted.
> 5. **Prefix conversion reverse mistake:** The Right-to-Left scan method requires **reversing the final output** OR using a prepend operation; merely doing infix-to-postfix logic produces wrong results.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Polish Expressions**
> - **Prefix (Polish):** Operator BEFORE operands. Invented by **Jan Łukasiewicz**. No parentheses needed.
> - **Postfix (Reverse Polish):** Operator AFTER operands. No parentheses, no precedence rules during evaluation.
> - **Infix Conversion Tool:** A **stack** holds operators and `(` brackets; operands go straight to output.
> - **Three rules for infix → postfix:**
>     1. Operand → emit.
>     2. Operator → pop all higher-or-equal (left-associative) precedence ops, then push.
>     3. `)` → pop until matching `(` is removed.
> - **Evaluation Rule:** On encountering an operator, **pop two operands (right first, then left)**, apply `a op b`, and **push the result**.
> - **Prefix conversion** uses **Right-to-Left** infix scan, then reverses (or prepends) the output.
> - **Stack machines** (JVM, Forth, HP calculators) use postfix as their native execution model.
> - **Compiler pipeline:** Source → Tokens → **Infix → Postfix (ICG)** → Assembly/Machine Code.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
