# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define a Queue ADT. Mention any two applications.
**Model Answer:**
A Queue is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First In, First Out)** principle. Insertions are made at the **REAR** end and deletions are made at the **FRONT** end. The two principal operations are `ENQUEUE` (insert) and `DEQUEUE` (delete).
**Two Applications:**
1. **CPU Scheduling** in Operating Systems (FCFS Ready Queue).
2. **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** in graph traversal algorithms.
*(Other acceptable answers: Disk Scheduling, Printer Spooler, Call Center Systems, I/O Buffers.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

### Q2. What is "False Overflow" in a linear queue? How is it overcome?
**Model Answer:**
**False Overflow** (also called **Phantom Overflow**) occurs in a linear array-based queue when the `REAR` pointer reaches the maximum index `MAX - 1`, causing the queue to be reported as full, even though there are empty slots at the **lower indices** (left side of the array) created by previous deletions.
**Solution:** Use a **Circular Queue** where the next index is computed using the modulo operation:
$$\text{next\_index} = (current\_index + 1) \mod N$$
This allows `REAR` to wrap around to index `0` and reuse the freed slots.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **array representation of a linear queue** in detail. Write the algorithms for ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations and trace them with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **drawbacks of linear queue** and explain how the **circular queue** resolves them. Provide the full condition-check logic. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Linear Queue Array Representation

A **linear queue** is implemented using a one-dimensional array `Q[MAX]`, with two integer pointers `FRONT` and `REAR`.

**Initial State:** `FRONT = -1, REAR = -1` (queue is empty).

**ENQUEUE Algorithm (Insertion):**
```
Step 1: IF (REAR == MAX - 1) THEN
            PRINT "Queue Overflow"
            RETURN
        END IF
Step 2: IF (FRONT == -1) THEN
            SET FRONT = 0
        END IF
Step 3: SET REAR = REAR + 1
Step 4: SET Q[REAR] = ITEM
Step 5: RETURN
```
**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ — a single conditional check and one assignment.

**DEQUEUE Algorithm (Deletion):**
```
Step 1: IF (FRONT == -1 OR FRONT > REAR) THEN
            PRINT "Queue Underflow"
            RETURN
        END IF
Step 2: SET ITEM = Q[FRONT]
Step 3: SET FRONT = FRONT + 1
Step 4: IF (FRONT > REAR) THEN
            SET FRONT = REAR = -1
        END IF
Step 5: RETURN ITEM
```
**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$.

**Example Trace (MAX = 5):**
| Operation | FRONT | REAR | Array Contents |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Init | -1 | -1 | `[ , , , , ]` |
| ENQUEUE(10) | 0 | 0 | `[10, , , , ]` |
| ENQUEUE(20) | 0 | 1 | `[10, 20, , , ]` |
| DEQUEUE() → 10 | 1 | 1 | `[10, 20, , , ]` |
| ENQUEUE(30) | 1 | 2 | `[ , 20, 30, , ]` |

> The "ghost" element `10` still occupies memory but is logically removed. The slot at `Q[0]` can never be reused.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Circular Queue Resolution

**Drawbacks of Linear Queue (3 marks):**
1. **False Overflow:** `REAR == MAX - 1` reports "full" even when slots are free.
2. **Memory Wastage:** Vacated positions at lower indices remain unusable.
3. **No Wrap-Around:** Linear pointer movement cannot return to index 0.

**Circular Queue Concept (2 marks):**
The array is treated as a **circular ring**. After index $N-1$, the next index is $0$. Index updates use:
$$\text{REAR} = (REAR + 1) \mod N, \quad \text{FRONT} = (FRONT + 1) \mod N$$

**Empty and Full Conditions (2 marks):**
- **Empty:** `FRONT = -1`
- **Full:** $(REAR + 1) \mod N = FRONT$ — this is the **empty-slot trick**. We sacrifice one slot, so only $N-1$ elements can be stored.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students write `FRONT == REAR` to detect a full circular queue. This is **wrong** — that condition is ambiguous (could mean empty or one-element). The correct condition uses the modulo expression. **You will lose 2 marks** if you write the wrong full-check formula.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Explain the **linked-list representation of a queue**. Write the algorithms for ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE and state the advantages over array representation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **linear queue, circular queue, and linked-list queue** based on memory utilization, time complexity, overflow detection, and space efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Linked-List Queue

A **linked-list queue** is implemented using a **singly linked list** where:
- `FRONT` pointer → points to the first node (for deletion).
- `REAR` pointer → points to the last node (for insertion).
- Each node has two fields: `DATA` and `NEXT` (pointer to the next node).

**Node Structure:**
```
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* next;
};
```

**ENQUEUE Algorithm (Insert at REAR):**
```
Step 1: Create NEW_NODE
Step 2: SET NEW_NODE->data = ITEM
Step 3: SET NEW_NODE->next = NULL
Step 4: IF (FRONT == NULL) THEN
            SET FRONT = REAR = NEW_NODE
        ELSE
            SET REAR->next = NEW_NODE
            SET REAR = NEW_NODE
        END IF
Step 5: RETURN
```
**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$.

**DEQUEUE Algorithm (Delete from FRONT):**
```
Step 1: IF (FRONT == NULL) THEN
            PRINT "Queue Underflow"
            RETURN
        END IF
Step 2: SET TEMP = FRONT
Step 3: SET ITEM = TEMP->data
Step 4: SET FRONT = FRONT->next
Step 5: IF (FRONT == NULL) THEN
            SET REAR = NULL
        END IF
Step 6: FREE(TEMP)
Step 7: RETURN ITEM
```
**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$.

**Advantages over Array Representation (2 marks):**
1. **No Overflow** (theoretically) — limited only by available heap memory.
2. **No False Overflow** — unlike linear queue, slots are never stranded.
3. **Dynamic Sizing** — queue can grow or shrink at runtime.
4. **Efficient Memory Usage** — no pre-allocated idle slots.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Linear Queue | Circular Queue | Linked-List Queue |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Memory Layout** | Static contiguous array | Static contiguous array (logical ring) | Dynamic scattered nodes |
| **ENQUEUE Time** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **DEQUEUE Time** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Overflow Detection** | $REAR = MAX - 1$ | $(REAR+1) \mod N = FRONT$ | $Heap\ Exhausted$ |
| **Underflow Detection** | $FRONT = -1$ | $FRONT = -1$ | $FRONT = NULL$ |
| **False Overflow?** | **YES** | **NO** | **NO** |
| **Space Wasted** | High (stranded slots) | Minimal (one-slot) | None (only pointer overhead) |
| **Max Capacity** | Fixed $N$ | $N - 1$ elements | Heap-limited |
| **Implementation Effort** | Easy | Moderate | Moderate-Hard |
| **Resizable?** | No | No | Yes |
| **Best Suited For** | Small fixed-size buffers | Producer-consumer, OS schedulers | Variable-size workloads |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** When asked to "compare", students often write a *paragraph* comparison. The board **expects a table**. Always use a **tabular comparison** with at least 4–5 distinguishing parameters. **You will lose 1–2 marks** for narrative-only answers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** When drawing the linked-list queue, students often forget to show the `NULL` terminator at the tail. The examiner **expects `REAR → NULL`** explicitly. Missing it costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Representation of Queues**

- ⭐ **Queue = FIFO** discipline. Insert at **REAR**, delete from **FRONT**.
- ⭐ Two pointer system: `FRONT` (for deletion) and `REAR` (for insertion).
- ⭐ **Linear Queue Drawback → False Overflow.** Vacated left-side slots cannot be reused.
- ⭐ **Circular Queue Fix → Modulo arithmetic:** `next = (current + 1) % N`.
- ⭐ **Empty Condition (Circular):** `FRONT = -1`.
- ⭐ **Full Condition (Circular):** `(REAR + 1) % N = FRONT` — **never write `FRONT == REAR` for Full.**
- ⭐ **Trade-off:** Circular queue of size $N$ can hold only $N-1$ elements (one-slot sacrifice).
- ⭐ **Linked-List Queue** uses `FRONT` (head) and `REAR` (tail) — supports dynamic size.
- ⭐ **Time Complexity of ENQUEUE/DEQUEUE** in all three representations = $O(1)$.
- ⭐ **Reset on Empty:** `FRONT = REAR = -1` (array) or `FRONT = REAR = NULL` (linked list).
- ⭐ **Applications:** CPU scheduling, BFS, disk scheduling, printer buffer, call center.
- ⭐ **Always use a TABLE** for comparison questions — board examiners award extra marks for structured presentation.
- ⭐ **For linked-list queue:** `ENQUEUE` happens at the **tail** via the `REAR` pointer for $O(1)$ efficiency.
- ⭐ **For linked-list queue:** `DEQUEUE` happens at the **head** via the `FRONT` pointer for $O(1)$ efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Queue** is a linear, non-primitive, abstract data structure (ADT) that follows the **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** discipline, also called **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)** discipline. In a queue, the element that is inserted *first* is the one that is removed *first*. The insertion operation is performed at the **rear** (also called the *tail* or *back*) end, and the deletion operation is performed at the **front** (also called the *head*) end.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim from Module 2):**
> A *queue* is an ordered collection of items where the addition of new items happens at one end, called the **rear** (or *tail*), and the removal of existing items occurs at the other end, called the **front** (or *head*). This ordering principle is called **FIFO — First In, First Out**.

Mathematically, a queue is a sequence of elements:

$$ Q = \{ q_0, q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_{n-1} \} $$

where $q_0$ is the element at the **front** (oldest) and $q_{n-1}$ is the element at the **rear** (newest).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **queue at a KSRTC bus ticket counter in Thiruvananthapuram**. Passengers stand one behind the other:
- The passenger who **arrived first** (stands at the front) gets the ticket and **leaves first**.
- A new passenger **joins at the rear** of the line.
- No one cuts in the middle — strict **FIFO** order.

This is precisely how a computer-science queue works:
- **Enqueue** (insertion) → A new passenger joins at the rear.
- **Dequeue** (deletion) → The passenger at the front buys the ticket and exits.
- **Peek / Front** → You can only see which passenger is *currently* at the counter, not the one at the back.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Two Ends, Two Names:**
> A queue has *two* operation ends, unlike a Stack (which has only one — the `top`). This **bi-directional access** is what makes queues suitable for *scheduling*, *buffering*, and *BFS traversals*.

## 1.3 Why a Queue? — Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How Queue Is Used |
|---|---|
| **CPU / Process Scheduling** | Round-Robin scheduler queues ready processes in FIFO order. |
| **Disk Scheduling** | FCFS disk-arm algorithm services I/O requests in arrival order. |
| **Network Buffers** | Routers spool packets in a queue before forwarding. |
| **Printer Spooling** | Print jobs are queued and executed in submission order. |
| **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** | Graph traversal uses a queue to visit nodes level-by-level. |
| **Call Centre Systems** | Incoming calls are placed in a queue and routed to free agents. |

> [!TIP]
> If a problem statement says "serve in the order of arrival" or "first come, first served" → **think Queue**.

## 1.4 Variants of Queues (KTU 2024 Module 2 Scope)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically prescribes the following queue variants:

1. **Simple Queue (Linear Queue)** — Basic FIFO array/linked structure.
2. **Circular Queue** — The rear wraps around to index 0, eliminating wasted space.
3. **Double-Ended Queue (Deque)** — Insertion/deletion possible at *both* ends.
4. **Priority Queue** — Elements are dequeued based on *priority*, not arrival order (covered briefly; full treatment in Heaps).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students confuse **Circular Queue** with **Deque**. A *circular* queue is still FIFO; a *deque* allows insertion/deletion at *both* ends and is **not strictly FIFO**.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Operations on a Queue

A queue, as an Abstract Data Type (ADT), supports the following **five primitive operations**. The KTU 2024 ESE questions are frequently set on the first three.

| # | Operation | Alternate Names | Action | Allowed End |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Enqueue(x)` | `Add`, `Insert`, `Push`, `Offer` | Insert element `x` at the rear | Rear |
| 2 | `Dequeue()` | `Remove`, `Delete`, `Pop`, `Poll` | Remove & return the element at the front | Front |
| 3 | `Front()` / `Peek()` | `GetFront`, `Element` | Return (without removing) the front element | Front |
| 4 | `isEmpty()` | `IsQueueEmpty` | Return `true` if queue has no elements | — |
| 5 | `isFull()` | `IsQueueFull` | Return `true` if queue cannot accept more | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity Guarantee:** All five operations on a *well-implemented* queue (using either a circular array **or** a linked list) run in **$O(1)$** — constant time. This is the principal reason queues are chosen for real-time systems.

## 2.2 Implementation Strategy #1 — Array-Based Linear Queue

In the **array-based (linear) queue**:
- A one-dimensional array `Q[0 \ldots (N-1)]` of capacity **$N$** is allocated.
- Two integer indices are maintained:
  - `front` — index of the element to be dequeued next.
  - `rear`  — index of the most recently enqueued element.
- An additional counter `count` (or a flag) is maintained to distinguish the *empty* state from the *full* state.

### 2.2.1 Initialisation

$$ \text{front} = -1, \quad \text{rear} = -1, \quad \text{count} = 0 $$

### 2.2.2 The Two State Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Famous Ambiguity of Linear Queue:** When `front == rear`, the queue may be **EMPTY** (just initialised) **OR** it may be **FULL** (all $N$ slots occupied). This ambiguity is the **single most-asked conceptual question** in KTU ESE Module 2.

The two interpretations used in textbooks are:

| Convention | isEmpty Condition | isFull Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Convention A** (use `count`) | $\text{count} = 0$ | $\text{count} = N$ |
| **Convention B** (use `front == -1` flag) | $\text{front} = -1$ | $\text{rear} = N - 1$ |

### 2.2.3 The Pseudocode Logic

**Enqueue(x):**
1. If `isFull()` → print **"Queue Overflow"** and abort.
2. If `front == -1` (queue is empty) → set `front = 0`.
3. Increment `rear` by 1.
4. Set `Q[rear] = x`.
5. Increment `count` by 1.

**Dequeue():**
1. If `isEmpty()` → print **"Queue Underflow"** and abort.
2. Store `x = Q[front]` temporarily.
3. If `front == rear` (only one element) → reset `front = rear = -1`.
4. Otherwise → increment `front` by 1.
5. Decrement `count` by 1.
6. Return `x`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Drawback of Linear Queue:** Even after many dequeues, `front` keeps moving to the right, leaving the left-side slots **permanently unusable**. After $N$ enqueue–dequeue cycles, the queue *appears* full but is logically empty. This is the **O(N) wasted-space problem** that motivates the **Circular Queue**.

## 2.3 Implementation Strategy #2 — Circular Queue (KTU 2024 High-Weight Topic)

The **circular queue** treats the array as a **ring**. After index $N-1$, the next index is $0$. This is implemented via the **modulo operator**.

### 2.3.1 The Modular Update Rule

Whenever `front` or `rear` is advanced, it is done as:

$$ \text{rear} = (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N $$

$$ \text{front} = (\text{front} + 1) \bmod N $$

### 2.3.2 Empty and Full Conditions (One-Slot-Wasted Convention)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Convention:** In the *one-slot-wasted* approach, we deliberately **leave one array slot empty** as a sentinel to disambiguate the full vs. empty case.

$$
\text{isEmpty}: \quad \text{front} = -1
$$

$$
\text{isFull}: \quad (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N = \text{front}
$$

When the queue is full, the number of valid elements is $N - 1$, i.e., **exactly one slot is always kept vacant**.

### 2.3.3 Circular Queue — Enqueue(x) Logic

1. If `isFull()` → report **Overflow**.
2. If `front == -1` (empty queue) → set `front = 0`.
3. Advance `rear = (rear + 1) mod N`.
4. Set `Q[rear] = x`.

### 2.3.4 Circular Queue — Dequeue() Logic

1. If `isEmpty()` → report **Underflow**.
2. Store `x = Q[front]`.
3. If `front == rear` (last element being removed) → reset `front = rear = -1`.
4. Otherwise → advance `front = (front + 1) mod N`.
5. Return `x`.

## 2.4 Implementation Strategy #3 — Linked-List-Based Queue

Using a **singly linked list** eliminates the fixed-capacity problem:
- A `front` pointer points to the first node.
- A `rear` pointer points to the last node.
- `Enqueue` adds a new node after `rear` and updates `rear` → $O(1)$.
- `Dequeue` removes the `front` node and advances `front` → $O(1)$.

> [!NOTE]
> In a linked-list queue, **Overflow** never occurs (limited only by heap memory); only **Underflow** needs to be checked (`front == NULL`).

## 2.5 Double-Ended Queue (Deque)

A **deque** (pronounced *"deck"*) permits insertion and deletion at **both** ends. It is *not* strictly FIFO.

| Operation | Front End | Rear End |
|---|---|---|
| Insert | `insertFront(x)` | `insertRear(x)` |
| Delete | `deleteFront()` | `deleteRear()` |

**Variants of Deque:**
- **Input-Restricted Deque** — Insertion allowed at only one end; deletion at both.
- **Output-Restricted Deque** — Deletion allowed at only one end; insertion at both.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU 2024 ESE — Print This Section.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FIFO ordering | Oldest element served first | Defining property |
| 2 | Linear queue empty | $\text{front} = -1$ (or $\text{count}=0$) | Initial state |
| 3 | Linear queue full | $\text{rear} = N - 1$ (or $\text{count}=N$) | Wasted slots possible |
| 4 | Circular empty | $\text{front} = -1$ | One-slot-wasted scheme |
| 5 | Circular full | $(\text{rear}+1) \bmod N = \text{front}$ | One slot always unused |
| 6 | Circular increment | $\text{idx} = (\text{idx}+1) \bmod N$ | Core update rule |
| 7 | Enqueue time | $O(1)$ | All implementations |
| 8 | Dequeue time | $O(1)$ | All implementations |
| 9 | Peek time | $O(1)$ | Read `Q[front]` only |
| 10 | Space — array | $O(N)$ | Static |
| 11 | Space — linked list | $O(n)$ | Dynamic, $n$ = current size |
| 12 | Number of elements | $\text{count} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N$ | Circular queue only |

## 2.7 Why These Operations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Embedded systems** (e.g., RTOS task schedulers in an Arduino-based weather station) use circular queues to buffer sensor samples because memory is limited and reuse is critical.
- **Kernel-level IPC** in Linux uses **`kfifo`**, which is precisely a circular byte-queue with $O(1)$ enqueue/dequeue — the *exact* data structure studied here.
- **Streaming pipelines** in Apache Kafka use queue-like log buffers to decouple producers from consumers.

> [!TIP]
> If the examiner asks *"Why circular queue and not linear?"* — the answer is always: **"To achieve $O(1)$ space utilisation by reusing vacated slots via the modulo operation, preventing the false-overflow problem."**

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Why One Slot Must Be Wasted in a Circular Queue

Let the array have capacity $N$ indices: $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1\}$.

We need a condition `isEmpty` and a *different* condition `isFull` such that:
- When the queue is **empty**, no element is present.
- When the queue is **full**, all $N$ slots are *not* simultaneously occupied, but the queue is logically full.

Consider the moment when the queue contains exactly $N$ valid elements. After one more enqueue, we would wrap around, and `rear` would catch up with `front`. But the *same* configuration (`rear == front`) also occurs when the queue is **empty** (after the last dequeue, both indices reset to `-1`, or after one element, `front == rear == 0`).

**Therefore, to disambiguate:** we sacrifice one slot. When the queue is full, the rear sits *one position behind* the front (modulo-wise), leaving exactly one slot vacant.

$$
\boxed{\text{isFull: } (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N \;=\; \text{front}}
$$

This is the canonical KTU answer. The maximum number of storable elements in a circular queue of size $N$ is therefore $N - 1$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Circular Queue Enqueue/Dequeue Trace

Let $N = 5$. Initial state: `front = -1`, `rear = -1`.

### Step 1: Enqueue(10)

- Queue is empty (`front == -1`), so set `front = 0`.
- `rear = (rear + 1) mod 5 = 0`.
- `Q[0] = 10`.

**State:** `front = 0`, `rear = 0`, `Q = [10, -, -, -, -]`.

### Step 2: Enqueue(20)

- `rear = (0 + 1) mod 5 = 1`.
- `Q[1] = 20`.

**State:** `front = 0`, `rear = 1`, `Q = [10, 20, -, -, -]`.

### Step 3: Enqueue(30), Enqueue(40), Enqueue(50)

After these three enqueues:

- `rear` becomes `4`.
- `Q = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]`.

### Step 4: Check isFull

$$
(\text{rear} + 1) \bmod 5 = (4 + 1) \bmod 5 = 0 = \text{front}
$$

**Queue is FULL** — even though slot 0 is logically the *next* insert position, it is not allowed because that would make `rear` catch up to `front`.

### Step 5: Dequeue()

- `x = Q[front] = Q[0] = 10`.
- `front != rear`, so `front = (0 + 1) mod 5 = 1`.
- Return `10`.

**State:** `front = 1`, `rear = 4`, `Q = [_, 20, 30, 40, 50]`.

### Step 6: Enqueue(60)

- Queue is not full.
- `rear = (4 + 1) mod 5 = 0`.
- `Q[0] = 60`.

**State:** `front = 1`, `rear = 0`, `Q = [60, 20, 30, 40, 50]`.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "wrap-around" moment.** Notice that `rear` (0) is now *less than* `front` (1) numerically. This is perfectly valid in a circular queue and demonstrates the modulo-based reuse of freed space — the linear queue could *never* do this.

### Step 7: Dequeue() four times

After four dequeues, the queue becomes empty. On the last dequeue, since `front == rear`, both are reset to `-1`.

## 3.3 Derivation — Number of Elements in a Circular Queue

Given `front`, `rear`, and size $N$:

- If $\text{rear} \geq \text{front}$: the queue is "non-wrapped," and the count is simply `rear - front + 1`.
- If $\text{rear} < \text{front}$: the queue has wrapped around. The count is the back portion *plus* the front portion: $(N - \text{front}) + (\text{rear} + 1)$.

Combining both cases using the modulo operator:

$$
\boxed{\text{count} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N}
$$

**Proof by case analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Case A:} \quad \text{rear} \geq \text{front} \\
\text{count} &= (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N \\
&= (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N \\
&= \text{rear} - \text{front} + 1 \quad \text{(since } \text{rear} - \text{front} + N < 2N \text{ and} \geq N) \\
\\
\textbf{Case B:} \quad \text{rear} < \text{front} \\
\text{count} &= (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N \\
&= (\text{rear} + N - \text{front}) \bmod N \\
&= (N - \text{front}) + \text{rear} + 1 \quad \text{(since } 0 < \text{rear} - \text{front} + N < N)
\end{aligned}
$$

Both cases reduce to the correct number of elements. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Queue Variants

| Property | Linear Queue (Array) | Circular Queue (Array) | Linked-List Queue | Deque |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Memory layout | Contiguous | Contiguous | Non-contiguous | Both |
| Resize possible? | No (fixed $N$) | No (fixed $N$) | Yes (dynamic) | Both |
| Wasted slots | Yes (after dequeues) | One slot only | None | Depends |
| enqueue complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ at both ends |
| dequeue complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ at both ends |
| Random access | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | Depends |
| Overflow condition | `rear == N-1` | `(rear+1) mod N == front` | `malloc` fails | Depends |
| FIFO strictly? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Space overhead | Lowest | Lowest | Higher (pointers) | Highest |

## 3.5 Derivation — Why `count` is Needed for Linear Queue (Alternative Convention)

Suppose we choose the `count`-based convention for a linear queue. We must answer the question: *"Is the queue full?"*

- If we rely *only* on `front` and `rear`:
  - Empty: `front == -1` (we set this after the last dequeue).
  - Full: ???
- The problem: when `front == 0` and `rear == N-1`, the queue is full. But when `front == 3` and `rear == 2`, the queue is also "full" if we treat the array as linear — yet `front != -1` and the queue is not logically empty.

The cleanest disambiguation is the **count variable**:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{isEmpty} &: \text{count} == 0 \\
\text{isFull}  &: \text{count} == N
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board answers, **always state the convention you are using** at the top of the solution. Examiners *penalise* ambiguity.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Linear Queue Trace (Count-Based Convention)

Let $N = 4$. Initial: `front = -1`, `rear = -1`, `count = 0`.

| Operation | Condition Check | `front` | `rear` | `count` | `Q` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Enqueue(5) | not full | 0 | 0 | 1 | [5, -, -, -] |
| Enqueue(10) | not full | 0 | 1 | 2 | [5, 10, -, -] |
| Enqueue(15) | not full | 0 | 2 | 3 | [5, 10, 15, -] |
| Enqueue(20) | not full | 0 | 3 | 4 | [5, 10, 15, 20] |
| Enqueue(25) | **count == 4 = N** → Overflow | — | — | — | — |
| Dequeue() → 5 | not empty | 1 | 3 | 3 | [-, 10, 15, 20] |
| Dequeue() → 10 | not empty | 2 | 3 | 2 | [-, -, 15, 20] |
| Dequeue() → 15 | not empty | 3 | 3 | 1 | [-, -, -, 20] |
| Dequeue() → 20 | not empty, then `front==rear` reset | -1 | -1 | 0 | [-, -, -, -] |
| Dequeue() | **count == 0** → Underflow | — | — | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> Note the **wasted space problem**: slots `Q[0]`, `Q[1]`, `Q[2]` are freed after dequeues, but we *cannot* reuse them in a linear queue — `rear` is still pinned at `3`. This is the defect fixed by the circular queue.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Queue as a Pipeline

The following diagram shows the **logical architecture** of a queue with two operation ports:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Producer / Caller] -->|Enqueue x| R[Rear End]
    R --> Q[Queue Storage]
    Q --> F[Front End]
    F -->|Dequeue returns y| B[Consumer / Caller]
    Q -.Peek.-> P[Read Q front]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- New elements enter through the **Rear** end (`Enqueue` port).
- Old elements leave through the **Front** end (`Dequeue` port).
- `Peek` is a *non-destructive* read operation that only inspects the front.

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram — Circular Queue Lifecycle

The following finite-state diagram traces the major state changes of a circular queue of size $N$:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0: Empty
    S0: front = -1, rear = -1
    S1: Has One Element
    S1: front = rear = k
    S2: Partial
    S2: 1 less than count less than N minus 1
    S3: Full
    S3: rear + 1 mod N = front

    S0 --> S1: Enqueue x
    S1 --> S2: Enqueue y
    S1 --> S0: Dequeue
    S2 --> S2: Enqueue or Dequeue
    S2 --> S3: Enqueue when rear+1 mod N = front
    S2 --> S1: Dequeue down to one
    S2 --> S0: Dequeue last
    S3 --> S2: Dequeue
```

> [!NOTE]
> The state `Full` is **reachable** from `Partial` via a single `Enqueue`, but **not** from `Empty` directly — the queue must first pass through `Has One Element`. This reflects the modulo arithmetic.

## 4.3 Array Layout Schematic — Circular Queue After Wrap-Around

A graphical view of the state from Section 3.2, Step 6:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ARR["Array Q of size N = 5"]
        A0["Q 0 = 60"]
        A1["Q 1 = 20"]
        A2["Q 2 = 30"]
        A3["Q 3 = 40"]
        A4["Q 4 = 50"]
    end

    R["rear points to index 0"] -.-> A0
    F["front points to index 1"] -.-> A1
```

Notice the **`rear` index (0) is numerically less than `front` (1)**, yet the queue is logically valid — the wrap-around has occurred. This is the defining visual cue of a circular queue.

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Linear vs Circular Queue

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIN["Linear Queue - After Several Dequeues"]
        L0["0 used"]
        L1["1 used"]
        L2["2 used"]
        L3["3 free"]
        L4["4 free"]
        L5["5 free"]
    end

    subgraph CIR["Circular Queue - Same Logical State"]
        C0["0 free reused"]
        C1["1 used"]
        C2["2 used"]
        C3["3 used"]
        C4["4 used"]
        C5["5 used"]
    end
```

In the **linear queue**, slots `0, 1, 2` are wasted (greyed-out). In the **circular queue**, the slot at index `0` has been *reused* for a new element after the wrap-around, achieving full $O(N)$ utilisation.

## 4.5 Linked-List Queue Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[front pointer] --> N1["Node 1 - data field + next pointer"]
    N1 --> N2["Node 2"]
    N2 --> N3["Node 3"]
    N3 --> N4["Node 4 - last node"]
    N4 --> Z["NULL - end of list"]
    Q[rear pointer] --> N4
```

**Insertion:** a new node is appended after `Node 4`; `rear` is updated to point to it. **Deletion:** `front` is advanced to `Node 2`; `Node 1` is freed.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q1(a)]**

### Q1. (a) Define a queue data structure. List its primitive operations.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A queue is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** principle, where insertion occurs at the **rear** end and deletion occurs at the **front** end. The five primitive operations of a queue ADT are:

1. **`Enqueue(x)`** — Insert element `x` at the rear. Time: $O(1)$.
2. **`Dequeue()`** — Remove and return the element at the front. Time: $O(1)$.
3. **`Front()` / `Peek()`** — Return the front element without removing it. Time: $O(1)$.
4. **`isEmpty()`** — Return `true` if the queue is empty. Time: $O(1)$.
5. **`isFull()`** — Return `true` if the queue cannot accept more elements. Time: $O(1)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark, List of operations = 1 mark, Time complexity note = 1 mark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Q1(b)]**

### Q1. (b) Differentiate between a stack and a queue.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Stack | Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Order of removal | **LIFO** (Last-In, First-Out) | **FIFO** (First-In, First-Out) |
| Insertion end | Top | Rear |
| Deletion end | Top (same as insertion) | Front (opposite of insertion) |
| Number of access ends | **One** (the `top`) | **Two** (`front` and `rear`) |
| Overflow condition | `top == N-1` | `rear == N-1` (linear) or `(rear+1) mod N == front` (circular) |
| Use case | Recursion, expression evaluation, undo | Scheduling, BFS, buffering |
| Example | Pile of plates | Queue at a billing counter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Any three valid differences = 3 marks (1 mark each).
