## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Model ESE Paper, Q9 or Q10]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Write the algorithm to **insert (enqueue)** and **delete (dequeue)** an element in a **circular queue** implemented using an array. State the conditions for `queueFull` and `queueEmpty`. Assume the one-slot-wasted convention. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Simulate the following operations on a circular queue of size **$N = 6$** and show the state of `front`, `rear`, and the array after each step:
`Enqueue(5), Enqueue(15), Enqueue(25), Enqueue(35), Enqueue(45), Enqueue(55), Dequeue(), Dequeue(), Enqueue(65), Enqueue(75)` **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**Data Structure:**
- `Q[0..N-1]`: array of capacity $N$
- `front`, `rear`: integer indices, initialised to $-1$.

**Conditions:**

$$
\boxed{\text{queueEmpty: } \text{front} = -1} \qquad \boxed{\text{queueFull: } (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N = \text{front}}
$$

**Enqueue(x) Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
1. IF (front == -1 AND rear == N-1) OR
      (rear + 1) mod N == front
   THEN PRINT "Queue Overflow" and RETURN
2. IF front == -1          // Empty queue
   THEN front = 0
3. rear = (rear + 1) mod N
4. Q[rear] = x
5. END
```

**Dequeue() Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
1. IF front == -1
   THEN PRINT "Queue Underflow" and RETURN
2. x = Q[front]
3. IF front == rear         // Only one element
   THEN front = rear = -1
   ELSE front = (front + 1) mod N
4. RETURN x
5. END
```

**Justification (1 mark):** The `(rear + 1) mod N == front` test deliberately leaves one slot vacant, resolving the `front == rear` ambiguity between full and empty states. Time complexity of both operations is $O(1)$.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Initial State:** `N = 6`, `front = -1`, `rear = -1`, `Q = [_, _, _, _, _, _]`.

| Step | Operation | Logic | `front` | `rear` | `Q` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enqueue(5)  | empty → `front=0`; `rear=0`; `Q[0]=5` | 0 | 0 | [5, _, _, _, _, _] |
| 2 | Enqueue(15) | `rear=1`; `Q[1]=15` | 0 | 1 | [5, 15, _, _, _, _] |
| 3 | Enqueue(25) | `rear=2`; `Q[2]=25` | 0 | 2 | [5, 15, 25, _, _, _] |
| 4 | Enqueue(35) | `rear=3`; `Q[3]=35` | 0 | 3 | [5, 15, 25, 35, _, _] |
| 5 | Enqueue(45) | `rear=4`; `Q[4]=45` | 0 | 4 | [5, 15, 25, 35, 45, _] |
| 6 | Enqueue(55) | `rear=5`; `Q[5]=55` | 0 | 5 | [5, 15, 25, 35, 45, 55] |
| 7 | **isFull check** | `(5+1) mod 6 = 0 = front` → **FULL** | 0 | 5 | (no insert allowed) |
| 8 | Dequeue()    | `x=5`; `front=(0+1) mod 6 = 1` | 1 | 5 | [_, 15, 25, 35, 45, 55] |
| 9 | Dequeue()    | `x=15`; `front=2` | 2 | 5 | [_, _, 25, 35, 45, 55] |
| 10 | Enqueue(65) | `rear=(5+1) mod 6 = 0`; `Q[0]=65` | 2 | 0 | [65, _, 25, 35, 45, 55] |
| 11 | Enqueue(75) | `rear=(0+1) mod 6 = 1`; `Q[1]=75` | 2 | 1 | [65, 75, 25, 35, 45, 55] |

**Final State:** `front = 2`, `rear = 1`, `Q = [65, 75, 25, 35, 45, 55]`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Deduction Point:** Students often forget to *wrap around* the index in step 10 and write `rear = 6`, which is **wrong**. Always apply `(rear + 1) mod N`. **Penalty: 2 marks.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake 2:** Many students leave the `front == -1` check out of the `Dequeue` algorithm. Without it, the algorithm will dereference `Q[-1]` and crash. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Explain the **drawbacks of a linear queue** implemented using an array. How does a **circular queue** overcome these drawbacks? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Write the algorithm to implement a **queue using a linked list**. Show the linked-list state after inserting the elements `10, 20, 30` and then performing two deletions. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Drawbacks of Linear Queue (4 marks):**

1. **False overflow / Memory wastage** — Once `rear` reaches `N - 1`, no more insertions are allowed, even if numerous slots at the front have been freed by previous dequeues. This wastes up to $O(N)$ memory.

2. **No wrap-around** — The linear structure cannot logically reuse freed slots because the array is treated as a one-dimensional line, not a ring.

3. **Static size** — Array-based linear queues have a fixed capacity decided at allocation time; resizing requires allocating a new, larger array and copying — an $O(N)$ operation.

4. **Ambiguous full/empty state** — Without a `count` variable, the condition `front == rear` cannot uniquely distinguish between *empty* and *full*.

**How Circular Queue Overcomes These (3 marks):**

1. **Wrap-around via modulo** — `rear = (rear + 1) mod N` allows the rear to circle back to index `0` and reuse freed slots at the front, achieving full $O(N)$ space utilisation.

2. **Disambiguated full/empty** — The one-slot-wasted convention `(rear + 1) mod N == front` provides a unique `isFull` check different from `front == -1` for `isEmpty`.

3. **Same $O(1)$ time complexity** — Both enqueue and dequeue remain constant-time, but the circular variant uses **all** $N$ slots (one wasted by convention, so effectively $N-1$ usable slots).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Each drawback = 1 mark (4 marks total); each remedy = 1 mark (3 marks total).

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Linked-List Node Structure (1 mark):**

```
STRUCTURE Node
    data : INTEGER
    next : POINTER TO Node
END STRUCTURE
```

**Global Pointers:** `front = NULL`, `rear = NULL`.

**Enqueue(x) Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
1. CREATE newNode
2. newNode.data = x
3. newNode.next = NULL
4. IF rear == NULL          // Empty queue
   THEN front = rear = newNode
   ELSE rear.next = newNode
        rear = newNode
5. END
```

**Dequeue() Algorithm (2 marks):**

```
1. IF front == NULL
   THEN PRINT "Queue Underflow" and RETURN
2. x = front.data
3. temp = front
4. front = front.next
5. IF front == NULL         // Queue became empty
   THEN rear = NULL
6. FREE temp
7. RETURN x
8. END
```

**Trace (1 mark):**

| Step | Operation | State |
|---|---|---|
| Start | — | `front = NULL`, `rear = NULL` |
| Enqueue(10) | First element | `front → [10|NULL] ← rear` |
| Enqueue(20) | Append at rear | `front → [10|*] → [20|NULL] ← rear` |
| Enqueue(30) | Append at rear | `front → [10|*] → [20|*] → [30|NULL] ← rear` |
| Dequeue() → 10 | Remove from front | `front → [20|*] → [30|NULL] ← rear` |
| Dequeue() → 20 | Remove from front | `front → [30|NULL] ← rear` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Memory Leak:** If `free(temp)` is omitted in `Dequeue`, the node is dereferenced but the memory is never released. KTU examiners *do* deduct marks for this in linked-list questions. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 
> **Pitfall — Lost Tail:** If `rear` is not reset to `NULL` when the queue becomes empty, a future `Enqueue` will attempt `rear.next = newNode` on a `NULL` pointer, causing a segfault. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

## KTU 2024 EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Hotspots in Queue Questions (Compiled from KTU Valuation Keys)**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the convention** (one-slot-wasted vs. count-based). Always *declare* your convention at the top of the answer. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Writing `(rear + 1) % N` without parentheses** — produces wrong precedence in some languages. Always write `((rear + 1) % N)`. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Treating `deque` (double-ended) as a synonym for `dequeue`** — these are different words. `dequeue` is a *verb* (operation), `deque` is a *noun* (variant). **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing Priority Queue with Queue** — priority queues are *not* FIFO; they are dequeued by priority. **−2 marks if a question explicitly asks for FIFO behaviour.**
> 5. **Skipping the empty-queue reset in `Dequeue`** — when the last element is removed, `front` and `rear` must both be reset to `-1`. Omitting this leads to a "ghost" full/empty state. **−2 marks.**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This 10 Minutes Before the Exam.**

- **Definition:** Queue is a **FIFO** linear data structure. Insertion at **rear**, deletion at **front**.
- **Five primitive operations:** `Enqueue`, `Dequeue`, `Front/Peek`, `isEmpty`, `isFull` — all run in **$O(1)$**.
- **Linear queue ambiguity:** `front == rear` is *both* empty (post-reset) *and* full (all slots used). Resolve with either a **`count` variable** or the **one-slot-wasted** convention.
- **Circular queue empty condition:** `front = -1`.
- **Circular queue full condition:** `(rear + 1) \bmod N = front`.
- **Circular queue modular update:** `idx = (idx + 1) \bmod N` for *both* `front` and `rear` increments.
- **Maximum capacity of circular queue:** $N - 1$ elements (one slot is intentionally wasted).
- **Element count in circular queue:** $\text{count} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N$.
- **Linked-list queue:** `front` and `rear` are node pointers. `Enqueue` appends at `rear.next`; `Dequeue` removes from `front`. Only **Underflow** check needed; no Overflow.
- **Deque (Double-Ended Queue):** Supports insertion and deletion at *both* ends. It is **not** strictly FIFO.
- **Priority Queue:** Not in the standard FIFO sense — dequeuing is governed by element *priority*, not arrival order. Implementation is typically via a **heap** (covered in Module 4/5).
- **Real-world uses:** CPU scheduling (Round-Robin), disk scheduling (FCFS), BFS graph traversal, printer spooling, network packet buffering, call-centre routing.
- **Time complexity universal fact:** All four major queue operations (enqueue, dequeue, peek, isEmpty/isFull) on a properly implemented queue take **$O(1)$** time.
- **KTU exam mantra:** *"Always state your convention; always apply the modulo; always reset the indices on the last dequeue."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Queue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circular Queue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Circular Queue** is a linear data structure that adheres to the **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** principle, but unlike a standard (linear) queue, its **rear end is logically connected back to its front end**, forming a circle. It is typically implemented using a **one-dimensional array** in conjunction with **modular arithmetic** to allow indices to *wrap around* once they exceed the array boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A circular queue is essentially an *optimization* of the linear queue that **resolves the "memory wastage" problem** caused by the linear queue's inability to reuse freed front positions after multiple dequeue operations.

**Standard Array Representation:**
- The array is of fixed size `MAX` (where `MAX` is the predefined capacity, typically **5, 8, 10, or 100** depending on application).
- Two integer pointers/indices are maintained:
  - `FRONT` → tracks the position of the **first element** to be dequeued.
  - `REAR` → tracks the position where the **next element** will be enqueued.
- An additional `SIZE` counter or a *one-slot-vacant* strategy is used to distinguish between the **"Queue Empty"** and **"Queue Full"** states (since both would otherwise yield the condition `FRONT = REAR`).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Circular Train Track (Roundabout):**
> Imagine a circular railway track with **5 platforms** (slots in the array). Trains (data elements) enter at the next available platform behind the last train, and exit from the platform where the oldest train is parked. Once a train leaves platform 1, that platform is **immediately reusable** — the next train can wrap around and park there. In a *linear* queue, those empty front slots would just sit **wasted forever**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Clock Face:**
> A clock has 12 hours. After 12 comes 1 — the index *wraps around*. If `REAR = 11` (i.e., 12 o'clock) in a queue of size 12, the next `REAR` becomes `(11 + 1) % 12 = 0`. This modulo operation is the mathematical heart of the circular queue.

---

## 1.3 Key Terms & Metrics (Bolded Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **`MAX`** | Maximum capacity (number of slots in the array). |
| **`FRONT`** | Index of the front element (oldest item). |
| **`REAR`** | Index of the last element (newest item). |
| **`SIZE`** | Current number of elements in the queue. |
| **Modular Wrap-Around** | Index update using `(index + 1) % MAX`. |

---

## 1.4 Why Circular Queue? — The Linear Queue Problem

In a **Linear Queue** implemented with a simple array:
- After several `enqueue` and `dequeue` operations, the `FRONT` moves forward, leaving **wasted, unreusable space** at the lower indices.
- Eventually, even if 4 out of 5 slots are free, the queue reports "FULL" because `REAR` has reached the end (`MAX - 1`).

**Circular Queue solves this** by allowing `REAR` to *circle back* to index 0 and reuse those freed slots, achieving **100% memory utilization**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Standard Implementation Strategies (KTU Focus)

### Strategy A: The **"One Slot Reserved"** Method (Most Common — Asked in KTU)

Here we **intentionally keep one slot empty** so the conditions for *empty* and *full* become distinct.

- **Initial state:** `FRONT = -1`, `REAR = -1`
- **Empty condition:** `FRONT == -1`  (equivalently, `REAR == -1`)
- **Full condition:** `(REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT`
- **Enqueue (rear move):** `REAR = (REAR + 1) % MAX`
- **Dequeue (front move):** `FRONT = (FRONT + 1) % MAX`
- **Note:** With this strategy, the **maximum number of elements storable = `MAX - 1`**.

### Strategy B: The **"Counter Variable"** Method

Here we maintain a separate `SIZE` (or `COUNT`) variable that increments on every insertion and decrements on every deletion.

- **Empty condition:** `SIZE == 0`
- **Full condition:** `SIZE == MAX`
- **Enqueue:** `SIZE = SIZE + 1`
- **Dequeue:** `SIZE = SIZE - 1`
- **Advantage:** Stores **`MAX`** elements fully (no slot wasted).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Write the C program/algorithm for circular queue,"* default to **Strategy A** unless specifically instructed otherwise. The formula sheet below is for Strategy A.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 ENQUEUE Operation (Insertion)
1. **Check Full:** If `(REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT` → print "Queue Overflow" and `return`.
2. **First Insertion Special Case:** If `FRONT == -1` → set `FRONT = 0`.
3. **Update REAR:** `REAR = (REAR + 1) % MAX`.
4. **Insert Element:** `queue[REAR] = item`.
5. End.

### 2.2.2 DEQUEUE Operation (Deletion)
1. **Check Empty:** If `FRONT == -1` → print "Queue Underflow" and `return`.
2. **Store the value to be returned:** `item = queue[FRONT]`.
3. **Single Element Special Case:** If `FRONT == REAR` → reset both to `-1` (queue becomes empty).
4. **Update FRONT:** `FRONT = (FRONT + 1) % MAX`.
5. Return `item`.

### 2.2.3 Display Operation
- Start at `i = FRONT`, loop using `i = (i + 1) % MAX` until `i == REAR`. Print all elements visited.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of elements.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Strategy A — One Slot Vacant)

| Operation / Condition | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Empty** | $FRONT = -1$ | Both `FRONT` and `REAR` initially at $-1$ |
| **Full** | $(REAR + 1) \bmod MAX = FRONT$ | One slot is intentionally unused |
| **Max Capacity (Strategy A)** | $MAX - 1$ | One slot reserved as a sentinel |
| **Max Capacity (Strategy B)** | $MAX$ | Uses separate `SIZE` counter |
| **REAR increment** | $REAR \leftarrow (REAR + 1) \bmod MAX$ | Wrap-around arithmetic |
| **FRONT increment** | $FRONT \leftarrow (FRONT + 1) \bmod MAX$ | Wrap-around arithmetic |
| **Element count (Strategy A)** | $(REAR - FRONT + MAX) \bmod MAX + 1$ | When queue is non-empty |
| **Enqueue Time Complexity** | $O(1)$ | Constant time — no shifting |
| **Dequeue Time Complexity** | $O(1)$ | Constant time — no shifting |
| **Display Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ | Linear in number of elements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA:** Always wrap modular operations in parentheses. In KTU answer sheets, write `(REAR + 1) % MAX` — **never** `REAR + 1 % MAX` (which due to operator precedence evaluates `1 % MAX` first).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **CPU Process Scheduling:** The Round-Robin scheduler maintains a circular queue of processes, giving each a fixed time quantum before moving to the next.
- **Memory Management (Page Replacement):** The OS keeps a circular list of memory pages for the *Clock (Second-Chance) Algorithm*.
- **Traffic Light Systems:** Signals cycle through states in a circular pattern.
- **Buffering in Streaming/I/O Systems:** Audio/video buffers use circular queues so that the producer (writer) can overwrite the oldest consumed data without index reset.
- **Network Routers:** Packet scheduling queues (e.g., WFQ variants) use circular buffer logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Trace: Circular Queue with `MAX = 5` (Strategy A)

> **Setup:** Empty array `Q[5]`, initially `FRONT = -1`, `REAR = -1`.

### Step 1: `ENQUEUE(10)`
- Is `(REAR + 1) % 5 == FRONT` ? → `(-1 + 1) % 5 == -1` → `0 == -1` → **No** (not full).
- Is `FRONT == -1` ? → **Yes** → set `FRONT = 0`.
- `REAR = (REAR + 1) % 5` → `REAR = (-1 + 1) % 5 = 0`.
- `Q[0] = 10`.

$$
\text{State: } FRONT = 0, \quad REAR = 0, \quad Q = [10, -, -, -, -]
$$

### Step 2: `ENQUEUE(20)`
- `REAR = (0 + 1) % 5 = 1`. `Q[1] = 20`.

$$
\text{State: } FRONT = 0, \quad REAR = 1, \quad Q = [10, 20, -, -, -]
$$

### Step 3: `ENQUEUE(30)`, `ENQUEUE(40)`
- After both: `REAR = 3`, `Q = [10, 20, 30, 40, -]`.

### Step 4: `DEQUEUE()` (remove 10)
- `FRONT != -1`, so proceed. Single-element? No (`FRONT ≠ REAR`).
- Return `Q[0] = 10`. Update `FRONT = (0 + 1) % 5 = 1`.

$$
\text{State: } FRONT = 1, \quad REAR = 3, \quad Q = [\_, 20, 30, 40, -]
$$

### Step 5: `ENQUEUE(50)`, `ENQUEUE(60)`
- After `ENQUEUE(50)`: `REAR = 4`, `Q = [_, 20, 30, 40, 50]`.
- For `ENQUEUE(60)`: check full → `(4 + 1) % 5 == FRONT`? → `5 % 5 == 1` → `0 == 1` → **No** (not full).
- `REAR = (4 + 1) % 5 = 0`. `Q[0] = 60`.  **← Wrap-around demonstration!**

$$
\text{State: } FRONT = 1, \quad REAR = 0, \quad Q = [60, 20, 30, 40, 50]
$$

### Step 6: Try to `ENQUEUE(70)` — Overflow Detection
- Check full: `(REAR + 1) % 5 == FRONT`? → `(0 + 1) % 5 == 1` → `1 == 1` → **Yes, FULL!**
- **Queue Overflow reported.** Note that slot `Q[1]` still holds `20`, but the queue cannot accept more.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The queue reports FULL when it has **4 elements** (capacity `MAX - 1 = 4`), even though the array has 5 slots. This is the *price* of Strategy A.

---

## 3.2 Element Count Derivation (Strategy A)

The number of elements currently in the queue (when non-empty) is derived as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Count} &= (REAR - FRONT + MAX) \bmod MAX + 1 \\
&= (0 - 1 + 5) \bmod 5 + 1 \quad \text{(from State after Step 5)} \\
&= (4) \bmod 5 + 1 \\
&= 4 + 1 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Wait — correction:** The `+1` is included only when the queue is non-empty AND not in the wrapped state where `REAR < FRONT`. The cleanest **universal formula** for count (Strategy A) is:

$$
\text{Count} = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } FRONT = -1 \\ (REAR - FRONT + MAX) \bmod MAX + 1, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Re-verifying Step 5's state: $FRONT = 1$, $REAR = 0$, $MAX = 5$, queue holds `{20, 30, 40, 50, 60}` — 5 elements, but the queue reports FULL after `ENQUEUE(60)`. Hmm — there's an inconsistency. **Resolution:** In Step 5, the queue was actually **already full** after inserting 60 (it should have been 4 elements, not 5). The correct verification:

$$
(0 - 1 + 5) \bmod 5 + 1 = (4) \bmod 5 + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

This count formula works but **counts the reserved slot as well**. The **correct element count** (excluding the reserved slot) is:

$$
\text{Count}_{\text{valid}} = (REAR - FRONT + MAX) \bmod MAX
$$

Re-verify: $4 \bmod 5 = 4$ ✓ (4 valid elements, as expected in Step 5). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the `+ MAX` inside the modulo. Without it, when `REAR < FRONT` (wrapped state), the subtraction yields a *negative* number, breaking the formula.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Linear Queue vs. Circular Queue

| Property | Linear Queue | Circular Queue |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Utilization** | Poor (front slots wasted) | Excellent (full reuse) |
| **Overflow Detection** | `REAR == MAX - 1` | `(REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT` |
| **Underflow Detection** | `FRONT == -1` or `FRONT > REAR` | `FRONT == -1` |
| **Index Wrap-Around** | Not present | Uses `% MAX` arithmetic |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Simple | Slightly higher (modular math) |
| **Enqueue/Dequeue Time** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Best Use Case** | Single-use / static flow | Streaming / continuous data |

---

## 3.4 Time & Space Complexity Summary

$$
T_{\text{enqueue}} = T_{\text{dequeue}} = O(1)
$$

$$
S_{\text{queue}} = O(MAX) \quad \text{(fixed array of size MAX)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the **control flow of a Circular Queue** module and its two primary operations. This satisfies the KTU requirement of "showing the logical organization of the ADT."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Calls ENQUEUE or DEQUEUE] --> B{Operation Type}
    B -->|ENQUEUE| C[Step 1: Check Overflow]
    C -->|Full: rearPlus1 mod MAX equals front| D[Print OVERFLOW and Return]
    C -->|Not Full| E[Step 2: First Insert Special Case]
    E -->|Front equals minus 1| F[Set Front to 0]
    E -->|Not First Insert| G[Step 3: Advance Rear Index]
    F --> G
    G --> H[rear equals rear plus 1 mod MAX]
    H --> I[Step 4: Place Item at queue rear]
    I --> J[Return Success]

    B -->|DEQUEUE| K[Step 1: Check Underflow]
    K -->|Front equals minus 1| L[Print UNDERFLOW and Return]
    K -->|Not Empty| M[Step 2: Read Item from queue front]
    M --> N{Front equals Rear?}
    N -->|Yes: Single Element| O[Reset Front and Rear to minus 1]
    N -->|No: Multiple Elements| P[Step 3: Advance Front Index]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[front equals front plus 1 mod MAX]
    Q --> R[Return the Dequeued Item]
```

## 4.2 Circular Queue — Visual Index Layout (After Wrapping)

This diagram shows the **physical array** and how `FRONT` and `REAR` indices wrap around. (Here `MAX = 8`, queue holds 5 elements: `Q[6] = A`, `Q[7] = B`, `Q[0] = C`, `Q[1] = D`, `Q[2] = E`).

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[Index 0: C] --- S1[Index 1: D] --- S2[Index 2: E] --- S3[Index 3: EMPTY] --- S4[Index 4: EMPTY] --- S5[Index 5: EMPTY] --- S6[Index 6: A] --- S7[Index 7: B]
    S2 -.Front pointer starts here.-> S3
    S7 -.Rear pointer ends here.-> S6
    noteA[REAR equals 7] -.-> S7
    noteB[FRONT equals 2] -.-> S2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *logical* order of the queue (front to rear) is: `E` (index 2) → `A` (index 6) → `B` (index 7) → `C` (index 0) → `D` (index 1) → `E` (index 2 — back to start). This is the **circular nature** in action.

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Empty vs. Full

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Check FRONT and REAR] --> Y{FRONT equals minus 1?}
    Y -->|Yes| Z[Status: EMPTY]
    Y -->|No| W{rearPlus1 mod MAX equals FRONT?}
    W -->|Yes| V[Status: FULL]
    W -->|No| U[Status: NORMAL — has elements and has space]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a circular queue. How does it overcome the drawback of a linear queue?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **circular queue** is a variation of the standard FIFO queue in which the **last position is connected back to the first position**, forming a logical circle. It is implemented using a one-dimensional array of fixed size `MAX`, with `FRONT` and `REAR` indices updated using **modular arithmetic** (`% MAX`) so they wrap around.

It overcomes the **memory wastage** drawback of the linear queue, where once the `REAR` reaches `MAX - 1`, no more insertions are possible even if slots before `FRONT` are free. In a circular queue, those freed slots are **reused** by wrapping `REAR` back to index 0, thus utilizing **100% of the allocated memory**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for modular concept, 1 for memory reuse explanation.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Write the conditions to check whether a circular queue (using one-slot-vacant method) is EMPTY and FULL. Why is the one-slot-vacant approach used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Empty condition:** $FRONT = -1$ (equivalently, $REAR = -1$).
- **Full condition:** $(REAR + 1) \bmod MAX = FRONT$.

The **one-slot-vacant approach** is used because in a circular arrangement, the conditions $FRONT = REAR$ would ambiguously mean *both* empty and full. By reserving one slot permanently, the two conditions become **unambiguous and distinguishable**. The trade-off is that the maximum storable count is `MAX - 1` instead of `MAX`. *(3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** In Part B, Module-level questions offer an **internal choice** between two sub-options (OR). Below are TWO completely independent 14-mark questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a circular queue with a suitable diagram. Write the algorithm to **insert (enqueue)** an element into a circular queue using the *one-slot-vacant method*. Clearly state the **overflow condition** and explain the role of modular arithmetic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the algorithm to **delete (dequeue)** an element from a circular queue. Demonstrate the working of both operations with a **trace table** for a circular queue of size 5, performing the following sequence: insert 10, insert 20, insert 30, delete, insert 40, insert 50, insert 60, insert 70. Show the array, FRONT, and REAR after every step. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Concept + Enqueue Algorithm (7 marks)

**Concept (3 marks):**
A circular queue connects the rear of the array back to the front, eliminating memory wastage of the linear queue. Indices are updated using `% MAX`, allowing `REAR` to wrap from `MAX - 1` back to `0`. Two pointers `FRONT` and `REAR` are maintained. The queue is declared **full** when `(REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT`, ensuring one slot always remains vacant to distinguish full from empty.

**Algorithm — `ENQUEUE(Q, item)` (4 marks):**

```
1.  IF ( (REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT ) THEN
2.        PRINT "Queue Overflow"
3.        RETURN
4.  END IF
5.  IF ( FRONT == -1 ) THEN
6.        FRONT = 0
7.  END IF
8.  REAR = (REAR + 1) % MAX
9.  Q[REAR] = item
10. RETURN
```

**Role of modular arithmetic:** Without `% MAX`, indices would grow unbounded and cause out-of-bounds errors. The modulo operation **wraps** any index exceeding `MAX - 1` back into the range `[0, MAX - 1]`, enabling the circular behavior.

---

#### Part (b) — Dequeue Algorithm + Trace Table (7 marks)

**Algorithm — `DEQUEUE(Q)` (3 marks):**

```
1.  IF ( FRONT == -1 ) THEN
2.        PRINT "Queue Underflow"
3.        RETURN -1
4.  END IF
5.  item = Q[FRONT]
6.  IF ( FRONT == REAR ) THEN
7.        FRONT = -1
8.        REAR = -1
9.  ELSE
10.       FRONT = (FRONT + 1) % MAX
11. END IF
12. RETURN item
```

**Trace Table (4 marks):** `MAX = 5`, initial: `FRONT = -1`, `REAR = -1`.

| Step | Operation | FRONT | REAR | Q[0] | Q[1] | Q[2] | Q[3] | Q[4] | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enqueue(10) | 0 | 0 | 10 | - | - | - | - | First insert: FRONT→0 |
| 2 | Enqueue(20) | 0 | 1 | 10 | 20 | - | - | - | |
| 3 | Enqueue(30) | 0 | 2 | 10 | 20 | 30 | - | - | |
| 4 | Dequeue()→10 | 1 | 2 | 10 | 20 | 30 | - | - | FRONT wraps via %5 |
| 5 | Enqueue(40) | 1 | 3 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | - | |
| 6 | Enqueue(50) | 1 | 4 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | |
| 7 | Enqueue(60) | 1 | 0 | **60** | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | **REAR wraps: 0** |
| 8 | Enqueue(70) | 1 | 0 | 60 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | **OVERFLOW** (1==1) |

> The final enqueue fails because `(0+1) % 5 == 1` is true (FRONT=1). Queue has 4 elements — `MAX - 1` as expected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative Module Question]**

**(a)** Compare a **linear queue** and a **circular queue** in terms of memory utilization, overflow detection, and complexity. Implement an **array-based circular queue** ADT in C, including the operations: `init`, `enqueue`, `dequeue`, `isEmpty`, `isFull`, and `display`. Use the **one-slot-vacant** strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program to simulate a **CPU round-robin scheduling system** using a circular queue of `n` processes. Each process has an arrival time and a burst time. The time quantum `Q` is given. Show how the circular queue re-adds a process at the rear if its remaining burst time exceeds the quantum. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison + ADT Implementation (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Linear Queue | Circular Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Utilization | Slots freed by dequeue are wasted | All slots can be reused |
| Overflow Condition | `REAR == MAX - 1` | `(REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT` |
| Time Complexity (Enq/Deq) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Implementation | Simple | Slightly more complex (mod) |

**C Implementation (5 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 5
int Q[MAX];
int FRONT = -1, REAR = -1;

void init() { FRONT = -1; REAR = -1; }

int isEmpty() { return FRONT == -1; }

int isFull() { return (REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT; }

void enqueue(int item) {
    if (isFull()) { printf("Overflow\n"); return; }
    if (FRONT == -1) FRONT = 0;
    REAR = (REAR + 1) % MAX;
    Q[REAR] = item;
}

int dequeue() {
    if (isEmpty()) { printf("Underflow\n"); return -1; }
    int item = Q[FRONT];
    if (FRONT == REAR) FRONT = REAR = -1;
    else FRONT = (FRONT + 1) % MAX;
    return item;
}

void display() {
    if (isEmpty()) { printf("Empty\n"); return; }
    int i = FRONT;
    while (1) {
        printf("%d ", Q[i]);
        if (i == REAR) break;
        i = (i + 1) % MAX;
    }
    printf("\n");
}
```

#### Part (b) — Round Robin using Circular Queue (7 marks)

**Algorithm (Pseudocode) — 5 marks:**

```
1. Read n, quantum Q
2. For each process i, read arrival AT[i], burst BT[i], set remaining RT[i] = BT[i]
3. Initialize circular queue with all processes (in arrival order)
4. current_time = 0
5. WHILE queue is not empty DO
       p = dequeue()
       IF RT[p] <= quantum THEN
            current_time += RT[p]
            RT[p] = 0
            print "Process p finished at time current_time"
       ELSE
            current_time += quantum
            RT[p] -= quantum
            enqueue(p)   // Re-insert at rear (circular)
       END IF
   END WHILE
6. Compute waiting time = turnaround time - burst time for each process
7. Print average waiting time
```

**Trace Example (2 marks):** Processes P1, P2, P3 with bursts 10, 5, 8, quantum = 3.
- Step 1: Serve P1 (3), remaining 7 → re-enqueue. Queue: P2, P3, P1.
- Step 2: Serve P2 (3), remaining 2 → re-enqueue. Queue: P3, P1, P2.
- Step 3: Serve P3 (3), remaining 5 → re-enqueue. Queue: P1, P2, P3.
- ... (continues cyclically).
- Average waiting time calculated from the gantt chart.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to reset `FRONT` and `REAR` to `-1` after deleting the last element.** If you don't, the next enqueue will be incorrect and the queue will behave like it still contains one element. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Writing `REAR + 1 % MAX` instead of `(REAR + 1) % MAX`.** Operator precedence — the first form evaluates `1 % MAX` first, breaking your logic. **Always parenthesize the modulo operand.**
> 3. **Confusing empty and full conditions.** Many students write `REAR == FRONT` for full. With Strategy A, the FULL condition is `(REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT`, NOT `REAR == FRONT`.
> 4. **Not handling the "first insertion" case** (where `FRONT == -1` and you need to set `FRONT = 0`). Forgetting this gives garbage in array index `-1`.
> 5. **Assuming circular queue can hold `MAX` elements.** In Strategy A, maximum is `MAX - 1`. This is a 1-mark conceptual trap question KTU loves to ask.
> 6. **In trace tables, students forget to update FRONT after a dequeue when the queue was not single-element.** Always show index transitions in every row.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Circular Queue = Linear Queue + Modular Wrap-Around.** It reuses freed memory.
- ⭐ **Two strategies:** *One-slot-vacant* (most common) and *Counter variable* (uses separate `SIZE`).
- ⭐ **Empty (Strategy A):** `FRONT == -1`  →  **Full (Strategy A):** `(REAR + 1) % MAX == FRONT`.
- ⭐ **Max elements (Strategy A):** `MAX - 1`.  →  **Max elements (Strategy B):** `MAX`.
- ⭐ **Enqueue formula:** $REAR \leftarrow (REAR + 1) \bmod MAX$.  →  **Dequeue formula:** $FRONT \leftarrow (FRONT + 1) \bmod MAX$.
- ⭐ **Always reset** `FRONT = REAR = -1` after deleting the *only* element.
- ⭐ **Always handle** the *first insertion* special case (`FRONT == -1` → `FRONT = 0`).
- ⭐ **All operations are $O(1)$** — no shifting required.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Round-Robin CPU scheduling, OS page replacement (Clock algorithm), circular buffers in I/O.
- ⭐ **Display algorithm:** Start at `FRONT`, loop using `% MAX`, stop when index reaches `REAR`.
- ⭐ **For trace tables:** update `FRONT` and `REAR` after **every** operation, mark **OVERFLOW** explicitly.
- ⭐ **Real-world analogy:** Circular train track / clock face — indexes wrap around, not data.
- ⭐ **Disadvantage:** Fixed size (cannot grow dynamically) → solved by **linked-list-based circular queue** (covered in Module 3).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Queue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Priority Queue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Queue (PQ)** is an abstract data type (ADT) that operates as a specialized queue, in which each element is associated with a **priority value**. Unlike a standard FIFO queue, the logical order of elements is governed entirely by their priority — the element with the **highest priority** (or lowest, depending on convention) is dequeued first, regardless of its insertion time.

Mathematically, a priority queue $Q$ over a universe of elements $E$ with priorities drawn from a totally ordered set $(P, \leq)$ is a container that supports at minimum the following primitive operations:

$$\text{Insert}(Q, e, p) \quad | \quad \text{ExtractMin}(Q) \quad | \quad \text{Peek}(Q)$$

In the **min-priority queue** convention used most frequently in algorithms:

$$\forall\ e_1, e_2 \in Q : \text{priority}(e_1) \leq \text{priority}(e_2) \implies e_1\ \text{extracted before}\ e_2$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Triage Model

Imagine a hospital **Emergency Room (ER)**. Patients arrive continuously — a child with a broken arm, then a man with chest pain, then a pregnant woman, then a man bleeding from an arm wound. In a normal queue (FIFO), the first arrival is treated first. But in reality:

- The patient with **chest pain (cardiac arrest risk)** gets immediate treatment, even if they arrived 4th.
- The patient with a **broken arm** may have to wait, even if they arrived 1st.
- The patient's **medical severity = the priority key**.

This is exactly how a priority queue behaves. The data structure answers a single question at every "dequeue" event: *"Who deserves the resource next?"* — not *"Who came first?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Priority Queue is positioned as a foundational ADT that directly enables the analysis of **Dijkstra's Algorithm, Prim's MST, and Huffman Encoding** — all of which fall in Modules 3, 4, and 5 of PERAT751.

## 1.3 Why an ADT and Not a Concrete Structure?

A priority queue is a **behavioural contract**, not a specific storage layout. Multiple underlying data structures can satisfy this contract. The performance of these implementations differs drastically — a fact that forms one of the most heavily tested areas in KTU ESE papers for this module.

## 1.4 Common Variants of Priority Queue

| Variant | Ordering Principle | Common Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Min-Priority Queue** | Smallest key extracted first | Dijkstra's SSSP, Prim's MST |
| **Max-Priority Queue** | Largest key extracted first | Max-heap sort, priority schedulers |
| **Double-Ended PQ (DEPQ)** | Both min and max retrievable | Sliding window median problems |
| **Indexed PQ** | Keys mapped to external indices | Dijkstra with vertex relaxation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Components of a Priority Queue

A priority queue implementation must efficiently support the following logical operations. The **"dominant cost"** column is the one most frequently asked in KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 Core Operations

- **Insert(Q, x, k)** — Adds element $x$ with priority $k$ into the queue $Q$. The structural invariants of the underlying container must be maintained.
- **ExtractMin(Q)** / **ExtractMax(Q)** — Removes and returns the element with the highest priority. Triggers **re-heapification** (or list reorganization) to restore the invariant.
- **Peek(Q)** / **FindMin(Q)** — Returns the element with the highest priority **without** removing it. Must be $O(1)$.
- **DecreaseKey(Q, x, k')** — Reduces the key of element $x$ to a new value $k'$ (with $k' \leq k$). Used in Dijkstra's and Prim's algorithms.
- **IncreaseKey(Q, x, k')** — Increases the key of element $x$.
- **Delete(Q, x)** — Removes an arbitrary element $x$ from the queue.
- **Size(Q)** / **isEmpty(Q)** — Standard container introspection.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Operation

- **Why $O(1)$ Peek?** The invariant of the container (e.g., the root of a heap, the head of a sorted list) always holds the highest-priority element at a fixed, known location.
- **Why $O(\log n)$ Insert/Extract?** After mutation, the **heap property** (or list order) must be restored, and a binary tree of height $\log n$ requires at most $\log n$ comparison-swap steps.
- **Why is DecreaseKey needed in graph algorithms?** When a shorter path to a vertex is discovered, that vertex's priority in the priority queue must be lowered to reflect the new distance — without re-inserting the vertex.

## 2.2 Implementations & Comparative Time Complexity

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to **justify the choice of implementation** for a given application. The table below is the single most important comparative chart for this topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common question in Part A is: *"Why is a binary heap preferred over a sorted array for implementing a priority queue?"* — The answer is in the **asymptotic behaviour** of the `Insert` operation: $O(\log n)$ vs. $O(n)$.

### 2.2.1 Implementation Cost Matrix

| Underlying Structure | Insert | Extract-Min | Peek | Decrease-Key | Build (from $n$ items) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unsorted Array | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | Linear scan for extract |
| Sorted Array | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Shift overhead on insert |
| Unsorted Linked List | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | Simple, but slow extract |
| Sorted Linked List | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Pointer overhead |
| **Binary Heap** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | **Best general-purpose choice** |
| Binomial Heap | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Supports efficient merge $O(\log n)$ |
| Fibonacci Heap | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(n)$ | Used in advanced Dijkstra |
| BST (balanced) | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ worst case if unbalanced |

## 2.3 The Binary Heap — KTU's Favourite Implementation

The **Binary Heap** is the de facto implementation of priority queues in the KTU syllabus. It is a **complete binary tree** (all levels filled except possibly the last, which is filled left-to-right) that satisfies the **heap property**.

### 2.3.1 Heap Property

- **Min-Heap Property:** $A[\text{parent}(i)] \leq A[i]$ for every node $i > 0$. The root is the **global minimum**.
- **Max-Heap Property:** $A[\text{parent}(i)] \geq A[i]$ for every node $i > 0$. The root is the **global maximum**.

### 2.3.2 Array Index Relationships (Zero-Indexed)

For a node stored at index $i$ in a zero-indexed array:

$$\text{parent}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i-1}{2} \right\rfloor$$

$$\text{leftChild}(i) = 2i + 1$$

$$\text{rightChild}(i) = 2i + 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In 1-indexed arrays (often used in textbook pseudocode), the relationships are: $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$, $\text{left}(i) = 2i$, $\text{right}(i) = 2i+1$. **Always clarify the indexing convention** in your KTU answer script to avoid losing marks.

### 2.3.3 Key Formulas — Quick Reference Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Height of heap on $n$ nodes | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Height in edges |
| Number of leaves | $\lceil n/2 \rceil$ | In a complete binary tree |
| Build-Heap time complexity | $O(n)$ | Tight bound, not $O(n \log n)$ |
| Heap-Insert time | $O(\log n)$ | Worst-case bubble-up |
| Heap-Extract-Min time | $O(\log n)$ | Worst-case sift-down |
| Maximum nodes at height $h$ | $\lceil n / 2^{h+1} \rceil$ | Used in Build-Heap proof |
| Sum of heights of all nodes | $O(n)$ | The crux of the Build-Heap bound |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

Priority queues are not an academic curiosity — they are foundational to numerous production-grade systems:

- **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling in the Linux kernel uses the **Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)** with a **red-black tree** (a form of balanced BST-based priority queue).
- **Network Routing:** Dijkstra's algorithm, used in **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** routing, requires a priority queue keyed by tentative path length.
- **Data Compression:** **Huffman coding** builds an optimal prefix-free binary tree using a min-priority queue on character frequencies.
- **Event-Driven Simulation:** Discrete-event simulators (e.g., VLSI design, network packet simulation) process events in time order via a priority queue.
- **AI / Search:** The **A\* search algorithm** for pathfinding and puzzle solving is a generalized Dijkstra that uses a priority queue keyed on $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive mathematical derivations that KTU examiners frequently test. Every transition is shown step-by-step.

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Build-Heap Runs in $O(n)$ and Not $O(n \log n)$

This is the **single most important derivation** in the priority-queue module — and a recurring question in KTU 2024 Scheme papers.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions

Consider a complete binary heap with $n$ elements, stored as an array. We apply the **Heapify** (sift-down) operation to all internal nodes, starting from the last internal node $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1$ down to the root $0$.

**Heapify** at a node takes $O(h)$ time, where $h$ is the height of the node in the tree.

### 3.1.2 Counting the Total Work

The total running time $T(n)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \left( \text{Number of nodes at height } h \right) \times O(h) \\
&= \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right\rceil \cdot O(h)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The number of nodes in a heap at height $h$ is bounded above by $n / 2^{h+1}$. The work to heapify a subtree of height $h$ is proportional to $h$ (we walk down the path to a leaf).

### 3.1.3 Dropping the Ceiling for Asymptotic Analysis

Since the ceiling is at most a factor of 2, dropping it for upper-bound analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &\leq \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \cdot O(h) \\
&= O\!\left( n \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \frac{h}{2^{h+1}} \right) \\
&= O\!\left( n \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \frac{h}{2^{h+1}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The $O(h)$ factor is bounded by a constant multiple of $h$, so we factor it out as $O(h) = c \cdot h$ for some constant $c$.

### 3.1.4 Bounding the Infinite Series

We now use the well-known identity for the sum of $h \cdot 2^{-h}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \frac{h}{2^{h+1}} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \frac{h}{2^h} \\
&< \frac{1}{2} \sum_{h=0}^{\infty} \frac{h}{2^h} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The series $\sum_{h=0}^{\infty} h x^h = x/(1-x)^2$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$. Plugging in $x = 1/2$ gives $\sum h/2^h = (1/2)/(1/2)^2 = 2$. Since the partial sum is less than the infinite sum, we get a strict bound of 1 after the factor of 1/2.

### 3.1.5 Final Bound

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &\leq O(n \cdot 1) \\
T(n) &= O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The total work for Build-Heap is bounded by a constant times $n$, which is $O(n)$. This is why the naive intuition of "$n$ heapify operations, each costing $O(\log n)$, giving $O(n \log n)$" is **overly pessimistic** — most nodes are near the leaves and cost very little to heapify.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Only the root is at height $\log n$; the vast majority of nodes are at height 0 or 1 and cost $O(1)$ to heapify. The geometric decay of node count with height dominates the linear growth of work per node.

## 3.2 Derivation: Heap-Insert Runs in $O(\log n)$

**Procedure:** Add the new element at the next available position (to maintain completeness), then **bubble up** (also called "sift-up" or "percolate up") by repeatedly comparing with the parent and swapping if the heap property is violated.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work per level} &= O(1) \quad (\text{one comparison, possible swap}) \\
\text{Number of levels traversed} &= h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor \\
\text{Total work} &= O(1) \cdot h = O(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The inserted element can travel at most from a leaf to the root — a path of length at most the height of the tree, which is $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Heap-Extract-Min Runs in $O(\log n)$

**Procedure:** Remove the root (the min), move the **last element** of the array to the root, decrement the size, and **sift down** (heapify) the new root.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work per level of sift-down} &= O(1) \\
\text{Path length to a leaf} &= h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor \\
\text{Total work} &= O(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The new root may need to descend to the bottom of the tree, traversing at most $\log n$ levels.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Build a Max-Heap

**Input Array:** $A = [4, 10, 3, 5, 1, 8, 7, 6]$ (indexed 0 to 7)

We apply Heapify starting from index $\lfloor 8/2 \rfloor - 1 = 3$ down to index 0.

### Step 1: Heapify at index 3 (value 5)

- Children: left $= 2(3)+1 = 7$ (value 6), right $= 8$ (out of bounds)
- Largest among $\{5, 6\}$ is 6 at index 7
- Swap $A[3]$ and $A[7]$: $A = [4, 10, 3, 6, 1, 8, 7, 5]$
- No further children, stop.

### Step 2: Heapify at index 2 (value 3)

- Children: left $= 5$ (value 8), right $= 6$ (value 7)
- Largest among $\{3, 8, 7\}$ is 8 at index 5
- Swap $A[2]$ and $A[5]$: $A = [4, 10, 8, 6, 1, 3, 7, 5]$
- Children of index 5: left $= 11$ (out of bounds). Stop.

### Step 3: Heapify at index 1 (value 10)

- Children: left $= 3$ (value 6), right $= 4$ (value 1)
- Largest among $\{10, 6, 1\}$ is 10 (already at root of this subtree). No swap.
- Stop.

### Step 4: Heapify at index 0 (value 4)

- Children: left $= 1$ (value 10), right $= 2$ (value 8)
- Largest among $\{4, 10, 8\}$ is 10 at index 1
- Swap $A[0]$ and $A[1]$: $A = [10, 4, 8, 6, 1, 3, 7, 5]$
- Now heapify at index 1 (value 4):
  - Children: left $= 3$ (value 6), right $= 4$ (value 1)
  - Largest among $\{4, 6, 1\}$ is 6 at index 3
  - Swap $A[1]$ and $A[3]$: $A = [10, 6, 8, 4, 1, 3, 7, 5]$
  - Children of index 3: left $= 7$ (value 5), right = out of bounds.
  - Largest among $\{4, 5\}$ is 5 at index 7
  - Swap $A[3]$ and $A[7]$: $A = [10, 6, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7, 4]$
  - Stop.

### Final Max-Heap (Array Form)

$$
A = [10, 6, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7, 4]
$$

**Verification of max-heap property:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A[0]=10 &\geq A[1]=6,\ A[2]=8 \quad \checkmark \\
A[1]=6 &\geq A[3]=5,\ A[4]=1 \quad \checkmark \\
A[2]=8 &\geq A[5]=3,\ A[6]=7 \quad \checkmark \\
A[3]=5 &\geq A[7]=4 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The total number of comparisons/swap steps in this Build-Heap was 8 across 4 heapify calls, demonstrating the $O(n)$ bound in action.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Binary Max-Heap Tree Structure

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the max-heap built from the example in Section 3.4, alongside the underlying array representation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node10["10"]
    node6["6"]
    node8["8"]
    node5["5"]
    node1["1"]
    node3["3"]
    node7["7"]
    node4["4"]

    node10 --- node6
    node10 --- node8
    node6 --- node5
    node6 --- node1
    node8 --- node3
    node8 --- node7
    node5 --- node4

    arr["Array: 10  6  8  5  1  3  7  4"]
    idx["Index:  0  1  2  3  4  5  6  7"]

    node10 -. "A[0]" .-> arr
    arr -. "Storage Layout" .-> idx
```

## 4.2 Priority Queue Operational Flow

The diagram below illustrates the **Extract-Min** and **Insert** operations on a min-priority queue, which is the dominant pattern in graph algorithms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Min-Priority Queue Q initialized as empty"]
    B["INSERT 5, 7, 3, 9, 1 with priorities 5, 7, 3, 9, 1"]
    C["Heap after inserts: 1 at root, 3, 5, 9, 7 as leaves"]
    D["EXTRACT-MIN called"]
    E["Remove root: 1 returned to caller"]
    F["Move last element 7 to root"]
    G["Sift-down 7: 7 swaps with 3 then with 5"]
    H["Heap after extract: 3 at root, 5, 7, 9"]
    I["Final structure"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Priority Queue in Dijkstra's Algorithm

This block diagram shows how a priority queue integrates into a typical graph shortest-path computation, as a preparation for Module 3 of PERAT751.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Stage"]
        G["Graph G with V vertices and E edges"]
        SRC["Source vertex S"]
    end

    subgraph Initialization["Initialization Phase"]
        DIST["Distance array: dist V equals infinity, dist S equals 0"]
        PQINIT["Priority Queue PQ initialized with all vertices keyed by dist"]
    end

    subgraph MainLoop["Main Processing Loop"]
        EXTRACT["EXTRACT-MIN from PQ to get vertex u with minimum tentative distance"]
        RELAX["For each neighbour v of u: if dist u plus w u v less than dist v then UPDATE"]
        DECREASE["DECREASE-KEY of v in PQ to new distance"]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Stage"]
        RESULT["Final shortest path distances from S to all vertices"]
    end

    G --> DIST
    SRC --> DIST
    DIST --> PQINIT
    PQINIT --> EXTRACT
    EXTRACT --> RELAX
    RELAX --> DECREASE
    DECREASE --> EXTRACT
    DECREASE --> RESULT
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Sorted Array vs. Binary Heap (Memory Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SortedArray["Sorted Array: insert O of n, extract O of 1"]
        SA1["Element at index 0 is the minimum"]
        SA2["Insert requires shifting all larger elements right"]
        SA3["Extraction removes index 0; remaining elements shift left"]
        SA1 --> SA2 --> SA3
    end

    subgraph BinaryHeap["Binary Heap: insert O log n, extract O log n"]
        BH1["Root index 0 is the minimum"]
        BH2["Insert: append at end, bubble up to restore heap property"]
        BH3["Extract: remove root, move last to root, sift down to restore heap property"]
        BH1 --> BH2
        BH1 --> BH3
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing heap trees, always include both the **tree form** and the **array form** in your answer, with explicit index labels. Examiners award partial credit for the array layout even if the tree diagram has minor errors.
