<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Priority Queue. State any two applications of a priority queue.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Priority Queue** is an abstract data type in which each element is associated with a priority, and the element with the highest priority is dequeued first regardless of its insertion order. The element ordering is governed by the priority key, not the temporal order of arrival.
>
> **Two Applications:**
>
> 1. **Dijkstra's Single-Source Shortest Path Algorithm** — Vertices are stored in a priority queue keyed by their current tentative shortest distance. The vertex with the smallest distance is extracted and processed in each iteration.
>
> 2. **Huffman Coding in Data Compression** — A min-priority queue is used to repeatedly merge the two lowest-frequency nodes to build an optimal prefix-free binary tree, achieving minimum expected code length.

*(Alternative valid applications: Prim's MST, A\* search, OS CPU scheduling, event-driven simulation, merge of $k$ sorted lists.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Compare the time complexities of Insert and Extract-Min operations for priority queue implemented using (i) unsorted array, (ii) sorted array, and (iii) binary heap.**

**Model Answer:**

| Implementation | Insert | Extract-Min |
|---|---|---|
| Unsorted Array | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ — requires linear scan to find the minimum |
| Sorted Array | $O(n)$ — requires shifting to maintain sorted order | $O(1)$ — minimum is at index 0 |
| Binary Heap | $O(\log n)$ — bubble-up from the new leaf | $O(\log n)$ — sift-down after moving last element to root |

**Conclusion:** The binary heap provides **balanced $O(\log n)$ performance** for both operations, making it the preferred general-purpose implementation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the different ways of implementing a priority queue. Compare their time complexities for the basic operations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A priority queue can be implemented using any of the following data structures, each with a different performance profile:

**1. Unsorted Array / Unsorted Linked List:**
   - Insert: $O(1)$ — append at the end.
   - Extract-Min: $O(n)$ — linear scan to find the minimum.
   - Peek: $O(n)$ — same linear scan if not precomputed.
   - Pros: Simple implementation, fast inserts.
   - Cons: Slow extracts make it unsuitable for algorithms that frequently remove the minimum.

**2. Sorted Array / Sorted Linked List:**
   - Insert: $O(n)$ — find the correct position via binary search (array) or linear scan (linked list), then shift elements.
   - Extract-Min: $O(1)$ — minimum is at the head.
   - Peek: $O(1)$.
   - Pros: Constant-time extraction.
   - Cons: Insertion cost is prohibitive for workloads with many inserts.

**3. Binary Heap (Array-based complete binary tree):**
   - Insert: $O(\log n)$ — append at the end and bubble up.
   - Extract-Min: $O(\log n)$ — move the last element to root and sift down.
   - Peek: $O(1)$ — root is the minimum.
   - Build-Heap: $O(n)$ — tighter than the naive $O(n \log n)$.
   - Pros: Balanced performance, simple array-based storage, cache-friendly.
   - Cons: Does not support efficient search for arbitrary keys.

**4. Balanced Binary Search Tree (e.g., AVL, Red-Black Tree):**
   - Insert: $O(\log n)$.
   - Extract-Min: $O(\log n)$ — find leftmost node, remove it, rebalance.
   - Peek: $O(\log n)$ — descend left from root.
   - Pros: Supports ordered traversal and predecessor/successor queries.
   - Cons: Slower constant factors than heaps; more complex code.

**5. Binomial Heap / Fibonacci Heap (Advanced):**
   - Provide efficient $O(\log n)$ merge operation.
   - Fibonacci Heap achieves $O(1)$ amortized Decrease-Key, used in the theoretical optimal version of Dijkstra's algorithm.
   - Not required at the KTU undergraduate level but mentioned for context.

**Conclusion:** The **binary heap** is the most commonly used implementation due to its $O(\log n)$ worst-case bounds for both Insert and Extract-Min, its $O(1)$ Peek, and its simple array-based storage with excellent cache locality.

---

**(b)** What is a binary heap? Explain the algorithm to build a max-heap from the array $[3, 19, 1, 14, 8, 11, 17, 6, 9, 15]$ with suitable diagrams. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Binary Heap Definition:**

A binary heap is a complete binary tree stored in an array that satisfies the **heap property**: for a max-heap, the key of every node is **greater than or equal to** the keys of its children. The minimum (or maximum, for max-heap) is always at the root, accessible in $O(1)$ time.

**Array Index Relationships (0-indexed):** $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$, $\text{left}(i) = 2i+1$, $\text{right}(i) = 2i+2$.

**Build-Max-Heap Algorithm:**

```
BuildMaxHeap(A, n):
    for i from floor(n/2) - 1 down to 0:
        MaxHeapify(A, n, i)

MaxHeapify(A, n, i):
    left = 2*i + 1
    right = 2*i + 2
    largest = i
    if left < n and A[left] > A[largest]: largest = left
    if right < n and A[right] > A[largest]: largest = right
    if largest != i:
        swap A[i] and A[largest]
        MaxHeapify(A, n, largest)
```

**Step-by-step Execution on $A = [3, 19, 1, 14, 8, 11, 17, 6, 9, 15]$:**

$n = 10$, start index $= \lfloor 10/2 \rfloor - 1 = 4$.

**i = 4 (value 8):** Children are indices 9 (value 15). Largest among {8, 15} is 15. Swap. Array becomes $[3, 19, 1, 14, 15, 11, 17, 6, 9, 8]$.

**i = 3 (value 14):** Children are indices 7 (6) and 8 (9). Largest is 14 (already at root of subtree). No swap.

**i = 2 (value 1):** Children are indices 5 (11) and 6 (17). Largest is 17. Swap. Array becomes $[3, 19, 17, 14, 15, 11, 1, 6, 9, 8]$. Recurse on index 6: no children. Stop.

**i = 1 (value 19):** Children are indices 3 (14) and 4 (15). Largest is 19 (already at root). No swap.

**i = 0 (value 3):** Children are indices 1 (19) and 2 (17). Largest is 19. Swap. Array becomes $[19, 3, 17, 14, 15, 11, 1, 6, 9, 8]$. Recurse on index 1:
   - Children: index 3 (14), index 4 (15). Largest is 15. Swap. Array becomes $[19, 15, 17, 14, 3, 11, 1, 6, 9, 8]$.
   - Recurse on index 4: Children: index 9 (8). Largest is 8. Largest $\neq$ index 4, but $8 < 3$, so largest is 3 (at index 4). Wait — recheck: at index 4 (value 3), child at index 9 is 8. $8 > 3$, so swap. Array becomes $[19, 15, 17, 14, 8, 11, 1, 6, 9, 3]$. No further children at index 9. Stop.

**Final Max-Heap Array:**

$$
A = [19, 15, 17, 14, 8, 11, 1, 6, 9, 3]
$$

**Tree Form (Final Heap):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    n19["19"]
    n15["15"]
    n17["17"]
    n14["14"]
    n8["8"]
    n11["11"]
    n1["1"]
    n6["6"]
    n9["9"]
    n3["3"]

    n19 --- n15
    n19 --- n17
    n15 --- n14
    n15 --- n8
    n17 --- n11
    n17 --- n1
    n14 --- n6
    n14 --- n9
    n8 --- n3
```

**Verification:** Every parent key is greater than its children: $19 > 15, 17$; $15 > 14, 8$; $17 > 11, 1$; $14 > 6, 9$; $8 > 3$. All heap properties are satisfied.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define Priority Queue ADT. List its operations and explain the working of Insert and Extract-Min operations using a binary heap. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A Priority Queue ADT is a container of elements, each associated with a priority (key), supporting the retrieval and removal of the element with the highest priority. The order of service is determined by priority, not by insertion time.

**Operations:**

| Operation | Description |
|---|---|
| `Insert(Q, x, k)` | Adds element $x$ with key $k$ to $Q$ |
| `ExtractMin(Q)` | Removes and returns the element with the smallest key |
| `FindMin(Q)` / `Peek(Q)` | Returns the element with the smallest key without removing it |
| `DecreaseKey(Q, x, k')` | Decreases the key of element $x$ to $k' < k$ |
| `Delete(Q, x)` | Removes an arbitrary element $x$ |
| `Size(Q)` | Returns the number of elements |
| `IsEmpty(Q)` | Returns true if $Q$ is empty |

**Insert using Binary Heap:**

1. Append the new element at the next available position at the end of the array (to maintain the complete-binary-tree property).
2. Increment the heap size.
3. **Bubble up** (percolate up): While the inserted element is not at the root and its key is smaller than its parent's key (for min-heap), swap it with its parent.
4. The number of swaps is bounded by the height of the tree, giving $O(\log n)$ time.

**Extract-Min using Binary Heap:**

1. Save the root element (the minimum) for return.
2. Move the **last element** of the array to the root position.
3. Decrement the heap size.
4. **Sift down** (heapify down): Repeatedly compare the new root with its two children; if a child is smaller, swap the root with the smaller child. Continue until the heap property is restored.
5. Each level of sift-down involves at most 2 comparisons, and the depth is $O(\log n)$, so the operation takes $O(\log n)$ time.

**Example Trace (Min-Heap):** Insert 5, 3, 8, 1 into an empty heap:

- After Insert 5: $[5]$
- After Insert 3: $[3, 5]$ (3 bubbles up past 5)
- After Insert 8: $[3, 5, 8]$
- After Insert 1: $[1, 3, 8, 5]$ (1 bubbles up past 3 to root)

Extract-Min returns 1, moves 5 to root, sift-down gives $[3, 5, 8]$.

---

**(b)** Explain the application of priority queue in Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem:** Given a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights and a source vertex $s$, find the shortest path from $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$.

**Role of the Priority Queue:**

Dijkstra's algorithm greedily selects the unvisited vertex with the **smallest tentative distance** from the source at each iteration. The priority queue stores all unvisited vertices, keyed by their current tentative distance. The operation required at each step is precisely the **Extract-Min** operation, followed by **Decrease-Key** when a shorter path to a neighbor is found.

**Algorithm (with priority queue):**

```
Dijkstra(G, w, s):
    for each vertex v in V:
        dist[v] = infinity
        prev[v] = undefined
    dist[s] = 0
    PQ = empty min-priority queue
    for each vertex v in V:
        PQ.Insert(v, dist[v])

    while not PQ.IsEmpty():
        u = PQ.ExtractMin()
        for each neighbor v of u:
            if v in PQ and dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v]:
                dist[v] = dist[u] + w(u,v)
                prev[v] = u
                PQ.DecreaseKey(v, dist[v])
```

**Complexity Analysis:**

With a **binary heap**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initialization} &= O(V) \text{ insertions} = O(V \log V) \\
\text{Main loop} &= O(V) \text{ Extract-Mins} = O(V \log V) \\
\text{Edge relaxation} &= O(E) \text{ Decrease-Keys} = O(E \log V) \\
\text{Total} &= O((V + E) \log V)
\end{aligned}
$$

With a **Fibonacci Heap** (advanced):

$$
T = O(V \log V + E)
$$

which is asymptotically optimal for sparse graphs where $E = o(V \log V)$.

**Why a priority queue is essential:** A naive linear search for the minimum-distance vertex in each iteration would yield $O(V^2)$ time. The priority queue reduces this to $O((V+E) \log V)$, a dramatic improvement for large sparse graphs.

**Concrete Example:** For a road network with $V = 10^5$ intersections and $E = 3 \times 10^5$ road segments, the priority-queue version runs in milliseconds, while the naive version takes hours.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Priority Queue Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing index conventions:** Mixing 0-indexed and 1-indexed parent/child formulas in the same answer. State the convention explicitly at the start.
> 2. **Incorrect Build-Heap direction:** Some students call Heapify from index 0 upward. The correct order is from the **last internal node** ($\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1$) **down to 0** (sift-down strategy).
> 3. **Stating $O(n \log n)$ for Build-Heap:** This is the loose bound. Examiners expect students to derive the **tight $O(n)$ bound** using the sum-over-heights argument (see Section 3.1).
> 4. **Forgetting heap property in Heap-Extract-Min:** After moving the last element to the root, you must sift down. Many students forget this and lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Confusing min-heap with max-heap:** Always verify which convention the question demands. The comparison operators in the heap property change accordingly.
> 6. **Writing Peek as $O(n)$:** Peek must be $O(1)$ — the root is always the min/max. Examiners dock full marks for this oversight.
> 7. **Skipping diagrams in heap construction questions:** A labelled tree diagram carries 2–3 marks. Skipping it almost guarantees significant mark loss.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Priority Queue)**
>
> - [x] **Priority Queue** = ADT where extraction order is by **priority key**, not by insertion order.
> - [x] Two main flavours: **Min-PQ** (smallest key first) and **Max-PQ** (largest key first).
> - [x] Core operations: `Insert`, `Extract-Min/Max`, `Peek`, `DecreaseKey`, `Delete`, `Size`, `IsEmpty`.
> - [x] **Binary Heap** is the standard implementation: complete binary tree stored in an array.
> - [x] **Min-Heap Property:** $A[\text{parent}(i)] \leq A[i]$; **Max-Heap Property:** $A[\text{parent}(i)] \geq A[i]$.
> - [x] **0-indexed array formulas:** $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$, $\text{left}(i) = 2i+1$, $\text{right}(i) = 2i+2$.
> - [x] **1-indexed array formulas:** $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$, $\text{left}(i) = 2i$, $\text{right}(i) = 2i+1$.
> - [x] **Time Complexities (Binary Heap):** Insert = $O(\log n)$, Extract-Min = $O(\log n)$, Peek = $O(1)$, Build-Heap = $O(n)$.
> - [x] **Unsorted array PQ:** Insert = $O(1)$, Extract-Min = $O(n)$ — fast inserts, slow extracts.
> - [x] **Sorted array PQ:** Insert = $O(n)$, Extract-Min = $O(1)$ — slow inserts, fast extracts.
> - [x] **Build-Heap derivation:** $T(n) = \sum \lceil n/2^{h+1} \rceil \cdot O(h) = O(n)$ using the series $\sum h/2^h = 2$.
> - [x] **Heap-Insert procedure:** Append at end → bubble up via parent swaps.
> - [x] **Heap-Extract-Min procedure:** Save root → move last element to root → sift down via child swaps.
> - [x] **Major applications:** Dijkstra's SSSP, Prim's MST, Huffman coding, A\* search, OS process scheduling, $k$-way merge, event simulation.
> - [x] **Dijkstra with binary heap:** $O((V + E) \log V)$. **With Fibonacci heap:** $O(V \log V + E)$.
> - [x] **Memory representation:** No pointers required — heap is stored in a contiguous array, giving excellent **cache locality**.
> - [x] **Key exam trick:** When asked to build a heap, always show the **array form** at each step **and** the final **tree diagram** with explicit index labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double-Ended Queue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Double-Ended Queue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double-Ended Queue (Deque / Deck):** A linear data structure that generalizes both the **Stack (LIFO)** and the **Queue (FIFO)** by allowing insertion and deletion of elements from **both ends** — the *front* and the *rear* — within a uniform set of operations. It is formally defined as an ordered collection of elements of the **same data type**, supporting a constant-time, $O(1)$, add and remove operation at each end.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the deque is classified under **Module 2: Linear Data Structures** and is treated as the natural extension of the singly-ended queue. The formal name is **Deque**, pronounced as *"deck"*, which is a portmanteau of *Double-Ended Queue*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Two-Way Escalator or a Tunnel:**
> Imagine a tunnel at a railway station where passengers can **enter from the left gate**, **exit from the right gate**, but the station manager is also allowed to let a passenger **enter from the right gate** and **exit from the left gate**. The tunnel itself is linear (people stand in a line), but the *operations* (entry/exit) are permitted on **either end**.
>
> Similarly, in a Deque:
> - **Front** ← Left end of the tunnel (passenger board/alight point)
> - **Rear** ← Right end of the tunnel (passenger board/alight point)
> - You can `insertFront()`, `insertRear()`, `deleteFront()`, or `deleteRear()` at will.

A simpler analogy: a **line of people in a movie queue** where the manager is allowed to politely let someone jump in at the **front** (VIP) or at the **back** (last-minute arrival), and similarly remove a person from either end. This is more flexible than a standard queue (FIFO) and a stack (LIFO).

## 1.3 Classification of Deques

KTU examiners frequently test the **two specialized variants** of a deque:

| # | Variant Name | Constraint | Real-World Usage |
|---|--------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Input-Restricted Deque** | Insertion is allowed at **only one end (rear)**; deletion is allowed at **both ends** | Useful in **palindrome-checking** algorithms where input must remain strictly linear but reading/cancellation happens both ways |
| 2 | **Output-Restricted Deque** | Deletion is allowed at **only one end (front)**; insertion is allowed at **both ends** | Useful in **steal-job scheduling** in parallel processing where tasks are added freely but consumed from a single end |

## 1.4 Abstract Data Type (ADT) Specification

The **Deque ADT** is formally defined by the following operations (as per the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 syllabus):

1. `createDeque()` → returns an empty deque
2. `insertFront(x)` → inserts element $x$ at the front
3. `insertRear(x)` → inserts element $x$ at the rear
4. `deleteFront()` → removes and returns the front element
5. `deleteRear()` → removes and returns the rear element
6. `getFront()` → returns (without removing) the front element
7. `getRear()` → returns (without removing) the rear element
8. `isEmpty()` → returns **true** if the deque is empty
9. `isFull()` → returns **true** if the deque is full (array implementation only)
10. `size()` → returns the current number of elements

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Operational Principle

A Deque, at its mathematical core, is a **sequence** of elements $D = \langle d_0, d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_{n-1} \rangle$ where:
- $d_0$ is the **front** element (logically first).
- $d_{n-1}$ is the **rear** element (logically last).
- The deque is **dynamic** — its size $n$ can grow or shrink up to a maximum capacity $N$.

The "why" of a deque is **algorithmic flexibility**: a problem that occasionally needs an LIFO behavior and at other times needs FIFO behavior can use a single deque, switching between insertion/deletion ends on the fly.

## 2.2 Implementation Strategies

The KTU 2024 syllabus demands mastery over **two implementations**:

### A. Array-Based (Circular Array) Implementation
Uses a **circular buffer** to avoid the wasted space of shifting elements. Two integer pointers, `front` and `rear`, navigate cyclically through indices $0$ to $N-1$ using **modular arithmetic**.

### B. Linked-List-Based Implementation
Uses a **doubly linked list**. Each node has two pointers (`prev` and `next`), and the deque maintains pointers to both the head (front) and the tail (rear) nodes. This version is **truly dynamic** (no overflow) at the cost of extra memory per node.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Circular Array)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU board exam questions on this topic. Memorize the boundary conditions.

| # | Operation / Condition | Formula / Logic | Time Complexity |
|---|-----------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Initialization** | `front = -1, rear = -1` | $O(1)$ |
| 2 | **IsEmpty** | `front == -1` | $O(1)$ |
| 3 | **IsFull** | `(rear + 1) mod N == front` | $O(1)$ |
| 4 | **InsertRear** | `rear = (rear + 1) mod N; arr[rear] = x` | $O(1)$ |
| 5 | **InsertFront** | `front = (front - 1 + N) mod N; arr[front] = x` | $O(1)$ |
| 6 | **DeleteFront** | `front = (front + 1) mod N` | $O(1)$ |
| 7 | **DeleteRear** | `rear = (rear - 1 + N) mod N` | $O(1)$ |
| 8 | **Size** | `(rear - front + N) mod N + 1` (when not empty) | $O(1)$ |
| 9 | **Single-element state** | When `front == rear`, the deque contains exactly **one** element | $O(1)$ |
| 10 | **Wrap-around** | Indices recycle from $N-1$ back to $0$ using mod operator | $O(1)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Point:** In a circular-array deque of capacity $N$, the **maximum number of stored elements is $N-1$**, *not* $N$. The condition `(rear + 1) mod N == front` uses the *one free slot* to distinguish "full" from "empty".

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> The deque is not merely a textbook concept. KTU examiners often award marks for mentioning real-world usage. The following are high-yield examples:

1. **A-Steal Scheduling Algorithm (Operating Systems):** In multiprocessor systems, each processor maintains a deque of ready threads. It pops new work from the **front**, but a *starving* processor can **steal** work from the **rear** of another's deque. This is the canonical application.
2. **Undo-Redo Functionality (Text/Graphic Editors):** The undo stack is one end, the redo stack is the other end of a single deque of commands.
3. **Palindrome Checking (Algorithms):** Compare front and rear characters while removing both; if everything matches, the string is a palindrome.
4. **Sliding-Window Maximum (Competitive Programming):** Monotonic deques solve this problem in $O(n)$ time.
5. **0-1 BFS in Graph Algorithms (Shortest Path):** Allows relaxation of edges of weight 0 and 1 in $O(V+E)$ time.
6. **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) Buffer in Hardware:** A two-port memory block (write to rear, read from front, but support reads from rear for flushing).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Circular-Array Deque Pointers

In this section, we mathematically derive the **pointer-update rules** of a circular-array deque. Let:

- $N$ = maximum capacity (number of slots) of the array.
- $f$ = current value of the `front` index.
- $r$ = current value of the `rear` index.
- All arithmetic is performed **modulo $N$** to ensure the indices always lie in $\{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$.

### 3.1.1 Why Modulo Arithmetic?

If the array has slots indexed $0, 1, \dots, N-1$, and `rear` is currently at slot $N-1$, then inserting a new element at the rear would ordinarily fail (array out of bounds). By treating the array as a **ring**, slot $N-1$'s "next" slot is slot $0$. The mathematical expression for this "next" is:

$$
\text{next}(i) = (i + 1) \bmod N
$$

Similarly, the "previous" slot is:

$$
\text{prev}(i) = (i - 1 + N) \bmod N
$$

The $+N$ term ensures the result is **non-negative** before the modulo operation, which is required by the mathematical definition of modulo for negative integers (e.g., in C, $-1 \bmod N$ should yield $N-1$, not $-1$).

### 3.1.2 IsFull Derivation

A deque is full when there is **no slot left** between `front` and `rear`. To distinguish a *full* state from an *empty* state, the convention is to **leave one slot empty**. Therefore:

$$
\text{IsFull} \iff (r + 1) \bmod N = f
$$

**Logical Justification:** If the slot immediately after `rear` (in circular order) coincides with `front`, then adding one more element would make `rear` catch up to `front` in the *full* state. Since the *empty* state also has a unique condition ($f = -1$ or a sentinel), the **one-slot-buffer convention** allows unambiguous detection.

### 3.1.3 IsEmpty Derivation

A deque is empty when there are no elements. The convention used here is to set both `front` and `rear` to $-1$ initially and after the last deletion:

$$
\text{IsEmpty} \iff f = -1
$$

**Alternative Convention (Size Counter):** Some implementations use a separate `count` variable initialized to $0$, and define `IsEmpty` as `count == 0`. This **removes the one-slot-buffer wastage**, allowing all $N$ slots to be filled. KTU examiners accept both.

### 3.1.4 InsertRear Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Check overflow} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{IsFull()} \\
\text{Step 2: First insertion special case} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{if } f = -1, \text{ set } f \gets 0 \\
\text{Step 3: Advance rear cyclically} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad r \gets (r + 1) \bmod N \\
\text{Step 4: Store element} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{arr}[r] \gets x
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 InsertFront Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Check overflow} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{IsFull()} \\
\text{Step 2: First insertion special case} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{if } f = -1, \text{ set } r \gets 0 \\
\text{Step 3: Retreat front cyclically} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad f \gets (f - 1 + N) \bmod N \\
\text{Step 4: Store element} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{arr}[f] \gets x
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 DeleteFront Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Check underflow} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{IsEmpty()} \\
\text{Step 2: Capture the front element} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad x \gets \text{arr}[f] \\
\text{Step 3: Single-element special case} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{if } f = r, \text{ set } f \gets -1, \, r \gets -1 \\
\text{Step 4: Otherwise, advance front} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad f \gets (f + 1) \bmod N
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.7 DeleteRear Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Check underflow} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{IsEmpty()} \\
\text{Step 2: Capture the rear element} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad x \gets \text{arr}[r] \\
\text{Step 3: Single-element special case} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{if } f = r, \text{ set } f \gets -1, \, r \gets -1 \\
\text{Step 4: Otherwise, retreat rear} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad r \gets (r - 1 + N) \bmod N
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU exams frequently include a **"trace the operations"** sub-question. The following is a model trace for a deque of capacity $N=5$, starting with `front = 1, rear = 1`. Operations: `insertRear(10), insertRear(20), insertFront(5), insertRear(30), deleteFront(), insertRear(40)`.

| Step | Operation | Front ($f$) | Rear ($r$) | IsFull? | Array State (indices 0..4) | Size |
|------|-----------|-------------|------------|---------|----------------------------|------|
| 0 | Initial | 1 | 1 | False | `[_, _, _, _, _]` | 1 (value 0 at idx 1) |
| 1 | `insertRear(10)` | 1 | 2 | False | `[_, 0, 10, _, _]` | 2 |
| 2 | `insertRear(20)` | 1 | 3 | False | `[_, 0, 10, 20, _]` | 3 |
| 3 | `insertFront(5)` | 0 | 3 | False | `[5, 0, 10, 20, _]` | 4 |
| 4 | `insertRear(30)` | 0 | 4 | False | `[5, 0, 10, 20, 30]` | 5 |
| 5 | `deleteFront()` | 1 | 4 | False | `[_, 0, 10, 20, 30]` | 4 |
| 6 | `insertRear(40)` | 1 | 0 | True (next-of-rear is 1=front) | `[40, 0, 10, 20, 30]` | 5 |

**Wrap-around Explanation for Step 6:** The next rear index after $4$ is $(4+1) \bmod 5 = 0$, which is the new `rear`. The condition $(r+1) \bmod N = (0+1) \bmod 5 = 1 = f$ correctly signals **IsFull = true**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** When the array is shown in linear form `[40, 0, 10, 20, 30]`, students wrongly assume the front is at index 0. The correct interpretation is to **always** read the array cyclically starting from `front`. The logical order is: $0 \to 10 \to 20 \to 30 \to 40$, traversing indices $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 0$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Circular Deque

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **circular-buffer architecture** with all four operations and the two pointer-update mechanisms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Slot 0] --> A1[Slot 1] --> A2[Slot 2] --> A3[Slot 3] --> A4[Slot 4]
    A4 -.wrap around.-> A0
    A1 --- P1((front pointer f=1))
    A4 --- P2((rear pointer r=4))
    OP1([insertFront x]) --> MODF["f = f-1+N mod N"]
    MODF --> A1
    OP2([insertRear x]) --> MODR["r = r+1 mod N"]
    MODR --> A4
    OP3([deleteFront x]) --> MODF2["f = f+1 mod N"]
    MODF2 --> A1
    OP4([deleteRear x]) --> MODR2["r = r-1+N mod N"]
    MODR2 --> A4
```

## 4.2 Deque Variant Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D([Deque]) --> B{Which ends are restricted?}
    B -- Insertion restricted to rear --> IRD[Input-Restricted Deque]
    B -- Deletion restricted to front --> ORD[Output-Restricted Deque]
    B -- No restrictions --> GD[General Purpose Deque]
    IRD --> I1[insertRear only]
    IRD --> I2[deleteFront and deleteRear]
    ORD --> O1[insertFront and insertRear]
    ORD --> O2[deleteFront only]
    GD --> G1[All four operations allowed]
```

## 4.3 Doubly Linked List Implementation Blueprint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD((HEAD pointer)) --> N1[Node A: data=10, prev=NULL]
    N1 --> N2[Node B: data=20, prev=Node A]
    N2 --> N3[Node C: data=30, prev=Node B]
    N3 --> N4[Node D: data=40, prev=Node C]
    N4 --> NULLX((NULL))
    N1 <--> N2
    N2 <--> N3
    N3 <--> N4
    TAIL((TAIL pointer))
    TAIL -. points to .-> N4
```

**Architectural Notes (Linked-List Deque):**
- The `HEAD` pointer enables $O(1)$ `insertFront` and `deleteFront`.
- The `TAIL` pointer enables $O(1)$ `insertRear` and `deleteRear`.
- Bidirectional links (`prev` and `next`) are essential for `deleteRear` to update the previous node in $O(1)$ time.
- Memory overhead: each node carries **two extra pointers** in addition to the data payload.

## 4.4 Operation-Complexity Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q([Deque Operation Set]) --> Q1[insertFront: O 1]
    Q --> Q2[insertRear: O 1]
    Q --> Q3[deleteFront: O 1]
    Q --> Q4[deleteRear: O 1]
    Q --> Q5[getFront: O 1]
    Q --> Q6[getRear: O 1]
    Q --> Q7[isEmpty: O 1]
    Q --> Q8[isFull: O 1]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All** basic deque operations run in **$O(1)$ constant time** in **both** array-based (circular) and linked-list-based implementations. This is precisely why the deque is preferred over a vector/array-list for problems that need two-end access (e.g., sliding window, 0-1 BFS).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions are short-answer questions, expected to be answered in **3 to 4 lines**. They target the **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define a Double-Ended Queue (Deque). List any four operations supported by a deque.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Double-Ended Queue (Deque)** is a linear data structure that allows insertion and deletion of elements from **both the front and rear ends**. It generalizes the stack and the queue, supporting both LIFO and FIFO access patterns within the same structure.

Four operations are:
1. `insertFront(x)` — adds $x$ at the front end.
2. `insertRear(x)` — adds $x$ at the rear end.
3. `deleteFront()` — removes and returns the front element.
4. `deleteRear()` — removes and returns the rear element.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between an Input-Restricted Deque and an Output-Restricted Deque. Give one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Input-Restricted Deque | Output-Restricted Deque |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Insertion | Only at **rear** | At **both** front and rear |
| Deletion | At **both** front and rear | Only at **front** |
| Application | **Palindrome checking** | **Work-stealing scheduling in multiprocessor systems** |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B questions are **long-answer**, carrying **14 marks** split typically as **7 + 7** (sub-parts a and b). KTU follows a strict internal-choice pattern — answer either **Question A or Question B**. Both are provided below for full practice coverage.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Write the algorithms (pseudocode) for `insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, and `deleteRear` in a **deque implemented using a circular array** of capacity $N$. Mention the **overflow** and **underflow** conditions clearly.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Trace the following sequence of operations on an empty circular-array deque of capacity $N=5$ (show `front`, `rear`, and the array state after each step):

`insertRear(15), insertFront(10), insertRear(20), deleteRear(), insertFront(5), insertRear(25), deleteFront(), insertRear(30)`

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### (a) Pseudocode (7 Marks)

```
Algorithm: Circular-Array Deque Operations
Variables: arr[0..N-1], front = -1, rear = -1

1. Algorithm insertFront(x):
   IF (front == -1) THEN              // Deque was empty
       front = 0; rear = 0;
       arr[front] = x;
   ELSE IF ((rear + 1) mod N == front) THEN
       PRINT "Overflow"; RETURN;
   ELSE
       front = (front - 1 + N) mod N;
       arr[front] = x;
   END IF

2. Algorithm insertRear(x):
   IF (front == -1) THEN
       front = 0; rear = 0;
       arr[rear] = x;
   ELSE IF ((rear + 1) mod N == front) THEN
       PRINT "Overflow"; RETURN;
   ELSE
       rear = (rear + 1) mod N;
       arr[rear] = x;
   END IF

3. Algorithm deleteFront():
   IF (front == -1) THEN
       PRINT "Underflow"; RETURN;
   END IF
   x = arr[front];
   IF (front == rear) THEN            // Single element
       front = -1; rear = -1;
   ELSE
       front = (front + 1) mod N;
   END IF
   RETURN x;

4. Algorithm deleteRear():
   IF (front == -1) THEN
       PRINT "Underflow"; RETURN;
   END IF
   x = arr[rear];
   IF (front == rear) THEN            // Single element
       front = -1; rear = -1;
   ELSE
       rear = (rear - 1 + N) mod N;
   END IF
   RETURN x;
```

**Overflow Condition:** $(rear + 1) \bmod N == front$
**Underflow Condition:** $front == -1$

##### (b) Trace Table (7 Marks)

| Step | Operation | `front` | `rear` | Array `[0][1][2][3][4]` | Logical Order (front → rear) |
|------|-----------|---------|--------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 0 | Initial | $-1$ | $-1$ | `[_][_][_][_][_]` | Empty |
| 1 | `insertRear(15)` | 0 | 0 | `[15][_][_][_][_]` | 15 |
| 2 | `insertFront(10)` | 4 | 0 | `[15][_][_][_][10]` | 10 → 15 |
| 3 | `insertRear(20)` | 4 | 1 | `[15][20][_][_][10]` | 10 → 15 → 20 |
| 4 | `deleteRear()` | 4 | 0 | `[15][_][_][_][10]` | 10 → 15 |
| 5 | `insertFront(5)` | 3 | 0 | `[15][_][_][5][10]` | 5 → 10 → 15 |
| 6 | `insertRear(25)` | 3 | 1 | `[15][25][_][5][10]` | 5 → 10 → 15 → 25 |
| 7 | `deleteFront()` | 4 | 1 | `[15][25][_][_][10]` | 10 → 15 → 25 |
| 8 | `insertRear(30)` | 4 | 2 | `[15][25][30][_][10]` | 10 → 15 → 25 → 30 |

**Step-by-Step Justification:**

- **Step 1:** Empty deque, `insertRear` sets `front = rear = 0`, stores 15.
- **Step 2:** `front = (0 - 1 + 5) mod 5 = 4`. Stores 10 at index 4.
- **Step 3:** `rear = (0 + 1) mod 5 = 1`. Stores 20 at index 1.
- **Step 4:** `rear = (1 - 1 + 5) mod 5 = 0`. Element at index 1 (value 20) is removed.
- **Step 5:** `front = (4 - 1 + 5) mod 5 = 3`. Stores 5 at index 3.
- **Step 6:** `rear = (0 + 1) mod 5 = 1`. Stores 25 at index 1.
- **Step 7:** `front = (3 + 1) mod 5 = 4`. Element at index 3 (value 5) is removed.
- **Step 8:** `rear = (1 + 1) mod 5 = 2`. Stores 30 at index 2.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the **doubly linked list implementation** of a deque. Write the algorithm for `insertRear` and `deleteFront` operations, and discuss its advantages over the array-based implementation.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Using a deque, write the **algorithm to check whether a given string is a palindrome**. Trace your algorithm on the input string `"M A D A M"` (characters with spaces ignored).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### (a) Doubly Linked List Deque (7 Marks)

**Structure of a Node:**

```
Node {
    data: integer
    next: pointer to Node
    prev: pointer to Node
}
```

**Global Pointers Maintained:**
- `HEAD` → points to the front-most node (for `insertFront` and `deleteFront`).
- `TAIL` → points to the rear-most node (for `insertRear` and `deleteRear`).
- `count` → optional counter of the number of nodes.

**Algorithm for `insertRear(x)`:**
```
1. Create a new node p with data = x, next = NULL.
2. IF HEAD = NULL THEN
       HEAD = p; TAIL = p;
   ELSE
       p.prev = TAIL;
       TAIL.next = p;
       TAIL = p;
   END IF
3. Increment count.
```

**Algorithm for `deleteFront()`:**
```
1. IF HEAD = NULL THEN PRINT "Underflow"; RETURN.
2. x = HEAD.data;
3. IF HEAD = TAIL THEN        // Single element
       HEAD = NULL; TAIL = NULL;
   ELSE
       HEAD = HEAD.next;
       HEAD.prev = NULL;
   END IF
4. Decrement count; RETURN x.
```

**Advantages over array-based implementation:**
1. **Truly dynamic size** — no overflow except due to memory exhaustion.
2. **No wasted slot** — unlike the circular array which loses 1 slot for distinguishing full from empty.
3. **$O(1)$ insert/delete at both ends** with no pointer arithmetic overhead.
4. **No resizing** required when capacity is reached.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Extra memory** per node due to `prev` and `next` pointers.
2. **No random access** — to reach an element, traversal from HEAD or TAIL is needed.
3. **Cache-unfriendly** — nodes are scattered in memory.

##### (b) Palindrome-Checking Algorithm (7 Marks)

**Algorithm: `isPalindrome(s)`**
```
1. Initialize an empty deque D.
2. FOR each character c in string s DO
       IF c is alphanumeric THEN
           D.insertRear(tolower(c));
       END IF
   END FOR
3. WHILE D.size() > 1 DO
       IF D.getFront() != D.getRear() THEN
           RETURN false;
       END IF
       D.deleteFront();
       D.deleteRear();
   END WHILE
4. RETURN true.
```

**Trace on `"MADAM"`:**

| Step | Operation | Deque (front → rear) | Comparison | Decision |
|------|-----------|----------------------|------------|----------|
| 0 | Insert M, A, D, A, M | `M A D A M` | — | Build |
| 1 | getFront=M, getRear=M, delete both | `A D A` | M == M ✓ | Continue |
| 2 | getFront=A, getRear=A, delete both | `D` | A == A ✓ | Continue |
| 3 | size = 1, exit loop | `D` | — | **PALINDROME** |

**Final Result:** `"MADAM"` is a **palindrome**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgot the `+N` term in modular arithmetic.** Writing `front = (front - 1) mod N` (without `+N`) causes **negative indices** in C/C++ implementations. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this omission.
> 2. **Confusing empty and full conditions.** If you use a separate `count` variable, your `IsEmpty` and `IsFull` conditions change. State the convention explicitly at the start of the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the single-element special case** (`front == rear`) in `deleteFront` / `deleteRear` results in dangling pointers or wrong pointer updates — **1 to 2 marks lost**.
> 4. **Writing pseudocode with syntax errors** in pointer manipulation (e.g., `p->next = p; p->next = HEAD`) — KTU deducts **1 mark** for sloppy pointer logic.
> 5. **Not drawing the array in circular form** during the trace — KTU expects a clear, tabular representation with modular arithmetic demonstrated step-by-step.
> 6. **Mixing up the roles of `insertFront` and `insertRear`** in palindrome problems — remember: a palindrome reader uses a **deque** because it can read from both ends simultaneously.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- ☐ A **Deque** is a generalized queue allowing **insertion and deletion at both ends** in $O(1)$ time.
- ☐ A deque **generalizes both stack and queue** — set appropriate ends to mimic either.
- ☐ Two specialized variants: **Input-Restricted** (insert only at rear) and **Output-Restricted** (delete only at front).
- ☐ The **Circular Array** implementation uses **two pointers** `front` and `rear` with **modular arithmetic** (modulo $N$).
- ☐ The `+N` term in `front = (front - 1 + N) mod N` and `rear = (rear - 1 + N) mod N` is **mandatory** to avoid negative indices.
- ☐ The **IsFull** condition is `(rear + 1) mod N == front`, and the **IsEmpty** condition is `front == -1`.
- ☐ At most $N-1$ elements can be stored in an $N$-slot circular deque (1 slot is reserved as a sentinel).
- ☐ The **single-element case** (`front == rear`) requires **special handling** during deletion to reset both pointers to $-1$.
- ☐ A **Doubly Linked List** is the alternative implementation; it allows $O(1)$ at both ends with no overflow, at the cost of two extra pointers per node.
- ☐ **Key applications:** work-stealing scheduling, palindrome checking, undo-redo, sliding-window maximum, 0-1 BFS, $A^{\*}$ search open sets.
- ☐ Every basic deque operation — `insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, `deleteRear`, `getFront`, `getRear`, `isEmpty`, `isFull` — runs in **$O(1)$ constant time**.
- ☐ In exam traces, **always** show the **modular arithmetic calculation** explicitly — KTU examiners award partial marks for this step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Queues, Notion of Linked Lists
Singly Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Singly Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Singly Linked List (SLL):** A *linear, dynamic* data structure consisting of a sequence of **nodes**, where each node stores two components: (i) a **data field** (the actual payload) and (ii) a **link/pointer field** containing the memory address of the *next* node in the sequence. Traversal is permitted **only in one direction** — from the *head* toward the *tail* — and the final node points to `NULL` (or `None`), signifying the end of the list.

Mathematically, a Singly Linked List can be represented as an ordered collection:

$$L = \{n_0, n_1, n_2, \dots, n_{k-1}\}$$

where each node $n_i$ is a structure:

$$n_i = (\text{data}_i,\ \text{next}_i)$$

with the pointer invariants:

$$
\text{next}_i = 
\begin{cases} 
\text{address}(n_{i+1}) & \text{if } i < k-1 \\
\text{NULL} & \text{if } i = k-1
\end{cases}
$$

---

## Conceptual Analogy — The Treasure Hunt

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a *treasure hunt* where each clue (node) tells you two things: **a fact (the treasure/value)** and **the location of the next clue (a single arrow pointing forward)**. You cannot jump backwards; you *must* walk from one clue to the next in the direction of the arrow. The last clue simply says *"the hunt ends here"* (i.e., points to `NULL`).

This analogy captures three crucial properties:

| Analogy Element | Linked List Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Treasure Hunt Sheet | Node (data + next pointer) |
| First Clue | Head pointer |
| Last Clue ("end") | Tail node's `next = NULL` |
| Forward Arrow | Unidirectional `next` link |
| Adding/Removing a clue | Insertion / Deletion |

The **head** is a special external pointer variable maintained by the program that holds the address of the *first* node. Without the head, the entire list becomes inaccessible — a fact that is heavily tested in KTU board examinations.

---

## Why Not Just Use an Array?

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** *"Arrays can do everything, why learn Linked Lists?"* — In KTU exams, students frequently fail to articulate *why* linked structures matter. Always remember:
> - Arrays need **contiguous memory**; SLL nodes can be **scattered** anywhere in the heap.
> - SLL allows **O(1) insertion/deletion** at the head without shifting elements.
> - SLL size is **dynamic** — no overflow (until heap memory itself is exhausted).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Node Structure (C-style)

The basic building block in KTU's preferred language (C) is:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;            // payload
    struct Node *next;   // address of successor
};
```

- `data` stores the user's value (could be `int`, `float`, `char`, or any struct).
- `next` is a self-referential pointer — a pointer to a `struct Node`.
- Size of one node ≈ **size_of(data) + size_of(pointer)** = e.g., **4 bytes + 8 bytes = 12 bytes** on a 64-bit system.

---

## Memory Layout vs. Array

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Board Point:** In arrays, elements occupy **consecutive memory addresses** (`base + i × sizeof(T)`). In a Singly Linked List, node addresses are **non-contiguous** — each node is independently allocated on the **heap** via `malloc()` / `calloc()` and connected only via pointers.

This is precisely why SLL excels at **dynamic resizing** but suffers at **random access**.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Complexity Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most important high-yield summary** for any KTU exam question on Singly Linked Lists. Memorize it.

| Operation | Time Complexity (Average) | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Access by index $i$ (k-th element) | $O(n)$ | Must traverse from head through $k-1$ nodes |
| Search for a value | $O(n)$ | Worst case: traverse entire list |
| Insertion at **head** | $O(1)$ | Just rewire `head` pointer |
| Insertion at **tail** (no tail ptr) | $O(n)$ | Must walk to last node |
| Insertion at **tail** (with tail ptr) | $O(1)$ | Tail pointer is updated directly |
| Insertion after given node $p$ | $O(1)$ | Only pointer rewiring |
| Deletion at **head** | $O(1)$ | Move head to `head->next`, free old head |
| Deletion at **tail** | $O(n)$ | Need predecessor to fix pointer |
| Deletion of given node $p$ | $O(n)$ | Need predecessor of $p$ |
| Traversal (visit all $n$ nodes) | $O(n)$ | Linear walk |

Memory overhead per node: **pointer overhead = 1 word = 8 bytes (on 64-bit)**.

---

## Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Singly Linked Lists form the **backbone of real-world systems** such as:
> - **Kernel process schedulers** (run-queues).
> - **LRU cache eviction** in operating systems.
> - **Symbol tables** in compiler design.
> - **Polynomial representation** (each term is a node).
> - **Adjacency lists** in Graph algorithms (BFS/DFS).
> - **Undo mechanisms** in editors with singly-linked history chains.

---

## Pointer Rewiring Rules (KTU Gold Standard)

For any pointer manipulation, follow this **3-step Golden Rule** every examiner checks:

1. **Save** the new connection (otherwise you lose the rest of the list).
2. **Rewire** the existing pointer.
3. **Free** the disconnected node (only for deletion).

A single missing step results in **memory leak** or **lost-node bugs** — a common 2–3 mark deduction in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Creation of a Singly Linked List (Head Insertion)

Let us build a list by inserting elements **10, 20, 30** at the head, in that order.

**Step 1 — Insert 10 (first node):**

Initially, `head = NULL`.

1. Allocate a new node `n1`: `n1->data = 10`, `n1->next = NULL`.
2. Set `head = n1`.

Result:
$$\text{head} \rightarrow [10 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

**Step 2 — Insert 20 at head:**

1. Allocate new node `n2`: `n2->data = 20`.
2. Save the current head: $\text{temp} = \text{head}$ (i.e., `n1`).
3. Point new node's next to old head: $\text{n2} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{temp}$.
4. Update head: $\text{head} = \text{n2}$.

$$\text{head} \rightarrow [20 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow [10 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

**Step 3 — Insert 30 at head:**

$$\text{head} \rightarrow [30 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow [20 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow [10 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the order:** Even though we inserted 10 first, the final list reads 30 → 20 → 10. **Head insertion always reverses the input order.**

---

## 3.2 Insertion at the Tail — Detailed Walkthrough

Let the current list be: `head → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`. Insert **40** at the tail.

**Step 1 — Allocate new node:**
$$\text{newNode} = [40 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 2 — Traverse to last node:**

Use a temporary pointer `temp` initialized to `head`. Advance while `temp->next != NULL`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1: } & \text{temp points to } [10 \mid \bullet],\ \text{temp->next} \neq \text{NULL} \\
\text{Iteration 2: } & \text{temp points to } [20 \mid \bullet],\ \text{temp->next} \neq \text{NULL} \\
\text{Iteration 3: } & \text{temp points to } [30 \mid \bullet],\ \text{temp->next} = \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \text{STOP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Link the new node:**

Set $\text{temp->next} = \text{newNode}$.

Final list:
$$\text{head} \rightarrow [10 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow [20 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow [30 \mid \bullet] \rightarrow [40 \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Time spent:** $O(n)$ — confirmed by traversal count.

---

## 3.3 Insertion After a Given Node (O(1) Operation)

Given a pointer `p` (which is **not** the tail), insert new node with value `X` after `p`.

**Step 1 — Allocate:** `newNode->data = X`.

**Step 2 — Save the successor of p** (otherwise we lose the rest of the chain):
$$\text{newNode->next} = \text{p->next}$$

**Step 3 — Rewire p's link:**
$$\text{p->next} = \text{newNode}$$

This is the **canonical KTU question** for "explain insertion in a linked list" and the **order of these two pointer assignments must NOT be swapped**, or the rest of the list is lost (a classic error students make).

---

## 3.4 Deletion of a Node — Pointer Rewiring Proof

Given `head → A → B → C → NULL`, delete node `B` (we are given pointer to `B` but need its predecessor).

**Step 1 — Find predecessor `A`:**
Traverse from head; stop when `temp->next == B`.

**Step 2 — Bypass B:**
$$\text{A->next} = \text{B->next}$$

After this step:
$$\text{head} \rightarrow [A \mid \bullet] \rightarrow [C \mid \text{NULL}]$$

**Step 3 — Free memory:**
$$\text{free(B)}$$

**Complexity:** Finding `A` takes $O(n)$; total deletion is therefore $O(n)$.

---

## 3.5 Traversal / Search Algorithm

To search for value $V$ in a list of $n$ nodes, define:

$$
\text{pos}(V) = 
\begin{cases}
i & \text{if } \text{data}_i = V \text{ found after traversing } i \text{ nodes} \\
-1 & \text{if no such node exists}
\end{cases}
$$

The recurrence for the worst-case number of comparisons:

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + 1,\quad T(1) = 1 \implies T(n) = n
$$

Hence **worst-case time complexity is $O(n)$**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation Classification

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is **frequently asked as a 7-mark question** in KTU: *"Compare the time complexities of various operations on arrays vs singly linked lists."*

| Operation | Array | Singly Linked List |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Random Access (i-th element) | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Insertion at Beginning | $O(n)$ (shift all) | $O(1)$ |
| Insertion at End | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(n)$ without tail / $O(1)$ with tail |
| Insertion in Middle | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ if pointer known + $O(n)$ to reach position |
| Deletion at Beginning | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion in Middle | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Memory | Contiguous, fixed | Scattered, dynamic |
| Extra Memory | None | 1 pointer per node |
| Cache Friendliness | High | Low (pointer chasing) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
