# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
*Define a Singly Linked List. Differentiate it from an array with respect to memory allocation and access time.*

**Model Answer:**

A *Singly Linked List* is a dynamic linear data structure where each element (called a **node**) contains a data field and a single pointer to the next node, with the last node pointing to `NULL`.

| Parameter | Array | Singly Linked List |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Memory | Contiguous | Non-contiguous (heap) |
| Access Time | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Size | Fixed at compile-time | Dynamic |

*(Marks distribution: 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation table.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** 
*What is the significance of the `head` pointer in a Singly Linked List? What happens if it is lost?*

**Model Answer:**

The `head` pointer stores the address of the first node of the list. It is the **only entry point** to traverse the list. If `head` is lost (e.g., overwritten without saving), all nodes become unreachable — leading to **memory leak** and total loss of the list, even though the memory is technically still allocated on the heap.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for entry-point significance, 1 for consequence.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** As per the 2024 scheme, you must attempt **ONE** of the two Part B questions provided below. Each sub-part is worth 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Write the algorithm (pseudocode) to **(i)** create a Singly Linked List by inserting $n$ integers at the *tail*, and **(ii)** display the list in order. Show the dry-run for the input sequence: `5, 10, 15, 20`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a diagram how you would insert a new node with value `25` *after* the node containing `20`. State the time complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Algorithm (Insertion at Tail + Display):**

```
INSERT_TAIL(head, value):
    newNode = (struct Node*) malloc(sizeof(struct Node))
    newNode->data = value
    newNode->next = NULL
    IF head == NULL:
        RETURN newNode
    temp = head
    WHILE temp->next != NULL:
        temp = temp->next
    temp->next = newNode
    RETURN head

DISPLAY(head):
    temp = head
    WHILE temp != NULL:
        PRINT temp->data
        temp = temp->next
```

**Dry Run for input 5, 10, 15, 20:**

| Step | Operation | List State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Init | `head = NULL` | (empty) |
| 1 | Insert 5 | `5 → NULL` |
| 2 | Insert 10 | `5 → 10 → NULL` |
| 3 | Insert 15 | `5 → 10 → 15 → NULL` |
| 4 | Insert 20 | `5 → 10 → 15 → 20 → NULL` |

#### Model Solution — (b)

The list before insertion: `head → 5 → 10 → 15 → 20 → NULL`.

**Step 1:** Locate the node with `data = 20`. Traverse from head — found after 4 hops.

**Step 2:** Create new node `n` with `n->data = 25`.

**Step 3:** Pointer rewiring (in this exact order):

```c
n->next = node20->next;   // n->next = NULL
node20->next = n;
```

**Final List:**
$$\text{head} \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow 15 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 25 \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

**Time Complexity:**
- To *locate* node 20: $O(n)$ where $n = 4$.
- To *insert* after it: $O(1)$.
- **Total: $O(n)$.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2022 | Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Write the algorithm to **delete a node** with a given value `KEY` from a Singly Linked List. Cover all three cases: deletion of head, middle node, and tail. Dry-run with list `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → 50` and `KEY = 30`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Singly Linked List with Doubly Linked List with respect to memory overhead and traversal capability. Mention one application where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

```
DELETE_KEY(head, KEY):
    IF head == NULL:
        PRINT "List Empty"; RETURN NULL

    // Case 1: Head deletion
    IF head->data == KEY:
        temp = head
        head = head->next
        free(temp)
        RETURN head

    // Case 2 & 3: Middle or Tail
    prev = head
    curr = head->next
    WHILE curr != NULL AND curr->data != KEY:
        prev = curr
        curr = curr->next

    IF curr == NULL:
        PRINT "Key Not Found"; RETURN head

    prev->next = curr->next   // bypass curr
    free(curr)
    RETURN head
```

**Dry Run for KEY = 30 in list `10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → 50`:**

| Step | prev | curr | curr->data | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Init | 10 | 20 | 20 | Move forward |
| 2 | 20 | 30 | **30** | Match! Stop |

Now execute: `prev->next = curr->next`, so `20->next = 40`. Free the node `30`.

**Final List:** `head → 10 → 20 → 40 → 50 → NULL` ✓

#### Model Solution — (b)

| Aspect | Singly Linked List | Doubly Linked List |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pointers per node | **1** (next) | **2** (prev + next) |
| Memory per node | Smaller | Larger (~16 bytes extra on 64-bit) |
| Forward traversal | Yes | Yes |
| Backward traversal | **No** | Yes |
| Deletion of given node | Needs predecessor (O(n)) | Direct (O(1)) if node pointer known |

**Applications:**
- **SLL Preferred:** LRU cache eviction list, polynomial representation, hash-map chaining — when memory is constrained and backward traversal is not needed.
- **DLL Preferred:** Browser history (back/forward navigation), undo-redo in editors, LRU cache where deletion must be O(1).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Deduction Points (verified from KTU valuation keys):**
> 1. **Swapped pointer order in insertion-after-node:** Writing `p->next = newNode` BEFORE `newNode->next = p->next` causes the rest of the list to be lost. Loss of **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Missing `free()` in deletion:** Examiners specifically look for memory deallocation. Skipping it loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not handling empty-list case:** If `head == NULL` and your algorithm dereferences `head->next`, you will lose **2 marks** for robustness.
> 4. **Forgetting the dry-run:** A dry-run with the given input is worth **at least 2 marks** out of the 7 in sub-part (a). Always show it explicitly.
> 5. **Writing `O(1)` for tail insertion without tail pointer:** Marked wrong. The correct answer is $O(n)$ unless a tail pointer is maintained.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** SLL is a linear, dynamic, unidirectional chain of nodes containing `data` + `next` pointer.
- **Last node:** `next = NULL` (mandatory).
- **Head:** Only entry point; losing it = losing the list.
- **Memory:** Nodes allocated on **heap** via `malloc`; addresses are non-contiguous.
- **Pointer Rule:** Always **save the next pointer first** before rewiring.
- **Complexity Highlights (most-tested):**
  - Head insert/delete → $O(1)$
  - Tail insert/delete (no tail ptr) → $O(n)$
  - Insert after given node → $O(1)$
  - Random access → $O(n)$
- **Dry-run:** KTU **always expects** a worked example for any algorithm in Part B.
- **Versus Array:** SLL wins on dynamic size and head insertion; loses on random access and cache performance.
- **Versus Doubly Linked List:** SLL is memory-efficient but cannot traverse backward.
- **Real-world Use:** Hash-map chaining, adjacency lists, polynomial representation, OS scheduling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doubly Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Doubly Linked List**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Doubly Linked List (DLL)** is a sequential, linear dynamic data structure composed of a collection of **nodes**, where each node contains three distinct fields:

1. **DATA field** — stores the actual element/value.
2. **PREV (Previous) pointer** — stores the memory address of the **immediately preceding** node.
3. **NEXT pointer** — stores the memory address of the **immediately succeeding** node.

Unlike its predecessor (the Singly Linked List), the Doubly Linked List permits **bidirectional traversal**, meaning the list can be navigated efficiently in **both forward and reverse directions** without needing auxiliary structures like stacks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A Doubly Linked List is a linked data structure in which each node contains two link fields (`prev` and `next`) referencing the previous and next nodes respectively, enabling O(1) deletion of a known node and bi-directional traversal. The first node's `prev` pointer and the last node's `next` pointer typically point to `NULL` (or `HEAD` in circular variants).

## 1.2 Structural Anatomy of a Node

```
+-----------+----------+-----------+
|  prev     |  data    |  next     |
|  pointer  |  field   |  pointer  |
+-----------+----------+-----------+
   (LEFT)               (RIGHT)
```

The **HEAD** pointer holds the address of the first node. The **TAIL** pointer (optional, often maintained for efficiency) holds the address of the last node.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🚂 Train Car Analogy:** Imagine a freight train where each railway wagon (node) is connected to the wagon in **front of it** AND the wagon **behind it**. If you are standing in any wagon, you can walk to the next wagon OR return to the previous wagon without walking back to the engine. This is exactly how a Doubly Linked List works — each node "knows" both its neighbors.

**Other real-world parallels:**
- **Browser Navigation History**: The *Back* and *Forward* buttons mirror the `prev` and `next` pointers of web pages you visit.
- **Music Playlist with Shuffle-Back**: Each song knows what came before and what comes after.
- **Undo/Redo Functionality in Editors**: Each state is doubly linked to allow bidirectional movement.

## 1.4 Memory Representation Summary

| Component | Size Contribution | Purpose |
|-----------|-------------------|---------|
| `data` | Depends on data type | Stores the actual value |
| `prev` pointer | Typically **8 bytes** (on 64-bit) | Points to previous node |
| `next` pointer | Typically **8 bytes** (on 64-bit) | Points to next node |
| Total per node | `sizeof(data) + 16 bytes` | Complete node footprint |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** The trade-off of a Doubly Linked List over a Singly Linked List is **extra memory for the `prev` pointer** in exchange for **bidirectional traversal** and **O(1) deletion** when a node pointer is already known.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

### A. Node Creation Logic
Every node in a DLL is created by allocating memory dynamically (using `malloc`/`new`), initializing the `prev` and `next` pointers, and assigning data.

### B. Core Operations — The "Why" and "How"

#### 🔹 Operation 1: Insertion at the Beginning (Head Insertion)
- **Why:** Add a new node so it becomes the new HEAD.
- **How:**
  1. Create new node `N`.
  2. Set `N->next = HEAD`.
  3. If `HEAD != NULL`, set `HEAD->prev = N`.
  4. Set `HEAD = N`.
  5. Set `N->prev = NULL`.

#### 🔹 Operation 2: Insertion at the End (Tail Insertion)
- **Why:** Append a node at the last position.
- **How:**
  1. Traverse to the last node `T` (or use TAIL pointer in O(1)).
  2. Create new node `N`.
  3. Set `N->prev = T`.
  4. Set `T->next = N`.
  5. Set `N->next = NULL`.

#### 🔹 Operation 3: Insertion After a Given Node
- **Why:** Insert `N` immediately after node `P`.
- **How:**
  1. Create `N`.
  2. Set `N->next = P->next`.
  3. Set `N->prev = P`.
  4. If `P->next != NULL`, set `P->next->prev = N`.
  5. Set `P->next = N`.

#### 🔹 Operation 4: Deletion of a Given Node
- **Why:** Remove node `D` from the list.
- **How:**
  1. If `D->prev != NULL`, set `D->prev->next = D->next`.
  2. If `D->next != NULL`, set `D->next->prev = D->prev`.
  3. Free memory of `D`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Observation:** In a **Singly Linked List**, deletion of a node with only its pointer given requires finding the **previous node** (O(n)). In a **Doubly Linked List**, the same operation is **O(1)** because the `prev` pointer is already available. This is a high-yield KTU exam point.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Time Complexity (DLL) | Time Complexity (SLL) | Space Complexity |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Insertion at HEAD | **O(1)** | O(1) | O(1) |
| Insertion at TAIL (with TAIL pointer) | **O(1)** | O(n) without TAIL | O(1) |
| Insertion after given node | **O(1)** | O(1) | O(1) |
| Deletion of a node (pointer known) | **O(1)** | O(n) | O(1) |
| Deletion by value | **O(n)** | O(n) | O(1) |
| Forward Traversal | **O(n)** | O(n) | O(1) |
| Backward Traversal | **O(n)** | Not directly possible | O(1) |
| Searching | **O(n)** | O(n) | O(1) |

## 2.3 Memory Footprint Formula

For a list containing **n** nodes:

$$
\text{Total Memory} = n \times (\text{sizeof(data)} + 2 \times \text{sizeof(pointer)})
$$

For a typical 64-bit system with `int` data (4 bytes) and pointers (8 bytes each):

$$
\text{Memory per node} = 4 + (2 \times 8) = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Total Memory for n nodes} = 20n \text{ bytes (excluding overhead)}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Applications of Doubly Linked Lists:**
> - **LRU Cache Implementation**: Used in operating systems and databases for cache replacement policies.
> - **Browser History (Back/Forward)**: As explained earlier.
> - **Undo/Redo in IDEs and Text Editors**: e.g., Microsoft Word, VS Code.
> - **Music Player Playlists**: Skip to previous/next track.
> - **Polynomial Arithmetic**: Each term doubly linked for efficient insertion/deletion.
> - **Hash Table Chaining**: Reduces collision resolution overhead.

## 2.5 Advantages vs. Disadvantages Matrix

| Property | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|----------|-----------|--------------|
| Memory | — | **Extra `prev` pointer** per node |
| Traversal | **Bidirectional** | More pointers to maintain |
| Deletion | **O(1)** with known pointer | Risk of **dangling pointers** if mishandled |
| Insertion | Efficient at any known position | **Reverse traversal** also O(n) for far nodes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Pointer Manipulation Trace — Insertion After a Node

### Initial State Setup

Consider the following Doubly Linked List:

```
NULL <- [10] <-> [20] <-> [30] -> NULL
```

Let `HEAD` point to the node containing 10. We want to insert **25 after node containing 20**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Let `P` = pointer to node containing 20.
**Step 2:** Create a new node `N` with data = 25. Initialize both pointers to `NULL`.

$$
N = \text{new node},\quad N\text{->data} = 25,\quad N\text{->prev} = \text{NULL},\quad N\text{->next} = \text{NULL}
$$

**Step 3:** Connect `N->next` to the node that originally followed `P` (i.e., node containing 30):

$$
N\text{->next} = P\text{->next}
$$

This means: if `P->next` was the 30-node, now `N->next` also points to the 30-node.

**Step 4:** Connect `N->prev` to `P`:

$$
N\text{->prev} = P
$$

**Step 5:** Update the `prev` pointer of the original next node (30-node) to point to `N`:

$$
\text{If } P\text{->next} \neq \text{NULL: } \quad P\text{->next->prev} = N
$$

**Step 6:** Update `P->next` to point to `N`:

$$
P\text{->next} = N
$$

### Final Resulting State

```
NULL <- [10] <-> [20] <-> [25] <-> [30] -> NULL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic Check:** The order of pointer reassignment is **CRITICAL**. If we update `P->next` before saving the original next node, we lose the reference to the 30-node, causing a **memory leak** and broken list.

## 3.2 Detailed Pointer Manipulation Trace — Deletion of a Node

### Initial State

```
NULL <- [10] <-> [20] <-> [25] <-> [30] -> NULL
```

We want to delete the node containing **25** (let's call it `D`).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Check if `D->prev` exists. If yes, bypass `D` from the left side:

$$
\text{If } D\text{->prev} \neq \text{NULL:} \quad D\text{->prev->next} = D\text{->next}
$$

In our case, `D->prev` is the 20-node, so:

$$
\text{[20] -> next} = \text{[30]}
$$

**Step 2:** Check if `D->next` exists. If yes, bypass `D` from the right side:

$$
\text{If } D\text{->next} \neq \text{NULL:} \quad D\text{->next->prev} = D\text{->prev}
$$

In our case, `D->next` is the 30-node, so:

$$
\text{[30] -> prev} = \text{[20]}
$$

**Step 3:** Free the memory of `D`:

$$
\text{free}(D)
$$

### Final State

```
NULL <- [10] <-> [20] <-> [30] -> NULL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why O(1)?** Because we never needed to traverse the list to find the previous node — `D->prev` already gave us direct access. This is the **defining advantage** of DLLs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Edge Case Handling

| Scenario | HEAD Update Needed? | TAIL Update Needed? | Special Consideration |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Insert at empty list | **Yes** (HEAD = N) | **Yes** (TAIL = N) | Both `prev` and `next` are NULL |
| Insert at HEAD | **Yes** | No | Old HEAD's `prev` becomes N |
| Insert at TAIL | No | **Yes** | New node's `next` = NULL |
| Delete HEAD | **Yes** (HEAD = HEAD->next) | No | New HEAD's `prev` = NULL |
| Delete TAIL | No | **Yes** (TAIL = TAIL->prev) | New TAIL's `next` = NULL |
| Delete middle node | No | No | Both sides must be linked |
| Delete single node | **Yes** (HEAD = NULL) | **Yes** (TAIL = NULL) | Edge case of empty list |

## 3.4 Reverse Traversal Logic Derivation

To print the list in **reverse order** starting from TAIL:

$$
\text{Initialize: } \quad \text{current} = \text{TAIL}
$$

$$
\text{While } \text{current} \neq \text{NULL:} \quad
\begin{cases}
\text{print current->data} \\
\text{current} = \text{current->prev}
\end{cases}
$$

This loop runs exactly **n** times for a list of **n** nodes, confirming **O(n)** complexity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Node Structure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph nodeA["NODE STRUCTURE"]
        A1["prev pointer"]
        A2["data field"]
        A3["next pointer"]
        A1 --> A2
        A2 --> A3
    end
```

## 4.2 Doubly Linked List Architecture (3 Nodes)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["HEAD"]
    N1["NULL"]
    D1["prev | 10 | next"]
    D2["prev | 20 | next"]
    D3["prev | 30 | next"]
    N2["NULL"]
    H --> D1
    D1 <--> D2
    D2 <--> D3
    D3 --> N2
    N1 --> D1
```

## 4.3 Insertion Operation Flow Diagram (Insert After Node)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Create new node N"]
    B["Set N to next equals P to next"]
    C["Set N to prev equals P"]
    D{"P to next is NOT NULL?"}
    E["Set P to next to prev equals N"]
    F["Skip prev update"]
    G["Set P to next equals N"]
    H["End: Insertion Complete"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E
    D -- No --> F
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H
```

## 4.4 Deletion Operation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Node D to be deleted"]
    B{"D to prev is NOT NULL?"}
    C["D to prev to next equals D to next"]
    D["Skip left linking"]
    E{"D to next is NOT NULL?"}
    F["D to next to prev equals D to prev"]
    G["Skip right linking"]
    H["Free memory of D"]
    I["End: Deletion Complete"]

    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> C
    B -- No --> D
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F
    E -- No --> G
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

## 4.5 Memory Layout Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph mem1["ADDRESS 1000"]
        V1["Node1: prev=NULL, data=10, next=2000"]
    end
    subgraph mem2["ADDRESS 2000"]
        V2["Node2: prev=1000, data=20, next=3000"]
    end
    subgraph mem3["ADDRESS 3000"]
        V3["Node3: prev=2000, data=30, next=NULL"]
    end
    HEAD["HEAD pointer = 1000"]
    HEAD --> mem1
    mem1 --> mem2
    mem2 --> mem3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks students to draw **before-and-after diagrams** of DLL operations. Always label both the data values and the pointer directions (`<->`) clearly in your diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Doubly Linked List. How does it differ from a Singly Linked List?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Doubly Linked List** is a linear dynamic data structure in which each node consists of three parts: a `data` field, a pointer to the **previous** node (`prev`), and a pointer to the **next** node (`next`). This enables traversal in both forward and backward directions.

**Differences:**

| Feature | Singly Linked List | Doubly Linked List |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Pointers per node | 1 (`next`) | 2 (`prev`, `next`) |
| Traversal direction | Forward only | Bidirectional |
| Memory per node | Lower | Higher |
| Deletion (with pointer) | O(n) | **O(1)** |
| Complexity | Simpler | More complex |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**List any three applications of Doubly Linked Lists.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Browser Navigation History** — Back and Forward buttons use DLL logic to navigate between visited pages.
2. **LRU Cache Implementation** — Doubly Linked Lists are used in cache eviction policies in operating systems.
3. **Undo/Redo Functionality in Text Editors** — Each state in the editor is doubly linked to its predecessor and successor.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the algorithm to **insert a new node at the end** of a Doubly Linked List. Provide the algorithm and a step-by-step diagram showing pointer changes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithm to **delete a node with a given value** from a Doubly Linked List. Show a worked example.

#### 🔹 Part (a) Solution: Insertion at End

**Algorithm:**
1. Create a new node `N` with given data.
2. If `HEAD == NULL`, set `HEAD = N`, set `N->prev = NULL`, `N->next = NULL`.
3. Else, traverse to the last node `T` where `T->next == NULL`.
4. Set `T->next = N`.
5. Set `N->prev = T`.
6. Set `N->next = NULL`.

**Step-by-Step Worked Example:**

Starting List: `NULL <- [5] <-> [15] <-> [25] -> NULL`

We insert **35** at the end.

| Step | Action | Pointer State |
|------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Traverse to node [25] (last node T) | T->next = NULL |
| 2 | Create N with data = 35 | N->prev = NULL, N->next = NULL |
| 3 | Set T->next = N | [25].next = N |
| 4 | Set N->prev = T | N->prev = [25] |
| 5 | Set N->next = NULL | N->next = NULL |

**Final List:** `NULL <- [5] <-> [15] <-> [25] <-> [35] -> NULL`

**Time Complexity:** **O(n)** if TAIL pointer not maintained; **O(1)** if TAIL pointer is used.

#### 🔹 Part (b) Solution: Delete Node with Given Value

**Algorithm:**
1. Traverse the list to find the node `D` with the given value.
2. If `D == NULL`, report "Value not found".
3. If `D->prev != NULL`, set `D->prev->next = D->next`.
4. If `D->next != NULL`, set `D->next->prev = D->prev`.
5. If `D == HEAD`, update `HEAD = HEAD->next`.
6. Free `D`.

**Worked Example:** Delete node with value **15** from `NULL <- [5] <-> [15] <-> [25] <-> [35] -> NULL`

| Step | Action | Pointer State |
|------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Find D = node [15] | D->prev = [5], D->next = [25] |
| 2 | Update [5]->next = [25] | Left bypass done |
| 3 | Update [25]->prev = [5] | Right bypass done |
| 4 | Free D | Memory released |

**Final List:** `NULL <- [5] <-> [25] <-> [35] -> NULL`

**Time Complexity:** **O(n)** — requires search.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of Doubly Linked Lists over Singly Linked Lists with a comparative table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithm to **insert a node after a given node** in a DLL. Illustrate with a diagram where the list is `10 <-> 20 <-> 40` and we insert **30 after 20**.

#### 🔹 Part (a) Solution: Advantages & Disadvantages

**Advantages of DLL over SLL:**
1. **Bidirectional Traversal**: Can traverse both forward and backward, essential for undo/redo operations.
2. **Efficient Deletion**: Deleting a node given its pointer is **O(1)**, versus O(n) for SLL.
3. **Quick Access to Previous Node**: No need to traverse from HEAD to find predecessor.

**Disadvantages of DLL over SLL:**
1. **Extra Memory**: Each node requires an additional pointer (`prev`), increasing memory overhead.
2. **Complex Implementation**: More pointer manipulations increase the chance of bugs.
3. **More Pointer Updates**: Insertions and deletions require updating 4 pointers instead of 2.

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | SLL | DLL |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Memory per node | 1 pointer | 2 pointers |
| Backward traversal | Not possible | Possible |
| Delete with known pointer | O(n) | O(1) |
| Complexity | Low | Medium |

#### 🔹 Part (b) Solution: Insert After a Given Node

**Initial List:** `NULL <- [10] <-> [20] <-> [40] -> NULL`

**Algorithm:**
1. Let `P` = pointer to node after which we want to insert (here, node [20]).
2. If `P == NULL`, return error.
3. Create new node `N` with data = 30.
4. Set `N->next = P->next`.
5. Set `N->prev = P`.
6. If `P->next != NULL`, set `P->next->prev = N`.
7. Set `P->next = N`.

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Operation | Effect |
|------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | `N = new node(30)` | N created |
| 2 | `N->next = P->next` | N->next = [40] |
| 3 | `N->prev = P` | N->prev = [20] |
| 4 | `P->next->prev = N` | [40]->prev = N |
| 5 | `P->next = N` | [20]->next = N |

**Final List:** `NULL <- [10] <-> [20] <-> [30] <-> [40] -> NULL`

**Time Complexity:** **O(1)** — direct pointer manipulation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update BOTH `prev` and `next` pointers** — leads to broken lists and full mark deduction.
> 2. **Not handling edge cases** — Empty list, single node, deletion of HEAD/TAIL must be explicitly addressed.
> 3. **Order of pointer reassignment** — Always save `P->next` BEFORE modifying it, otherwise the original next node reference is lost.
> 4. **Memory leak** — After deletion, students often forget to `free()` the node's memory.
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams** — KTU examiners award marks specifically for **before-and-after diagrams**. Always include them.
> 6. **Wrong complexity analysis** — Confusing O(1) deletion (with pointer) vs. O(n) deletion (by value).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 High-Density Revision Checklist for Doubly Linked Lists:**

- ✅ A DLL node has **three fields**: `prev`, `data`, `next`.
- ✅ DLL supports **bidirectional traversal**.
- ✅ Insertion at HEAD or TAIL (with TAIL pointer) is **O(1)**.
- ✅ Deletion of a known node is **O(1)** — the **defining advantage** of DLL.
- ✅ Searching for a value remains **O(n)**.
- ✅ Memory overhead: each node requires **2 pointers**, doubling pointer storage vs. SLL.
- ✅ Always handle **NULL pointer** cases for `prev` (at HEAD) and `next` (at TAIL).
- ✅ **HEAD** is the pointer to the first node; **TAIL** (optional) points to the last.
- ✅ Circular DLL: last node's `next` points back to HEAD, and HEAD's `prev` points to TAIL.
- ✅ Real-world uses: **Browser history, LRU cache, Undo/Redo, Music playlists**.
- ✅ Time complexity table must be memorized for exam:
  - Insertion (any position, with reference): **O(1)**
  - Deletion (with node pointer): **O(1)**
  - Traversal/Search: **O(n)**
- ✅ Always draw **before-and-after diagrams** in exam answers for full marks.
- ✅ Edge cases to always mention: **Empty list, single-node list, deletion of first/last node**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circular Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a linear data structure in which the nodes are arranged in a sequential chain, but unlike a standard singly linked list, the **last node does not store a `NULL` pointer**. Instead, its `next` (or `link`) field points back to the **first node (head)** of the list, forming a closed loop or "ring" topology.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):** A circular linked list is a variation of the linked list in which the last element is linked to the first element, thus forming a circular chain. The reference to the `head` node is preserved for entry-point access, and traversal terminates when the pointer revisits the `head` node.

Mathematically, for a Circular Linked List with $n$ nodes and head pointer $H$:

$$\text{last} \rightarrow \text{next} = H$$

$$\forall \, i \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, n-1\}, \quad \text{node}_i \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{node}_{(i+1) \bmod n}$$

The modulo operator $\bmod \, n$ on the index $i$ is the mathematical signature of circularity — when $i = n-1$, it wraps back to $0$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Conga Line:** Imagine a group of people holding hands in a conga line at a party. The first person (head) starts the line, each person holds the hand of the person in front, and crucially, **the last person reaches around and holds the hand of the first person** — closing the loop. There is no "end" of the line; you can keep walking around forever. To find a specific person, you start from the leader (head) and walk around the circle until you either find them or return to the leader.

A more **engineering-friendly analogy** is a **circular train track (ring railway)**:
- Trains (nodes) run on the track.
- The track is a closed loop with no terminal station.
- A control room (head pointer) keeps a record of the train currently at the dispatch point.

### 1.3 Variants of Circular Linked List

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **two principal variants**:

| Variant | Structure | Pointer per Node | Traversal Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL)** | One-way ring | `next` | `ptr->next != head` |
| **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL)** | Two-way ring | `next` + `prev` | `ptr->next != head` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property (Board Exam Favorite):** In a Circular Linked List, **no pointer is ever `NULL`**. This is the single most distinguishing feature that separates it from a linear linked list and is frequently asked in KTU exams.

### 1.4 Head and Tail Conventions

In circular linked list literature and in KTU examinations, two pointer conventions are used:

- **Only Head pointer stored:** Traversal may need to traverse $n$ nodes to reach the last node.
- **Head + Tail pointers stored:** The tail pointer is preferred because **tail->next = head** is an $O(1)$ access, making both `head` and `tail` instantly reachable. This is the **standard KTU convention** for most algorithm derivations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students incorrectly assume that the `tail` pointer is redundant in a CLL because "you can go around the ring." In practice, maintaining a `tail` pointer **drastically reduces** the time complexity of `insertAtEnd` and `deleteAtEnd` from $O(n)$ to $O(1)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Node

The node structure (in C, as per KTU lab/exam convention) is:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
```

Each node occupies memory of size:

$$S_{\text{node}} = S_{\text{int}} + S_{\text{pointer}} = 4 \text{ bytes} + 8 \text{ bytes} = 12 \text{ bytes}$$

(on a 64-bit system; **8 bytes** on 32-bit systems)

> [!NOTE]
> For a Circular Linked List with $n$ nodes, the total memory consumed is:
> $$\text{Memory}_{\text{total}} = n \cdot S_{\text{node}} + S_{\text{head}} + S_{\text{tail}}$$

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

#### Operation 1: Traversal of a Circular Linked List

The traversal logic differs from a linear list because the termination condition is **"return to head"** rather than **"reach NULL"**.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Initialize a temporary pointer `ptr = head`.
2. If `head == NULL` (empty list), exit.
3. Use a **do-while** loop (must execute at least once even for a single-node list).
4. Process `ptr->data`.
5. Advance: `ptr = ptr->next`.
6. Continue until `ptr == head`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The use of a **do-while** loop is **mandatory** for CLL traversal. A standard `while` loop will fail for the single-node case because `head->next == head` from the start.

#### Operation 2: Insertion at the Beginning (Head)

1. Allocate a new node `newNode`.
2. If list is empty: `newNode->next = newNode; head = newNode; tail = newNode;`
3. If list is non-empty: traverse to `tail`, set `tail->next = newNode`, then `newNode->next = head`, finally `head = newNode`.

#### Operation 3: Insertion at the End (Tail)

1. Allocate `newNode`.
2. If empty: same as above.
3. If non-empty: `tail->next = newNode; newNode->next = head; tail = newNode;` — **$O(1)$ with tail pointer**.

#### Operation 4: Insertion After a Given Node

1. Locate the target node `target` (requires $O(k)$ traversal where $k$ is the position).
2. `newNode->next = target->next;`
3. `target->next = newNode;`
4. If `target == tail`, update `tail = newNode`.

#### Operation 5: Deletion from the Beginning

1. If single node: free it; `head = tail = NULL`.
2. Otherwise: `temp = head; tail->next = head->next; head = head->next; free(temp);`

#### Operation 6: Deletion from the End

1. If single node: same as above.
2. Otherwise: traverse to find `secondLast` (where `secondLast->next == tail`); `secondLast->next = head; free(tail); tail = secondLast;`

#### Operation 7: Deletion of a Specific Node (by value)

1. Traverse to find the target node and its predecessor.
2. `pred->next = target->next;`
3. If `target == head` or `target == tail`, update the respective pointers.
4. Free `target`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready summary** of all critical formulas, time complexities, and boundary conditions for Circular Linked List operations as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

| Operation | Time Complexity (Avg) | Time Complexity (Worst) | Space Complexity | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Creation / Insert at Head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | `head == NULL` check |
| Insert at Tail (with tail ptr) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | `head == NULL` check |
| Insert at Tail (only head ptr) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Full traversal to last |
| Insert After Node | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Search + update tail if needed |
| Deletion at Head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Single-node check mandatory |
| Deletion at Tail (with tail ptr) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Must find `secondLast` |
| Deletion by Value | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Update head/tail if matched |
| Traversal / Search | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Termination: `ptr == head` |
| Length Calculation | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Use do-while counter |

| Metric | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total Memory (n nodes) | $n \cdot (S_{\text{data}} + S_{\text{ptr}})$ | Excludes head/tail pointers |
| Pointer Count per Node | $1$ (SCLL) or $2$ (DCLL) | SCLL is the KTU default |
| Termination Identifier | `ptr \rightarrow \text{next} == \text{head}` | Never `NULL` |
| Empty List Test | `head == NULL` | Both head and tail must be NULL |
| Single Node Test | `head == head \rightarrow \text{next}` | The only self-loop case |
| Number of Pointer Updates (Insert at Tail, SCLL) | $2$ | `tail->next` and `newNode->next` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Where are Circular Linked Lists actually used in industry?**

- **Operating Systems:** Round-robin process scheduling (the OS cycles through processes indefinitely).
- **Multiplayer Games:** Turn-based player rotation (e.g., passing the dice clockwise indefinitely).
- **Buffer Management:** Circular buffers (ring buffers) used in audio streaming, network packet handling, and producer-consumer queues.
- **Distributed Systems:** Token ring topology in networking.
- **Audio/Video Applications:** Continuous looping of media playlists.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Building a Circular Linked List

**Problem:** Insert elements $10, 20, 30$ into an initially empty SCLL using **insertAtEnd**, then delete `30`. Show the state of the list after every operation.

> [!NOTE]
> We use a **head + tail** convention. The key invariant after every operation is: **`tail->next == head`** and **the list contains zero `NULL` pointers** in the `next` fields (except when the list is empty).

#### Step 1: Insert 10 (List was empty)

**Initial State:** `head = NULL`, `tail = NULL`

**Logic Execution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{newNode} &= \text{allocate}() \\
\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{data} &= 10 \\
\text{Check: head} == \text{NULL} &\rightarrow \text{TRUE (empty list)} \\
\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} &= \text{newNode} \quad \text{(points to itself)} \\
\text{head} &= \text{newNode} \\
\text{tail} &= \text{newNode}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State After:**

```
head --> [10] <-- tail
         /   \
        (self-loop)
```

> **Invariant Check:** `tail->next == head` → `[10]->next == [10]` → `head` itself → **TRUE**

#### Step 2: Insert 20 at End

**Initial State:** `head` and `tail` both point to node `[10]`.

**Logic Execution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{newNode} &= \text{allocate}() \\
\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{data} &= 20 \\
\text{Check: head} == \text{NULL} &\rightarrow \text{FALSE} \\
\text{tail} \rightarrow \text{next} &= \text{newNode} \\
\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} &= \text{head} \\
\text{tail} &= \text{newNode}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State After:**

```
head --> [10] --> [20] <-- tail
         /                \
        (-----------------)
```

> **Invariant Check:** `tail->next == head` → `[20]->next == [10]` → **TRUE**

#### Step 3: Insert 30 at End

**Logic Execution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{data} &= 30 \\
\text{tail} \rightarrow \text{next} &= \text{newNode} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [20] \rightarrow \text{next} = [30] \\
\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} &= \text{head} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [30] \rightarrow \text{next} = [10] \\
\text{tail} &= \text{newNode} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{tail} = [30]
\end{aligned}
$$

**State After:**

```
head --> [10] --> [20] --> [30] <-- tail
         /                         \
        (---------------------------)
```

> **Invariant Check:** `tail->next == head` → `[30]->next == [10]` → **TRUE**

#### Step 4: Delete 30 (from the tail)

**Logic Execution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Identify target} &= \text{tail} = [30] \\
\text{Find secondLast}: & \text{ traverse until } \text{ptr} \rightarrow \text{next} == \text{tail} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{secondLast} = [20] \\
\text{secondLast} \rightarrow \text{next} &= \text{head} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [20] \rightarrow \text{next} = [10] \\
\text{tail} &= \text{secondLast} = [20] \\
\text{free}(\text{target}) &\Rightarrow \text{memory of } [30] \text{ deallocated}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State After:**

```
head --> [10] --> [20] <-- tail
         /                \
        (-----------------)
```

> **Final Invariant Check:** `tail->next == head` → `[20]->next == [10]` → **TRUE**

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Insert/Delete Operations

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a high-yield KTU revision tool — it condenses all the conditional branches of CLL operations into a single table.

| Operation | Empty List (head=NULL)? | Single Node (head->next==head)? | General Case (n ≥ 2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insert at Head** | `new->next=new; head=new; tail=new;` | `new->next=head; head->next=new; head=new;` (tail unchanged) | `new->next=head; tail->next=new; head=new;` |
| **Insert at Tail** | Same as above | `new->next=head; head->next=new; tail=new;` | `new->next=head; tail->next=new; tail=new;` |
| **Insert After K** | Not applicable (cannot insert "after" a non-existent node) | `new->next=head->next; head->next=new;` then if `K==head`, `tail=new;` | Standard $O(n)$ search + 2 pointer updates |
| **Delete from Head** | `free(head); head=tail=NULL;` | `free(head); head=tail=NULL;` | `temp=head; tail->next=head->next; head=head->next; free(temp);` |
| **Delete from Tail** | Same as above | Same as above | Find `secondLast` in $O(n)$; `secondLast->next=head; tail=secondLast; free(oldTail);` |
| **Delete by Value** | Return / "not found" | `free(head); head=tail=NULL;` | Traverse to find + update predecessor's link + update head/tail if matched |

### 3.3 Traversal Trace: Printing the List

Given the final state of our list `[10] -> [20] -> back to [10]`, let us trace the traversal:

**C-Code Equivalent:**

```c
if (head != NULL) {
    struct Node *ptr = head;
    do {
        printf("%d ", ptr->data);
        ptr = ptr->next;
    } while (ptr != head);
}
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Iteration | `ptr` points to | `ptr->data` printed | `ptr = ptr->next` | `ptr == head`? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `[10]` | `10` | `[20]` | No (10 ≠ 20) |
| 2 | `[20]` | `20` | `[10]` | Yes (10 == 10) — EXIT |

**Output:** `10 20`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a `while (ptr != head)` loop were used **without** the `do`, the first iteration would fail to execute when `head->next == head` (single node list), printing **nothing**. This is the most common traversal bug in KTU lab exams.

### 3.4 Algorithmic Time Derivation — Why Deletion at Tail is $O(n)$

Even with a `tail` pointer, deletion at the tail in a SCLL is $O(n)$ because:

> Although `tail` gives us the last node in $O(1)$, we still need the **second-last node** to re-link it to `head`. Since SCLL is **one-way**, we must walk forward from `head` to find it.

**Number of pointer jumps required:**

$$T_{\text{deleteTail}} = n - 1 \text{ iterations to find secondLast} + O(1) \text{ for re-link} = O(n)$$

For a **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL)**, this becomes $O(1)$ because `tail->prev` directly gives the second-last node. This trade-off is a frequent **2-mark KTU question**.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Singly Linked List and a Circular Linked List. Mention any two advantages of a Circular Linked List over a linear linked list.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Singly Linked List | Circular Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Last Node Pointer | `last->next == NULL` | `last->next == head` |
| Termination of Traversal | Detect `NULL` | Detect return to `head` |
| End Accessibility | $O(n)$ to reach last | $O(1)$ with `tail` pointer |
| Memory Wastage (NULLs) | Yes (one NULL per list) | No NULLs in `next` pointers |

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Any node can be a starting point:** From any node, the entire list can be traversed — useful in round-robin scheduling.
2. **No NULL pointer overhead:** The list has no termination marker, allowing seamless cycling.
3. **Tail operations become O(1):** With a maintained `tail` pointer, `tail->next == head` gives instant access to the head.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition: 1 mark, Comparison Table: 1 mark, Two Advantages: 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the use of a `do-while` loop for traversing a Circular Linked List. Why cannot a `while` loop be used directly?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A `do-while` loop is mandatory for CLL traversal because the loop body must execute **at least once** even when the list contains a **single node**.

**In a single-node CLL:** `head->next == head`. If we use:
```c
struct Node *ptr = head;
while (ptr != head) {   // BUG: ptr is already equal to head
    printf("%d", ptr->data);
    ptr = ptr->next;
}
```
The loop body **never executes**, and the single element is never printed.

**With `do-while`:**
```c
struct Node *ptr = head;
do {
    printf("%d", ptr->data);
    ptr = ptr->next;
} while (ptr != head);
```
The body executes once (printing the data), then the condition is checked. This correctly handles the single-node edge case.

**Mark Distribution:** Explanation: 1 mark, Code: 1 mark, Single-node justification: 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Write a C function to **insert a node at the end** of a Circular Linked List. Provide the complete algorithm and explain the boundary conditions for an empty list and a single-node list. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C function to **delete a node from the end** of a Circular Linked List. Trace the function for the list: `10 → 20 → 30 → (back to 10)`. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Algorithm: InsertAtEnd (SCLL)**

```c
void insertAtEnd(struct Node **head, struct Node **tail, int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;

    // Case 1: Empty list
    if (*head == NULL) {
        newNode->next = newNode;  // self-loop
        *head = newNode;
        *tail = newNode;
        return;
    }

    // Case 2: Non-empty list
    (*tail)->next = newNode;   // old tail points to new node
    newNode->next = *head;     // new node points to head
    *tail = newNode;           // update tail
}
```

**Boundary Condition Analysis (7 Marks):**

1. **Empty list (head = NULL):** The new node points to itself (`newNode->next = newNode`). Both `head` and `tail` are set to `newNode`. This creates a valid single-node CLL.
2. **Single-node list (head->next = head):** The else branch executes. `tail->next = newNode` (old head now points to new node), `newNode->next = head` (new node closes the loop), `tail = newNode`. **2 pointer updates** are performed.
3. **General case (n ≥ 2):** Identical to case 2. The `tail` pointer makes this **O(1)**.

**Mark Allocation:** Code: 3 marks, Boundary conditions: 2 marks, Complexity reasoning: 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Algorithm: DeleteAtEnd (SCLL)**

```c
void deleteAtEnd(struct Node **head, struct Node **tail) {
    if (*head == NULL) {
        printf("List is empty.\n");
        return;
    }

    // Case 1: Single node
    if (*head == *tail) {
        free(*head);
        *head = *tail = NULL;
        return;
    }

    // Case 2: Multiple nodes — find secondLast
    struct Node *secondLast = *head;
    while (secondLast->next != *tail) {
        secondLast = secondLast->next;
    }

    secondLast->next = *head;  // re-link to head
    free(*tail);
    *tail = secondLast;
}
```

**Trace for List: 10 → 20 → 30 → (back to 10)**

| Step | Operation | State |
|---|---|---|
| Initial | `head = [10]`, `tail = [30]` | `[10]→[20]→[30]→[10]` |
| 1 | `secondLast` starts at `head = [10]` | `[10]→[20]→[30]→[10]` |
| 2 | Check `[10]->next == tail?` → `[20] == [30]`? → NO | Move to `[20]` |
| 3 | Check `[20]->next == tail?` → `[30] == [30]`? → YES | Stop |
| 4 | `[20]->next = [10]` (head) | `[10]→[20]→[10]` (looped) |
| 5 | `free([30])` | Memory deallocated |
| 6 | `tail = [20]` | New tail assigned |

**Final State:** `[10] → [20] → (back to [10])`, `head = [10]`, `tail = [20]`.

**Mark Allocation:** Code: 3 marks, Trace table: 3 marks, Final state verification: 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **complete algorithm to insert a new node at the beginning** of a Circular Linked List. Handle all edge cases (empty list, single node, multi-node). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C function to **count the number of nodes** in a Circular Linked List without modifying the list. What is the time and space complexity? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Algorithm: InsertAtBeginning (SCLL)**

```c
void insertAtBeginning(struct Node **head, struct Node **tail, int value) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;

    // Case 1: Empty list
    if (*head == NULL) {
        newNode->next = newNode;
        *head = newNode;
        *tail = newNode;
        return;
    }

    // Case 2 & 3: Non-empty (works for both single and multi-node)
    newNode->next = *head;       // (i) new node points to old head
    (*tail)->next = newNode;     // (ii) old tail now points to new head
    *head = newNode;             // (iii) update head
}
```

**Edge Case Verification (7 Marks):**

- **Empty list:** `newNode->next = newNode` (self-loop), `head = tail = newNode`. **3 pointer assignments.**
- **Single-node list:** Suppose `head = tail = [X]`. Then `newNode->next = [X]`, `[X]->next = newNode` (closes the loop), `head = newNode`. The list is now `[newNode] → [X] → (back to newNode)`. **Tail is correctly preserved.**
- **Multi-node list:** The same 3 pointer updates work seamlessly because `(*tail)->next` is updated to point to the new node, which then points to the old head.

**Crucial Point:** Unlike `insertAtEnd`, here we must update `tail->next` **first** (or equivalently, the new node's `next` first), because once we change `head`, the old head is no longer accessible via `*head`. However, in C we use `*head` only **after** saving the necessary links, so order is: `newNode->next = *head` (save link), `(*tail)->next = newNode` (close loop), `*head = newNode` (update).

**Complexity:** Time = $O(1)$, Space = $O(1)$.

**Mark Allocation:** Code: 3 marks, Edge cases (3 cases × 1 mark): 3 marks, Complexity: 1 mark.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Algorithm: CountNodes (SCLL)**

```c
int countNodes(struct Node *head) {
    if (head == NULL) return 0;

    int count = 0;
    struct Node *ptr = head;
    do {
        count++;
        ptr = ptr->next;
    } while (ptr != head);

    return count;
}
```

**Complexity Analysis:**

$$T(n) = n \text{ iterations} \cdot O(1) \text{ per iteration} = O(n)$$

$$S(n) = O(1) \quad \text{(only a few pointer variables and one integer counter)}$$

**Why `do-while` is essential here (7 Marks reasoning):**
- If the list is empty (`head == NULL`), we return `0` immediately.
- If the list has one node, `ptr == head` is true from the start, but the `do` block executes once, incrementing `count` to 1. The `while` then checks `ptr->next == head` which is also `head == head`, so it exits correctly.
- A `while (ptr != head)` would terminate immediately for a single-node list, returning `0` — **a classic bug**.

**Mark Allocation:** Code: 2 marks, Trace for single node: 2 marks, Complexity: 2 marks, do-while justification: 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Scenarios in CLL Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `head == NULL` check** (−1 to −2 marks): Almost every CLL operation has an empty-list boundary. Skipping it is the #1 reason students lose marks on 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Using `while` instead of `do-while` for traversal** (−1 mark): This fails for the single-node case. Always justify your loop choice.
> 3. **Not updating `tail->next = head` after deletion** (−2 marks): This is the **circular** invariant. Forgetting to close the loop leaves a dangling `NULL`-pointing or "open" list, which is functionally incorrect.
> 4. **Confusing SCLL with DCLL pointer counts** (−1 mark): SCLL has **1 pointer per node**; DCLL has **2**. Mixing these in a comparison question loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the time complexity** (−1 mark): KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes analysis. Always append "Time: $O(n)$, Space: $O(1)$" to your algorithms.
> 6. **Memory leaks in deletion** (−1 mark): In C, every `malloc` must have a matching `free`. Examiners specifically check for this.
> 7. **Wrong tail update after `insertAfter` at the last node** (−1 mark): If you insert after the current `tail`, the `tail` pointer **must** be updated to the new node.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Circular Linked List (KTU Module 2)**

- **Definition:** A linked list where `last->next == head`, forming a closed ring. **No NULL pointers** (except in the empty list case).
- **Two Variants:** SCLL (1 pointer) and DCLL (2 pointers per node). KTU default is SCLL.
- **Empty List Test:** `head == NULL` (both `head` and `tail` are NULL).
- **Single-Node Test:** `head->next == head` (self-loop).
- **Mandatory Loop:** Use `do-while` for traversal — the body must execute at least once.
- **Traversal Termination:** `ptr == head` (NOT `ptr == NULL`).
- **Insert at Head:** 3 pointer updates — `newNode->next = head`, `tail->next = newNode`, `head = newNode`.
- **Insert at Tail (with tail ptr):** $O(1)$ — only 2 updates.
- **Delete at Head:** $O(1)$ — uses `tail->next` to bypass the old head.
- **Delete at Tail:** $O(n)$ in SCLL (must find secondLast), but $O(1)$ in DCLL.
- **Head + Tail Convention:** Preferred in KTU exams for $O(1)$ tail operations.
- **Circular Invariant:** After every operation, verify `tail->next == head`.
- **Applications:** Round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, token ring networks, game turn rotation.
- **Time Complexity Summary:** Traversal/Search/Delete by value = $O(n)$; Insert at head = $O(1)$; Insert at tail (with tail) = $O(1)$.
- **Memory Cost:** $n \cdot S_{\text{node}}$ for $n$ nodes. Each SCLL node = 12 bytes (int + pointer on 64-bit).
- **Common Pitfall:** A `while` loop instead of `do-while` fails for single-node lists.
- **C Convention:** Use `struct Node *head, *tail;` and pass as `struct Node **` to functions for in-place modification.
- **Empty List Re-check:** After deleting the last node, both `head` and `tail` must be set to `NULL`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operations on Linked List, Concepts and Types of Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Linked List — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Linked List):** A **linked list** is a linear, dynamic data structure in which a collection of *nodes* is connected sequentially using **pointers (links)**. Each node is a composite data unit containing two fields: a **DATA field** (storing the payload) and a **LINK/NEXT field** (storing the address of the subsequent node). The first node is accessed via an external pointer called the **HEAD**, and the last node points to **NULL**.

**Variants covered under Module 2 operations:**

| Variant | Structural Property | Tail Pointer |
|---|---|---|
| Singly Linked List (SLL) | Each node points to next only | NULL |
| Doubly Linked List (DLL) | Each node has *next* AND *prev* pointers | NULL |
| Circular Singly Linked List (CSLL) | Last node points back to HEAD | HEAD |
| Circular Doubly Linked List (CDLL) | Last→next = HEAD, HEAD→prev = last | HEAD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 mandates operational fluency — not just theoretical definition. You must be able to **draw before/after diagrams** of insertion and deletion for ALL three variants.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Treasure Hunt

Imagine a **treasure hunt** with 100 sealed envelopes placed in 100 *different buildings* across Kerala:

- Each **envelope** is a **node**.
- The **treasure map inside** is the **DATA**.
- A **note saying "Go to Building #42 next"** is the **LINK/pointer**.
- The **first envelope's location** is the **HEAD pointer**.

Now compare this with an **array** — that would be like keeping all 100 envelopes *in a single row of 100 lockers in one building*. Inserting a new envelope in the middle means **shifting every later envelope** (O(n) work). In a linked list, you just slip a new envelope into any building and rewrite *two* notes — **no shifting required**.

**Key operational consequences:**

- **No random access** — to reach the *k*-th envelope, you must walk through the previous *k − 1* envelopes. This is why linked list access is **O(n)**.
- **No wastage of memory** — nodes can be scattered across RAM; size grows and shrinks dynamically.
- **Insertion/Deletion at the HEAD is O(1)** — the most celebrated property in KTU exams.

---

## 1.3 The Tree — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Tree):** A **tree** *T* is a finite, non-linear, hierarchical data structure consisting of a set of *nodes* (or *vertices*) connected by *edges*, satisfying four axioms:
> 1. There is exactly one distinguished node called the **ROOT**.
> 2. Every other node is reachable from the root via a *unique* path of edges.
> 3. There are **no cycles**.
> 4. The graph is **connected**.
>
> **Recursive Definition:** A tree is either an empty set ∅, or a root node with a finite ordered/unordered collection of disjoint **subtrees**.

**A formal corollary:** A tree with *n* nodes has exactly **(n − 1) edges**. This single identity is the foundation of nearly every KTU tree-math problem.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Organizational Chart

Visualize a **KTU University hierarchy**:

- The **Vice-Chancellor** is the **ROOT**.
- Deans of various faculties are **children of the root**.
- Each HOD is a **child of a Dean**.
- Professors reporting to an HOD form the **leaves** of that subtree.
- Two professors reporting to the same HOD are **siblings**.

There is **exactly one path** from the VC down to any professor — this is what makes a tree a *tree* and not a graph. The "no cycles" rule means you cannot have two professors reporting to each other simultaneously.

---

## 1.5 The Eight Major Tree Types — At a Glance

| # | Tree Type | Defining Property | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Tree** | Any node may have any number of children | File systems, XML/HTML DOM |
| 2 | **Binary Tree (BT)** | Each node has at most 2 children (Left, Right) | Expression parsing, Huffman coding |
| 3 | **Binary Search Tree (BST)** | BT with `Left < Root < Right` ordering | Ordered dictionaries, set membership |
| 4 | **AVL Tree** | Self-balancing BST with **\|Balance Factor\| ≤ 1** | Database indexing |
| 5 | **Red-Black Tree** | BST with red/black color invariants | C++ `std::map`, Linux kernel scheduler |
| 6 | **B-Tree** | Generalized BST; nodes hold multiple keys | Disk-based DB indexes (MySQL InnoDB) |
| 7 | **Heap** | Complete binary tree with heap-order property | Priority queues, Dijkstra's algorithm |
| 8 | **Trie (Prefix Tree)** | Characters on edges from root to leaf | Autocomplete, IP routing tables |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pattern Recognition:** When a KTU question says *"self-balancing"*, it is almost always **AVL or Red-Black**. When it says *"disk storage"* or *"block-based"*, the answer is **B-Tree**. When it says *"priority"*, the answer is **Heap**.

---

## 1.6 Critical Distinctions the Examiner Tests

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear vs Non-Linear:**
> - Linked List = **Linear** (one predecessor, one successor at most).
> - Tree = **Non-linear** (one parent, but multiple children possible).
>
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common 3-mark question asks: *"Why is a tree called a non-linear data structure?"* The answer lies in the fact that the **next-pointer ratio is 1:N**, not 1:1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Linked List Node

A linked list node is a **struct** in C / a **class instance** in C++/Java. The textbook template:

```
┌─────────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────────┐
│  DATA  |  NEXT  ───────────► │  DATA  |  NEXT  ───────► ...
└─────────────────────┘         └─────────────────────┘
   Node 1                          Node 2
```

For a **Doubly Linked List**, the structure is upgraded to three fields:

```
NULL ◄─── PREV  |  DATA  |  NEXT  ────►
```

---

## 2.2 The Six Cardinal Operations — Algorithmic Breakdown

### Operation 1: Insertion at the Beginning (HEAD) — Time **O(1)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why O(1)?** No traversal is needed. Only two pointer assignments.

**Algorithmic Steps:**
1. Allocate a new node `NEW`.
2. Store the payload into `NEW->DATA`.
3. Point `NEW->NEXT` to the current `HEAD`.
4. Reassign `HEAD = NEW`.

**Visual trace on a list `10 → 20 → 30`:**

```
BEFORE:  HEAD ──► [10|*] ──► [20|*] ──► [30|NULL]

Step 1:  NEW = [5|?]
Step 2:  NEW->DATA = 5
Step 3:  NEW->NEXT = HEAD       →  NEW points to old HEAD
Step 4:  HEAD = NEW

AFTER:   HEAD ──► [5|*] ──► [10|*] ──► [20|*] ──► [30|NULL]
```

**Pointer assignments count: exactly 2.** Hence O(1).

---

### Operation 2: Insertion at the End (TAIL) — Time **O(n)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a maintained `TAIL` pointer, you must **walk the entire list** to find the last node. This is the classic reason why keeping an auxiliary `TAIL` pointer reduces tail operations to O(1).

**Algorithmic Steps:**
1. Allocate `NEW`.
2. If `HEAD == NULL`, set `HEAD = NEW` and return.
3. Initialize `TEMP = HEAD`.
4. While `TEMP->NEXT != NULL`, advance `TEMP = TEMP->NEXT`.
5. Set `TEMP->NEXT = NEW`.

**Loop iterations: at most n.** Hence O(n).

---

### Operation 3: Insertion at Position *k* (1-indexed) — Time **O(n)**

1. Allocate `NEW`.
2. If `k == 1`, perform HEAD insertion.
3. Else, traverse with two pointers `PREV` and `CURR` until `PREV` is at position *k − 1*.
4. Set `NEW->NEXT = PREV->NEXT`.
5. Set `PREV->NEXT = NEW`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to handle `k > n + 1` or `k < 1`. Always validate bounds before traversal.

---

### Operation 4: Deletion at the Beginning — Time **O(1)**

1. If `HEAD == NULL`, report underflow.
2. Store `TEMP = HEAD`.
3. Reassign `HEAD = HEAD->NEXT`.
4. Free `TEMP`.

---

### Operation 5: Deletion at the End — Time **O(n)**

1. If `HEAD == NULL`, underflow.
2. If `HEAD->NEXT == NULL`, free HEAD and set `HEAD = NULL`.
3. Else, traverse with `PREV` and `CURR` until `CURR->NEXT == NULL`.
4. Set `PREV->NEXT = NULL` and free `CURR`.

---

### Operation 6: Deletion by Value *x* — Time **O(n)**

1. Initialize `PREV = NULL`, `CURR = HEAD`.
2. While `CURR != NULL` and `CURR->DATA != x`: advance both.
3. If `CURR == NULL`, value not found.
4. If `PREV == NULL`, delete HEAD (use Operation 4 logic).
5. Else, set `PREV->NEXT = CURR->NEXT` and free `CURR`.

---

## 2.3 Doubly Linked List — The Premium Upgrade

A **DLL** node has two links: `PREV` and `NEXT`. The trade-off is **one extra pointer per node** (memory cost) for **bidirectional traversal** (operational gain).

**Insertion after node `P` in a DLL requires FOUR pointer assignments** (not two as in SLL):

1. `NEW->NEXT = P->NEXT`
2. If `P->NEXT != NULL`, then `P->NEXT->PREV = NEW`
3. `P->NEXT = NEW`
4. `NEW->PREV = P`

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to insert/delete in a DLL, the answer must show **all four pointer reassignments in the correct order**. Skipping step 2 is the most common deduction trigger.

---

## 2.4 Circular Linked List — The Endless Loop

In a **Circular Singly Linked List (CSLL)**, the last node points back to HEAD instead of NULL. Operations must check `TEMP->NEXT == HEAD` (instead of `NULL`) as the stopping condition.

**Advantage:** Any node can serve as a starting point, making CSLL ideal for **round-robin scheduling** in operating systems (CPU process scheduling).

**Disadvantage:** Infinite loops if termination is poorly coded. Always maintain a counter or use `do-while` carefully.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Linked List Complexity Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Auxiliary Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Insert at Beginning | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Insert at End (no tail ptr) | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) |
| Insert at End (with tail ptr) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Insert at Position k | O(1) | O(k) | O(n) | O(1) |
| Delete from Beginning | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Delete from End | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) |
| Delete by Value x | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) |
| Search for x | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) |
| Traversal | Θ(n) | Θ(n) | Θ(n) | O(1) |
| Reverse (iterative) | Θ(n) | Θ(n) | Θ(n) | O(1) |

### Tree Mathematics Master Table

| # | Property | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Edges in tree | $E = n - 1$ | Every node except root has exactly one incoming edge |
| 2 | Max nodes at level *i* (root = level 0) | $2^{i}$ | Each node has at most 2 children |
| 3 | Max nodes in tree of height *h* | $2^{h+1} - 1$ | Geometric series sum |
| 4 | Min nodes in tree of height *h* | $h + 1$ | Skewed tree (single chain) |
| 5 | Max height with *n* nodes | $n$ | Degenerate tree |
| 6 | Min height with *n* nodes | $\lceil \log_{2}(n+1) \rceil - 1$ | Perfect binary tree bound |
| 7 | Leaves vs Degree-2 nodes (any BT) | $n_{0} = n_{2} + 1$ | Fundamental identity |
| 8 | Full BT internal vs leaf | $L = I + 1$ | All internal nodes have 2 children |
| 9 | Strict BT total nodes | $n = 2I + 1$ | Since $L = I+1$, $n = I + (I+1)$ |
| 10 | External path length | Sum over all leaves of (depth of leaf) | Used in Huffman coding |

> [!IMPORTANT]

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

|---|---|
| **Singly Linked List** | Dynamic memory allocation free-lists, polynomial arithmetic, undo stacks |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose of this section:** Every identity in the Formula Sheet is **derived from first principles** below. KTU examiners award full marks only when derivations are shown step-by-step, not just quoted.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Edges in a Tree

**Given:** A tree *T* with *n* nodes.

**Axiom:** A tree is connected and acyclic.

**Step 1.** Identify the root. The root has **no parent**, hence **no incoming edge**.

**Step 2.** Every other node has **exactly one parent** (uniqueness is guaranteed by the acyclic property; otherwise, two paths to a node would create a cycle).

**Step 3.** Each child relationship corresponds to **one edge** going from parent to child.

**Step 4.** Count: there are *n − 1* non-root nodes, each contributing exactly one incoming edge.

$$\boxed{E = n - 1}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Number of Nodes at Level *i*

**Setup:** Binary tree, root at level 0, each node has at most 2 children.

**Step 1 — Base case (i = 0):** The root is the only node.

$$\text{Max nodes at level } 0 = 1 = 2^{0}$$

**Step 2 — Inductive hypothesis:** Assume the maximum at level *i* is $2^{i}$.

**Step 3 — Inductive step:** Each of these $2^{i}$ nodes spawns at most 2 children at level *i + 1*.

$$\text{Max nodes at level } (i+1) = 2 \times 2^{i} = 2^{i+1}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** By the principle of mathematical induction:

$$\boxed{\text{Max nodes at level } i = 2^{i} \quad \text{for all } i \geq 0}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Total Nodes in a Tree of Height *h*

**Setup:** The tree has levels 0, 1, 2, ..., *h*. The total node count is the sum of maximums at each level.

$$\text{Total nodes}_{\max} = \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^{i}$$

**Step 1.** Recognize this as a finite geometric series with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = 2$, and $h+1$ terms.

**Step 2.** Apply the geometric series formula:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{h} r^{i} = \frac{r^{h+1} - 1}{r - 1}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $r = 2$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^{i} = \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} = \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{1}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Max nodes} = 2^{h+1} - 1}$$

**Worked Example:** A perfect binary tree of height 3 has $2^{3+1} - 1 = 2^{4} - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15$ nodes. ✓ (Verifiable by drawing.)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimum Height for *n* Nodes

**Setup:** For the minimum height, we need a *perfectly filled* tree (every level except possibly the last is completely full).

**Step 1.** Maximum nodes in height *h* is $2^{h+1} - 1$ (from Derivation 3).

**Step 2.** For *n* nodes, the minimum height must satisfy:

$$n \leq 2^{h_{\min} + 1} - 1$$

**Step 3.** Algebraic manipulation:

$$n + 1 \leq 2^{h_{\min} + 1}$$

**Step 4.** Apply $\log_{2}$ to both sides:

$$\log_{2}(n + 1) \leq h_{\min} + 1$$

**Step 5.** Solve for $h_{\min}$:

$$h_{\min} \geq \log_{2}(n + 1) - 1$$

**Step 6.** Since height is an integer, take the ceiling:

$$\boxed{h_{\min} = \lceil \log_{2}(n + 1) \rceil - 1}$$

**Worked Example:** With *n* = 10 nodes:

$$h_{\min} = \lceil \log_{2}(11) \rceil - 1 = \lceil 3.459 \rceil - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$$

So a balanced binary tree with 10 nodes has height **at least 3**. ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — The Fundamental Identity $n_{0} = n_{2} + 1$

**This is the most-tested identity in KTU Tree papers.** Let us derive it from scratch.

**Definitions:**
- $n_{0}$ = number of leaf nodes (degree = 0)
- $n_{1}$ = number of nodes with exactly 1 child
- $n_{2}$ = number of nodes with exactly 2 children
- $n$ = total nodes
- $E$ = total edges

**Step 1 — Node count equation:** Every node belongs to exactly one of the three classes:

$$n = n_{0} + n_{1} + n_{2} \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

**Step 2 — Edge count via tree property:** From Derivation 1:

$$E = n - 1$$

**Step 3 — Edge count via degree contribution:** Each edge connects a parent to a child. The total number of edges equals the total number of *outgoing* parent-to-child links.

- A degree-0 node contributes 0 edges.
- A degree-1 node contributes 1 edge.
- A degree-2 node contributes 2 edges.

$$E = 0 \cdot n_{0} + 1 \cdot n_{1} + 2 \cdot n_{2} = n_{1} + 2n_{2} \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for E:**

$$n - 1 = n_{1} + 2n_{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Equation A into the left side:**

$$(n_{0} + n_{1} + n_{2}) - 1 = n_{1} + 2n_{2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify by subtracting $n_{1}$ from both sides:**

$$n_{0} + n_{2} - 1 = 2n_{2}$$

**Step 7 — Subtract $n_{2}$ from both sides:**

$$n_{0} - 1 = n_{2}$$

$$\boxed{n_{0} = n_{2} + 1}$$

**Worked Example:** Suppose a binary tree has 25 leaves. How many nodes have exactly 2 children?

$$n_{2} = n_{0} - 1 = 25 - 1 = 24$$

So there are **24 internal nodes** with 2 children. ✓

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Full Binary Tree Properties

**Definition:** A *full* (or *strict*) binary tree is one in which every internal node has **exactly 2 children** — never 1.

**Step 1.** Let $I$ = number of internal nodes, $L$ = number of leaves.

**Step 2.** Total nodes:

$$n = I + L$$

**Step 3.** Total edges via tree property:

$$E = n - 1 = I + L - 1$$

**Step 4.** Total edges via degree contribution: every internal node contributes exactly 2 edges (one to each child).

$$E = 2I$$

**Step 5 — Equate:**

$$2I = I + L - 1$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**

$$I = L - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{L = I + 1}$$

**Step 7 — Total nodes in terms of internal nodes:**

$$n = I + L = I + (I + 1) = 2I + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{n = 2I + 1}$$

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Maximum Height with *n* Nodes

This is the **degenerate** (or *skewed*) case — a tree that is essentially a linked list hanging from the root.

**Step 1.** Consider a chain: ROOT → child → grandchild → ... → *n*-th node.

**Step 2.** This chain has *n* nodes and height *n − 1*. No tree with *n* nodes can have height greater than *n − 1* because:

- The root contributes 1 node.
- Each additional level can contribute at most 1 node in a chain.
- After *n* nodes are exhausted, no more levels can exist.

$$\boxed{h_{\max} = n - 1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students answer $h_{\max} = n$. The correct answer is $n - 1$. This is a 1-mark deduction trap — remember to subtract 1.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Tree Types at a Glance

| Property | General Tree | Binary Tree | BST | AVL Tree | Heap |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Children per node | Any (0 to k) | ≤ 2 | ≤ 2 | ≤ 2 | ≤ 2 |
| Ordering constraint | None | None | Left < Root < Right | Same as BST + balanced | Min-heap or Max-heap |
| Search time | O(n) | O(n) | Avg O(log n), Worst O(n) | O(log n) guaranteed | O(n) |
| Insert time | O(1) at root, else O(n) | O(n) | O(log n) avg | O(log n) | O(log n) |
| Self-balancing | No | No | No | Yes | Yes (by structure) |
| Use case | File system | Expression tree | Symbol table | DB index | Priority queue |

---

## 3.9 Time Complexity Comparison: Linked List vs Array

| Operation | Array | Singly Linked List | Doubly Linked List |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access k-th element | O(1) | O(k) | O(k) |
| Insert at front | O(n) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Insert at end | O(1) amortized | O(n) without tail | O(1) with tail |
| Delete from front | O(n) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Delete from end | O(1) | O(n) | O(1) with tail |
| Memory overhead | 0 extra | 1 pointer/node | 2 pointers/node |
| Cache locality | Excellent | Poor | Poor |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below follow strict Mermaid syntax: **alphanumeric node IDs**, **no reserved keywords as standalone names**, and **raw uppercase labels inside double quotes**.

---

## 4.1 Singly Linked List — Insertion at Beginning (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        HEAD1[HEAD POINTER] --> N1A[NODE 10]
        N1A --> N1B[NODE 20]
        N1B --> N1C[NODE 30]
        N1C --> N1D[NULL]
    end

    subgraph STEP1
        NEWNODE[NEW NODE 5] -.->|PENDING LINK| N2A[NODE 10]
        HEAD2[HEAD POINTER] -.->|STILL POINTS HERE| N2A
    end

    subgraph AFTER
        HEAD3[HEAD POINTER] --> N3A[NODE 5]
        N3A --> N3B[NODE 10]
        N3B --> N3C[NODE 20]
        N3C --> N3D[NODE 30]
        N3D --> N3E[NULL]
    end

    BEFORE ==> STEP1 ==> AFTER
```

**Explanation:** The `HEAD POINTER` is detached from NODE 10 and reattached to NEW NODE 5, which itself now points to NODE 10. Two pointer assignments complete the operation in **O(1)**.

---

## 4.2 Doubly Linked List — Standard Node Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NULL1[NULL] <--> N1[NODE A LEFT POINTER DATA POINTER RIGHT POINTER]
    N1 <--> N2[NODE B LEFT POINTER DATA POINTER RIGHT POINTER]
    N2 <--> N3[NODE C LEFT POINTER DATA POINTER RIGHT POINTER]
    N3 <--> NULL2[NULL]
```

**Architectural Note:** Each node maintains a **bidirectional link**, enabling traversal in both directions at the cost of one extra pointer per node. The HEAD's `LEFT POINTER` and the TAIL's `RIGHT POINTER` both terminate at NULL.

---

## 4.3 Circular Singly Linked List — Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD[HEAD POINTER] --> N1[NODE 10]
    N1 --> N2[NODE 20]
    N2 --> N3[NODE 30]
    N3 -.->|BACK TO HEAD| HEAD

    style HEAD fill:#FFE08A
    style N3 fill:#A8E6CF
```

**Termination Note:** There is **no NULL** in a circular structure. The traversal stopping condition is checked against the HEAD pointer, not NULL.

---

## 4.4 General Tree — Hierarchical Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[ROOT NODE A] --> B[CHILD B]
    ROOT --> C[CHILD C]
    ROOT --> D[CHILD D]
    B --> E[GRANDCHILD E]

    C --> G[GRANDCHILD G]

    style J fill:#BAE1FF
```

**Reading the diagram:** ROOT A has 3 children (B, C, D). Node D has 3 children (H, I, J), illustrating that a **general tree** places no restriction on the number of children per node. The **degree** of node D is 3.

## 4.5 Binary Tree — Anatomy with Annotations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[ROOT A] --> L[LEFT CHILD B]
    R --> RT[RIGHT CHILD C]
    L --> LL[LEFT GRANDCHILD D]
    L --> LR[RIGHT GRANDCHILD E]
    RT --> RL[LEFT GRANDCHILD F]
    RT -.-> X[NO RIGHT CHILD]

    R:::root
    L:::internal
    RT:::internal
    LL:::leaf
    LR:::leaf
    RL:::leaf
    X:::absent

    classDef root fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef internal fill:#FFD93D,color:#000000,stroke:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#6BCB77,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000
    classDef absent fill:#E0E0E0,color:#999999,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

**Annotations:**
- ROOT A is at **depth 0, height 3**.
- Node E is at **depth 2, height 1**.
- Leaves are D, E, F → 3 leaves, so $n_{2} = 3 - 1 = 2$ nodes with 2 children (which are B and C — verifying the identity $n_{0} = n_{2} + 1$).
