# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are **calibrated to the KTU 2024 Scheme assessment pattern**: Part A = 3 marks each (direct, definitions), Part B = 14 marks with internal choice (deep, application-oriented).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between an array and a linked list. Mention any two advantages of a linked list over an array.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Array | Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Memory allocation | Static (compile-time) | Dynamic (run-time) |
| Memory layout | Contiguous | Scattered |
| Access time | O(1) random access | O(n) sequential access |
| Size | Fixed | Growable |
| Insertion/Deletion | O(n) due to shifting | O(1) at head |

**Two advantages of linked list over array:**
1. **Dynamic size:** No need to declare size in advance; nodes are allocated as needed, eliminating memory wastage.
2. **Efficient insertion/deletion:** Insertion/deletion at the beginning takes **O(1)** time, whereas in arrays it requires shifting all subsequent elements, costing **O(n)**.

*(Award 1 mark for the table, 1 mark per advantage, totaling 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the following tree terminologies with one-line illustrations:**
**(i) Leaf node   (ii) Degree of a node   (iii) Sibling nodes**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(i) Leaf Node (1 mark):** A node with **zero children** is called a *leaf node* or *terminal node*. Example: In a tree `A → B, C`, both B and C are leaves if they have no children.

- **(ii) Degree of a Node (1 mark):** The **number of children** a node has is its degree. In `A → B, C, D`, the root A has degree **3**.

- **(iii) Sibling Nodes (1 mark):** Nodes that share the **same parent** are siblings. In `A → B, C, D`, nodes B, C, and D are all siblings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain in detail the different operations that can be performed on a singly linked list. Provide algorithms for insertion at the beginning, insertion at the end, and deletion of a given node.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A singly linked list is a sequence of nodes where each node stores a data field and a pointer to the next node. The list is accessed via a HEAD pointer, and the last node's NEXT points to NULL.

**Operation 1: Insertion at Beginning (2 marks):**

```
Algorithm INSERT_AT_BEGINNING(HEAD, DATA)
1. NEW ← ALLOCATE_NODE()
2. NEW.DATA ← DATA
3. NEW.NEXT ← HEAD
4. HEAD ← NEW
5. RETURN HEAD
```

**Visual trace on `10 → 20 → 30`:**

```
BEFORE: HEAD → [10|N] → [20|N] → [30|NULL]

Step 1: NEW = [5|?]
Step 2: NEW.DATA = 5
Step 3: NEW.NEXT = HEAD       →  NEW now points to old HEAD
Step 4: HEAD = NEW            →  HEAD re-points to NEW

AFTER:  HEAD → [5|N] → [10|N] → [20|N] → [30|NULL]
```

**Time complexity: O(1)** — only two pointer assignments.

**Operation 2: Insertion at End (2 marks):**

```
Algorithm INSERT_AT_END(HEAD, DATA)
1. NEW ← ALLOCATE_NODE()
2. NEW.DATA ← DATA
3. NEW.NEXT ← NULL
4. IF HEAD = NULL THEN
5.     HEAD ← NEW
6.     RETURN HEAD
7. END IF
8. TEMP ← HEAD
9. WHILE TEMP.NEXT ≠ NULL DO
10.    TEMP ← TEMP.NEXT
11. END WHILE
12. TEMP.NEXT ← NEW
13. RETURN HEAD
```

**Time complexity: O(n)** — traversal required.

**Operation 3: Deletion of Node with Value X (2 marks):**

```
Algorithm DELETE_NODE(HEAD, X)
1. IF HEAD = NULL THEN
2.     PRINT "List empty"; RETURN
3. END IF
4. IF HEAD.DATA = X THEN
5.     TEMP ← HEAD
6.     HEAD ← HEAD.NEXT
7.     FREE(TEMP); RETURN
8. END IF
9. PREV ← HEAD
10. CURR ← HEAD.NEXT
11. WHILE CURR ≠ NULL AND CURR.DATA ≠ X DO
12.     PREV ← CURR
13.     CURR ← CURR.NEXT
14. END WHILE
15. IF CURR ≠ NULL THEN
16.     PREV.NEXT ← CURR.NEXT
17.     FREE(CURR)
18. END IF
```

**Time complexity: O(n)** — single traversal.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Define a binary tree. Explain its different types — full, complete, perfect, degenerate, and balanced — with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A binary tree is a tree data structure in which each node has **at most two children**, referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**.

**Type 1: Full (Strict) Binary Tree (1 mark):**
Every node has **either 0 or 2 children**. No node has exactly 1 child.

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
     / \
    D   E
```
Here A has 2 children, B has 2 children, C, D, E are leaves. Total = 5 nodes. Formula: $n = 2I + 1 = 2(2) + 1 = 5$. ✓

**Type 2: Complete Binary Tree (1 mark):**
All levels are completely filled **except possibly the last**, which is filled from **left to right** with no gaps.

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
     / \
    D   E
```
Note: Last level filled from left.

**Type 3: Perfect Binary Tree (1 mark):**
A complete binary tree in which **all leaves are at the same depth**. Every internal node has exactly 2 children, and all leaves are at the same level.

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
     / \ / \
    D  E F  G
```
Total nodes = $2^{h+1} - 1 = 2^{3} - 1 = 7$ (height 2). ✓

**Type 4: Degenerate (Skewed) Tree (1 mark):**
Every internal node has **exactly one child** — essentially a linked list. Height equals $n - 1$.

```
    A
     \
      B
       \
        C
         \
          D
```
With 4 nodes, height = 3.

**Type 5: Balanced Binary Tree (2 marks):**
A binary tree is balanced if, for **every node**, the height difference between its left and right subtrees is **at most 1**.

```
           A
         /   \
        B     C
       / \   /
      D   E F
```
- At A: left height 2, right height 1, diff = 1 ✓
- At B: left height 1, right height 0, diff = 1 ✓
- At C: left height 1, right height -1 (absent, treated as 0), diff = 1 ✓

**Importance (1 mark):** Balanced trees guarantee **O(log n)** search/insert/delete time, making them essential for high-performance applications.

---

### 📕 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the structure and operations of a doubly linked list and a circular linked list with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**Doubly Linked List (DLL) (3.5 marks):**

**Definition:** Each node of a DLL contains three fields — `PREV`, `DATA`, and `NEXT`. The `PREV` of the first node and the `NEXT` of the last node are NULL.

```
NULL ← [P|D|N] ⇄ [P|D|N] ⇄ [P|D|N] → NULL
```

**Insertion of a new node NEW after node P (1.5 marks):**

```
Algorithm DLL_INSERT_AFTER(P, DATA)
1. NEW ← ALLOCATE_NODE()
2. NEW.DATA ← DATA
3. NEW.NEXT ← P.NEXT
4. IF P.NEXT ≠ NULL THEN
5.     P.NEXT.PREV ← NEW
6. END IF
7. P.NEXT ← NEW
8. NEW.PREV ← P
```

**Visual trace:**

```
BEFORE:  ... ⇄ [P|A| ] ⇄ [ |B| ] ⇄ ...

Step 3: NEW.NEXT ← P.NEXT      →  NEW points to where P used to point
Step 5: P.NEXT.PREV ← NEW      →  Former next's PREV now points to NEW
Step 7: P.NEXT ← NEW           →  P now points to NEW
Step 8: NEW.PREV ← P           →  NEW's PREV points back to P

AFTER:   ... ⇄ [P|A|N] ⇄ [N|X|N] ⇄ [N|B| ] ⇄ ...
```

**Deletion of a node (1 mark):** Requires updating the predecessor's NEXT and successor's PREV — exactly the inverse of insertion.

**Circular Linked List (CSLL) (3.5 marks):**

**Definition:** A circular singly linked list is a variation where the **last node points back to the HEAD** instead of NULL.

```
HEAD → [10|] → [20|] → [30|]
  ↑________________________|
```

**Insertion at the end (1.5 marks):**

```
Algorithm CSLL_INSERT_END(HEAD, DATA)
1. NEW ← ALLOCATE_NODE()
2. NEW.DATA ← DATA
3. NEW.NEXT ← HEAD
4. IF HEAD = NULL THEN
5.     HEAD ← NEW
6.     RETURN
7. END IF
8. TEMP ← HEAD
9. WHILE TEMP.NEXT ≠ HEAD DO
10.    TEMP ← TEMP.NEXT
11. END WHILE
12. TEMP.NEXT ← NEW
```

**Applications (1 mark):** Round-robin CPU scheduling, multiplayer game turns, circular buffers in embedded systems.

**Advantage:** Any node can be the starting point, providing flexibility. **Disadvantage:** Risk of infinite loops if termination is not carefully coded.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Define a tree. Explain the following tree terminologies with a diagram: Root, Edge, Parent, Child, Sibling, Leaf, Internal Node, Degree, Depth, Height, Level, and Subtree.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A tree is a non-linear, hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes connected by edges, with exactly one root node, no cycles, and a unique path between any two nodes.

**Reference Diagram (2 marks):**

```
                A          ← Level 0, Depth of A = 0, Height of A = 3
              / | \
             B  C  D       ← Level 1
            /|     |
           E F     G       ← Level 2
           |
           H                ← Level 3
```

**Terminologies (4 marks):**

| # | Term | Definition | Example from Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Root** | The topmost node with no parent | A |
| 2 | **Edge** | The link connecting a parent to its child | A–B, A–C, A–D, B–E, B–F, D–G, E–H |
| 3 | **Parent** | A node that has children | A is parent of B, C, D |
| 4 | **Child** | A node descending from another | B, C, D are children of A |
| 5 | **Sibling** | Nodes sharing the same parent | B, C, D are siblings |
| 6 | **Leaf** | Node with no children (terminal) | C, F, G, H |
| 7 | **Internal Node** | Node with at least one child | A, B, D, E |
| 8 | **Degree** | Number of children of a node | A=3, B=2, D=1, E=1, C=0 |
| 9 | **Depth** | Distance (in edges) from root to node | Depth of H = 3 |
| 10 | **Height** | Longest path from node to a leaf | Height of A = 3, Height of H = 0 |
| 11 | **Level** | All nodes at the same depth | Level 2 = {E, F, G} |
| 12 | **Subtree** | A node and all its descendants | Tree rooted at B is a subtree |

**Verification of edge formula (bonus 0.5 mark):**

$$n = 8, \quad E = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = n - 1 \;\; ✓$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Missing NULL handling:** In DLL insertion, forgetting `IF P.NEXT ≠ NULL THEN P.NEXT.PREV ← NEW` triggers a **segmentation fault** at runtime. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if this guard is absent in your algorithm.
>
> 2. **Mislabeling tree height:** Students frequently confuse **depth** (root → node distance) with **height** (node → leaf distance). The root's depth is 0; the root's height is the tree's overall height. Confusing these costs **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Skipping base cases in recursion:** Tree algorithms are often recursive. Always write the **base case first** (e.g., "if root is NULL, return"). Examiners specifically check for this.
>
> 4. **Omitting "Why" in complexity analysis:** Writing only "Time complexity is O(n)" without explaining *why* (e.g., "because we traverse the list once") loses **1 mark** in 7-mark algorithm questions.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to update TAIL in CSLL insertion:** When inserting at the end of a circular list, the new node must point to HEAD — students often write NULL, causing logical errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Save This for the Day Before the Exam:**

### Linked List Essentials
- [ ] A linked list node contains **DATA + NEXT** (SLL) or **PREV + DATA + NEXT** (DLL).
- [ ] **HEAD** is the entry point; **NULL** marks the end of a non-circular list.
- [ ] **Insertion at HEAD** is **O(1)**; **insertion at TAIL** is **O(n)** without a tail pointer.
- [ ] **Search** is always **O(n)** — there is no random access.
- [ ] **DLL deletion** requires updating **both** predecessor and successor pointers.
- [ ] **Circular list** has no NULL — termination is checked against HEAD.
- [ ] Always **handle the empty list** case (`HEAD = NULL`) first in every algorithm.

### Tree Mathematics Essentials
- [ ] A tree with *n* nodes has exactly **n − 1 edges**.
- [ ] Maximum nodes at level *i* = **2^i** (root at level 0).
- [ ] Maximum nodes in tree of height *h* = **2^(h+1) − 1**.
- [ ] Minimum height with *n* nodes = **⌈log₂(n+1)⌉ − 1**.
- [ ] Maximum height with *n* nodes = **n − 1** (degenerate case).
- [ ] **Fundamental Identity: n₀ = n₂ + 1** for ANY binary tree.
- [ ] **Full BT: L = I + 1**, hence **n = 2I + 1**.

### Tree Terminology Essentials
- [ ] **Root**: topmost, no parent.
- [ ] **Leaf**: zero children.
- [ ] **Internal node**: at least one child.
- [ ] **Degree**: number of children of a node.
- [ ] **Depth**: edges from root to that node.
- [ ] **Height**: edges from that node to deepest leaf.
- [ ] **Level**: set of all nodes at the same depth.

### Tree Types — One-Line Differentiator
- [ ] **Full BT**: every node has 0 or 2 children.
- [ ] **Complete BT**: all levels full except last, filled left-to-right.
- [ ] **Perfect BT**: complete BT where all leaves are at the same depth.
- [ ] **Degenerate BT**: every node has exactly 1 child (linked list shape).
- [ ] **Balanced BT**: |height(left) − height(right)| ≤ 1 for every node.
- [ ] **BST**: BT with Left < Root < Right.
- [ ] **AVL**: self-balancing BST.
- [ ] **Heap**: complete BT with heap-order property.
- [ ] **B-Tree**: multi-way search tree for disk storage.
- [ ] **Trie**: prefix tree for string operations.

### Common KTU Question Patterns
- [ ] "Insert a node at position *k* in SLL" → always walk *k − 1* steps with `PREV` and `CURR`.
- [ ] "Prove that leaves = nodes with 2 children + 1" → use the dual edge-counting derivation (Section 3.5).
- [ ] "What is the minimum height of a binary tree with 1000 nodes?" → apply ⌈log₂(1001)⌉ − 1.
- [ ] "Delete the last node of a CSLL" → traverse until `TEMP->NEXT == HEAD`, then update second-last's NEXT to HEAD.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tree Traversal Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tree Traversal Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tree Traversal** is the systematic process of visiting every node in a tree data structure **exactly once** in a specific, well-defined order. Unlike linear data structures (arrays, linked lists) which have a single natural sequence, a tree is hierarchical and non-linear; therefore, we must impose an explicit order to visit its nodes.

For a binary tree with root node $R$, left subtree $L$, and right subtree $R'$, the problem of traversal is essentially reduced to three decisions:
1. When to visit the **Root**? (Before, Between, or After subtrees)
2. When to traverse the **Left Subtree**?
3. When to traverse the **Right Subtree**?

This three-decision paradigm generates the canonical **Depth First Search (DFS)** traversals, while a level-by-level visit generates the **Breadth First Search (BFS)** traversal.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Family Photo Album

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a multi-generation family tree printed in a photo album. You cannot read it line-by-line like a list. To "see" everyone, you must choose a rule:
> - **Preorder (Root-Left-Right):** "Print the patriarch, then all photos of the *eldest* branch, then the *younger* branch." (Parent before children)
> - **Inorder (Left-Root-Right):** "Scan the album chronologically by age." (Used in BST to get sorted output)
> - **Postorder (Left-Right-Root):** "Delete the album — first remove all descendants, only then remove the patriarch." (Children before parent)
> - **Level Order (BFS):** "Arrange photos by generation — grandparents first row, parents second row, children third row." (Level by level)

This is the same tree, but **4 different visits**, each producing a different output sequence. Tree traversal algorithms are simply the formal coding of these rules.

### 1.3 Syllabus Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, traversal is a **CO2 (Apply)** and **CO3 (Analyze)** level topic. It is the foundational building block for advanced operations: expression evaluation, BST searching, heap construction, tree construction from traversals, and graph DFS.

| Metric | Value (for a tree with $n$ nodes) |
|---|---|
| Number of nodes visited | $n$ |
| Total operations | $O(n)$ |
| Standard metrics | **Time: $O(n)$**, **Space: $O(h)$** (recursion stack) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three DFS Traversals — Operational Logic

Each DFS traversal is defined by the position of the **ROOT visit** relative to its two subtrees. We use the notation:
- $L$ = Traverse left subtree
- $R$ = Traverse right subtree
- $D$ (Data) = Visit root

#### 2.1.1 Preorder Traversal (DLR)
**Logic Steps:**
1. **Visit the Root** first.
2. Recursively traverse the **Left** subtree.
3. Recursively traverse the **Right** subtree.

**Use Case:** Used to create a copy of the tree, get prefix expression of an expression tree.

#### 2.1.2 Inorder Traversal (LDR)
**Logic Steps:**
1. Recursively traverse the **Left** subtree.
2. **Visit the Root** in the middle.
3. Recursively traverse the **Right** subtree.

**Use Case:** For a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**, inorder traversal produces nodes in **ascending sorted order**. This is the most-tested property in KTU exams.

#### 2.1.3 Postorder Traversal (LRD)
**Logic Steps:**
1. Recursively traverse the **Left** subtree.
2. Recursively traverse the **Right** subtree.
3. **Visit the Root** last.

**Use Case:** Used to delete a tree (children freed before parent), evaluate postfix expression, compute directory size.

### 2.2 Breadth First Search — Level Order Traversal

**Logic Steps:**
1. Enqueue the **Root** node.
2. While the queue is **not empty**:
   a. Dequeue a node, visit it.
   b. Enqueue its **left child** (if exists).
   c. Enqueue its **right child** (if exists).

**Use Case:** Used to find the **shortest path** in an unweighted tree, compute tree height iteratively, serialize a tree level-by-level.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important cheat sheet** for traversal problems. Memorize the relationships and you can solve **80%** of KTU 2-mark and 14-mark questions on this module.

| Traversal Type | Visit Order | Recurrence for Output $T(n)$ | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Recursion Stack Depth |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Preorder** | Root $\rightarrow$ Left $\rightarrow$ Right | $T(n) = 1 + T(k) + T(n-k-1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ | $h$ (height) |
| **Inorder** | Left $\rightarrow$ Root $\rightarrow$ Right | $T(n) = T(k) + 1 + T(n-k-1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ | $h$ |
| **Postorder** | Left $\rightarrow$ Right $\rightarrow$ Root | $T(n) = T(k) + T(n-k-1) + 1$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ | $h$ |
| **Level Order** | Level 0, then Level 1, …, Level $h$ | Uses queue, not recursion | $O(n)$ | $O(w)$ | $O(1)$ explicit |

Where:
- $n$ = total number of nodes
- $h$ = height of the tree (best case $\log_2 n$ for balanced, worst case $n$ for skewed)
- $w$ = maximum width of the tree (largest level size)
- $k$ = number of nodes in left subtree of root

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Compiler Design:** Expression trees (arithmetic syntax trees) are traversed using **postorder** (to evaluate), **preorder** (to generate prefix/polish notation), and **inorder** (to generate infix with parentheses).
> - **File Systems (OS):** `du` (disk usage) uses postorder; `tree` command uses level order.
> - **Database Indexing (B-Trees/B+ Trees):** Inorder traversal retrieves sorted records for range queries.
> - **XML/HTML DOM Parsers:** Document Object Model is a tree; traversals enable XPath queries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example Tree (Used Throughout)

Let us define a sample binary tree $BT$ with **7 nodes** to trace all traversals:

$$
BT = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}
$$

Structure:
- Root = $1$
- Left child of $1$ = $2$, Right child of $1$ = $3$
- Left child of $2$ = $4$, Right child of $2$ = $5$
- Left child of $3$ = $6$, Right child of $3$ = $7$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace — Preorder (DLR)

**Algorithm Function:** `Preorder(node)`
1. If `node = NULL`, return.
2. Visit `node` (print data).
3. `Preorder(node.left)`
4. `Preorder(node.right)`

**Trace Table** (each row = one recursive call):

| Call # | Function Call | Action | Output So Far |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Preorder(1)` | Visit 1 | **1** |
| 2 | `Preorder(1.left=2)` | Visit 2 | **1, 2** |
| 3 | `Preorder(2.left=4)` | Visit 4 | **1, 2, 4** |
| 4 | `Preorder(4.left=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 5 | `Preorder(4.right=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 6 | `Preorder(2.right=5)` | Visit 5 | **1, 2, 4, 5** |
| 7 | `Preorder(5.left=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 8 | `Preorder(5.right=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 9 | `Preorder(1.right=3)` | Visit 3 | **1, 2, 4, 5, 3** |
| 10 | `Preorder(3.left=6)` | Visit 6 | **1, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6** |
| 11 | `Preorder(6.left/right=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 12 | `Preorder(3.right=7)` | Visit 7 | **1, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6, 7** |

**Final Preorder Output:** $1, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6, 7$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace — Inorder (LDR)

**Trace Table:**

| Call # | Function Call | Action | Output So Far |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Inorder(1)` | Go left | — |
| 2 | `Inorder(1.left=2)` | Go left | — |
| 3 | `Inorder(2.left=4)` | Go left | — |
| 4 | `Inorder(4.left=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 5 | Visit **4** | Print | **4** |
| 6 | `Inorder(4.right=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 7 | Visit **2** | Print | **4, 2** |
| 8 | `Inorder(2.right=5)` | Go left | — |
| 9 | `Inorder(5.left=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 10 | Visit **5** | Print | **4, 2, 5** |
| 11 | `Inorder(5.right=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 12 | Visit **1** | Print | **4, 2, 5, 1** |
| 13 | `Inorder(1.right=3)` | Go left | — |
| 14 | `Inorder(3.left=6)` | Go left | — |
| 15 | `Inorder(6.left=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 16 | Visit **6** | Print | **4, 2, 5, 1, 6** |
| 17 | `Inorder(6.right=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 18 | Visit **3** | Print | **4, 2, 5, 1, 6, 3** |
| 19 | `Inorder(3.right=7)` | Go left | — |
| 20 | `Inorder(7.left=NULL)` | Return | — |
| 21 | Visit **7** | Print | **4, 2, 5, 1, 6, 3, 7** |

**Final Inorder Output:** $4, 2, 5, 1, 6, 3, 7$

### 3.4 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace — Postorder (LRD)

**Trace Table (key steps only):**

| Call # | Function Call | Action | Output So Far |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Postorder(1)` | Recurse left | — |
| 2 | `Postorder(2)` | Recurse left | — |
| 3 | `Postorder(4)` | Both children NULL | — |
| 4 | Visit **4** | Print | **4** |
| 5 | `Postorder(5)` | Both children NULL | — |
| 6 | Visit **5** | Print | **4, 5** |
| 7 | Visit **2** | Print | **4, 5, 2** |
| 8 | `Postorder(6)` | Both children NULL | — |
| 9 | Visit **6** | Print | **4, 5, 2, 6** |
| 10 | `Postorder(7)` | Both children NULL | — |
| 11 | Visit **7** | Print | **4, 5, 2, 6, 7** |
| 12 | Visit **3** | Print | **4, 5, 2, 6, 7, 3** |
| 13 | Visit **1** | Print | **4, 5, 2, 6, 7, 3, 1** |

**Final Postorder Output:** $4, 5, 2, 6, 7, 3, 1$

### 3.5 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace — Level Order (BFS)

**Data Structure Used:** Queue $Q$ (FIFO)

**Trace Table:**

| Step | Action | Queue State (front → rear) | Visited Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enqueue root 1 | **[1]** | — |
| 2 | Dequeue 1, Visit 1, Enqueue 2, 3 | **[2, 3]** | **1** |
| 3 | Dequeue 2, Visit 2, Enqueue 4, 5 | **[3, 4, 5]** | **1, 2** |
| 4 | Dequeue 3, Visit 3, Enqueue 6, 7 | **[4, 5, 6, 7]** | **1, 2, 3** |
| 5 | Dequeue 4, Visit 4, no children | **[5, 6, 7]** | **1, 2, 3, 4** |
| 6 | Dequeue 5, Visit 5, no children | **[6, 7]** | **1, 2, 3, 4, 5** |
| 7 | Dequeue 6, Visit 6, no children | **[7]** | **1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6** |
| 8 | Dequeue 7, Visit 7, no children | **[]** | **1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7** |
| 9 | Queue empty → STOP | — | — |

**Final Level Order Output:** $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7$

### 3.6 Complexity Derivation

Let $T(n)$ be the time to traverse a tree of $n$ nodes, where the root's left subtree has $k$ nodes and right subtree has $n-k-1$ nodes.

For **preorder**, the algorithm performs:
- 1 unit to visit root
- $T(k)$ to traverse left subtree
- $T(n-k-1)$ to traverse right subtree
- $O(1)$ overhead for the null check

$$
T(n) = T(k) + T(n-k-1) + 1
$$

For a **balanced** tree, $k = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1$. Applying the Master Theorem (Case 2, $a=2, b=2, f(n)=1$):

$$
T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T(n) = O(n)
$$

For a **right-skewed** tree (worst case), $k = 0$:

$$
T(n) = T(0) + T(n-1) + 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T(n) = O(n)
$$

**Conclusion:** All traversals have **$O(n)$ time** in all cases. The recursion stack depth (space) depends on tree height $h$: $O(h) = O(\log n)$ balanced, $O(n)$ skewed.

### 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Compare Inorder, Preorder, Postorder and Level Order traversals." Use this table directly in your answer.

| Property | Preorder | Inorder | Postorder | Level Order |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Order of root visit | **1st** | **2nd (middle)** | **3rd (last)** | By depth |
| Notation | DLR | LDR | LRD | BFS |
| Stack/Queue used | Stack (recursion) | Stack (recursion) | Stack (recursion) | **Queue** |
| BST gives sorted output? | No | **YES** | No | No |
| Used to **delete** tree | No | No | **YES** | No |
| Used to **create** copy | **YES** | No | No | No |
| Expression tree → notation | Prefix | Infix | **Postfix** | — |
| Height of tree evaluable? | No | No | **YES (in 1 pass)** | **YES** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sample Binary Tree Visual Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Node 1: Root] --> B[Node 2: Left Subtree]
    A --> C[Node 3: Right Subtree]
    B --> D[Node 4]
    B --> E[Node 5]
    C --> F[Node 6]
    C --> G[Node 7]
```

### 4.2 DFS Traversal Order Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start: Preorder/Inorder/Postorder] --> Check{Node is NULL?}
    Check -- Yes --> Return1[Return to caller]
    Check -- No --> Visit[Visit node / Push to output]
    Visit --> RecL[Recurse: Left Subtree]
    RecL --> RecR[Recurse: Right Subtree]
    RecR --> Return2[Return to caller]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **order** of "Visit", "Recurse Left", and "Recurse Right" changes between traversals:
> - **Preorder:** Visit → Recurse Left → Recurse Right
> - **Inorder:** Recurse Left → Visit → Recurse Right
> - **Postorder:** Recurse Left → Recurse Right → Visit

### 4.3 BFS Level Order — Queue-Based Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Init[Initialize: Enqueue Root] --> Loop{Queue Empty?}
    Loop -- No --> Deq[Dequeue Front Node]
    Deq --> Process[Visit / Process Node]
    Process --> EnqL[Enqueue Left Child if exists]
    EnqL --> EnqR[Enqueue Right Child if exists]
    EnqR --> Loop
    Loop -- Yes --> Stop[End of Traversal]
```

### 4.4 Traversal Output Map (For the Sample Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Preorder[Preorder Output: Root First]
        P1[1] --> P2[2] --> P3[4] --> P4[5] --> P5[3] --> P6[6] --> P7[7]
    end
    subgraph Inorder[Inorder Output: Root Middle]
        I1[4] --> I2[2] --> I3[5] --> I4[1] --> I5[6] --> I6[3] --> I7[7]
    end
    subgraph Postorder[Postorder Output: Root Last]
        O1[4] --> O2[5] --> O3[2] --> O4[6] --> O5[7] --> O6[3] --> O7[1]
    end
    subgraph LevelOrder[Level Order Output: Row by Row]
        L1[1] --> L2[2] --> L3[3] --> L4[4] --> L5[5] --> L6[6] --> L7[7]
    end
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Recursion Stack Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    CallMain[Call Traversal Root] --> PushR[Push Root on Recursion Stack]
    PushR --> CheckNode{Node Null?}
    CheckNode -- Yes --> PopReturn[Pop and Return]
    CheckNode -- No --> DoAction[Perform visit at correct stage]
    DoAction --> RecurseLeft[Push Left Child]
    RecurseLeft --> RecurseRight[Push Right Child]
    RecurseRight --> PopReturn
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define tree traversal. List the different tree traversal techniques with their order of node visitation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Tree traversal is the process of visiting every node of a tree data structure exactly once in a systematic order, to access or process each element.

**List of Traversals (2 Marks):**
1. **Preorder (DLR):** Root → Left → Right
2. **Inorder (LDR):** Left → Root → Right
3. **Postorder (LRD):** Left → Right → Root
4. **Level Order (BFS):** Visit nodes level by level, from root (Level 0) downward using a queue.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the significance of inorder traversal in a Binary Search Tree (BST)?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When an **inorder traversal** is performed on a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**, the resulting output sequence is in **ascending sorted order** of the node values. This is because of the BST property: for every node, all keys in the left subtree are smaller and all keys in the right subtree are larger. Since inorder visits (Left → Root → Right), the natural sorted sequence emerges. This property is exploited in K-ary sorting, range queries, and validation of BSTs. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** For the binary tree shown below, write the **Preorder, Inorder, Postorder, and Level Order** traversals. Show the trace of the recursive calls clearly. **(7 Marks)**

```
                A
              /   \
             B     C
            / \   / \
           D   E F   G
          / \
         H   I
```

**(b)** Construct a binary tree whose **Preorder** is `A B D H I E J C F G` and **Inorder** is `H D I B J E A F C G`. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Recursive Algorithm Used:** Standard Pre/In/Post with `Visit` at D/L/R positions.

**Step 1 — Preorder (Root-Left-Right):**

Starting at root A:
- Visit A → Go left to B → Visit B → Go left to D → Visit D → Go left to H → Visit H → H has no children → Backtrack → H's right NULL → Back to D → Go right to I → Visit I → Backtrack to B → Go right to E → Visit E → Go left to J → Visit J → Backtrack to E → E's right NULL → Back to A → Go right to C → Visit C → Go left to F → Visit F → Backtrack → Go right to G → Visit G

**Preorder Output:** `A B D H I E J C F G`

**Step 2 — Inorder (Left-Root-Right):**

- Go to leftmost: H → Visit H → Back to D → Visit D → Go to I → Visit I → Back to B → Visit B → Go to E's left: J → Visit J → Back to E → Visit E → Back to A → Visit A → Go to C's left: F → Visit F → Back to C → Visit C → Go to G → Visit G

**Inorder Output:** `H D I B J E A F C G`

**Step 3 — Postorder (Left-Right-Root):**

- Visit H (leaf) → Visit I (leaf) → Visit D → Visit J (leaf) → Visit E → Visit B → Visit F (leaf) → Visit G (leaf) → Visit C → Visit A

**Postorder Output:** `H I D J E B F G C A`

**Step 4 — Level Order (BFS using Queue):**

- Level 0: A
- Level 1: B, C
- Level 2: D, E, F, G
- Level 3: H, I, J

**Level Order Output:** `A B C D E F G H I J`

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Algorithm:** First element of Preorder is always the **Root**.

**Step 1:** Preorder[0] = `A` → **Root = A**. Locate A in Inorder.
- Inorder splits at A into: Left = `H D I B J E`, Right = `F C G`
- So left subtree of A has 6 nodes; right subtree has 3 nodes.

**Step 2:** Next in Preorder = `B` → Root of left subtree of A.
- Locate B in left inorder part `H D I B J E`: position 4.
- B's left = `H D I` (3 nodes), B's right = `J E` (2 nodes).

**Step 3:** Next in Preorder = `D` → Root of B's left subtree.
- Locate D in `H D I`: position 2.
- D's left = `H` (1 node), D's right = `I` (1 node).

**Step 4:** Next in Preorder = `H` → leaf (no children). Next `I` → leaf.

**Step 5:** Next in Preorder = `E` → Root of B's right subtree.
- Locate E in `J E`: position 2.
- E's left = `J` (leaf), E's right = NULL.

**Step 6:** Next in Preorder = `C` → Root of A's right subtree.
- Locate C in `F C G`: position 2.
- C's left = `F` (leaf), C's right = `G` (leaf).

**Final Constructed Tree:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    D --> H
    D --> I
    E --> J
    C --> F
    C --> G
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **Level Order Traversal** algorithm using a queue with a suitable example. Write its **time and space complexity**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the three depth-first traversals: **Preorder, Inorder, and Postorder** in terms of order of root visit, use cases, and recursive call structure. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Algorithm — Level Order Traversal:**

1. Create an empty queue `Q`.
2. Enqueue the root node.
3. **While** `Q` is not empty:
   (a) Dequeue the front node; call it `current`.
   (b) Visit `current` (process/print its data).
   (c) If `current.left` ≠ NULL, enqueue `current.left`.
   (d) If `current.right` ≠ NULL, enqueue `current.right`.
4. End when `Q` becomes empty.

**Worked Example — Tree:**

```
        10
       /  \
      20   30
     / \
    40  50
```

**Trace Table:**

| Step | Dequeue | Visit | Enqueue | Queue State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | — | 10 | [10] |
| 2 | 10 | **10** | 20, 30 | [20, 30] |
| 3 | 20 | **20** | 40, 50 | [30, 40, 50] |
| 4 | 30 | **30** | — | [40, 50] |
| 5 | 40 | **40** | — | [50] |
| 6 | 50 | **50** | — | [] |

**Output:** `10, 20, 30, 40, 50`

**Complexity:**
- **Time Complexity:** Every node is enqueued and dequeued exactly once, and each operation is $O(1)$. Hence $T(n) = O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of nodes.
- **Space Complexity:** The queue holds at most all nodes of one level. The maximum width of a tree is $w$. So space is $O(w)$. In the worst case (complete binary tree at the last level), $w \approx n/2$, giving $O(n)$ space.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

| Aspect | Preorder | Inorder | Postorder |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visit Order** | Root → Left → Right | Left → Root → Right | Left → Right → Root |
| **When root is visited** | **First** | **Middle (after left, before right)** | **Last** |
| **Recursive structure** | `Visit → GoLeft → GoRight` | `GoLeft → Visit → GoRight` | `GoLeft → GoRight → Visit` |
| **Use case 1** | Copying/cloning a tree | BST → sorted output | Deleting a tree |
| **Use case 2** | Prefix expression | Validating BST | Postfix expression |
| **Use case 3** | Directory preorder listing | Kth smallest in BST | Directory size (disk usage) |
| **Stack depth** | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ |
| **Time** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |

**Key Insight:** All three are DFS (use recursion/stack) and have the same complexity. The **only difference is the position of the root's visit** in the recursive call sequence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up Inorder and Preorder (2 marks lost):** Many students write `1 2 3 4 5 6 7` for *both*. Memorize: **In**order = **In**fix (Left-Root-Right), BST gives ascending order.
> 2. **Skipping NULL checks in the trace table (2 marks lost):** When showing recursive calls, you MUST show what happens when `node = NULL` — write "return to caller". A trace table without null returns is considered incomplete.
> 3. **Confusing Stack with Queue for Level Order (1 mark lost):** Level order uses a **Queue (FIFO)**, not a stack. DFS uses a stack (explicit or implicit recursion). This is a favorite KTU trick question.
> 4. **Tree construction: Not locating root in inorder (3 marks lost):** When given Preorder + Inorder, you MUST show the step "Locate root in Inorder, split into left and right inorder subarrays." This step carries 3 of the 7 marks in construction problems.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention space complexity (1 mark lost):** Every traversal question in KTU 2024 scheme expects **both time and space complexity**. Missing space loses easy marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **Definition:** Traversal = visiting every node **exactly once** in a defined order.
- [x] **Three DFS:** Preorder (DLR), Inorder (LDR), Postorder (LRD). The **only difference** is the position of Root visit.
- [x] **One BFS:** Level Order — uses **Queue**, visits level-by-level.
- [x] **BST Property:** Inorder traversal of BST $\Rightarrow$ **ascending sorted order**.
- [x] **Tree Construction:** Preorder + Inorder $\Rightarrow$ Unique tree; Postorder + Inorder $\Rightarrow$ Unique tree. Preorder + Postorder $\Rightarrow$ **NOT unique** (cannot construct unique binary tree).
- [x] **Time Complexity:** All traversals are $O(n)$ because each node is visited exactly once.
- [x] **Space Complexity (Recursive):** $O(h)$ where $h$ = height of tree. $O(\log n)$ for balanced, $O(n)$ for skewed.
- [x] **Space Complexity (Level Order):** $O(w)$ where $w$ = max width (worst case $O(n)$).
- [x] **Application — Expression Tree:** Preorder = Prefix, Inorder = Infix, Postorder = Postfix.
- [x] **Application — Tree Deletion:** Always use **Postorder** (children freed before parent).
- [x] **Application — Tree Copying:** Always use **Preorder** (parent created before children).
- [x] **Common Mistake:** BFS uses a **Queue** (FIFO); DFS uses a **Stack/Recursion** (LIFO).
- [x] **Master Theorem Reminder:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1)$ for balanced trees $\Rightarrow O(n)$.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Mark Pattern:** 2-mark definition + 7-mark trace + 7-mark construction/algorithm = 14 marks typical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Search Trees, Definition of Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Search Trees, Definition of Graphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Search Trees

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
A **Search Tree** is a non-linear, hierarchical data structure that stores a collection of elements (nodes) in a parent-child relationship, organized such that the structure enables efficient **searching, insertion, and deletion** of keys. The most fundamental variant is the **Binary Search Tree (BST)**, a binary tree where for every node **N**:

$$\text{key}(\text{left subtree of } N) \leq \text{key}(N) \leq \text{key}(\text{right subtree of } N)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Binary Search Tree is formally defined as either an **empty tree** or a **non-empty tree** where the left subtree contains only keys **strictly less** than the node's key, and the right subtree contains only keys **strictly greater** than the node's key.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **university library catalogue drawer system**. Every drawer has folders. To find a specific book:
1. Walk to the central catalogue drawer.
2. If your book's call number is **less than** the drawer's label, look in the **left** drawer.
3. If your book's call number is **greater than** the drawer's label, look in the **right** drawer.
4. Repeat until found.

This is exactly how a BST works — at every node, you **prune half** the remaining search space (in the ideal case), making lookups extremely fast.

---

## 1.2 Graphs

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
A **Graph G** is a mathematical structure that models pairwise relationships between objects. It is formally defined as an ordered pair:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where:
- **V** is a **non-empty finite set of vertices** (also called nodes).
- **E** is a set of **edges**, where each edge is a connection between **two vertices**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction from Trees:** A tree is a **special case** of a graph (a connected, acyclic graph). A general graph may contain **cycles**, **disconnected components**, and **self-loops** — none of which are permitted in a tree.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a graph as a **social network like Instagram or Facebook**:
- **People** = Vertices (Nodes)
- **Friendships/Follows** = Edges
- **Instagram's friendship graph** is **undirected** (mutual): A → B means B → A.
- **Twitter's follow graph** is **directed** (one-way): A → B does **not** mean B → A.

Every map (Google Maps), every network (the internet itself), and every dependency diagram (course prerequisites) can be represented as a graph.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties

### Key Metrics for a Binary Search Tree of **n** nodes

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Maximum Height (skewed tree)** | **n − 1** |
| **Minimum Height (balanced tree)** | **⌊log₂(n)⌋** |
| **Time Complexity – Search (best/average)** | **O(log n)** |
| **Time Complexity – Search (worst, skewed)** | **O(n)** |

### Key Metrics for a Graph G = (V, E)

| Property | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Order of Graph** | The number of vertices, denoted **|V|** or **n** |
| **Size of Graph** | The number of edges, denoted **|E|** or **m** |
| **Degree of a vertex** | Number of edges incident on it, denoted **deg(v)** |
| **Sum of all degrees (Handshake Theorem)** | **Σ deg(v) = 2 \|E\|** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Handshake Theorem** is a high-yield KTU formula. It states that the sum of the degrees of all vertices equals **twice** the number of edges (since every edge contributes to the degree of exactly two vertices).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Binary Search Tree (BST) — Structural Logic

### The BST Property (Step-by-Step Logic)

A binary tree **T** is a BST **if and only if** all of the following hold:

1. **The left subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys less than the node's key.**
2. **The right subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys greater than the node's key.**
3. **Both the left and right subtrees must also be Binary Search Trees** (recursive property).
4. **There are no duplicate keys** in the strict definition (some textbook variants allow duplicates in the right subtree only — note this carefully in KTU exams).

### Core BST Operations — Logic Breakdown

**A. Searching for a key K in a BST:**
- **Start** at the root node.
- **If K == root.key** → Return root (search successful).
- **If K < root.key** → Recursively search the **left subtree**.
- **If K > root.key** → Recursively search the **right subtree**.
- **If a null child is reached** → Return "Not Found".

**B. Insertion of a key K:**
- Use the same logic as search, traversing left or right at each node.
- When a **null pointer** is reached, insert the new node at that position.
- This ensures the BST property is maintained automatically.

**C. Deletion of a key K — Three Cases:**
- **Case 1: Leaf node** → Simply remove it.
- **Case 2: Node with one child** → Replace the node with its single child.
- **Case 3: Node with two children** → Replace the node's key with its **inorder successor** (smallest in right subtree) or **inorder predecessor** (largest in left subtree), then delete that successor/predecessor from the subtree.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Inorder Traversal of a BST gives Sorted Order:** Because of the BST ordering property, an inorder traversal (Left → Root → Right) visits the nodes in **ascending key order**. This is a heavily tested KTU concept.

### Inorder Traversal of a BST (Algorithm)

```
INORDER(node):
  if node ≠ NULL:
    INORDER(node.left)
    visit(node)
    INORDER(node.right)
```

### Real-World Utility of BSTs
- **Database indexing** for fast range queries.
- **File system directories** (e.g., NTFS, ext4).
- **Auto-complete** features in search engines.
- **Symbol tables** in compilers.

---

## 2.2 Graph — Structural Logic

### Formal Components of a Graph

- **Vertex (Node)**: A fundamental unit representing an entity.
- **Edge**: A connection between two vertices. Represented as:
  - **Undirected edge**: An unordered pair **(u, v)** — symmetric relationship.
  - **Directed edge (Arc)**: An ordered pair **(u, v)** — one-way relationship (u → v).
- **Adjacent vertices**: Two vertices connected by an edge.
- **Incident edge**: An edge that touches a vertex.
- **Path**: A sequence of vertices where each adjacent pair is connected by an edge.
- **Cycle**: A path that starts and ends at the same vertex, with no repeated vertices (simple cycle).
- **Connected graph**: Every vertex is reachable from every other vertex.

### Graph Terminology Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Simple Graph** | No self-loops, no parallel edges. |
| **Multi-graph** | Allows parallel edges between the same pair of vertices. |
| **Pseudo-graph** | Allows self-loops and parallel edges. |
| **Complete Graph (Kₙ)** | Every pair of distinct vertices is connected by an edge. **\|E\| = n(n−1)/2**. |
| **Bipartite Graph** | Vertices can be partitioned into two sets with edges only between sets. |
| **Subgraph** | A graph formed from a subset of vertices and edges of G. |
| **Degree Sequence** | The sequence of degrees of all vertices (sorted in non-increasing order). |

### KTU Formula Sheet — Graphs

| Formula | Expression | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Handshake Theorem** | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2\vert E\vert$ | Sum of degrees = 2 × edges. |
| **Maximum Edges (undirected)** | $\vert E\vert_{\max} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | For a simple undirected graph with n vertices. |
| **Maximum Edges (directed)** | $\vert E\vert_{\max} = n(n-1)$ | For a simple directed graph with n vertices. |
| **Complete Graph Edges** | $\vert E\vert = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Every pair connected. |
| **In-degree Sum (directed)** | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg^-(v) = \vert E\vert$ | Sum of in-degrees = total edges. |
| **Out-degree Sum (directed)** | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg^+(v) = \vert E\vert$ | Sum of out-degrees = total edges. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In a **directed graph**, the sum of in-degrees equals the sum of out-degrees equals **|E|** (NOT 2|E|). The factor of 2 only applies to undirected graphs.

### Real-World Utility of Graphs
- **Google Maps / GPS navigation** (shortest path algorithms like Dijkstra's).
- **Social networks** (friend suggestions, community detection).
- **Internet routing** (BGP, OSPF use graph models).
- **Compiler design** (Directed Acyclic Graphs for expression optimization).
- **Bioinformatics** (protein-protein interaction networks).
- **Recommendation systems** (movie/product graphs on Netflix/Amazon).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing a BST

### Problem
Insert the keys **[50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80]** into an initially empty BST, in that order. Then:
1. Draw the final BST.
2. Find the inorder traversal.
3. Search for key **40** and list the nodes visited.
4. Search for key **45** and state the result.
5. Delete key **30** using inorder successor replacement.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Insert 50 (root)**

$$T = \{50\}$$

**Step 2: Insert 30** — 30 < 50 → goes to the left of 50.

$$T = \{50, \text{left} \to 30\}$$

**Step 3: Insert 70** — 70 > 50 → goes to the right of 50.

$$T = \{50, 30 \text{ (left)}, 70 \text{ (right)}\}$$

**Step 4: Insert 20** — 20 < 50 (go left), 20 < 30 (go left) → left child of 30.

**Step 5: Insert 40** — 40 < 50 (go left), 40 > 30 (go right) → right child of 30.

**Step 6: Insert 60** — 60 > 50 (go right), 60 < 70 (go left) → left child of 70.

**Step 7: Insert 80** — 80 > 50 (go right), 80 > 70 (go right) → right child of 70.

### Final BST (after all insertions)

```
            50
           /  \
         30    70
        /  \   / \
      20   40 60  80
```

### Step 2 — Inorder Traversal (Left → Root → Right)

**Logic:** Inorder recursively visits the left subtree, prints the node, then visits the right subtree.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inorder}(20) &= 20 \\
\text{Inorder}(30) &= \text{Inorder}(20) \to 30 \to \text{Inorder}(40) = 20, 30, 40 \\
\text{Inorder}(50) &= \text{Inorder}(30) \to 50 \to \text{Inorder}(70) \\
&= (20, 30, 40), 50, (60, 70, 80) \\
\text{Final Inorder} &= 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The inorder traversal yields keys in **strictly ascending sorted order** — confirming the BST property.

### Step 3 — Search for Key 40

**Trace the algorithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Visit } 50 &: \; 40 < 50 \Rightarrow \text{Go LEFT} \\
\text{Visit } 30 &: \; 40 > 30 \Rightarrow \text{Go RIGHT} \\
\text{Visit } 40 &: \; 40 == 40 \Rightarrow \textbf{SUCCESS} \\
\text{Nodes visited} &= \{50, 30, 40\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Search for Key 45

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Visit } 50 &: \; 45 < 50 \Rightarrow \text{Go LEFT} \\
\text{Visit } 30 &: \; 45 > 30 \Rightarrow \text{Go RIGHT} \\
\text{Visit } 40 &: \; 45 > 40 \Rightarrow \text{Go RIGHT} \\
\text{Node 40's right child} &= \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \textbf{SEARCH FAILED} \\
\text{Nodes visited} &= \{50, 30, 40\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Delete Key 30 (Inorder Successor Method)

**Logic:** Node **30** has two children. Find the **inorder successor** — the leftmost node in its right subtree.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Right subtree of 30} &= \{40\} \\
\text{Leftmost node in right subtree} &= 40 \\
\text{Replace 30's key with 40} \\
\text{Delete original node 40 (which is a leaf — Case 1)} \\
\text{Final Tree} &: \\
&\quad\quad\quad 50 \\
&\quad\quad / \quad \backslash \\
&\quad 40 \quad\quad 70 \\
&\quad \backslash \quad\quad/ \quad \backslash \\
&\quad\quad 60 \quad\quad 80
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Graph Handshake Theorem Application

### Problem
In an undirected graph with **8 vertices**, the sum of degrees is **20**. Determine:
1. Number of edges.
2. Whether a **simple graph** with 8 vertices can have this degree sum.
3. Maximum possible edges.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Apply the Handshake Theorem**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) &= 2 \vert E\vert \\
20 &= 2 \vert E\vert \\
\vert E\vert &= \dfrac{20}{2} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of edges = 10.**

**Step 2: Validity Check**

For a simple graph with **n = 8** vertices, maximum degree of any vertex = **7** (connected to all other 7).

The maximum sum of degrees for a simple graph:

$$
\sum_{\max} \deg(v) = 8 \times 7 = 56 \Rightarrow \vert E\vert_{\max} = 28
$$

Since **20 ≤ 56**, a simple graph **CAN** have this degree sum. ✓

**Step 3: Maximum Possible Edges**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert E\vert_{\max} &= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{8 \times 7}{2} = 28
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** For an undirected graph, the sum of degrees must be **even** (since it equals 2|E|). This is a quick sanity check students should always apply in exams.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Graph Representation Analysis

### Problem
Given a directed graph with **V = {A, B, C, D}** and **E = {(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (C,D), (D,A)}**, compute:
1. In-degree and out-degree of every vertex.
2. Total edges.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vertex A:} \quad & \deg^+(A) = 2 \; (A \to B, A \to C) \\
                        & \deg^-(A) = 1 \; (D \to A) \\
\text{Vertex B:} \quad & \deg^+(B) = 1 \; (B \to D) \\
                        & \deg^-(B) = 1 \; (A \to B) \\
\text{Vertex C:} \quad & \deg^+(C) = 1 \; (C \to D) \\
                        & \deg^-(C) = 1 \; (A \to C) \\
\text{Vertex D:} \quad & \deg^+(D) = 1 \; (D \to A) \\
                        & \deg^-(D) = 2 \; (B \to D, C \to D)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\sum \deg^+ = 2+1+1+1 = 5 = \vert E\vert \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\sum \deg^- = 1+1+1+2 = 5 = \vert E\vert \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **directed graph**, the sum of in-degrees equals the sum of out-degrees equals **|E|** (not 2|E|). The factor of 2 appears only for undirected graphs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BST Construction Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below illustrates the **stepwise insertion flow** of a BST, showing decision logic at every node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Insert Key K] --> B{K is NULL?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return NULL - Empty Tree]
    B -- No --> D{K < node.key?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Recurse into Left Subtree]
    D -- No --> F{K > node.key?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Recurse into Right Subtree]
    F -- No --> H[Duplicate - Reject or Update]
    E --> I{Left child NULL?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Create New Node Here]
    I -- No --> D
    G --> K{Right child NULL?}
    K -- Yes --> J
    K -- No --> D
    J --> L[Insertion Complete]
    C --> M[End]
    H --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 BST Deletion State Machine (Three Cases)

The following flowchart captures the **three-case decision logic** for BST deletion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Delete Key K] --> Q{Find Node N with key K}
    Q -- Not Found --> R[Return - No Deletion]
    Q -- Found --> S{Case Check}
    S --> S1[Case 1: Leaf Node]
    S --> S2[Case 2: One Child]
    S --> S3[Case 3: Two Children]
    S1 --> T1[Remove Node N - Set Parent Link to NULL]
    S2 --> T2[Replace N with its Single Child]
    S3 --> T3[Find Inorder Successor S in Right Subtree]
    T3 --> T4[Copy S.key to N.key]
    T4 --> T5[Delete S from Right Subtree - Recursive]
    T1 --> U[Deletion Complete]
    T2 --> U
    T5 --> U
    R --> V[End]
    U --> V
```

## 4.3 Graph Terminology — Visual Taxonomy

The diagram below maps the **classification hierarchy of graphs** as per KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GR[Graph G of order n] --> UNDIR[Undirected Graph]
    GR --> DIR[Directed Graph - Digraph]
    UNDIR --> SIMPLE[Simple Graph: No loops, no parallel edges]
    UNDIR --> MULTI[Multi-graph: Parallel edges allowed]
    UNDIR --> PSEUDO[Pseudo-graph: Self-loops allowed]
    SIMPLE --> COMP[Complete Graph Kn: n n-1 / 2 edges]
    SIMPLE --> BIP[Bipartite Graph: Two vertex sets]
    SIMPLE --> REG[Regular Graph: All degrees equal]
    DIR --> SIMPLED[Simple Digraph]
    DIR --> TOURN[Tournament: Exactly one edge between every pair]
```

## 4.4 Sample Graph G = (V, E) for Analysis

The diagram below depicts an **undirected graph** with V = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and E = {(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5)}.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1((1)) --- node2((2))
    node1((1)) --- node3((3))
    node2((2)) --- node3((3))
    node3((3)) --- node4((4))
    node4((4)) --- node5((5))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Graph:**
> - **|V| = 5**, **|E| = 5**
> - **deg(1) = 2**, **deg(2) = 2**, **deg(3) = 3**, **deg(4) = 2**, **deg(5) = 1**
> - **Sum of degrees = 2+2+3+2+1 = 10 = 2|E|** ✓ (Handshake Theorem verified)
> - The path from vertex 2 to vertex 5 is: **2 → 3 → 4 → 5** (length 3)
> - There is a **cycle**: 1 → 2 → 3 → 1 (length 3)
