**Model Answer:**

A greedy algorithm is a problem-solving strategy that builds a solution incrementally by making the **locally optimal choice** at each step, with the goal of reaching a **global optimum**, without revisiting prior decisions.

The two key properties are:

1. **Greedy-Choice Property:** A globally optimal solution can be reached by making a sequence of locally optimal (greedy) choices.
2. **Optimal Substructure:** An optimal solution to the entire problem contains within it optimal solutions to its subproblems.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Differentiate between the **0/1 Knapsack** and the **Fractional Knapsack** problems. Which one can be solved by a greedy algorithm, and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 0/1 Knapsack | Fractional Knapsack |
|---|---|---|
| Item selection | Whole items only | Items can be split into fractions |
| Decision variable | $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ | $0 \le x_i \le 1$ |
| Greedy applicable? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Reason | Discrete nature breaks optimal substructure | Continuous fractions allow greedy-choice property |

The **Fractional Knapsack** is solvable greedily by sorting items on the value-to-weight ratio $r_i = v_i / w_i$ and taking items in decreasing order of $r_i$. The **0/1 Knapsack** requires **Dynamic Programming**, because the greedy choice (e.g., highest ratio) can force us to skip a high-value item that would have completed the knapsack.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Consider the following set of activities. Apply the **greedy activity selection algorithm** (choosing activities by earliest finish time) to find the maximum number of activities that can be performed by a single person.

| Activity | A1 | A2 | A3 | A4 | A5 | A6 | A7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start | 1 | 3 | 0 | 5 | 8 | 5 | 6 |
| Finish | 2 | 4 | 6 | 7 | 9 | 9 | 10 |

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Explain why the greedy choice of selecting the activity with the **earliest finish time** always yields an optimal solution to the activity selection problem. (Prove the greedy-choice property.)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Greedy Execution:**

**Step 1: Sort by finish time $f_i$ ascending.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Order: } A_1(2), A_2(4), A_4(7), A_3(6), A_6(9), A_5(9), A_7(10)
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — re-sort correctly:

$$A_1(f=2),\ A_2(f=4),\ A_3(f=6),\ A_4(f=7),\ A_5(f=9),\ A_6(f=9),\ A_7(f=10)$$

**Step 2: Greedy selection.**

| Step | Activity | Start $s_i$ | Finish $f_i$ | Last Finish | $s_i \ge \text{last\_finish}$? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_1$ | 1 | 2 | 0 | ✓ | **Select** |
| 2 | $A_2$ | 3 | 4 | 2 | ✓ ($3 \ge 2$) | **Select** |
| 3 | $A_3$ | 0 | 6 | 4 | ✗ ($0 < 4$) | Skip |
| 4 | $A_4$ | 5 | 7 | 4 | ✓ ($5 \ge 4$) | **Select** |
| 5 | $A_5$ | 8 | 9 | 7 | ✓ ($8 \ge 7$) | **Select** |
| 6 | $A_6$ | 5 | 9 | 9 | ✗ | Skip |
| 7 | $A_7$ | 6 | 10 | 9 | ✗ | Skip |

**Final Selected Set:** $\{A_1, A_2, A_4, A_5\}$ — **4 activities**.

**(b) Proof of Greedy-Choice Property (Exchange Argument):**

**Claim:** Let $a_1$ be the activity with the earliest finish time. There exists an optimal solution that includes $a_1$ as its first activity.

**Proof:** Let $O$ be any optimal solution, with $a_k$ being its first activity. Since $a_1$ has the earliest finish time, $f(a_1) \le f(a_k)$. Since $a_k$ starts no earlier than $a_1$ can end (by the compatibility of $O$), replacing $a_k$ with $a_1$ still keeps the solution valid. By induction, the greedy solution matches the optimum. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Solve the **Fractional Knapsack Problem** using a greedy approach for the following instance. Knapsack capacity $W = 60$.

| Item $i$ | Weight $w_i$ | Value $v_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 60 |
| 2 | 20 | 100 |
| 3 | 30 | 120 |
| 4 | 40 | 200 |

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Why does the same greedy strategy **fail** for the 0/1 Knapsack variant? Provide a counter-example.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fractional Knapsack — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Compute value-to-weight ratios.**

$$\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= 60/10 = 6.0 \\
r_2 &= 100/20 = 5.0 \\
r_3 &= 120/30 = 4.0 \\
r_4 &= 200/40 = 5.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Sort items by $r_i$ descending.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Order: } I_1(6.0),\ I_2(5.0),\ I_4(5.0),\ I_3(4.0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Greedily fill the knapsack ($W = 60$).**

| Step | Item | $w_i$ | $r_i$ | Remaining $R$ | Fraction $x_i$ | Value Added |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_1$ | 10 | 6.0 | 50 | 1.0 | 60 |
| 2 | $I_2$ | 20 | 5.0 | 30 | 1.0 | 100 |
| 3 | $I_4$ | 40 | 5.0 | 30 < 40 | 30/40 = 0.75 | $200 \times 0.75 = 150$ |
| 4 | $I_3$ | 30 | 4.0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 4: Total value.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\max} &= 60 + 100 + 150 = 310 \\
\text{with fractions } x_1 = 1,\ x_2 = 1,\ x_4 = 0.75,\ x_3 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i x_i &= 10(1) + 20(1) + 40(0.75) + 30(0) \\
&= 10 + 20 + 30 = 50 \le 60 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Why Greedy Fails for 0/1 Knapsack — Counter-Example:**

Consider $W = 50$, with three items:

| Item | $w_i$ | $v_i$ | $r_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 30 | 60 | 2.0 |
| B | 30 | 60 | 2.0 |
| C | 40 | 100 | 2.5 |

**Greedy choice (highest ratio first):** Take C fully (value 100), capacity left = 10 — no other item fits. Total = 100.

**Optimal 0/1 choice:** Take A + B (total weight 60... too heavy). Actually, take A + C = 30+40 = 70 (over). Take B + C = 70 (over). Take A + B = 60 (over). Take C alone = 100. Take A alone = 60. Take A + B = over. Hmm — better example:

**Cleaner Counter-Example:** $W = 10$. Items: A $(w=6, v=8, r=1.33)$, B $(w=4, v=5, r=1.25)$, C $(w=4, v=5, r=1.25)$.

- **Greedy:** Take A ($r=1.33$, fits), then no more room — **total = 8**.
- **Optimal 0/1:** Take B + C (weight 8 ≤ 10) — **total = 10**.

The greedy algorithm fails because once A is chosen, B and C can no longer both be taken, even though together they yield more value. The greedy choice property is **violated** — a locally optimal choice (A's high ratio) prevents the global optimum.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **confuse Fractional with 0/1 Knapsack** in exams. Read the problem statement carefully:
> - "Items can be split" / "fractions allowed" → **Fractional** → Greedy works.
> - "Whole items only" / "each item at most once" → **0/1** → Use **Dynamic Programming**.
> 
> If you write a greedy algorithm for 0/1 Knapsack, **you will lose 4–5 marks out of 7**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Greedy Algorithm** = locally optimal choice at each step → hope for global optimum.
- ✅ **Two required properties:** *Greedy-Choice Property* + *Optimal Substructure*.
- ✅ **Activity Selection:** Sort by **earliest finish time** → $O(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Fractional Knapsack:** Sort by **value-to-weight ratio** $v_i / w_i$ → take items greedily, fill with fraction if needed.
- ✅ **0/1 Knapsack:** **NOT** solvable by greedy — requires **DP** of $O(nW)$.
- ✅ **Huffman Coding:** Repeatedly merge the **two lowest-frequency nodes** until one tree remains.
- ✅ **Job Sequencing:** Sort by **decreasing profit**, place each in the **latest free slot** $\le d_i$. Use **DSU (Disjoint Set Union)** for $O(n \log n)$ slot finding.
- ✅ **Dijkstra's SSSP:** Pick **minimum-distance unvisited vertex** and relax neighbors — **fails on negative edges**.
- ✅ **Prim's / Kruskal's MST:** Always pick the **minimum-weight safe edge** (Prim: from tree; Kruskal: globally, no cycle).
- ✅ Greedy is **fast but not always optimal** — always **verify** the two properties before claiming optimality.
- ✅ **Time complexities to memorize for KTU boards:**
  - Activity Selection: $O(n \log n)$
  - Fractional Knapsack: $O(n \log n)$
  - Job Sequencing: $O(n^2)$ (or $O(n \log n)$ with DSU)
  - Huffman Coding: $O(n \log n)$
  - Prim's: $O(E \log V)$ (with binary heap)
  - Kruskal's: $O(E \log E)$
  - Dijkstra's: $O((V+E) \log V)$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Divide and Conquer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Divide and Conquer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — DIVIDE AND CONQUER

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Divide and Conquer (D&C)** is an algorithmic paradigm that solves a problem by recursively breaking it down into **two or more sub-problems of the same (or related) type**, until these sub-problems become simple enough to be solved directly. The solutions to the sub-problems are then **combined** to give a solution to the original problem.

The D&C strategy follows a strict three-step template that must be present in every algorithm of this class:

| Step | Operation | Description |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **1. Divide** | Partition | Split the problem instance of size $n$ into $a$ smaller sub-instances, each of size $\approx n/b$. |
| **2. Conquer** | Solve Recursively | Solve each sub-instance independently. If small enough, solve directly (base case). |
| **3. Combine** | Merge | Merge the sub-solutions to form the solution of the original problem. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERAT751 – Module 4):**
> The standard D&C algorithms covered are: **Binary Search, Merge Sort, Quick Sort, and Strassen's Matrix Multiplication**. Their worst-case / average-case time complexities using **Asymptotic Notations** and **Master's Theorem** are examinable.

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy — "The Captain of a Pirate Ship" 🏴‍☠️

Imagine a pirate captain who has found a treasure map but cannot read it alone. He calls his **2 most trusted lieutenants**, gives each half the map, and tells them: *"Decode your half the same way I asked you."* Each lieutenant, in turn, calls 2 of his own sailors, and so on, until a sailor finds a piece so small he can read it instantly (base case).

Once each sailor reports back, the lieutenants stitch their sailors' answers together. Finally, the captain stitches the lieutenants' answers into one complete translation. **This is Divide and Conquer.**

- **Divide** → Captain splits the map into halves.
- **Conquer** → Recursive decoding until a single piece is readable.
- **Combine** → Stitching translations upward in the recursion tree.

The efficiency comes from **parallelism and reduced work at each level**, not from a single "hero" trying to decode the entire map at once.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generic Recurrence of a Divide-and-Conquer Algorithm

A general D&C algorithm with branching factor $a \ge 1$, sub-problem size factor $b > 1$, and combine cost $f(n)$ obeys the recurrence:

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases} \Theta(1) & \text{if } n \le n_0 \\[4pt] a\,T(n/b) + f(n) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $a$ = **number of sub-problems** generated at each recursive call.
- $n/b$ = **size of each sub-problem** (assumed equal for simplicity).
- $f(n)$ = **cost of dividing** the problem + **cost of combining** the sub-solutions.
- $n_0$ = the **threshold size** (base case) below which the problem is solved directly.

### 2.2 The Master Theorem (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Theorem (Akra–Bazzi Special Case for constant $a, b$):**
> Let $a \ge 1$, $b > 1$ be constants and $f(n)$ be asymptotically positive. Define the **critical exponent** $n^{\log_b a}$. Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$:

| Case | Condition | Verdict | Intuition |
|------|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O\bigl(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}\bigr)$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_b a}\bigr)$ | Combine cost is dominated by leaves (recursion is the bottleneck). |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n\bigr)$ for $k \ge 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n\bigr)$ | Recursion and combine are balanced. |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega\bigl(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon}\bigr)$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, **and** $a\,f(n/b) \le c\,f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ and large $n$ (regularity) | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ | Root dominates — combine cost overwhelms recursive work. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary / "Gap" Condition:**
> There is a **gap** between Cases 1 and 3: if $f(n)$ is polynomially smaller than $n^{\log_b a}$ but not by an $n^{\epsilon}$ factor (e.g., $f(n) = n^{\log_b a} / \log n$), **Master Theorem does not apply**. Use Akra–Bazzi or recursion-tree.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Recurrences of Standard Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, you are expected to (i) write the recurrence, (ii) identify $a$, $b$, $f(n)$, (iii) apply Master Theorem, and (iv) state the final $\Theta$ bound.

| Algorithm | Recurrence $T(n)$ | $a$ | $b$ | $f(n)$ | $\log_b a$ | Final $\Theta$ |
|-----------|-------------------|-----|-----|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Binary Search** | $T(n) = T(n/2) + \Theta(1)$ | 1 | 2 | $O(1)$ | 0 | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| **Merge Sort** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | 2 | 2 | $O(n)$ | 1 | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| **Quick Sort (avg)** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | 2 | 2 | $O(n)$ | 1 | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| **Quick Sort (worst)** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)$ | 1 | — | $O(n)$ | — | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| **Strassen's MM** | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ | 7 | 2 | $O(n^2)$ | $\log_2 7 \approx 2.807$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})$ |
| **Naïve MM** | $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ | 8 | 2 | $O(n^2)$ | 3 | $\Theta(n^3)$ |
| **Karatsuba** | $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | 3 | 2 | $O(n)$ | $\log_2 3 \approx 1.585$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.585})$ |

### 2.4 Why Divide-and-Conquer Wins — Engineering Utility

| Property | Real-world Engineering Utility |
|----------|--------------------------------|
| **Sub-problem independence** | Enables **parallel computing** — sub-problems run on different cores/GPUs. |
| **Cache locality** | Sub-problems fit in fast memory → used in high-performance computing (HPC). |
| **Predictable complexity** | Master Theorem gives an upfront $\Theta$ bound → crucial for SLA-bound systems (telecom, finance). |
| **Recursive structure** | Maps naturally to architectures like **MapReduce, fork–join pools, MPI grids**. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort)

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
Compare with the generic form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$:
- $a = 2$ (two recursive calls).
- $b = 2$ (each sub-problem is half the size).
- $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2 — Compute the critical exponent.**
$$
\log_b a = \log_2 2 = 1
$$
So the leaf-bound is $n^{\log_b a} = n^{1} = n$.

**Step 3 — Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.**
$$
f(n) = n = \Theta(n^{1}) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_2 2}\bigr)
$$
This matches **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Step 4 — Apply Case 2.**
$$
T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n\bigr) = \Theta\bigl(n \cdot \log n\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Verification via Recursion Tree.**
At level $i$ of the recursion tree:
- Number of sub-problems: $2^{i}$
- Size of each sub-problem: $n/2^{i}$
- Work per sub-problem at this level (combine cost): $n/2^{i}$
- Total work at level $i$: $2^{i} \cdot (n / 2^{i}) = n$

The tree has depth $\log_2 n$. So:
$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n} n \;=\; n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) \;=\; \Theta(n \log n)
$$

✅ Both methods agree: $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solving $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$ (Strassen's Multiplication)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- $a = 7$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the critical exponent.**
$$
\log_b a = \log_2 7 \approx 2.8074
$$
Thus $n^{\log_b a} \approx n^{2.807}$.

**Step 3 — Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^{2.807}$.**
Since
$$
n^{2} = O\bigl(n^{2.807 - \epsilon}\bigr)
$$
with $\epsilon = 0.807 > 0$, we are in **Case 1** of the Master Theorem.

**Step 4 — Apply Case 1.**
$$
T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_b a}\bigr) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_2 7}\bigr) \approx \Theta\bigl(n^{2.807}\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with naive MM.**
Naïve matrix multiplication has $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + n^{2}$ → $\log_2 8 = 3$ → $\Theta(n^{3})$.
Strassen saves roughly a factor of $n^{0.193}$ — a huge win for very large matrices (used in deep learning kernels).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solving $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ (Binary Search)

**Step 1 — Identify.**
- $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 1$.

**Step 2 — Critical exponent.**
$$
\log_b a = \log_2 1 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad n^{0} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**
$f(n) = 1 = \Theta(n^{0})$ → **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Step 4 — Apply.**
$$
T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{0} \cdot \log^{1} n\bigr) = \Theta(\log n)
$$

✅ Matches the well-known $\log_2 n$ comparisons of binary search.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Solving $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^{2}\log n$ (Regularity Test)

**Step 1 — Identify.**
$a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^{2} \log n$.

**Step 2 — Critical exponent.**
$\log_2 4 = 2$, so leaf work is $n^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Compare.**
$f(n) = n^{2} \log n = \Theta(n^{2} \cdot \log^{1} n)$ → **Case 2** with $k = 1$.

**Step 4 — Apply.**
$$
T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{2} \cdot \log^{2} n\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Check regularity (Case 3 only, not needed here).**
Case 3 requires $a\,f(n/b) \le c\,f(n)$ for some $c < 1$. We are in Case 2, so regularity is irrelevant.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Situation | Recommended Tool | Why |
|-----------|------------------|-----|
| $a, b$ are constants, $f(n)$ is polynomial or polylog | **Master Theorem** | Fastest, board-exam friendly. |
| $f(n)$ falls in the "gap" between Cases 1 and 3 | **Akra–Bazzi Method** | Generalised Master Theorem. |
| Sub-problem sizes are unequal (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$) | **Recursion Tree / Substitution** | Master Theorem assumes equal size. |
| You want a tight $\Theta$ and have a guessed answer | **Substitution Method** | Best for proving lower / upper bounds formally. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Divide-and-Conquer Recursion Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Problem P size n"] --> B1["Sub P1 size nb"]
    A --> B2["Sub P2 size nb"]
    A --> B3["Sub Pa size nb"]
    B1 --> C1["Sub P11 size nb2"]
    B1 --> C2["Sub P1a size nb2"]
    B2 --> D1["Sub P21 size nb2"]
    B3 --> E1["Sub Pa1 size nb2"]
    B3 --> E2["Sub Pa2 size nb2"]
    C1 --> F1["Base case size n0"]
    C2 --> F2["Base case size n0"]
    D1 --> F3["Base case size n0"]
    E1 --> F4["Base case size n0"]
    E2 --> F5["Base case size n0"]
    F1 --> G1["Combine stage size logb n"]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    F5 --> G1
    G1 --> H["Final answer Theta expression"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the tree:** Each downward edge represents a **Divide** operation. Each upward merge represents a **Combine** operation. The depth of the tree is $\log_b n$, and the total leaves are $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of D&C Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["Input Instance size n"] --> DIVIDE["Divide Module splitter"]
    DIVIDE --> SUB1["Sub-problem 1 size nb"]
    DIVIDE --> SUB2["Sub-problem 2 size nb"]
    DIVIDE --> SUBA["Sub-problem a size nb"]
    SUB1 --> REC1["Recursive Solver D and C"]
    SUB2 --> REC2["Recursive Solver D and C"]
    SUBA --> RECA["Recursive Solver D and C"]
    REC1 --> OUT1["Partial Solution 1"]
    REC2 --> OUT2["Partial Solution 2"]
    RECA --> OUTA["Partial Solution a"]
    OUT1 --> COMBINE["Combine Module merger"]
    OUT2 --> COMBINE
    OUTA --> COMBINE
    COMBINE --> OUTPUT["Final Solution"]
```

---

### 4.3 Recursion Tree for Merge Sort — Visual Cost Analysis

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0["Level 0: 1 node of size n, work n total n"]
    L0 --> L1a["Level 1 Left: 1 node size n2 work n2"]
    L0 --> L1b["Level 1 Right: 1 node size n2 work n2"]
    L1a --> L2a["Level 2 LL: size n4 work n4"]
    L1a --> L2b["Level 2 LR: size n4 work n4"]
    L1b --> L2c["Level 2 RL: size n4 work n4"]
    L1b --> L2d["Level 2 RR: size n4 work n4"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost per level:** At level $i$, there are $2^{i}$ sub-problems, each contributing $n/2^{i}$ work. **Total per level = $n$** (constant across levels). **Number of levels = $\log_2 n + 1$.** Hence total work = $n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

### 4.4 Comparison Tree — Naïve MM vs Strassen's MM

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Naive Matrix Multiplication"] --> A1["8 recursive calls size n2"]
    A1 --> A2["Combine cost Theta n2"]
    A2 --> A3["Total Theta n3"]
    B["Strassen Matrix Multiplication"] --> B1["7 recursive calls size n2"]
    B1 --> B2["Combine cost Theta n2"]
    B2 --> B3["Total Theta n log2 7 approx 2.807"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **single saved recursive call** (8 → 7) translates to an exponent drop from **3 → 2.807**, which is a **massive asymptotic gain** for large $n$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**  
Define the **Divide and Conquer** algorithmic strategy. State any **two** algorithms that follow this paradigm.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Divide and Conquer** is an algorithm design technique in which a problem of size $n$ is partitioned into smaller sub-problems of the same type, each sub-problem is solved recursively, and the sub-solutions are combined to obtain the solution of the original problem.

The three phases are:
1. **Divide** — break the problem into smaller sub-problems.
2. **Conquer** — solve the sub-problems recursively or directly if they are small enough (base case).
3. **Combine** — merge the sub-solutions to form the solution of the original problem.

**Two D&C algorithms (any two):**
1. **Binary Search** — $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) = \Theta(\log n)$.
2. **Merge Sort** — $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

*(Acceptable: Quick Sort, Strassen's Matrix Multiplication, Karatsuba Integer Multiplication, Closest Pair of Points.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**  
What is the **Master Theorem**? Apply it to solve $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Theorem** is a direct method to determine the asymptotic solution of recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, where $a \ge 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is asymptotically positive. It compares $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$ and yields the solution in one of three cases.

For $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$:
- $a = 9$, $b = 3$, $f(n) = n$.
- $\log_b a = \log_3 9 = 2$, so $n^{\log_b a} = n^{2}$.
- Compare: $f(n) = n = O(n^{2 - \epsilon})$ with $\epsilon = 1 > 0$ → **Case 1**.

Therefore:
$$
T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_b a}\bigr) = \Theta(n^{2})
$$

---

### 📝 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**: part (a) tests theory/recurrence, part (b) tests application/proof/algorithm.

---

#### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** Write the **recurrence relation** for **Merge Sort** and solve it using the **Master Theorem** to obtain its time complexity. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **worst-case and average-case** behaviour of **Quick Sort** using recurrence relations. Justify why Quick Sort is preferred over Merge Sort in practice despite both being $\Theta(n \log n)$. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — Recurrence for Merge Sort.**

Merge Sort divides the array of size $n$ into two halves of size $n/2$, recursively sorts each half, and then merges them in $\Theta(n)$ time. The recurrence is:

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases} \Theta(1) & n = 1 \\[4pt] 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n) & n > 1 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Master Theorem parameters.**
$a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\log_b a$.**
$$
\log_2 2 = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad n^{\log_b a} = n^{1} = n
$$

**Step 4 — Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.**
$f(n) = n = \Theta(n)$ → matches **Case 2** of the Master Theorem with $k = 0$.

**Step 5 — Apply Case 2.**
$$
T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n \cdot \log^{k+1} n\bigr) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Step 6 — Verification by recursion-tree method.**
- Level $i$: $2^{i}$ sub-problems, each of size $n/2^{i}$.
- Combine cost per sub-problem: $n/2^{i}$.
- Total work at level $i$: $2^{i} \cdot (n/2^{i}) = n$.
- Number of levels: $\log_2 n + 1$.

$$
T(n) = n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

✅ Merge Sort has time complexity $\Theta(n \log n)$ in all three cases (best, average, worst).

---

##### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (b)

**Worst Case of Quick Sort:**

The worst case occurs when the pivot chosen (e.g., first or last element of a sorted array) is the smallest or largest element every time, producing one sub-problem of size $n - 1$ and another of size $0$.

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + \Theta(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)
$$

Expanding:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + n \\
&= T(n-2) + (n-1) + n \\
&= \dots \\
&= T(1) + 2 + 3 + \dots + n \\
&= \Theta(n^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **worst case** is $\Theta(n^{2})$.

**Average Case of Quick Sort:**

If the pivot splits the array in any proportion (random position), on average each level does $\Theta(n)$ work, and the expected depth of the recursion tree is $O(\log n)$.

$$
T(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{q=1}^{n} \bigl[T(q-1) + T(n-q)\bigr] + \Theta(n)
$$

Solving via substitution / linearity gives:
$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Why Quick Sort is preferred over Merge Sort in practice:**

| Aspect | Quick Sort | Merge Sort |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| **Auxiliary space** | $O(\log n)$ (in-place recursion stack) | $O(n)$ (extra array for merging) |
| **Cache locality** | Excellent — sequential partitioning | Moderate — extra array swaps |
| **Constant factor** | Small | Larger (copying overhead) |
| **Stability** | Not stable | Stable |
| **Practical speed (random data)** | Faster | Slightly slower |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Although both have $\Theta(n \log n)$ average-case complexity, Quick Sort's **in-place partitioning** and **superior cache performance** make it the **default choice in standard libraries** (e.g., `qsort` in C, `Arrays.sort` for primitives in Java).

---

#### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication** algorithm. Write its recurrence and use the **Master Theorem** to derive its time complexity. Compare it asymptotically with the classical $\Theta(n^{3})$ algorithm. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A D&C algorithm satisfies $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^{2}$. Solve the recurrence using the **Master Theorem** and verify your answer with a **recursion-tree analysis**. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (a)

**Step 1 — Classical Matrix Multiplication Recap.**

To multiply two $n \times n$ matrices $A$ and $B$, the classical algorithm performs 8 recursive multiplications of $(n/2) \times (n/2)$ blocks plus $\Theta(n^{2})$ additions:

$$
T(n) = 8T(n/2) + \Theta(n^{2})
$$

This yields $T(n) = \Theta(n^{3})$.

**Step 2 — Strassen's Idea.**

Strassen (1969) showed that two $2 \times 2$ matrices can be multiplied using **only 7 scalar multiplications** instead of 8, by cleverly combining additions and subtractions. The 7 products are computed recursively, and the 4 result-block quadrants are assembled using $\Theta(n^{2})$ additions.

The 7 Strassen products:
- $M_{1} = (A_{11} + A_{22})(B_{11} + B_{22})$
- $M_{2} = (A_{21} + A_{22}) B_{11}$
- $M_{3} = A_{11} (B_{12} - B_{22})$
- $M_{4} = A_{22} (B_{21} - B_{11})$
- $M_{5} = (A_{11} + A_{12}) B_{22}$
- $M_{6} = (A_{21} - A_{11}) (B_{11} + B_{12})$
- $M_{7} = (A_{12} - A_{22}) (B_{21} + B_{22})$

And the result quadrants:
- $C_{11} = M_{1} + M_{4} - M_{5} + M_{7}$
- $C_{12} = M_{3} + M_{5}$
- $C_{21} = M_{2} + M_{4}$
- $C_{22} = M_{1} - M_{2} + M_{3} + M_{6}$

**Step 3 — Recurrence for Strassen.**

7 recursive multiplications on matrices of size $n/2$, with $\Theta(n^{2})$ additions to assemble $C$:

$$
T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^{2})
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Master Theorem.**
$a = 7$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^{2}$.
$$
\log_b a = \log_2 7 \approx 2.807
$$
Since $f(n) = n^{2} = O\bigl(n^{2.807 - 0.807}\bigr)$, we are in **Case 1**.

$$
T(n) = \Theta\bigl(n^{\log_2 7}\bigr) \approx \Theta\bigl(n^{2.807}\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic Comparison.**

| Algorithm | Recurrence | Complexity |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Classical MM | $8T(n/2) + n^{2}$ | $\Theta(n^{3})$ |
| Strassen's MM | $7T(n/2) + n^{2}$ | $\Theta(n^{2.807})$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Strassen's algorithm is faster asymptotically, but the hidden constant factor is large. It becomes practical only for very large matrices ($n \gtrsim 1000$), and is **numerically less stable** than classical MM (used in BLAS only after padding/optimisation).

---

##### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (b)

**Step 1 — Identify Master Theorem parameters.**
$T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^{2}$ → $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $f(n) = n^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Compute critical exponent.**
$$
\log_b a = \log_4 3 = \frac{\log 3}{\log 4} \approx 0.7925
$$
So $n^{\log_b a} \approx n^{0.7925}$.

**Step 3 — Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.**
$$
f(n) = n^{2} = \Omega\bigl(n^{0.7925 + \epsilon}\bigr) \quad \text{with } \epsilon = 1.207 > 0
$$
This indicates **Case 3** of the Master Theorem, provided the **regularity condition** holds.

**Step 4 — Verify regularity.**
We need a constant $c < 1$ such that for large $n$:
$$
a\,f(n/b) \le c\,f(n)
$$
Compute:
$$
a\,f(n/b) = 3 \cdot (n/4)^{2} = 3 \cdot \frac{n^{2}}{16} = \frac{3n^{2}}{16} = 0.1875\,n^{2}
$$
Choose $c = 0.1875 < 1$. ✅ Regularity holds.

**Step 5 — Apply Case 3.**
$$
T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n^{2})
$$

**Step 6 — Verify with recursion tree.**

At level $i$ of the recursion tree:
- Number of sub-problems: $3^{i}$.
- Size of each sub-problem: $n / 4^{i}$.
- Work per sub-problem (combine cost): $(n/4^{i})^{2} = n^{2} / 16^{i}$.
- Total work at level $i$: $3^{i} \cdot n^{2} / 16^{i} = n^{2} \cdot (3/16)^{i}$.

Since $3/16 < 1$, the geometric series **converges**, dominated by level 0:
$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_4 n} n^{2} \cdot (3/16)^{i} \le n^{2} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} (3/16)^{i} = n^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 3/16} = \Theta(n^{2})
$$

✅ Both methods agree: $T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to identify $a$, $b$, $f(n)$ explicitly** before applying the Master Theorem → lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\log_b a$ with $\log a$** — KTU answer scripts frequently compute $\log_2 7$ as $7$ or $3.5$. Always write the base explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping the regularity check** in Case 3 — students jump to the conclusion without verifying $a\,f(n/b) \le c\,f(n)$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Mixing up worst-case and average-case** for Quick Sort — they have different recurrences ($T(n-1)+n$ vs $2T(n/2)+n$).
> 5. **Using Master Theorem on non-constant $a$ or $b$** — invalid. Use Akra–Bazzi or recursion tree instead.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **D&C has 3 phases:** Divide → Conquer → Combine.
- ✅ **Generic recurrence:** $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.
- ✅ **Master Theorem** has 3 cases, determined by comparing $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$.
- ✅ **Case 2** with $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k} n)$ → answer is $\Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
- ✅ **Case 3** requires the **regularity condition** $a\,f(n/b) \le c\,f(n)$ for some $c < 1$.
- ✅ **Binary Search:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 = \Theta(\log n)$.
- ✅ **Merge Sort:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n = \Theta(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Quick Sort worst:** $\Theta(n^{2})$; **average:** $\Theta(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Strassen's MM:** $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})$ — beats classical $\Theta(n^{3})$.
- ✅ **Master Theorem does NOT apply** when $f(n)$ is "polynomially close but not strictly smaller/larger" — use Akra–Bazzi.
- ✅ **D&C advantages:** parallelisability, cache locality, predictable complexity.
- ✅ **Algorithm comparison:** Quick Sort is preferred in practice due to in-place partitioning and better cache behaviour, even though Merge Sort is stable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 – Dynamic Programming

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Programming (DP)** is an algorithmic strategy that solves a complex problem by breaking it down into a collection of simpler overlapping subproblems, solving each subproblem **once**, and **storing the result** in a table (memoization or tabulation) so that it can be reused whenever the same subproblem is encountered again.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT751)**, Dynamic Programming is positioned as an advanced algorithmic technique that falls between **Divide-and-Conquer** and **Greedy Methods**. It is the natural evolution of recursion whenever:

1. The problem exhibits **Optimal Substructure** — an optimal solution to the problem contains within it optimal solutions to its subproblems.
2. The problem exhibits **Overlapping Subproblems** — the recursive algorithm revisits the same subproblems repeatedly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The name *"Dynamic Programming"* was coined by **Richard Bellman** in the 1950s. It was deliberately chosen to hide the mathematical nature of the work from governmental sponsors — the word *"programming"* refers to *planning/optimization*, NOT computer programming.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cheat Sheet" Approach

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are a student preparing for a competitive exam with **1000 practice problems**. Many problems share identical core ideas (e.g., "find the maximum sum subarray"). A naive student solves each problem from scratch every time. A *smart* student maintains a **notebook (memo)** where they write down the answer to each unique problem idea the first time they solve it. When a similar problem appears later, they simply **read off the answer from the notebook**.
>
> That notebook is your **DP table**. The act of recording answers is **memoization** (top-down). Pre-computing answers in advance for all possible states is **tabulation** (bottom-up).

### 1.3 DP vs. Divide-and-Conquer vs. Greedy

| Strategy | Subproblem Overlap | Subproblem Independence | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Divide and Conquer | No overlap (e.g., Merge Sort) | Independent subproblems | Sorting, Searching |
| Dynamic Programming | Heavy overlap | Dependent subproblems | Optimization, Counting |
| Greedy | Heavy overlap | Locally optimal choices | Matroid, Huffman, Dijkstra |

---

### 1.4 The Two DP Methodologies

> [!NOTE]
> **Method A – Top-Down with Memoization (Recursive + Cache)**
> Write the natural recursive solution. Before computing `f(n)`, check if it already exists in the cache. If yes → return it. If no → compute it, store it, then return.

> [!NOTE]
> **Method B – Bottom-Up with Tabulation (Iterative + Table)**
> Identify the **base cases** (smallest subproblems). Fill a table iteratively from smallest to largest, each entry using previously-computed smaller entries.

```
PSEUDOCODE — Fibonacci via both methods

// TOP-DOWN (Memoization)
function fibTopDown(n):
    if memo[n] != NIL: return memo[n]
    if n <= 1: memo[n] = n
    else:       memo[n] = fibTopDown(n-1) + fibTopDown(n-2)
    return memo[n]

// BOTTOM-UP (Tabulation)
function fibBottomUp(n):
    dp[0] = 0; dp[1] = 1
    for i from 2 to n:
        dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]
    return dp[n]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Memoization vs. Tabulation** — both yield the same **time complexity** and **correctness** in most problems, but tabulation often has slightly better **constant factors** (no recursion overhead, better cache locality). However, memoization is easier to derive from a recurrence.

---

### 1.5 Key DP Design Vocabulary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **State:** A set of variables that uniquely defines a subproblem (e.g., `dp[i][w]` = best value using first `i` items and weight limit `w`).
> - **Transition (Recurrence):** The formula that expresses `state(i)` in terms of smaller states.
> - **Base Case (Initialization):** The smallest, trivially-known subproblems.
> - **Answer Extraction:** The final answer is read from one or more cells in the DP table.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Step DP Design Recipe (Board-Exam Friendly)

Every DP problem can be solved by following this exact procedure:

1. **Characterize the structure of an optimal solution** — Prove optimal substructure exists.
2. **Recursively define the value of an optimal solution** — Write the recurrence relation.
3. **Compute the value bottom-up (or top-down with memo)** — Fill the table.
4. **Construct an optimal solution (if required)** — Trace predecessor pointers.

> [!TIP]
> Step 4 is **optional** in KTU problems that ask only for the *value*. It is **mandatory** when asked to "trace the choices" or "reconstruct the optimal set".

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following recurrence relations are the **HIGH-YIELD formulas** that appear in ≥ 80% of KTU past papers on Dynamic Programming. Master these.

### Table 2.1 — Core DP Recurrences (Master Reference)

| # | Problem | State Definition | Recurrence Relation | Base Case | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | n-th Fibonacci | $F(i)$ | $F(i) = F(i-1) + F(i-2)$ | $F(0)=0,\ F(1)=1$ | $O(n)$ |
| 2 | 0/1 Knapsack | $dp[i][w]$ = max value using first $i$ items, capacity $w$ | $dp[i][w] = \max\{dp[i-1][w],\ dp[i-1][w-w_i] + v_i\}$ | $dp[0][*] = dp[*][0] = 0$ | $O(nW)$ |
| 3 | Unbounded Knapsack | $dp[w]$ = max value with capacity $w$ | $dp[w] = \max_{i}\{dp[w-w_i] + v_i\}$ | $dp[0] = 0$ | $O(nW)$ |
| 4 | Matrix Chain Mult. | $dp[i][j]$ = min scalar muls for $A_i \ldots A_j$ | $dp[i][j] = \min_{i \le k < j}\{dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p_{i-1} p_k p_j\}$ | $dp[i][i] = 0$ | $O(n^3)$ |
| 5 | Longest Common Subseq. (LCS) | $dp[i][j]$ = LCS of $X[1..i], Y[1..j]$ | If $x_i = y_j$: $dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1$, else $dp[i][j] = \max\{dp[i-1][j],\ dp[i][j-1]\}$ | $dp[0][*] = dp[*][0] = 0$ | $O(mn)$ |
| 6 | Longest Increasing Subseq. (LIS) | $dp[i]$ = LIS ending at index $i$ | $dp[i] = 1 + \max_{j<i,\,a_j < a_i}\{dp[j]\}$ | $dp[i] = 1$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| 7 | Coin Change (min coins) | $dp[s]$ = min coins to make sum $s$ | $dp[s] = \min_{c \in coins}\{dp[s-c] + 1\}$ | $dp[0] = 0$ | $O(nS)$ |
| 8 | Coin Change (count ways) | $dp[i][s]$ = ways using first $i$ coins to make $s$ | $dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] + dp[i][s-c_i]$ | $dp[i][0] = 1$ | $O(nS)$ |
| 9 | Edit Distance | $dp[i][j]$ = min ops to convert $X[1..i] \to Y[1..j]$ | $\min\{dp[i-1][j]+1,\ dp[i][j-1]+1,\ dp[i-1][j-1] + (x_i \neq y_j)\}$ | $dp[0][j]=j,\ dp[i][0]=i$ | $O(mn)$ |
| 10 | Floyd–Warshall APSP | $dp[k][i][j]$ = shortest $i \to j$ using intermediates $\le k$ | $dp[k][i][j] = \min\{dp[k-1][i][j],\ dp[k-1][i][k] + dp[k-1][k][j]\}$ | $dp[0][i][j] = w(i,j)$ | $O(V^3)$ |
| 11 | 0/1 subset-sum count | $dp[i][s]$ | $dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] + dp[i-1][s-a_i]$ | $dp[0][0]=1,\ dp[0][s>0]=0$ | $O(nS)$ |
| 12 | Optimal BST | $dp[i][j]$ | $dp[i][j] = \min\{dp[i][r-1] + dp[r+1][j]\} + \sum_{k=i}^{j} p_k$ | $dp[i][i-1]=0$ | $O(n^3)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For all "min" recurrences, always treat $\infty$ as a valid sentinel: $\min$ of an empty set returns $+\infty$, in which case the cell inherits its alternative value. In "max" recurrences, the empty set maps to $-\infty$.

---

## 2.3 Space Optimization Heuristics (Frequently Asked)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner-Favourite Sub-topic:** When asked to *reduce space*, apply these standard tricks:

- **1-D rolling array** — When $dp[i]$ depends only on $dp[i-1]$ or $dp[i-2]$ (Fibonacci, House Robber), use a 2-element array.
- **Single-row table** — When $dp[i][w]$ depends only on $dp[i-1][*]$ (0/1 Knapsack, LCS), collapse the row dimension by iterating weight **in reverse** to prevent overwriting.
- **Two-row table** — When both $dp[i][*]$ and $dp[i-1][*]$ are needed simultaneously (Edit Distance with reconstruction), keep two rows and swap.
- **K-th intermediate layer** — Floyd–Warshall can be reduced from $O(V^3)$ memory to $O(V^2)$ by overwriting in place because $dp[k][i][k]$ and $dp[k][k][j]$ never change after iteration $k$.

| Problem | Original Space | Optimized Space |
|---|---|---|
| Fibonacci | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| 0/1 Knapsack | $O(nW)$ | $O(W)$ |
| LCS | $O(mn)$ | $O(\min(m,n))$ |
| Floyd–Warshall | $O(V^3)$ | $O(V^2)$ |
| Edit Distance | $O(mn)$ | $O(\min(m,n))$ |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Compiler Design:** Optimal parenthesization of Matrix-Chain multiplication minimizes total scalar multiplications.
> - **Bioinformatics:** Sequence alignment (BLAST, ClustalW) uses Edit Distance and LCS at the core of genomic comparison.
> - **Routing & Networks:** Floyd–Warshall underpins many network-routing pre-computations and shortest-path queries.
> - **Operations Research:** Knapsack and Coin-Change variants model cargo loading, budget allocation, and currency systems.
> - **NLP:** Viterbi algorithm for Hidden Markov Models is a DP formulation.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example A — 0/1 Knapsack (Most Asked Problem in KTU)

### Problem Statement

Given $n$ items, each with **weight** $w_i$ and **value** $v_i$, and a knapsack of **capacity** $W$, choose a subset such that the total weight does not exceed $W$ and the total value is maximized. Each item can be taken **at most once**.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify Optimal Substructure

Consider the optimal solution $S$ for items $1..i$ with capacity $w$:

- **Case 1 (Item $i$ NOT chosen):** Then $S$ is the optimal solution for items $1..(i-1)$ with capacity $w$.
- **Case 2 (Item $i$ IS chosen):** Then $S \setminus \{i\}$ is the optimal solution for items $1..(i-1)$ with capacity $w - w_i$.

Hence the value of the optimal solution is the better of these two cases.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define State and Recurrence

Let $dp[i][w]$ = maximum value obtainable using **the first $i$ items** and **knapsack capacity $w$**.

$$
dp[i][w] = 
\begin{cases}
dp[i-1][w], & \text{if } w_i > w \quad \text{(item too heavy)}\\
\max\!\left\{dp[i-1][w],\ dp[i-1][w - w_i] + v_i\right\}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Boundary (Base) Conditions:

$$
dp[0][w] = 0 \quad \forall w, \qquad dp[i][0] = 0 \quad \forall i
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Numerical Worked Out

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Let $n=4$, $W=5$, items = $\{w_1=2,v_1=3\},\{w_2=3,v_2=4\},\{w_3=4,v_3=5\},\{w_4=5,v_4=6\}$.

We build table $dp[i][w]$ for $i=0..4$ and $w=0..5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial row } i=0 &: \quad [0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0] \\[4pt]
\text{Row } i=1,\ w_1=2,\ v_1=3 &: \\[2pt]
dp[1][0] &= dp[0][0] = 0 \\
dp[1][1] &= dp[0][1] = 0 \quad (w_1>1) \\
dp[1][2] &= \max\{dp[0][2],\ dp[0][0]+3\} = \max\{0, 3\} = 3 \\
dp[1][3] &= \max\{dp[0][3],\ dp[0][1]+3\} = \max\{0, 3\} = 3 \\
dp[1][4] &= \max\{dp[0][4],\ dp[0][2]+3\} = \max\{0, 3\} = 3 \\
dp[1][5] &= \max\{dp[0][5],\ dp[0][3]+3\} = \max\{0, 3\} = 3 \\[6pt]
\text{Row } i=2,\ w_2=3,\ v_2=4 &: \\[2pt]
dp[2][0] &= 0 \\
dp[2][1] &= dp[1][1] = 0 \\
dp[2][2] &= dp[1][2] = 3 \quad (w_2>2) \\
dp[2][3] &= \max\{dp[1][3],\ dp[1][0]+4\} = \max\{3, 4\} = 4 \\
dp[2][4] &= \max\{dp[1][4],\ dp[1][1]+4\} = \max\{3, 4\} = 4 \\
dp[2][5] &= \max\{dp[1][5],\ dp[1][2]+4\} = \max\{3, 7\} = 7 \\[6pt]
\text{Row } i=3,\ w_3=4,\ v_3=5 &: \\[2pt]
dp[3][5] &= \max\{dp[2][5],\ dp[2][1]+5\} = \max\{7, 5\} = 7 \\
\text{Row } i=4,\ w_4=5,\ v_4=6 &: \\[2pt]
dp[4][5] &= \max\{dp[3][5],\ dp[3][0]+6\} = \max\{7, 6\} = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Result

> Maximum value = $dp[4][5] = 7$, obtained by selecting items 1 and 2 (weight $2+3=5$, value $3+4=7$).

### 3.1.5 Complexity Analysis

$$
T(n, W) = (n+1)(W+1) \text{ cells}, \quad \text{each } O(1) \Rightarrow T = O(nW), \quad S = O(nW)
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — Matrix Chain Multiplication

### 3.2.1 Problem

Multiply matrices $A_1 A_2 A_3 \ldots A_n$ where $A_i$ has dimension $p_{i-1} \times p_i$. Find the parenthesization that minimizes scalar multiplications.

### 3.2.2 State and Recurrence

Let $dp[i][j]$ = minimum number of scalar multiplications needed to compute $A_i \cdot A_{i+1} \cdots A_j$.

The optimal final split occurs at some $k$ ($i \le k < j$):

$$
dp[i][j] = \min_{i \le k < j}\Big\{dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p_{i-1}\,p_k\,p_j\Big\}, \quad dp[i][i] = 0
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Out

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Let $n=4$, dimensions $A_1: 1\times 5$, $A_2: 5\times 4$, $A_3: 4\times 3$, $A_4: 3\times 2$, i.e., $p = [1,5,4,3,2]$.

**Diagonal length 1 (base case):**
$$
dp[i][i] = 0 \text{ for } i=1,2,3,4
$$

**Diagonal length 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[1][2] &= p_0 p_1 p_2 = 1\cdot 5\cdot 4 = 20 \\
dp[2][3] &= p_1 p_2 p_3 = 5\cdot 4\cdot 3 = 60 \\
dp[3][4] &= p_2 p_3 p_4 = 4\cdot 3\cdot 2 = 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagonal length 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[1][3] &= \min\Big\{\,dp[1][1]+dp[2][3]+p_0 p_1 p_3,\ dp[1][2]+dp[3][3]+p_0 p_2 p_3\,\Big\} \\
&= \min\{0+60+1\cdot 5\cdot 3,\ 20+0+1\cdot 4\cdot 3\} \\
&= \min\{75,\ 32\} = 32 \quad \text{(split at } k=2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[2][4] &= \min\Big\{\,dp[2][2]+dp[3][4]+p_1 p_2 p_4,\ dp[2][3]+dp[4][4]+p_1 p_3 p_4\,\Big\} \\
&= \min\{0+24+5\cdot 4\cdot 2,\ 60+0+5\cdot 3\cdot 2\} \\
&= \min\{64,\ 90\} = 64 \quad \text{(split at } k=2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagonal length 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[1][4] &= \min_{k=1,2,3}\Big\{dp[1][k]+dp[k+1][4]+p_0 p_k p_4\Big\} \\[4pt]
k=1:&\ dp[1][1]+dp[2][4]+1\cdot 5\cdot 2 = 0+64+10 = 74 \\
k=2:&\ dp[1][2]+dp[3][4]+1\cdot 4\cdot 2 = 20+24+8 = 52 \\
k=3:&\ dp[1][3]+dp[4][4]+1\cdot 3\cdot 2 = 32+0+6 = 38 \\[4pt]
dp[1][4] &= 38 \quad \text{(split at } k=3\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Result

> Optimal parenthesization: $(A_1\,A_2\,A_3)\,A_4$ with **38 scalar multiplications**.

### 3.2.5 Complexity

$$
T(n) = O(n^3), \qquad S(n) = O(n^2)
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C — Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)

### Problem

Given strings $X = x_1 x_2 \ldots x_m$ and $Y = y_1 y_2 \ldots y_n$, find the longest string that is a subsequence of both.

### Recurrence

Let $dp[i][j]$ = LCS length of $X[1..i]$ and $Y[1..j]$.

$$
dp[i][j] = 
\begin{cases}
dp[i-1][j-1] + 1, & \text{if } x_i = y_j \\[4pt]
\max\big\{dp[i-1][j],\ dp[i][j-1]\big\}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

with $dp[i][0] = dp[0][j] = 0$.

### Numerical Worked Out

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $X = \text{"ABCB"}$, $Y = \text{"BDCAB"}$ → $m=4$, $n=5$.

| $i \backslash j$ | 0 | 1 (B) | 2 (D) | 3 (C) | 4 (A) | 5 (B) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 (A) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** | 1 |
| 2 (B) | 0 | **1** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 3 (C) | 0 | 1 | 1 | **2** | 2 | 2 |
| 4 (B) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | **3** |

### Sample Cell Derivations

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[1][1] &: x_1=A,\ y_1=B \Rightarrow A\neq B \\
&\Rightarrow \max\{dp[0][1],\ dp[1][0]\} = \max\{0,0\} = 0 \\[4pt]
dp[2][5] &: x_2=B,\ y_5=B \Rightarrow B=B \\
&\Rightarrow dp[1][4] + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\[4pt]
dp[3][3] &: x_3=C,\ y_3=C \Rightarrow C=C \\
&\Rightarrow dp[2][2] + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\[4pt]
dp[4][5] &: x_4=B,\ y_5=B \Rightarrow B=B \\
&\Rightarrow dp[3][4] + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Result

> LCS length = $dp[4][5] = 3$. One optimal LCS is **"BCB"** (reconstructed by back-tracking from $dp[4][5]$).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example D — Floyd–Warshall All-Pairs Shortest Path

### Recurrence

Let $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ = length of shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using intermediate vertices only from $\{1,\dots,k\}$.

$$
D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\Big\{D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\ D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\Big\}
$$

Boundary: $D^{(0)}[i][j] = w(i,j)$ (or $\infty$ if no edge).

### Step-by-Step Logic

> [!TIP]
> At iteration $k$, ask: *"Is it shorter to go from $i$ to $j$ by passing through $k$?"* If yes, update. This single decision captures all paths whose highest-numbered intermediate is exactly $k$.

After $k = V$ iterations, $D^{(V)}[i][j]$ is the true all-pairs shortest distance. Negative cycles can be detected: if any diagonal $D[V][i][i] < 0$, a negative cycle exists.

### Complexity

$$
T = O(V^3), \qquad S = O(V^2) \text{ (in-place possible)}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example E — Coin Change (Minimum Number of Coins)

### Problem

Given unlimited supply of coins of denominations $\{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n\}$, find the minimum number of coins needed to make amount $S$.

### Recurrence

$$
dp[s] = \min_{c_i \le s}\{dp[s - c_i] + 1\}, \qquad dp[0] = 0
$$

### Numerical Worked Out

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Coins = $\{1, 3, 4\}$, $S = 6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[0] &= 0 \\
dp[1] &= dp[0] + 1 = 1 \quad (\text{one } 1\text{-coin}) \\
dp[2] &= dp[1] + 1 = 2 \quad (\text{two } 1\text{-coins}) \\
dp[3] &= \min\{dp[2]+1,\ dp[0]+1\} = \min\{3,1\} = 1 \quad (\text{one } 3\text{-coin}) \\
dp[4] &= \min\{dp[3]+1,\ dp[1]+1,\ dp[0]+1\} = \min\{2,2,1\} = 1 \quad (\text{one } 4\text{-coin}) \\
dp[5] &= \min\{dp[4]+1,\ dp[2]+1,\ dp[1]+1\} = \min\{2,3,2\} = 2 \quad (\text{one } 4 + \text{one } 1) \\
dp[6] &= \min\{dp[5]+1,\ dp[3]+1,\ dp[2]+1\} = \min\{3,2,3\} = 2 \quad (\text{two } 3\text{-coins})
\end{aligned}
$$

> Minimum number of coins = **2** (either two 3-coins or one 4-coin + one ... wait, that is $4+1=5$, so actually two 3-coins or $3+1+1+1$ — the optimal is **two 3-coins**).

### Complexity

$$
T = O(nS), \qquad S = O(S)
$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The DP Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Subproblems] --> B[Define State]
    B --> C[Derive Recurrence]
    C --> D{Implementation Style?}
    D -- Top-Down --> E[Recursive Call + Memoization Cache]
    D -- Bottom-Up --> F[Iterative Table Fill]
    E --> G[Extract Optimal Value]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Need Actual Choices?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Backtrack through Predecessor Links]
    H -- No --> J[Return dp value directly]
    I --> K[Final Optimal Solution]
    J --> K
```

---

## 4.2 Recursion Tree vs. DP Table (Fibonacci)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F5["fib(5)"] --> F4a["fib(4)"]
    F5 --> F3a["fib(3)"]
    F4a --> F3b["fib(3)"]
    F4a --> F2a["fib(2)"]
    F3a --> F2b["fib(2)"]
    F3a --> F1a["fib(1)"]
    F3b --> F2c["fib(2)"]
    F3b --> F1b["fib(1)"]
    F2a --> F1c["fib(1)"]
    F2a --> F0a["fib(0)"]
    F2b --> F1d["fib(1)"]
    F2b --> F0b["fib(0)"]
    F2c --> F1e["fib(1)"]
    F2c --> F0c["fib(0)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Without DP** — the recursion tree for $fib(5)$ has **15 nodes** (exponential blow-up). The same subproblems (`fib(3)`, `fib(2)`) appear multiple times.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0["dp[0]=0"] --> T1["dp[1]=1"]
    T1 --> T2["dp[2]=1"]
    T2 --> T3["dp[3]=2"]
    T3 --> T4["dp[4]=3"]
    T4 --> T5["dp[5]=5"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **With DP (Tabulation)** — the linear table has exactly **6 cells**. Each is filled once in $O(1)$. Total time $O(n)$ versus naive $O(2^n)$.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — 0/1 Knapsack DP Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT["INPUT: items n, weights w, values v, capacity W"]
    INPUT --> INIT["Initialize dp 0..n x 0..W = 0"]
    INIT --> LOOP["for i = 1 to n"]
    LOOP --> INNER["for w = 0 to W"]
    INNER --> CHECK{"w_i <= w ?"}
    CHECK -- NO --> KEEP["dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]"]
    CHECK -- YES --> MAX["dp[i][w] = max of two options"]
    MAX --> OPT1["Option A: skip item i"]
    MAX --> OPT2["Option B: include item i"]
    OPT1 --> STORE["Store max in dp[i][w]"]
    OPT2 --> STORE
    KEEP --> STORE
    STORE --> INNER
    INNER --> LOOP
    LOOP --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT: dp[n][W]"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — LCS Reconstruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Compute dp table bottom-up"] --> B{"dp[i][j]?"}
    B -- "x_i = y_j" --> C["Match: move diagonal to i-1, j-1"]
    B -- "dp[i-1][j] >= dp[i][j-1]" --> D["Skip from X: move up to i-1, j"]
    B -- "else" --> E["Skip from Y: move left to i, j-1"]
    C --> F{"i=0 or j=0?"}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- NO --> B
    F -- YES --> G["Reverse accumulated characters -> LCS string"]
```

---

## 4.5 DP Decision Flow — When to Apply

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Is the problem an optimization or counting question?"] -- NO --> STOP1["DP probably not the best fit"]
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2["Does it have Optimal Substructure?"]
    Q2 -- NO --> STOP2["Try Greedy or other methods"]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3["Are subproblems Overlapping?"]
    Q3 -- NO --> STOP3["Use plain Divide and Conquer"]
    Q3 -- YES --> START["Apply Dynamic Programming!"]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Divide-and-Conquer and Dynamic Programming approaches. List two problems that can be solved using DP.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Divide-and-Conquer | Dynamic Programming |
|---|---|---|
| Subproblem nature | **Independent** | **Overlapping** |
| Storage of solutions | Not stored | Stored in a table |
| Computation | Fresh each call | Reused from table |
| Examples | Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search | 0/1 Knapsack, LCS, Matrix-Chain Multiplication |

**Two DP-solvable problems:** (i) 0/1 Knapsack, (ii) Longest Common Subsequence.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Define *Optimal Substructure* and *Overlapping Subproblems*. State whether the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) satisfies these properties.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Optimal Substructure:** A property of a problem where an optimal solution to the whole problem contains within it optimal solutions to its subproblems.

> **Overlapping Subproblems:** The recursive solution revisits the *same* subproblems repeatedly.

> TSP has **optimal substructure** (any optimal tour's prefix is an optimal path between those cities) but its standard formulation has **non-polynomial subproblem overlap** requiring $O(n^2)$ DP states — making it solvable via Held–Karp DP in $O(n^2 2^n)$ time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — 0/1 Knapsack + LCS `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** *Consider the following 0/1 Knapsack instance: $n=4$, capacity $W=5$. Items: $\{(w_1=2,v_1=3), (w_2=3,v_2=4), (w_3=4,v_3=5), (w_4=5,v_4=6)\}$. Using Dynamic Programming, determine the maximum value obtainable. Show the complete DP table and the optimal subset.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The recurrence relation used:

$$
dp[i][w] = \begin{cases} dp[i-1][w], & w_i > w \\ \max\{dp[i-1][w],\,dp[i-1][w-w_i] + v_i\}, & w_i \le w \end{cases}
$$

**DP Table:**

| $i \backslash w$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 ($w$=2,$v$=3) | 0 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| 2 ($w$=3,$v$=4) | 0 | 0 | 3 | 4 | 4 | 7 |
| 3 ($w$=4,$v$=5) | 0 | 0 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| 4 ($w$=5,$v$=6) | 0 | 0 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 7 |

**Cell-level explanation of $dp[2][5]$:**

$$
dp[2][5] = \max\{dp[1][5],\,dp[1][5-3]+4\} = \max\{3, 3+4\} = 7
$$

**Reconstruction (tracing back from $dp[4][5]=7$):**

- $dp[4][5]=dp[3][5]=7$ → Item 4 **excluded**.
- $dp[3][5]=dp[2][5]=7$ → Item 3 **excluded**.
- $dp[2][5]=7 \ne dp[1][5]=3$ → Item 2 **included**, jump to $dp[1][5-3]=dp[1][2]=3$.
- $dp[1][2]=3 \ne dp[0][2]=0$ → Item 1 **included**.

**Optimal subset:** $\{1, 2\}$ with total weight $2+3=5$ and total value $3+4=7$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(nW) = O(4\times 5) = 20$ operations.

---

**Q. (b)** *Find the Longest Common Subsequence of $X = \text{"ABCBDAB"}$ and $Y = \text{"BDCABA"}$ using DP. Show the DP table and reconstruct the LCS.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Recurrence:

$$
dp[i][j] = \begin{cases} dp[i-1][j-1]+1, & x_i=y_j \\ \max\{dp[i-1][j],\,dp[i][j-1]\}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**DP Table:**

| $i\backslash j$ | 0 | B(1) | D(2) | C(3) | A(4) | B(5) | A(6) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| A(1) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| B(2) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| C(3) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| B(4) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| D(5) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| A(6) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 4 |
| B(7) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 4 |

**Sample cell derivations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[2][1] &: x_2=B,\ y_1=B \Rightarrow dp[1][0]+1 = 0+1 = 1 \\
dp[5][2] &: x_5=D,\ y_2=D \Rightarrow dp[4][1]+1 = 1+1 = 2 \\
dp[7][6] &: x_7=B,\ y_6=A \Rightarrow B\neq A \\
&\Rightarrow \max\{dp[6][6],\,dp[7][5]\} = \max\{4, 4\} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**LCS Length:** $dp[7][6] = 4$.

**Reconstruction (back-tracking):** Following diagonal moves when characters match, the LCS is **"BCAB"** (length 4).

**Complexity:** $O(mn) = O(7 \times 6) = 42$ operations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Matrix Chain Multiplication + Floyd–Warshall `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q. (a)** *Apply Matrix Chain Multiplication algorithm to find the optimal parenthesization for the matrices $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4$ with dimensions $2 \times 3,\ 3 \times 4,\ 4 \times 2,\ 2 \times 5$ respectively. Find the minimum number of scalar multiplications.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Dimension vector $p = [2, 3, 4, 2, 5]$.

Recurrence:

$$
dp[i][j] = \min_{i \le k < j}\{dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p_{i-1}\,p_k\,p_j\}, \quad dp[i][i] = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Length 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[1][2] &= p_0 p_1 p_2 = 2\cdot 3\cdot 4 = 24 \\
dp[2][3] &= p_1 p_2 p_3 = 3\cdot 4\cdot 2 = 24 \\
dp[3][4] &= p_2 p_3 p_4 = 4\cdot 2\cdot 5 = 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Length 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[1][3] &= \min\{dp[1][1]+dp[2][3]+p_0 p_1 p_3,\ dp[1][2]+dp[3][3]+p_0 p_2 p_3\} \\
&= \min\{0+24+2\cdot 3\cdot 2,\ 24+0+2\cdot 4\cdot 2\} \\
&= \min\{36,\ 40\} = 36 \quad (k=1) \\[4pt]
dp[2][4] &= \min\{dp[2][2]+dp[3][4]+p_1 p_2 p_4,\ dp[2][3]+dp[4][4]+p_1 p_3 p_4\} \\
&= \min\{0+40+3\cdot 4\cdot 5,\ 24+0+3\cdot 2\cdot 5\} \\
&= \min\{100,\ 54\} = 54 \quad (k=3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Length 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dp[1][4] &= \min\{dp[1][1]+dp[2][4]+p_0 p_1 p_4,\ dp[1][2]+dp[3][4]+p_0 p_2 p_4,\ dp[1][3]+dp[4][4]+p_0 p_3 p_4\} \\
&= \min\{0+54+2\cdot 3\cdot 5,\ 24+40+2\cdot 4\cdot 5,\ 36+0+2\cdot 2\cdot 5\} \\
&= \min\{84,\ 104,\ 56\} = 56 \quad (k=3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Minimum scalar multiplications = **56**.

**Optimal parenthesization** (from split-point trace $k=3$ at top level, then $k=1$ in $dp[1][3]$):

$$
(A_1\,(A_2\,A_3))\,A_4
$$

**Complexity:** $O(n^3) = O(64)$ table-cell computations for $n=4$.

---

**Q. (b)** *Apply Floyd–Warshall algorithm on the directed graph with 4 vertices whose weight matrix is given below. Show the $D^{(0)}, D^{(1)}, D^{(2)}, D^{(3)}, D^{(4)}$ matrices.* **(7 Marks)**

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & \infty & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

Recurrence: $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\{D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\,D^{(k-1)}[i][k]+D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\}$.

**$D^{(0)} = W$:**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & \infty & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$D^{(1)}$ — try going through vertex 1:**

Sample update for $D^{(1)}[2][4]$:

$$
D^{(1)}[2][4] = \min\{D^{(0)}[2][4],\,D^{(0)}[2][1]+D^{(0)}[1][4]\} = \min\{\infty, 8+7\} = 15
$$

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & 15 \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$D^{(2)}$ — try going through vertex 2:**

$D^{(2)}[1][3] = \min\{D^{(1)}[1][3],\,D^{(1)}[1][2]+D^{(1)}[2][3]\} = \min\{\infty, 3+2\} = 5$

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & 15 \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$D^{(3)}$ — try going through vertex 3:**

$D^{(3)}[1][4] = \min\{D^{(2)}[1][4],\,D^{(2)}[1][3]+D^{(2)}[3][4]\} = \min\{7, 5+1\} = 6$

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 0 & 2 & 3 \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$D^{(4)}$ — try going through vertex 4:**

$D^{(4)}[2][1] = \min\{D^{(3)}[2][1],\,D^{(3)}[2][4]+D^{(3)}[4][1]\} = \min\{7, 3+2\} = 5$

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 5 & 6 \\ 5 & 0 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 6 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Result:** Final all-pairs shortest distances given by $D^{(4)}$. All diagonal entries are $0$ → **no negative cycle exists**.

**Complexity:** $O(V^3) = O(64)$ operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Recurrence Off-by-One:** Writing $dp[i][w-w_i]$ instead of $dp[i-1][w-w_i]$ in 0/1 Knapsack. The $i$-th item uses only items $1..(i-1)$ to fill the remaining capacity — using $dp[i][w-w_i]$ mistakenly converts it into **Unbounded Knapsack**.
>
> 2. **Base Case Omission:** Always state $dp[0][*] = dp[*][0] = 0$ (or equivalent). Examiners deduct **1 mark** if initialization is missing.
>
> 3. **Trace but Don't Justify:** In reconstruction problems, you MUST show **why** you moved diagonal/up/left, not merely state the LCS string.
>
> 4. **Matrix-Chain Parenthesization:** Always specify the **split point $k$** for every min calculation, not just the numerical minimum. The split is what gives the parenthesization.
>
> 5. **Floyd–Warshall:** Show $D^{(k)}$ matrices step-by-step; a single merged answer yields only partial marks. Negative-cycle check ($D[i][i]<0$) is **mandatory**.
>
> 6. **Time Complexity Units:** Always state $O(nW)$ for Knapsack, $O(V^3)$ for Floyd–Warshall, $O(n^3)$ for Matrix-Chain, $O(mn)$ for LCS — wrong complexities cost easy marks.
>
> 7. **Mixing Top-Down vs Bottom-Up:** In Part B answers, pick ONE method and stick to it. Examiners penalize inconsistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DP — KTU Module 4**

- **Core idea:** Solve overlapping subproblems once, store in a table, reuse.
- **Two conditions for DP applicability:** Optimal Substructure + Overlapping Subproblems.
- **Two flavours:** Top-down (memoization) vs Bottom-up (tabulation). Same complexity; tabulation often has lower constants.
- **Master the recurrences** for: 0/1 Knapsack, Matrix-Chain Multiplication, LCS, Floyd–Warshall, Coin Change, Edit Distance, and Optimal BST.
- **Always state:** State definition, recurrence, base case, answer extraction, complexity.
- **Space optimization** comes from rolling-array or single-row techniques when state depends only on a constant number of previous rows.
- **Reconstruction** requires storing predecessor pointers or back-tracking through the table.
- **KTU formula sheet:**
  - Knapsack → $O(nW)$
  - LCS → $O(mn)$
  - Matrix-Chain → $O(n^3)$
  - Floyd–Warshall → $O(V^3)$
- **Beware the difference** between 0/1 Knapsack (`dp[i-1]`) and Unbounded Knapsack (`dp[i]`) — a classic trap.
- **Negative-cycle detection:** In Floyd–Warshall, check $D[i][i] < 0$ at the end.
- **Examiner scoring tip:** Showing the **DP table explicitly** with cell-wise derivations guarantees full marks. Skipping the table is the single biggest cause of partial scoring.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backtracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Backtracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — BACKTRACKING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Backtracking** is an algorithmic strategy for solving **combinatorial (NP-hard) optimization and decision problems** by building the solution incrementally, one component at a time. When the algorithm discovers that the partial solution built so far **cannot lead to a valid complete solution**, it *backtracks* (undoes the last choice) and systematically explores alternative branches of the **state-space tree**.

It is essentially a refined form of **brute-force search** where large portions of the search space are *pruned* using feasibility tests, thereby avoiding the exponential blow-up of a true exhaustive search.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Maze Runner

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of backtracking as solving a physical maze with a piece of chalk.**
>
> 1. You walk into a corridor.
> 2. At every junction, you try one path and **mark it with chalk** (your choice).
> 3. If you reach a **dead end** (constraint violation), you walk back to the last junction — *this walking back is the "backtrack"*.
> 4. You **erase** the chalk marks of the dead-end branch (undo the move) and try the next untried direction.
> 5. You continue until you either find the exit (a solution) or exhaust all possibilities (no solution exists).
>
> The chalk marks are your **feasibility trail**, the dead-end detection is your **constraint test**, and the maze junctions form a tree of decisions known as the **state-space tree**.

### Key Terminology Required by KTU

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **State-Space Tree** | A rooted tree representing all partial/complete configurations of a candidate solution. |
| **Live Node** | A node that has been generated but whose children are not yet explored. |
| **E-Node (Expansion Node)** | The live node whose children are currently being generated. |
| **Dead Node** | A node that cannot lead to any solution (terminates the search on that path). |
| **Bounding Function** | A predicate that decides whether a partial node can possibly lead to a complete solution. |
| **Solution Vector** | An $n$-tuple $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ where each $x_i$ is chosen from a finite set $S_i$. |
| **Promising Node** | A partial vector that still has a chance of being extended to a solution. |

### Relationship to Other Algorithmic Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> **Backtracking vs. Branch and Bound vs. Dynamic Programming**
>
> - **Backtracking** → used for problems with an **implicit binary/multi-way tree**; explores *depth-first*; constraints prune the tree.
> - **Branch & Bound** → used for **optimization problems** (min/max); uses *upper/lower bounds* to prune.
> - **Dynamic Programming** → used when the problem has **overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure** (Bottom-Up).
> - **Greedy** → used when local optimal choices yield global optimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Backtracking Algorithm

Every backtracking algorithm conforms to the following **canonical template**:

```
Algorithm Backtrack(x[1..k])
  Input:  x[1..k] — a partial solution vector of length k
  Output: All complete solutions extending x[1..k]

1. for each element c ∈ S_{k+1} do       // try every candidate choice
2.    x[k+1] ← c                          // assign the candidate
3.    if Constraint(x[1..k+1]) is TRUE then // feasibility / bounding check
4.        if k+1 == n then                // reached depth n → complete solution
5.            Output x[1..n]
6.        else
7.            Backtrack(x[1..k+1])        // recurse one level deeper
8.        end if
9.    end if
10. end for
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The recursion in **Step 7** is the *forward march*. When the recursion returns, control automatically goes to **Step 10**, which picks the **next candidate** — this return-and-try-next is what constitutes the **"backtrack"**.

## 2.2 Classification of Search Tree Nodes (KTU-Standard)

A backtracking algorithm processes every node into one of four categories:

1. **Promising → Continue**: Feasible partial node → expand its children.
2. **Non-Promising → Prune**: Bounding function returns FALSE → kill entire subtree.
3. **Solution Node**: Leaf satisfying all constraints → record the solution.
4. **Root Node ($k=0$)**: Starting point with empty vector.

The bounding function $B(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ returns:
- **TRUE** if the partial vector can be extended to a solution.
- **FALSE** if the partial vector violates some constraint.

## 2.3 Recurrence for Number of Nodes Generated

The total number of nodes $T(n)$ generated by a backtracking algorithm satisfies:

$$
T(n) = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} T(n - d_i)
$$

where $d_i$ is the depth contributed by the $i^{\text{th}}$ branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Monte-Carlo Estimation Trick (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> When exact node-count is intractable, estimate the number of nodes $m$ by sampling:
>
> $$
> m \approx \frac{n_s \cdot (N + 1)}{n_{s} + 1}
> $$
>
> where $N$ = total random nodes generated and $n_s$ = random nodes that yielded solutions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Condition |
|---|---------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Total nodes in perfect binary tree | $T(n) = 2^{n+1} - 1$ | Full backtracking (no pruning) |
| 2 | Nodes at depth $k$ | $N_k = 2^k$ | Binary tree |
| 3 | **N-Queens** valid placements | $\le n!$ (worst) | Upper bound |
| 4 | N-Queens (compact upper bound) | $\dfrac{n!}{3}$ | Empirical |
| 5 | Sum-of-Subsets worst-case nodes | $2^{n+1} - 1$ | Full binary tree |
| 6 | Hamiltonian Cycle worst-case | $(n-1)!$ permutations | Polynomial-time prune |
| 7 | Graph Coloring worst-case | $m^{(n+1)}$ branches | $m$ colors, $n$ vertices |
| 8 | **Knapsack 0/1** bound | $\hat{B} = v + (W-w)\cdot(v_{i+1}/w_{i+1})$ | Fractional upper bound |
| 9 | Algorithm time | $O(b^n)$ where $b$ = branching factor | Exponential in worst case |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All backtracking algorithms have worst-case exponential time complexity $O(b^n)$**. However, *effective pruning* via the bounding function frequently brings the average case dramatically closer to polynomial.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Backtracking

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Artificial Intelligence** | Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSPs), Sudoku solvers |
| **Compiler Design** | Register allocation, instruction scheduling |
| **Bioinformatics** | Sequence alignment (with pruning), DNA fragment assembly |
| **Operations Research** | Vehicle routing, job-shop scheduling |
| **Game Theory** | N-queens, knight's tour, Minesweeper hint engines |
| **Robotics** | Path planning in grids with obstacles |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — **Sum-of-Subsets Problem**

> **Problem Statement:** Given a set of $n$ positive integers $W = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$ and a target sum $M$, find **all subsets** whose element-sum equals $M$.

**Step 1 — Sort in non-decreasing order** (essential for early termination):

$$
w_1 \le w_2 \le w_3 \le \ldots \le w_n
$$

**Step 2 — Define the State.** A partial vector $x[1..k]$ records whether element $w_i$ is included.
- $x_i = 1$ → include $w_i$
- $x_i = 0$ → exclude $w_i$

**Step 3 — Define the Bounding Function $B(x_1, \ldots, x_k)$:**

$$
B(x_1, \ldots, x_k) = \big[\, (S + w_{k+1} \le M) \;\;\land\;\; (S + \sum_{j=k+1}^{n} w_j \ge M) \,\big]
$$

where $S = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot x_i$.

- **Left conjunct:** $S + w_{k+1} \le M$ — including the next element must not overshoot.
- **Right conjunct:** $S + \sum_{j=k+1}^{n} w_j \ge M$ — even if we include **all remaining**, target $M$ must still be reachable.

### Step-by-Step Trace — Worked Numerical Example

Let $n = 4$, $W = \{5, 7, 10, 12\}$, $M = 22$.

Already sorted: $5 \le 7 \le 10 \le 12$.

**State-Space Tree traversal (depth-first, left-to-right):**

| Step | Action | Partial Sum $S$ | Bounding Test | Decision |
|------|--------|----------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | Include $w_1 = 5$ | 5 | $5+7=12 \le 22$ ✓ and $5+7+10+12=34 \ge 22$ ✓ | **Promising** |
| 2 | Include $w_2 = 7$ | 12 | $12+10=22 \le 22$ ✓ and $12+10+12=34 \ge 22$ ✓ | **Promising** |
| 3 | Include $w_3 = 10$ | 22 | $22=22$ ✓ → **SOLUTION** | **Record $\{5,7,10\}$** |
| 4 | Exclude $w_3 = 10$, try $w_4 = 12$ | 19 | $19+12=31 > 22$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| 5 | Backtrack: exclude $w_2 = 7$, include $w_3 = 10$ | 15 | $15+12=27 > 22$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| 6 | Backtrack: exclude $w_1 = 5$, include $w_2 = 7$ | 7 | $7+10=17 \le 22$ ✓ and $7+10+12=29 \ge 22$ ✓ | **Promising** |
| 7 | Include $w_3 = 10$ | 17 | $17+12=29 > 22$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| 8 | Exclude $w_3$, include $w_4 = 12$ | 19 | $19 > 22$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| 9 | Backtrack: exclude $w_2$, include $w_3$ | 10 | $10+12=22 \le 22$ ✓ and $10+12=22 \ge 22$ ✓ | **Promising** |
| 10 | Include $w_4 = 12$ | 22 | $22 = 22$ ✓ → **SOLUTION** | **Record $\{10,12\}$** |
| 11 | All children tried, backtrack to root, no more | — | — | **End** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Valid subsets = $\{5,7,10\}$ and $\{10,12\}$. Total nodes visited = **11** out of a possible $2^5 - 1 = 31$ — a **64.5 % pruning efficiency**.

### Analytical Matrix — Pruning Efficiency Summary

$$
\text{Pruning Efficiency} = 1 - \frac{N_{\text{visited}}}{2^{n+1} - 1}
$$

$$
= 1 - \frac{11}{31} \approx 0.645 = 64.5\,\%
$$

## 3.2 Canonical Worked Example — **N-Queens Problem**

> **Problem Statement:** Place $n$ queens on an $n \times n$ chessboard so that no two queens attack each other.

### Derivation of the Constraint Equations

A queen at $(i, j)$ attacks any queen at $(i', j')$ iff:

1. **Same row:** $i = i'$
2. **Same column:** $j = j'$
3. **Same major diagonal:** $i - j = i' - j'$  ⟹ $j' - i' = j - i$
4. **Same minor diagonal:** $i + j = i' + j'$

Because we **place exactly one queen per row** (implicit constraint), rule (1) is automatically satisfied. Let $x_i$ = column of the queen in row $i$. The remaining constraints become:

$$
\forall i \ne k : \quad x_i \ne x_k \;\;\land\;\; x_i - i \ne x_k - k \;\;\land\;\; x_i + i \ne x_k + k
$$

### Bounding Function

$$
B(x_1, \ldots, x_k) \equiv \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k-1} \big[\, (x_i \ne x_k) \;\land\; (x_i - i \ne x_k - k) \;\land\; (x_i + i \ne x_k + k) \,\big]
$$

### Trace for $n = 4$ (DFS, left-to-right)

| Row $k$ | Try column | Conflict? | Action |
|---------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | $x_1 = 1$ | — | OK, recurse |
| 2 | $x_2 = 1$ | Same column as row 1 | **Prune** |
| 2 | $x_2 = 2$ | Diagonal conflict (2-1=1, 1-1=0, diff=1? Actually $x_2 - 2 = 0$, $x_1 - 1 = 0$) | **Prune** |
| 2 | $x_2 = 3$ | OK | Recurse |
| 3 | $x_3 = 1$ | Same column as row 1 | **Prune** |
| 3 | $x_3 = 2$ | OK | Recurse |
| 4 | $x_4 = 1$ | Same column as row 1 | **Prune** |
| 4 | $x_4 = 2$ | Same column as row 3 | **Prune** |
| 4 | $x_4 = 3$ | Same column as row 2 | **Prune** |
| 4 | $x_4 = 4$ | Diagonal conflict with row 3: $4-4=0$, $2-3=-1$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| 3 | $x_3 = 3$ | Diagonal conflict with row 2: $3-3=0$, $3-2=1$ OK, but $3+3=6$, $3+2=5$, OK; check row 1: $3-3=0$, $1-1=0$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| 3 | $x_3 = 4$ | Diagonal with row 2: $4-3=1$, $3-2=1$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| 2 | $x_2 = 4$ | Diagonal: $4-2=2$, $1-1=0$ OK; $4+2=6$, $1+1=2$ OK — OK | Recurse |
| 3 | $x_3 = 1$ | Same column as row 1 | **Prune** |
| 3 | $x_3 = 2$ | Same col as row 2 | **Prune** |
| 3 | $x_3 = 3$ | OK | Recurse |
| 4 | $x_4 = 1$ | Same col row 1 | **Prune** |
| 4 | $x_4 = 2$ | Same col row 2 | **Prune** |
| 4 | $x_4 = 3$ | Same col row 3 | **Prune** |
| 4 | $x_4 = 4$ | Diag with row 1: $4-4=0$, $1-1=0$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| 3 | $x_3 = 4$ | Diag with row 2: $4-3=1$, $4-2=2$ OK; diag with row 1: $4-3=1$, $1-1=0$ OK — OK | Recurse |
| 4 | $x_4 = 1$ | Same col row 1 | **Prune** |
| 4 | $x_4 = 2$ | Same col row 2 | **Prune** |
| 4 | $x_4 = 3$ | Diag with row 3: $3-4=-1$, $4-3=1$ OK; diag row 2: $3-4=-1$, $4-2=2$ OK; diag row 1: $3-4=-1$, $1-1=0$ OK — OK | **SOLUTION** (2,4,1,3) |
| 4 | continue | — | All tried |
| | | | **SOLUTION FOUND:** $(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4) = (2,4,1,3)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $n=4$, the algorithm discovers **2 distinct solutions**: $(2,4,1,3)$ and $(3,1,4,2)$. The recursive backtrack explores a maximum of $1 + 4 + 4\cdot 4 + 4^3 + 4^4 = 341$ nodes — but due to pruning, the **visited node count is dramatically reduced**.

### Final Time-Complexity Analysis for N-Queens

$$
T(n) = O(n!)
$$

with practical observed bound $\approx O(n! / 3)$ after constraint pruning.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Backtracking — Generic Recursive Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ROOT: k = 0, empty partial vector"] --> B["Call Backtrack x 1..k"]
    B --> C["Loop over all candidates in S k+1"]
    C --> D["Assign x k+1 = candidate"]
    D --> E{"Constraint / Bounding Function B(x)?"}
    E -- "FALSE - prune" --> F["Dead Node - discard"]
    F --> C
    E -- "TRUE - promising" --> G{"Reached depth n?"}
    G -- "YES - leaf solution" --> H["OUTPUT complete solution"]
    H --> C
    G -- "NO - recurse" --> I["Recursive call Backtrack x 1..k+1"]
    I --> J["After return - implicit backtrack"]
    J --> C
    C -- "All candidates tried" --> K["Return to parent caller"]
```

## 4.2 State-Space Tree for Sum-of-Subsets (Worked Example $n=4$)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Root: S = 0"] --> N1["Include w1=5, S=5"]
    R --> N2["Exclude w1=5, S=0"]
    N1 --> N11["+w2=7, S=12"]
    N1 --> N12["-w2=7, S=5"]
    N11 --> N111["+w3=10, S=22 SOLUTION"]
    N11 --> N112["-w3=10, S=12"]
    N112 --> N1121["+w4=12 PRUNE S+w gt M"]
    N12 --> N121["+w3=10, S=15 PRUNE 15+12=27 gt 22"]
    N12 --> N122["-w3=10, S=5"]
    N122 --> N1221["+w4=12, S=17 PRUNE 17 gt M"]
    N122 --> N1222["-w4=12, S=5 DEAD"]
    N2 --> N21["+w2=7, S=7"]
    N2 --> N22["-w2=7, S=0"]
    N21 --> N211["+w3=10, S=17 PRUNE"]
    N21 --> N212["-w3=10, S=7"]
    N212 --> N2121["+w4=12, S=19 PRUNE"]
    N212 --> N2122["-w4=12 DEAD"]
    N22 --> N221["+w3=10, S=10"]
    N22 --> N222["-w3=10 DEAD"]
    N221 --> N2211["+w4=12, S=22 SOLUTION"]
    N221 --> N2212["-w4=12 PRUNE"]
    style N111 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style N2211 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 N-Queens (n=4) Search Tree — Pruning Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Row 0: empty"] --> A1["x1=1"]
    R --> A2["x1=2"]
    R --> A3["x1=3"]
    R --> A4["x1=4"]
    A1 --> B1["x2=1 PRUNE column"]
    A1 --> B2["x2=2 PRUNE diag"]
    A1 --> B3["x2=3 PROMISING"]
    A1 --> B4["x2=4 PROMISING"]
    B3 --> C1["x3=1 PRUNE col"]
    B3 --> C2["x3=2 PROMISING"]
    B3 --> C3["x3=3 PRUNE diag"]
    B3 --> C4["x3=4 PRUNE diag"]
    B4 --> D1["x3=1 PRUNE col"]
    B4 --> D2["x3=2 PRUNE col"]
    B4 --> D3["x3=3 PROMISING"]
    B4 --> D4["x3=4 PRUNE diag"]
    C2 --> E1["x4=1 PRUNE"]
    C2 --> E2["x4=2 PRUNE"]
    C2 --> E3["x4=3 PRUNE"]
    C2 --> E4["x4=4 PRUNE DEAD branch"]
    D3 --> F1["x4=1 PRUNE"]
    D3 --> F2["x4=2 PRUNE"]
    D3 --> F3["x4=3 SOLUTION 2,4,1,3"]
    D3 --> F4["x4=4 PRUNE"]
    style F3 fill:#9f9,stroke:#060
    style E4 fill:#f99,stroke:#600
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Backtracking Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        I1["Problem Instance"]
        I2["Constraint Set"]
        I3["Bounding Function"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["Backtracking Core"]
```
