```mermaid
        P["Partial Solution Builder"]
        B["Feasibility Tester"]
        E["E-Node Manager"]
        S["State-Space Tree Traversal"]
        R["Recursion Stack"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        O1["Solution Collector"]
        O2["State-Space Tree Report"]
        O3["Performance Metrics"]
    end
    I1 --> P
    I2 --> B
    I3 --> B
    P --> S
    B --> S
    S --> E
    E --> R
    R --> P
    S --> O1
    S --> O2
    S --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** The **Input Layer** feeds the problem instance and constraints to the **Backtracking Core**, which iterates: (i) builds a partial vector, (ii) tests it for feasibility via the bounding function, (iii) recurses or prunes accordingly. The **Output Layer** collects all valid complete solutions, reports the explored sub-tree, and emits performance counters.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define backtracking. List any four problems that can be solved using backtracking.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Backtracking is an algorithmic technique for solving combinatorial problems by building solutions incrementally and abandoning a partial solution ("backtracking") as soon as it is determined that the partial solution cannot be extended to a complete valid solution.
>
> **Four Problems (1 mark — ¼ each):**
> 1. **N-Queens Problem** — placing $n$ queens on an $n \times n$ board.
> 2. **Sum-of-Subsets Problem** — finding subsets whose elements sum to a target $M$.
> 3. **Graph (m-)Coloring Problem** — coloring vertices with $m$ colors such that no adjacent vertices share a color.
> 4. **Hamiltonian Cycle Problem** — finding a cycle visiting each vertex exactly once.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between backtracking and branch-and-bound algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect (½ mark each) | Backtracking | Branch and Bound |
|----------------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Search Strategy** | Depth-First Search (DFS) | BFS or best-first with cost |
| **Pruning Criterion** | Constraint feasibility (binary feasible / infeasible) | Bound on the objective value (upper/lower) |
| **Problem Type** | Decision / enumeration problems | **Optimization** problems (min/max) |
| **Tree Type** | Implicit tree of partial solutions | State-space tree with costs |
| **Goal** | Find *all* (or one) feasible solution | Find the *optimal* solution |
| **Example** | N-Queens, Subset Sum | 0/1 Knapsack, TSP |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — **14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / KTU Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **general method of backtracking** with the help of a state-space tree. Use a suitable problem to illustrate the concept of **bounding function** and **promising / non-promising nodes**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Solve the **Sum-of-Subsets problem** using backtracking for the instance:
$$
W = \{11, 13, 24, 7\} \quad \text{with} \quad M = 31
$$
Sort the weights in non-decreasing order, construct the **state-space tree**, and list **all valid subsets**. Clearly indicate which nodes are pruned by the bounding function. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — General Method:**
Backtracking solves problems whose solution is an $n$-tuple $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ where each $x_i$ is drawn from a finite, ordered set $S_i$. The algorithm builds the tuple component by component; at each stage, it tests whether the partial tuple can lead to a complete solution. If not, it *backtracks* to try the next candidate.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Recursive Template (canonical form):**

```
Algorithm Backtrack(k):
   for each x[k] ∈ S_k do
       if Bound(x[1..k]) then
            if k == n then Output x[1..n]
            else Backtrack(k+1)
```

**Step 3 (2 marks) — State-Space Tree Description:**
A state-space tree is a rooted tree where:
- The **root** corresponds to the empty partial vector.
- **Internal nodes** correspond to promising partial vectors.
- **Leaves** are either solutions or dead (non-promising) nodes.
- **Bounding function $B$** decides which branches to prune.

**Example:** For N-Queens with $n=4$, the root has children $(1, *, *, *), (2, *, *, *), (3, *, *, *), (4, *, *, *)$. After placing $x_1 = 1$, only $x_2 \in \{3, 4\}$ are *promising*; $x_2 \in \{1, 2\}$ are *non-promising* and pruned.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Sort:** $W = \{7, 11, 13, 24\}$, $M = 31$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Bounding Function:**
For partial sum $S$ at depth $k$:
$$
B : \quad (S + w_{k+1} \le M) \;\;\land\;\; \big(S + \sum_{j=k+1}^{n} w_j \ge M\big)
$$

**Step 3 (5 marks) — State-Space Tree Traversal:**

| Step | Move | $S$ | Test | Status |
|------|------|-----|------|--------|
| 1 | Include 7 | 7 | $7+11=18 \le 31$, $7+11+13+24=55 \ge 31$ | **Promising** |
| 2 | Include 11 | 18 | $18+13=31 \le 31$, $18+13+24=55 \ge 31$ | **Promising** |
| 3 | Include 13 | 31 | $31 = 31$ → **SOLUTION** $\{7,11,13\}$ | ✓ |
| 4 | Exclude 13, include 24 | 42 | $42 > 31$ | **PRUNE** |
| 5 | Back: exclude 11, include 13 | 20 | $20+24=44 > 31$ | **PRUNE** |
| 6 | Back: exclude 7, include 11 | 11 | $11+13=24 \le 31$, $11+13+24=48 \ge 31$ | **Promising** |
| 7 | Include 13 | 24 | $24+24=48 > 31$ | **PRUNE** |
| 8 | Exclude 13, include 24 | 35 | $35 > 31$ | **PRUNE** |
| 9 | Back: exclude 11, include 13 | 13 | $13+24=37 > 31$ | **PRUNE** |
| 10 | Back: include 24 | 24 | $24 \le 31$, $24+13+11+7=55 \ge 31$ | **Promising** |
| 11 | Include 13 | 37 | $37 > 31$ | **PRUNE** |
| 12 | All branches exhausted | — | — | **END** |

**Final Answer (1 mark):** Solution subset = $\{7, 11, 13\}$ only. Sum $= 7 + 11 + 13 = 31$ ✓.

---

### Question B — **14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **N-Queens problem**. Write the **constraint equations** and demonstrate the backtracking algorithm for $n = 4$, drawing the corresponding state-space tree. List all valid solutions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **0/1 Knapsack problem** solved via backtracking. Define the bounding function used in the branch-and-bound variant and explain how the upper bound $\hat{B}$ is computed using the **fractional knapsack relaxation**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Problem:** Place $n$ queens on an $n \times n$ chessboard such that no two queens attack each other (no shared row, column, or diagonal).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Solution Representation:** Let $x_i$ = column of the queen placed in row $i$ ($1 \le i \le n$). Because we place exactly one queen per row, the row-constraint is implicit.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Constraint Equations:** For all $i \ne k$:
$$
x_i \ne x_k \quad \land \quad x_i - i \ne x_k - k \quad \land \quad x_i + i \ne x_k + k
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — State-Space Tree and Solutions for $n=4$:**

The tree was illustrated in **Section 4.3** above. The two valid solutions are:

- **Solution 1:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4) = (2, 4, 1, 3)$
- **Solution 2:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4) = (3, 1, 4, 2)$

Board visualization for Solution 1:

| Row \ Col | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | . | Q | . | . |
| **2** | . | . | . | Q |
| **3** | Q | . | . | . |
| **4** | . | . | Q | . |

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — 0/1 Knapsack as a Backtracking Problem:**
Given $n$ items with weights $\{w_1, \ldots, w_n\}$, profits $\{p_1, \ldots, p_n\}$ and knapsack capacity $W$, the backtracking solution vector is $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Bounding function (feasibility):**
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i x_i + w_{k+1} \le W
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Branch-and-Bound Optimization Upper Bound $\hat{B}$:**
At any node $k$, compute the optimistic upper bound by **relaxing the 0/1 constraint to fractional**, i.e., allow taking a *fraction* of the next item:

$$
\hat{B} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i x_i}_{\text{committed profit}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=k+1}^{j-1} p_i}_{\text{take whole remaining items up to } j-1} + \underbrace{(W - W') \cdot \frac{p_j}{w_j}}_{\text{fraction of item } j}
$$

where $W' = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i x_i + \sum_{i=k+1}^{j-1} w_i$ is the weight after including all remaining full items up to index $j-1$, and item $j$ is the first item that *cannot* fit in entirety.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Worked Numerical Example:**
Let $n = 3$, $W = 30$, $(w_1, w_2, w_3) = (15, 10, 18)$, $(p_1, p_2, p_3) = (40, 30, 25)$.

Sort by $p_i / w_i$ (descending): Item 1 = $40/15 \approx 2.67$, Item 2 = $30/10 = 3.0$, Item 3 = $25/18 \approx 1.39$.

Reorder: Item 2, Item 1, Item 3 → $(w', p') = (10, 30), (15, 40), (18, 25)$.

At root ($k = 0$):
$$
\hat{B} = 0 + 30 + (30 - 10) \cdot \frac{40}{15} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = 30 + 20 \cdot 2.67 = 30 + 53.33 = 83.33
$$

(Since $W - w_2 = 20 \ge w_1 = 15$, take all of Item 1 → weight now = 25, $W - 25 = 5$.)
$$
\hat{B} = 30 + 40 + 5 \cdot \frac{25}{18} = 70 + 6.94 = 76.94
$$

This bound is then used to prune any node whose upper bound is ≤ the best feasible profit found so far.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting to SORT** weights/profits before bounding → bounding function gives incorrect prune decisions → **–2 marks**.
> 2. **❌ Using the wrong constraint** (e.g., row-conflict in N-Queens while one-queen-per-row is implicit) → **–1.5 marks**.
> 3. **❌ Confusing backtracking with branch-and-bound** in 14-mark questions → write both definitions explicitly to avoid ambiguity → **–2 marks**.
> 4. **❌ Skipping the state-space tree diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions → KTU examiner expects the diagram with prune marks → **–3 marks**.
> 5. **❌ Not labelling "promising" vs "non-promising"** nodes in the tree → examiners specifically check this distinction → **–1 mark**.
> 6. **❌ Computing fractional bound incorrectly** — make sure $W - W' < w_j$ before taking the fractional part, else you may include full item $j$ → **–2 marks**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Backtracking — KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- [x] Backtracking is **DFS on an implicit state-space tree** with **constraint-based pruning**.
- [x] **Bounding function** $B(x_1, \ldots, x_k)$ decides promising vs. non-promising nodes.
- [x] **E-Node** = node being expanded; **Live Node** = generated but not yet expanded; **Dead Node** = cannot yield a solution.
- [x] **Recurrence** for total nodes in a perfect binary backtracking tree: $T(n) = 2^{n+1} - 1$.
- [x] **Sum-of-Subsets:** sort first; bounding uses $S + w_{k+1} \le M$ AND $S + \sum_{\text{remaining}} w_j \ge M$.
- [x] **N-Queens:** $x_i \ne x_k$, $x_i - i \ne x_k - k$, $x_i + i \ne x_k + k$ — solve by row.
- [x] **0/1 Knapsack (BB variant):** upper bound = committed profit + sum of remaining full profits + fractional part of next item.
- [x] **Graph Coloring:** $B$ checks no two adjacent vertices share a color.
- [x] **Hamiltonian Cycle:** $B$ checks next vertex is adjacent and not yet visited.
- [x] **Time complexity is exponential** $O(b^n)$ in worst case but **pruning is the key**.
- [x] **Monte-Carlo estimate** of total nodes: $m \approx \dfrac{n_s (N+1)}{n_s + 1}$.
- [x] **Backtracking ≠ Branch & Bound** — the latter optimizes; the former enumerates/feasibility-tests.
- [x] Always **draw the state-space tree** in KTU 14-mark answers — examiners expect it.
- [x] **Sort inputs in non-decreasing order** for subset/knapsack-style problems (critical for bounding).
- [x] **Pre-emptively label** nodes as "Promising" / "Non-Promising (Pruned)" / "Solution" in the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Branch and Bound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Branch and Bound (B&B)** is a systematic, state-space search algorithmic paradigm used primarily to solve **combinatorial optimization problems** and **integer programming problems**. It systematically enumerates candidate solutions by means of *state space representation*, where the set of candidate solutions is thought of as forming a rooted tree (the **search tree**) with the full set at the root.

The method operates on two complementary mechanisms:

1. **Branching** — the process of decomposing a non-atomic problem (a *live node*) into two or more disjoint sub-problems (child nodes), each representing a partial or refined candidate solution.
2. **Bounding** — the computation of a *bound* (lower bound for minimization, upper bound for maximization) on the best possible solution value achievable from a given sub-problem, used to **prune (fathom)** entire branches of the search tree that cannot yield a better solution than the best already known.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Branch and Bound is an algorithm design strategy that combines the space-search structure of Backtracking with intelligent pruning based on estimated bound values, guaranteeing optimality for problems where bounding functions are valid (admissible).

A node in the search tree is classified as one of:
- **Live Node** — a node that has been generated but whose children have not yet been explored.
- **E-Node (Expansion Node)** — the live node currently being explored/expanded (its children are generated next).
- **Dead Node** — a generated node that is *not* to be expanded further (either because it cannot yield a better solution than the current best, or it has already produced a complete solution).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Treasure Hunter with a Treasure Map"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are a treasure hunter dropped into a vast underground cave system. You have a magical device that tells you the **minimum possible treasure** hidden in any unexplored tunnel (the **bound**). You always walk down the most promising tunnel first (the one with the highest minimum potential). The moment a tunnel's maximum possible treasure is *less than* the best treasure you've already found, you **collapse that tunnel** (pruning) and never look back.

Translating the analogy:
- The cave system = the **state-space tree** of all possible solutions.
- Each fork in the tunnel = a **branching decision** (e.g., assign job A to worker 1, or to worker 2).
- The magical device's reading = the **bounding function** estimate.
- Collapsing a tunnel = **fathoming/pruning** an unpromising sub-tree.
- Walking down the most promising tunnel first = the strategy (FIFO / LIFO / LC).

### 1.3 Bounding Functions — The Heart of B&B

A **bounding function** $B(v)$ assigned to a node $v$ provides a guaranteed estimate of the best objective value reachable from $v$:

- For a **minimization** problem: $B(v) \le \text{OPT}(v)$ (a *lower* bound on the optimal cost from $v$).
- For a **maximization** problem: $B(v) \ge \text{OPT}(v)$ (an *upper* bound on the best profit from $v$).

If at any point the bound of a live node is *worse* than the current best-known solution, that node is **killed (pruned)** — there is no need to explore its descendants.

### 1.4 Why Branch and Bound? — Engineering Relevance

| Application Domain | Problem Solved |
|---|---|
| Operations Research | Assignment Problem, Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) |
| Logistics & Supply Chain | Vehicle Routing, Job-Shop Scheduling |
| VLSI Design | Circuit Partitioning, Placement, Routing |
| AI / Game Theory | Adversarial Search with $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning (conceptual cousin) |
| Bioinformatics | Sequence Alignment with bounded DP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Difference from Backtracking:** Backtracking only solves *feasibility* problems (does a solution exist?), whereas Branch and Bound solves *optimization* problems (find the *best* feasible solution). Backtracking uses no quantitative bound — B&B uses explicit numerical bounds to prune.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Algorithmic Structure

Branch and Bound for a minimization problem $\Pi$ proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Initialize:**
- Compute a lower bound $LB(\text{root})$ for the entire problem. Set $\text{BestSolution} = +\infty$.

**Step 2 — Select a live node:**
- According to a chosen strategy, pick the next E-node (Expansion Node) from the set of live nodes.

**Step 3 — Branch:**
- Generate all children of the E-node by making one additional decision (e.g., assign worker $i$ to job $j$).

**Step 4 — Bound:**
- For each child node $v$, compute a bound $B(v)$.

**Step 5 — Compare & Prune:**
- If $v$ represents a *complete feasible solution*: update $\text{BestSolution}$ if $B(v)$ is better.
- Else if $B(v) \ge \text{BestSolution}$: **prune** $v$ (it cannot yield a better solution).
- Else: add $v$ to the set of live nodes.

**Step 6 — Terminate:**
- Repeat Step 2–5 until the live-node set is empty. Report $\text{BestSolution}$.

### 2.2 The Three Principal Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The choice of data structure for storing live nodes defines the variant of B&B. This is a **favourite 7-mark question**.

| Strategy | Data Structure for Live Nodes | Order of Expansion | Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FIFO Branch and Bound** | **Queue** (First-In-First-Out) | Oldest live node first | Equivalent to **Breadth-First Search** on the state-space tree |
| **LIFO Branch and Bound** | **Stack** (Last-In-First-Out) | Newest live node first | Equivalent to **Depth-First Search** on the state-space tree |
| **LC Branch and Bound** (Least-Cost Search) | **Min-Heap / Priority Queue** | Node with **best (lowest) bound** first | *Best-First* — typically explores the fewest nodes |

- The **LC (Least Cost) Search** is the most efficient in practice because it always expands the node that is *closest* to the optimal solution.
- The node selected for expansion is called the **E-node**; after expansion, it becomes a **dead node**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Assignment Problem (Cost Matrix Reduction)

For the **Assignment Problem** with $n \times n$ cost matrix $C = [c_{ij}]$, the **lower bound** for the root is computed via matrix reduction:

$$
LB_{\text{root}} \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i}_{\text{row reduction}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j^{\,'}}_{\text{column reduction}}
$$

where:
- $r_i = \min_{j} c_{ij}$ — minimum of row $i$.
- $c_j^{\,'} = \min_{i}\bigl(c_{ij} - r_i\bigr)$ — minimum of the row-reduced column $j$.

After branching (assigning worker $i$ to job $j$), the lower bound of a child node is:

$$
LB_{\text{child}} \;=\; LB_{\text{parent}} \;+\; \Delta_{ij}
$$

where $\Delta_{ij}$ is the extra cost incurred due to setting $c_{ij}=0$ in the reduced matrix and re-reducing.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — 0/1 Knapsack via B&B

For a knapsack of capacity $W$ with $n$ items, items sorted by **profit-to-weight ratio** $p_i / w_i$ in decreasing order. The **upper bound** (optimistic bound) for a partial assignment node is:

$$
UB(v) \;=\; \text{profit accrued so far at } v \;+\; \sum_{\text{remaining items } i \text{ (fractional)}} w_i \cdot \frac{p_i}{w_i}
$$

A child node explores "take item $i$" ($x_i = 1$) or "skip item $i$" ($x_i = 0$). If $UB(v) \le \text{BestProfit}$, prune.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)

For a symmetric TSP with cost matrix $C$, the lower bound at any node is obtained by:
$$
LB(v) \;=\; \sum_{\text{rows } i \notin \text{path}} \min_j c_{ij} \;+\; \text{fixed edges from partial path}
$$

### 2.6 Summary Cheat-Sheet Table

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Lower Bound (Min Problem) | $LB(v) \le \text{OPT}$ | Minimum cost achievable from $v$ |
| Upper Bound (Max Problem) | $UB(v) \ge \text{OPT}$ | Maximum profit achievable from $v$ |
| Fathoming (Pruning) | $LB(v) \ge \text{Best}$ or $UB(v) \le \text{Best}$ | Kill node $v$ |
| FIFO B&B E-node selection | Oldest in queue | BFS order |
| LIFO B&B E-node selection | Top of stack | DFS order |
| LC B&B E-node selection | $\min_v B(v)$ over all live $v$ | Best-first order |
| Assignment LB increment | $\Delta_{ij}$ | Extra reduction after assigning row $i$ to col $j$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

Branch and Bound underpins modern **Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solvers** such as CPLEX, Gurobi, and COIN-OR. Industrial applications include airline crew scheduling, semiconductor wafer manufacturing, supply-chain optimization, and protein-structure prediction. In every case, the *bounding function* determines the practical tractability — a tighter bound means exponentially fewer nodes explored.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Worked examples on the **Assignment Problem** are the most commonly asked 14-mark questions. Master the cost-matrix reduction technique.

### 3.1 Worked Example — Assignment Problem via LC Branch & Bound

**Problem Statement:** Solve the following $3 \times 3$ assignment problem (rows = workers A, B, C; columns = jobs J1, J2, J3). Minimize total assignment cost.

$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 11 & 12 & 18 \\ 5 & 10 & 15 \\ 13 & 14 & \text{inf} \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 1 — Row Reduction (subtract row minimum from each row)

Row minima: $r_A = 11$, $r_B = 5$, $r_C = 13$. Subtract:

$$
C^{(1)} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 7 \\ 0 & 5 & 10 \\ 0 & 1 & \text{inf} \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 2 — Column Reduction (subtract column minimum from each column)

Column minima of $C^{(1)}$: $c_1 = 0$, $c_2 = 1$, $c_3 = 7$.

$$
C^{(2)} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & \text{inf} \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 3 — Lower Bound of the Root

$$
LB(\text{root}) \;=\; (11 + 5 + 13) \;+\; (0 + 1 + 7) \;=\; 29 + 8 \;=\; 37
$$

#### Step 4 — Choose Branching Variable

For each row $i$, identify the smallest penalty $\Delta_i$ of *not* choosing the zero in column $j$. The penalty of assigning row $i$ to column $j$ is the **second-smallest value in row $i$** of $C^{(2)}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_A &= \text{second smallest in row A} - \text{smallest} = 1 - 0 = 1 \quad (\text{assign } A \to J1) \\
\Delta_B &= 4 - 0 = 4 \quad (\text{assign } B \to J1) \\
\Delta_C &= \text{inf} - 0 = \text{inf} \quad (\text{assign } C \to J1, \text{ infeasible})
\end{aligned}
$$

Best choice: $B \to J1$ has $\Delta_B = 4$. But we explore all three branches, ranked by their lower bounds.

#### Step 5 — Branch A → J1

Set $c_{A,J1} = 0$, eliminate row A and column J1. Remaining cost:

$$
C_A \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 0 & \text{inf} \end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{rows B, C ; cols J2, J3})
$$

Row reductions: row B: 3; row C: 0. Column reductions: col J2: 0; col J3: 3.

$$
LB(A \to J1) \;=\; LB(\text{root}) \;+\; \Delta_A \;=\; 37 \;+\; 1 \;=\; 38
$$

#### Step 6 — Branch B → J1

Eliminate row B and column J1. Remaining:

$$
C_B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 7 \\ 1 & \text{inf} \end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{rows A, C ; cols J2, J3})
$$

Row reductions: row A: 1; row C: 1. Column reductions: both already 0.

$$
LB(B \to J1) \;=\; LB(\text{root}) \;+\; \Delta_B \;=\; 37 \;+\; 4 \;=\; 41
$$

#### Step 7 — Branch C → J1 (infeasible — column J1 already optimal for B since $c_{B,J1} = 5$)

Skip (penalty = inf).

#### Step 8 — LC Selection

Live nodes: $\{A \to J1 : 38,\; B \to J1 : 41\}$. Pick $A \to J1$ (smaller bound = more promising).

#### Step 9 — Expand Node (A → J1)

Remaining assignment: rows B, C to cols J2, J3. Cost matrix:

$$
C_{A} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 0 & \text{inf} \end{bmatrix}
$$

- Branch **B → J2** (penalty $\Delta = 3 - 0 = 3$): only feasible left is C → J3 (cost 14). Total: $11 + 10 + 14 = 35$. Update $\text{Best} = 35$.
- Branch **B → J3** (penalty = inf since C cannot take J3): prune.

#### Step 10 — Conclusion

Optimal Assignment: $A \to J1$ (cost 11), $B \to J2$ (cost 10), $C \to J3$ (cost 14).

$$
\text{Optimal Total Cost} \;=\; 11 + 10 + 14 \;=\; \mathbf{35}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — 0/1 Knapsack via LC Branch & Bound

**Problem:** Knapsack capacity $W = 10$. Three items:

| Item | Weight $w_i$ | Profit $p_i$ | Ratio $p_i/w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 40 | **10.0** |
| 2 | 7 | 42 | **6.0** |
| 3 | 5 | 25 | **5.0** |

Items already sorted by ratio. Build B&B tree:

- **Root** $v_0$: profit = 0, weight = 0. Upper bound (greedy fractional): $40 + 42 + (25 \times \frac{3}{5}) = 40 + 42 + 15 = 97$.

#### Branch 1: $x_1 = 1$ (take item 1)
- Profit = 40, weight = 4. Remaining capacity = 6.
- $UB = 40 + 42 + \frac{1}{7} \times 25 \to $ but item 2 needs 7 (cannot fully fit). Take item 3 fully (weight 5 ≤ 6): profit 25. So $UB = 40 + 25 = 65$.

#### Branch 2: $x_1 = 0$ (skip item 1)
- Profit = 0, weight = 0. Capacity = 10.
- $UB = 42 + 25 \times \frac{3}{5} = 42 + 15 = 57$.

LC picks Branch 1 ($UB = 65 > 57$). Expand Branch 1.

#### Expand Branch 1 ($x_1 = 1$, profit = 40):

**$x_2 = 1$:** weight would become $4 + 7 = 11 > 10$ → **infeasible, prune**.

**$x_2 = 0$:** profit = 40, weight = 4, capacity left = 6. $UB = 40 + 25 = 65$ (same as before since item 2 wasn't taken).

#### Expand $x_2 = 0$ (profit = 40):

**$x_3 = 1$:** weight = $4 + 5 = 9 \le 10$ ✓. Profit = $40 + 25 = 65$. Complete solution: $\text{Best} = 65$.

**$x_3 = 0$:** profit = 40, weight = 4. Complete solution: $\text{Best} = \max(40, 65) = 65$.

#### Prune Branch 2 (initial $x_1 = 0$): $UB = 57 < 65 = \text{Best}$. **Killed**.

#### Final Optimal Solution

$$
(x_1, x_2, x_3) \;=\; (1, 0, 1), \quad \text{Total Profit} \;=\; \mathbf{65}, \quad \text{Total Weight} \;=\; \mathbf{9}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (For KTU Theory)

| Feature | Backtracking | Branch and Bound | Dynamic Programming |
|---|---|---|---|
| Problem Type | Decision / Feasibility | Optimization | Optimization |
| Pruning Criterion | Constraint violation | Bound vs. current best | Sub-problem overlap |
| Optimality Guarantee | No (unless exhaustive) | Yes (with valid bound) | Yes |
| Bounding Function | Not used | Mandatory | Implicit (via DP table) |
| Typical Complexity | Exponential (unavoidable) | Exponential but often tractable | Polynomial (often) |
| E-node Storage | Implicit (recursion) | Explicit (queue / stack / heap) | Not applicable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — General Branch and Bound Search Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["ROOT NODE: Lower Bound = LB0"]
    nodeA["CHILD A: Bound = LB_A best promising"]
    nodeB["CHILD B: Bound = LB_B moderate"]
    nodeC["CHILD C: Bound = LB_C pruned worse than best"]
    leafA1["FEASIBLE SOLUTION: Cost = C1 updates BEST"]
    leafA2["FEASIBLE SOLUTION: Cost = C2 worse than BEST"]
    leafB1["FEASIBLE SOLUTION: Cost = C3 updates BEST"]
    deadNode["DEAD NODE: Pruned by bounding function"]

    root --> nodeA
    root --> nodeB
    root --> nodeC
    nodeA --> leafA1
    nodeA --> leafA2
    nodeB --> leafB1
    nodeC --> deadNode

    classDef liveNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000;
    classDef deadNodeStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000;
    classDef solutionNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000;
    class root,nodeA,nodeB liveNode;
    class nodeC,deadNode deadNodeStyle;
    class leafA1,leafA2,leafB1 solutionNode;
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** = Live nodes (still in the priority queue / stack / queue).
- **Green nodes** = Feasible complete solutions; compared against the current BEST.
- **Red nodes** = Dead/pruned nodes — killed because their bound cannot beat the current BEST.

### 4.2 Mermaid — LC Branch & Bound Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START: Input problem and cost matrix"]
    initA["Initialize Priority Queue PQ and Best = Infinity"]
    compA["Compute Lower Bound LB at root node"]
    pushA["Push root into PQ with key = LB"]
    loopA{"PQ is empty?"}
    popA["Pop node v with smallest bound LB_v"]
    branchA["Generate child nodes of v by branching"]
    boundA["For each child u: compute bound LB_u"]
    checkA{"Is u a complete solution?"}
    updateA{"LB_u less than Best?"}
    setBest["Best = LB_u, record assignment"]
    pruneA["Prune u, mark as DEAD"]
    push2["Push u into PQ"]

    startA --> initA --> compA --> pushA --> loopA
    loopA -- NO --> resultA["Return Best Solution"]
    loopA -- YES --> popA --> branchA --> boundA --> checkA
    checkA -- YES --> updateA
    checkA -- NO --> push2
    updateA -- YES --> setBest --> push2
    updateA -- NO --> pruneA

    classDef startStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000;
    classDef processStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000;
    classDef pruneStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000;
    classDef resultStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000;

    class startA startStyle;
    class initA,compA,pushA,popA,branchA,boundA,push2 processStyle;
    class pruneA pruneStyle;
    class resultA,setBest resultStyle;
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Live-Node Data Structure Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FIFOQ["FIFO BRANCH AND BOUND"]
        F1["QUEUE OLDEST FIRST"] --> F2["BREADTH FIRST ORDER"]
    end

    subgraph LIFOS["LIFO BRANCH AND BOUND"]
        L1["STACK NEWEST FIRST"] --> L2["DEPTH FIRST ORDER"]
    end

    subgraph LCS["LC BRANCH AND BOUND"]
        P1["PRIORITY QUEUE MIN HEAP"] --> P2["BEST FIRST ORDER BY BOUND"]
    end

    classDef fifoStyle fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000;
    classDef lifoStyle fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000;
    classDef lcStyle fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000;
    class F1,F2 fifoStyle;
    class L1,L2 lifoStyle;
    class P1,P2 lcStyle;
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — State Transitions of a Node in B&B

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NewNode: Generated by Branching
    NewNode --> LiveNode: Bound computed and promising
    LiveNode --> ENode: Selected by strategy for expansion
    ENode --> LiveNode: Children generated, parent becomes DEAD
    ENode --> DeadNode: Expanded fully no better solution
    LiveNode --> DeadNode: Pruned by bounding function
    DeadNode --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Define Branch and Bound. Differentiate between FIFO and LC Branch and Bound strategies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Branch and Bound is an algorithmic technique for solving combinatorial optimization problems by systematically exploring the state-space tree, using a *bounding function* to prune branches that cannot yield a solution better than the current best.

> **FIFO vs LC (2 marks):**
> | Aspect | FIFO B&B | LC B&B |
> |---|---|---|
> | Live-node storage | Queue | Min-Heap (priority queue) |
> | E-node selection rule | Oldest live node | Node with **smallest bound** |
> | Search order | Breadth-first | Best-first |
> | Nodes explored | Usually more | Usually fewer (more efficient) |

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a bounding function? Why is it important in Branch and Bound?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **bounding function** $B(v)$ assigned to a node $v$ estimates the best objective value achievable from $v$. For minimization, it gives a **lower bound** ($B(v) \le \text{OPT}$); for maximization, it gives an **upper bound** ($B(v) \ge \text{OPT}$).
>
> **Importance (1 mark each):**
> 1. **Pruning:** It allows killing entire sub-trees that cannot improve the current best, drastically reducing search space.
> 2. **Optimality guarantee:** A *valid (admissible) bounding function* ensures the algorithm finds the global optimum.
> 3. **Efficiency:** A *tight* bound leads to fewer nodes explored → better time complexity in practice.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]** — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the general Branch and Bound method with FIFO and LIFO strategies. Describe the role of **E-node**, **live node**, and **dead node**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following assignment problem using the **LC Branch and Bound** method (compute lower bounds by cost matrix reduction). **(7 marks)**

$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 2 & 7 \\ 6 & 4 & 3 \\ 5 & 8 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Branch and Bound Overview (2 marks):** B&B is an exhaustive search strategy for optimization. It builds a state-space tree where each node represents a partial assignment. Branching decomposes the problem; bounding prunes unfruitful branches.

2. **Node Classification (3 marks):**
   - **Live Node:** Generated but not yet expanded. A candidate for future expansion.
   - **E-node (Expansion Node):** The live node currently being expanded. Its children are generated next.
   - **Dead Node:** A node that has been generated but will *never* be expanded — either because it represents a complete (feasible or infeasible) solution, or its bound cannot improve the current best.

3. **FIFO vs LIFO (2 marks):**
   - **FIFO B&B** uses a queue — live nodes are processed in first-in-first-out order, equivalent to **BFS**. It expands older partial solutions before newer ones.
   - **LIFO B&B** uses a stack — live nodes are processed in last-in-first-out order, equivalent to **DFS**. Memory-efficient, useful when a feasible solution is found early to enable aggressive pruning.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:** Assignment problem solved via LC Branch & Bound.

> **Step 1 — Row Reduction:**
>
> Row A: min = 2. Row B: min = 3. Row C: min = 1.
>
> $$
> C^{(1)} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 0 & 5 \\ 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 4 & 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
> $$

> **Step 2 — Column Reduction:**
>
> Column 1: min = 3. Column 2: min = 0. Column 3: min = 0.
>
> $$
> C^{(2)} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
> $$

> **Step 3 — Root Lower Bound:**
>
> $$
> LB_{\text{root}} \;=\; (2 + 3 + 1) + (3 + 0 + 0) \;=\; 6 + 3 \;=\; \mathbf{9}
> $$

> **Step 4 — Compute Penalties at Root:** For each zero in $C^{(2)}$, penalty = (second smallest in row) − (smallest = 0).
>
> - Zero at $(A, J2)$: row A second-smallest = 4, so $\Delta = 4$.
> - Zero at $(B, J1)$: row B second-smallest = 1, so $\Delta = 1$.
> - Zero at $(B, J3)$: row B second-smallest = 1, so $\Delta = 1$.
> - Zero at $(C, J1)$: row C second-smallest = 1, so $\Delta = 1$.
> - Zero at $(C, J3)$: row C second-smallest = 7, so $\Delta = 7$.

> **Step 5 — Best Branch (largest penalty = most decisive):** Branch on $(A, J2)$ with $\Delta = 4$. Set $A \to J2$ (cost = 2). Eliminate row A and column J2.
>
> Reduced sub-matrix:
>
> $$
> C_{AJ2} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{rows B, C ; cols J1, J3})
> $$
>
> Row reductions: B: 0 (no change), C: 0 (no change). Column 1 min = 3, Column 3 min = 0.
>
> $$
> LB(A \to J2) \;=\; LB_{\text{root}} + \Delta(A,J2) \;=\; 9 + 4 \;=\; \mathbf{13}
> $$

> **Step 6 — Expand $A \to J2$:** Two sub-branches $B \to J1$ and $B \to J3$.
>
> - **Branch $B \to J1$:** Eliminate row B, col J1. Only assignment left: $C \to J3$, cost = 1. Total: $2 + 6 + 1 = \mathbf{9}$. Update $\text{Best} = 9$.
> - **Branch $B \to J3$:** Eliminate row B, col J3. Only assignment left: $C \to J1$, cost = 5. Total: $2 + 3 + 5 = \mathbf{10}$. Worse than current best (9); **prune**.

> **Step 7 — Prune Other Branches:** Other branches at root have $LB \le 9 + 1 = 10$ (since their $\Delta \le 1$), all $\ge \text{Best} = 9$. **All pruned.**

> **Final Optimal Assignment:**
>
> $$
> A \to J2, \quad B \to J1, \quad C \to J3
> $$
>
> $$
> \text{Minimum Cost} \;=\; 2 + 6 + 1 \;=\; \mathbf{9}
> $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Deduction Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract BOTH row AND column minima** when computing the root lower bound: lose up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up penalty (Δ) calculation** — penalty is the second-smallest in a row with a zero, not the largest: lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not comparing child bounds against the current BEST** before pruning: lose up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to mark nodes as DEAD after expansion**: conceptual loss of **1 mark** if asked explicitly.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]** — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **LC Branch and Bound** method. Discuss how it differs from FIFO and LIFO strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following **0/1 Knapsack problem** using Branch and Bound (LC search). Capacity $W = 10$. Items: $(w_1, p_1) = (2, 40)$, $(w_2, p_2) = (3, 50)$, $(w_3, p_3) = (5, 100)$. Show the full search tree and indicate which nodes are pruned. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **LC Branch and Bound (3 marks):** LC (Least Cost) search uses a **priority queue** (min-heap) keyed by the bounding function value. At each step, the live node with the **smallest bound** (most promising for minimization) is selected as the E-node. This is also called **best-first search**.

2. **Why LC is Efficient (2 marks):** Because it always expands the node closest to the optimum, LC generally explores **far fewer nodes** than FIFO or LIFO. The number of nodes explored is data-dependent, not fixed.

3. **Comparison with FIFO and LIFO (2 marks):**
   - **FIFO B&B:** Queue → BFS → explores by levels. May waste effort on non-optimal branches.
   - **LIFO B&B:** Stack → DFS → memory efficient, finds *a* feasible solution quickly for pruning.
   - **LC B&B:** Priority queue → Best-first → minimum-node exploration; ideal for optimization but requires computing & maintaining bounds in a heap.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Step 1 — Sort by Profit-to-Weight Ratio:**
>
> | Item | $w_i$ | $p_i$ | $p_i/w_i$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 2 | 3 | 50 | **16.67** |
> | 3 | 5 | 100 | **20.00** |
> | 1 | 2 | 40 | **20.00** |
>
> Reorder by ratio: **Item 3 → Item 1 → Item 2** (ratios 20.0, 20.0, 16.67).

> **Step 2 — Root Bound (fractional knapsack greedy):** Capacity = 10, take all of item 3 (weight 5, profit 100), all of item 1 (weight 2, profit 40), then fraction of item 2 (remaining capacity = 3, take all 3): profit 50.
>
> $$
> UB_{\text{root}} \;=\; 100 + 40 + 50 \;=\; \mathbf{190}
> $$

> **Step 3 — Branch on Item 3 ($x_3 = 1$ or $x_3 = 0$):**
>
> **Node A ($x_3 = 1$):** Profit = 100, weight = 5. Remaining capacity = 5.
> - $UB_A = 100 + 40 + \frac{5}{3} \times 50 \approx 100 + 40 + 83.33 = 223.33$.
> - Wait, remaining capacity 5: take item 1 fully (weight 2, profit 40), capacity left 3, take item 2 fully (weight 3, profit 50). Total: $100 + 40 + 50 = 190$. So $UB_A = 190$.
>
> **Node B ($x_3 = 0$):** Profit = 0, weight = 0. Capacity = 10.
> - $UB_B = 40 + 50 \times \frac{8}{3} = 40 + 133.33 = 173.33$. (Take item 1: 40, weight 2; remaining 8; take all item 2: weight 3, profit 50, remaining 5; take fraction: 100 × (5/5) = 100, total = 190.) Hmm let me recompute.
> - Take item 1 (weight 2): profit 40, capacity 8. Take item 2 (weight 3): profit 50, capacity 5. No more items. Total = $40 + 50 = 90$. So $UB_B = 90$.
>
> Re-summing: actually remaining items are 1 and 2. Item 1 ratio = 20, item 2 ratio = 16.67. Take item 1 fully (w=2, p=40), cap=8. Take item 2 fully (w=3, p=50), cap=5. Done. So $UB_B = 40 + 50 = 90$.

> **Step 4 — LC picks Node A ($UB_A = 190 > UB_B = 90$). Expand Node A.**
>
> **Branch $x_1 = 1$ (take item 1) at A:** weight = 5+2 = 7 ≤ 10. Profit = 140. Remaining cap = 3.
> - $UB = 140 + 50 = 190$ (item 2 fits fully in cap 3).
>
> **Branch $x_1 = 0$ (skip item 1) at A:** weight = 5. Profit = 100. Remaining cap = 5.
> - $UB = 100 + 50 = 150$ (item 2 weight 3 ≤ 5).
>
> LC picks $x_1 = 1$ branch (190 > 150). Expand.

> **Step 5 — Expand $x_1 = 1$ (profit 140, weight 7):**
>
> **$x_2 = 1$:** weight = 7+3 = 10 ≤ 10 ✓. **Complete solution: profit = 140 + 50 = 190.** Update $\text{Best} = 190$.
>
> **$x_2 = 0$:** weight = 7, profit = 140. **Complete solution: profit = 140.** Worse than 190.

> **Step 6 — Backtrack. Visit $x_1 = 0$ branch at A (UB = 150).** Since $150 < \text{Best} = 190$ → **PRUNED**.

> **Step 7 — Backtrack to Root. Visit $x_3 = 0$ branch (UB = 90).** Since $90 < \text{Best} = 190$ → **PRUNED**.

> **Final Optimal Solution:**
>
> $$
> (x_3, x_1, x_2) \;=\; (1, 1, 1), \quad \text{Total Profit} \;=\; \mathbf{190}, \quad \text{Total Weight} \;=\; \mathbf{10}
> $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Deduction Alert:**
> 1. **Not sorting items by $p_i/w_i$ before computing bound**: lose **1 mark** (bounding function requires this order).
> 2. **Using fractional knapsack value as the bound** without stating the upper-bound property: lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting to compare UB with the current BestProfit** at every live node before pruning: lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **Incorrectly computing the remaining capacity** after each "take" branch: lose **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not labelling pruned nodes in the tree diagram**: lose **1 mark**.

---

### 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Branch and Bound**

- **Definition:** B&B = systematic state-space search + bounding-based pruning for *optimization* problems.
- **Two pillars:** **Branching** (split into sub-problems) and **Bounding** (estimate the best value reachable from each sub-problem).
- **Three node types:** **Live**, **E-node (Expansion)**, **Dead**.
- **Three strategies:**
  - **FIFO B&B** → Queue → BFS order.
  - **LIFO B&B** → Stack → DFS order.
  - **LC B&B** → Priority queue (min-heap) → **Best-first** order (most efficient in practice).
- **Pruning Rule (minimization):** Kill any live node whose **lower bound ≥ current Best**.
- **Pruning Rule (maximization):** Kill any live node whose **upper bound ≤ current Best**.
- **Assignment Problem bound:** Row-reduction + Column-reduction of cost matrix.
- **0/1 Knapsack bound:** Greedy **fractional** knapsack value (valid upper bound).
- **TSP bound:** Reduced cost matrix sum.
- **Difference from Backtracking:** B&B solves optimization; Backtracking solves feasibility. B&B uses numerical bounds.
- **Difference from DP:** DP exploits overlapping sub-problems (polynomial time); B&B is exponential but bounded by pruning.
- **Admissibility:** A bounding function is **admissible** if it never *overestimates* the cost in minimization (or *underestimates* the profit in maximization) — this guarantees optimality.
- **Termination:** When the live-node set is empty, the current **BestSolution** is the global optimum.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always show *which node is pruned and why* (state the comparison: $LB_v \ge \text{Best}$ or $UB_v \le \text{Best}$).
- **Memory note:** LIFO B&B uses the least memory (stack only); LC B&B uses the most (priority queue).
- **Industrial relevance:** Foundation of all modern MILP solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi, COIN-OR Branch-and-Cut).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Randomized Algorithms, Asymptotic Notations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Randomized Algorithms & Asymptotic Notations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Randomized Algorithms — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Randomized Algorithm** is an algorithm that incorporates **randomness** (typically through a pseudorandom number generator, PRNG) as a fundamental part of its logic. The running time and/or the output of the algorithm may vary across different executions on the **same input**, unlike deterministic algorithms whose behavior is fully predictable.

Mathematically, a randomized algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ can be viewed as a probability distribution over a family of deterministic algorithms $\{\mathcal{A}_1, \mathcal{A}_2, \dots, \mathcal{A}_k\}$. For an input $x$, the algorithm's behavior is governed by random coin flips, giving rise to a **random variable** representing running time $T(x)$ or output correctness.

$$\text{Randomized Algorithm } \mathcal{A} : \{0,1\}^* \times \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^*$$

The second input represents the **random bit string** $r$ drawn uniformly at random from $\{0,1\}^n$.

### 1.2 Two Canonical Classes of Randomized Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification is a KTU Frequently Asked Area (2–3 marks)**

| Class | Guarantee | Example |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| **Las Vegas Algorithms** | Always produce **correct** output; running time is a random variable | Randomized QuickSort, Randomized Selection |
| **Monte Carlo Algorithms** | Running time is **deterministic/bounded**; output is correct with probability $\geq 1 - \epsilon$ | Primality Testing (Miller–Rabin), Min-Cut (Karger) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are searching for a name in a **phone book of 10,000 names**:
- **Deterministic**: Start from page 1 → go sequentially. Worst case = 10,000 checks.
- **Randomized**: Pick a random page, see if name is alphabetically before/after, then **repeat** with a freshly chosen random page from the narrowed range.

> Both run the *same logic*, but randomized picks the **starting point** randomly. On average, you finish much faster than sequential scan, and the result is always correct (Las Vegas property).

### 1.4 Asymptotic Notations — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Asymptotic notations** are mathematical tools used to describe the **limiting behavior** of a function as the input size $n \rightarrow \infty$. They abstract away hardware constants and lower-order terms, enabling a **machine-independent** comparison of algorithmic efficiency.

The five standard notations are summarized below with their bounding direction:

| Notation | Name | Bounding Direction |
|----------|------|--------------------|
| $O(g(n))$ | Big-O | **Upper** bound (worst case ceiling) |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | Big-Omega | **Lower** bound (best case floor) |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | Big-Theta | **Tight** bound (both upper & lower) |
| $o(g(n))$ | Little-o | **Strict upper** bound |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | Little-omega | **Strict lower** bound |

### 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy for Asymptotic Notations

Think of a function $f(n)$ describing the **fuel consumption** of a car as speed $n$ increases:
- **Big-O**: "Fuel will **never exceed** $g(n)$ gallons" → a *ceiling* on consumption.
- **Big-Omega**: "Fuel will **always be at least** $g(n)$ gallons" → a *floor* on consumption.
- **Big-Theta**: "Fuel is **sandwiched** between two copies of $g(n)$" → tight behavior.
- **Little-o**: "Fuel grows **strictly slower** than $g(n)$" → eventually dominated.
- **Little-omega**: "Fuel grows **strictly faster** than $g(n)$" → dominates eventually.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Randomized Algorithms — Types Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Las Vegas Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Las Vegas):** For every input $x$, the algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ always produces a **correct** output. The **expected running time** $E[T(x)]$ is bounded.

$$P[\mathcal{A}(x) \text{ is correct}] = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad E[T(x)] \leq T(n)$$

**Canonical Example — Randomized QuickSort:**
1. Pick pivot **uniformly at random** from the array.
2. Partition around pivot.
3. Recursively sort left and right subarrays.

The expected number of comparisons is bounded by $2n \ln n$, regardless of input ordering — **defeating the adversary** that constructs worst-case input for deterministic QuickSort.

### 2.1.2 Monte Carlo Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Monte Carlo):** The running time is deterministic or bounded, but the output may be **incorrect** with some small probability $\epsilon$.

$$P[\mathcal{A}(x) \text{ is correct}] \geq 1 - \frac{1}{2^c} \quad (c \in \mathbb{Z}^+)$$

If a Monte Carlo algorithm has a **one-sided error**, it can be amplified:
- Run independently $k$ times.
- Take majority vote.
- Error probability drops to $\leq \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^k$.

**Canonical Example — Karger's Min-Cut Algorithm:**
- Repeat contract-random-edge step until 2 nodes remain.
- Each iteration is independent.
- Success probability $\geq \frac{2}{n(n-1)}$.
- Running $O(n^2 \log n)$ repetitions gives error $< \frac{1}{n}$.

### 2.1.3 Comparison Table

| Property | Las Vegas | Monte Carlo |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Output correctness | **Always** correct | Correct w.h.p. |
| Running time | Random variable | Deterministic/Bounded |
| Amplification | Repeat until done | Repeat & take majority |
| Example | Randomized QuickSort | Miller–Rabin Primality |

## 2.2 Asymptotic Notations — Mathematical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Big-O Notation (Upper Bound) — $O(g(n))$

$$f(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \; \forall\, n \geq n_0$$

### 2.2.2 Big-Omega Notation (Lower Bound) — $\Omega(g(n))$

$$f(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \leq c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \; \forall\, n \geq n_0$$

### 2.2.3 Big-Theta Notation (Tight Bound) — $\Theta(g(n))$

$$f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff f(n) = O(g(n)) \text{ AND } f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$$

Equivalently:

$$\exists\, c_1, c_2 > 0,\, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } c_1 \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 \cdot g(n) \; \forall\, n \geq n_0$$

### 2.2.4 Little-o Notation (Strict Upper Bound) — $o(g(n))$

$$f(n) = o(g(n)) \iff \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0$$

### 2.2.5 Little-omega Notation (Strict Lower Bound) — $\omega(g(n))$

$$f(n) = \omega(g(n)) \iff \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$$

### 2.2.6 Complexity Class Hierarchy (Growth Rate Reference)

| Complexity | Name | Example Algorithm |
|------------|------|-------------------|
| $O(1)$ | Constant | Hash table lookup |
| $O(\log n)$ | Logarithmic | Binary search |
| $O(n)$ | Linear | Linear scan |
| $O(n \log n)$ | Log-linear | Merge Sort, Heap Sort |
| $O(n^2)$ | Quadratic | Bubble, Selection, Insertion Sort |
| $O(n^3)$ | Cubic | Naive matrix multiplication |
| $O(2^n)$ | Exponential | Recursive Fibonacci (naive) |
| $O(n!)$ | Factorial | Brute-force TSP |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of KTU Module 4 numerical questions.**

| Notation | Mathematical Definition | Verbal Meaning | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------------------|----------------|-------------|
| $O(g(n))$ | $\exists\, c, n_0 : f(n) \leq c\,g(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ | $f$ grows **no faster** than $g$ | Worst-case upper bound |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | $\exists\, c, n_0 : f(n) \geq c\,g(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ | $f$ grows **no slower** than $g$ | Best-case lower bound |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | $f(n) = O(g(n))$ **and** $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ | $f$ grows **at same rate** as $g$ | Tight bound (average/worst) |
| $o(g(n))$ | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0$ | $f$ grows **strictly slower** than $g$ | Sub-polynomial dominance |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$ | $f$ grows **strictly faster** than $g$ | Super-polynomial dominance |

### 2.3.1 Key Properties & Theorems

| Property | Statement |
|----------|-----------|
| Transitivity | $f = O(g)$ and $g = O(h) \Rightarrow f = O(h)$ |
| Reflexivity | $f = \Theta(f)$ |
| Symmetry | $f = \Theta(g) \Leftrightarrow g = \Theta(f)$ |
| Transpose Symmetry | $f = O(g) \Leftrightarrow g = \Omega(f)$ |
| $O$-$\Omega$-$\Theta$ relation | $f = \Theta(g) \Rightarrow f = O(g)$ and $f = \Omega(g)$ |

### 2.3.2 Limit Method for Asymptotic Comparisons

For functions $f, g > 0$:

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)}$$

| Result | Conclusion |
|--------|-----------|
| $L = c$ where $0 < c < \infty$ | $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ |
| $L = 0$ | $f(n) = o(g(n))$ and $g(n) = \omega(f(n))$ |
| $L = \infty$ | $f(n) = \omega(g(n))$ and $g(n) = o(f(n))$ |
| Limit does not exist | Use definition-based approach |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application Area | Utility |
|------------------|---------|
| **Cryptography** | Randomized primality testing underpins RSA key generation |
| **Network Routing** | Randomized load balancing in data center switches |
| **Machine Learning** | Stochastic Gradient Descent is a randomized optimization algorithm |
| **Compiler Design** | Randomized register allocation reduces spilling |
| **Computational Geometry** | Randomized incremental convex hull construction |
| **Compiler Optimization** | Asymptotic analysis guides choice of sorting routines in STL |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Proving $5n^2 + 3n + 10 = O(n^2)$

**Goal:** Find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $5n^2 + 3n + 10 \leq c \cdot n^2$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Step 1:** Identify dominant term. As $n$ grows large, $5n^2$ dominates $3n$ and $10$.

**Step 2:** Choose $n_0 = 1$ and attempt to find a value of $c$.

For $n \geq 1$:
$$3n \leq 3n^2 \quad \text{and} \quad 10 \leq 10n^2$$

Substituting:
$$5n^2 + 3n + 10 \leq 5n^2 + 3n^2 + 10n^2 = 18n^2$$

**Step 3:** Therefore choose $c = 18$ and $n_0 = 1$.

$$\boxed{f(n) = 5n^2 + 3n + 10 \leq 18n^2 = c \cdot g(n) \quad \forall\, n \geq 1}$$

Hence $5n^2 + 3n + 10 = O(n^2)$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proving $f(n) = 3n^3 - 5n = \Omega(n^3)$

**Goal:** Find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $3n^3 - 5n \geq c \cdot n^3$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Step 1:** We want a lower bound. For large $n$, $-5n$ becomes negligible compared to $3n^3$.

**Step 2:** For $n \geq 1$:
$$3n^3 - 5n \geq 3n^3 - 5n^3 = -2n^3$$

This is negative — not useful for $\Omega$.

**Step 3:** Try $n \geq 2$:
$$3n^3 - 5n \geq 3n^3 - \frac{5}{2}n^3 = \frac{1}{2}n^3$$

The inequality $5n \leq \frac{5}{2}n^3$ holds iff $n^2 \geq 2$, i.e., $n \geq 2$.

**Step 4:** Choose $c = \frac{1}{2}$ and $n_0 = 2$.

$$\boxed{3n^3 - 5n \geq \frac{1}{2}n^3 \quad \forall\, n \geq 2}$$

Hence $3n^3 - 5n = \Omega(n^3)$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Showing $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ when $f(n) = 6n^2 + 2n$ and $g(n) = n^2$

**Step 1:** Prove $f(n) = O(n^2)$.

For $n \geq 1$:
$$6n^2 + 2n \leq 6n^2 + 2n^2 = 8n^2$$

Choose $c_2 = 8$, $n_0 = 1$. So $f(n) = O(n^2)$.

**Step 2:** Prove $f(n) = \Omega(n^2)$.

For $n \geq 1$:
$$6n^2 + 2n \geq 6n^2 \geq 6n^2$$

Choose $c_1 = 6$, $n_0 = 1$. So $f(n) = \Omega(n^2)$.

**Step 3:** Combine both:

$$\boxed{f(n) = \Theta(n^2) \text{ with } c_1 = 6,\ c_2 = 8,\ n_0 = 1}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Limit Method Application

**Problem:** Compare $f(n) = n^2 \log n$ and $g(n) = n^{2.5}$.

**Step 1:** Form the ratio:
$$\frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \frac{n^2 \log n}{n^{2.5}} = \frac{\log n}{n^{0.5}}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the limit:
$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\log n}{n^{0.5}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply L'Hôpital's Rule. Let $x = n$, numerator derivative $= \frac{1}{n \ln 2}$, denominator derivative $= 0.5 n^{-0.5}$.

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\frac{1}{n \ln 2}}{0.5 n^{-0.5}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{0.5 \ln 2 \cdot n^{0.5}} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Since $L = 0$:

$$\boxed{n^2 \log n = o(n^{2.5}) \text{ and } n^2 \log n = O(n^{2.5})}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Expected Comparisons in Randomized QuickSort

**Setup:** Let $X$ = total number of comparisons in Randomized QuickSort on $n$ distinct elements. Indicator random variable:

$$X_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if elements } z_i \text{ and } z_j \text{ (with } i < j\text{) are compared} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Total comparisons:**
$$X = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} X_{ij}$$

**Step 1:** Compute probability that $z_i$ and $z_j$ are compared.

The elements $z_i, z_{i+1}, \dots, z_j$ form a subarray. Two elements are compared **iff one of them is the first pivot chosen** from this set of $(j - i + 1)$ elements.

$$P[X_{ij} = 1] = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

**Step 2:** Compute expected value using linearity of expectation:
$$E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

**Step 3:** Change variables: let $k = j - i$, where $k$ ranges from $1$ to $n - 1$. The inner sum has $(n - k)$ terms with $j - i + 1 = k + 1$:
$$E[X] = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} (n - k) \cdot \frac{2}{k + 1}$$

**Step 4:** Bound the sum using the integral approximation:

$$E[X] < \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{2n}{k} = 2n \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k} = 2n \cdot H_n$$

where $H_n$ is the $n$-th harmonic number. Since $H_n \leq \ln n + 1$:

$$\boxed{E[X] \leq 2n \ln n + 2n = O(n \log n)}$$

**Conclusion:** Randomized QuickSort achieves **expected** $O(n \log n)$ comparisons — independent of the input distribution.

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Analyzing a Recurrence via Asymptotic Notation

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, with $T(1) = 1$.

**Master's Theorem:** For $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ where $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

Compute $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$.

Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^0 n)$, Case 2 applies:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

This matches Merge Sort's complexity.

## 3.7 Comparative Matrix — Worst, Best, Average Case

| Algorithm | Best Case | Worst Case | Average Case |
|-----------|-----------|------------|--------------|
| Linear Search | $\Omega(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Binary Search | $\Omega(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| Bubble Sort | $\Omega(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| Insertion Sort | $\Omega(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| QuickSort (deterministic) | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| QuickSort (randomized) | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ with prob $\frac{1}{2^n}$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ expected |
| Merge Sort | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| Heap Sort | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Common Function Comparisons

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix to quickly determine asymptotic relationships between standard functions.**

| Comparison | Result | Reasoning |
|------------|--------|-----------|
| $n^{1.5}$ vs $n \log n$ | $n \log n = o(n^{1.5})$ | Polynomial beats polylog |
| $\log n$ vs $\sqrt{n}$ | $\log n = o(\sqrt{n})$ | Any root beats any log |
| $\log^2 n$ vs $n$ | $\log^2 n = o(n)$ | Polynomial beats polylog |
| $n^k$ vs $2^n$ | $n^k = o(2^n)$ | Exponential beats polynomial |
| $n!$ vs $2^n$ | $2^n = o(n!)$ | Factorial beats exponential |
| $n^n$ vs $n!$ | $n! = o(n^n)$ | Power beats factorial |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Randomized Algorithm Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Algorithm Input x] --> B[RANDOMIZED ALGORITHM]
    R[Random Bit Stream r] --> B
    B --> C{Output Guarantee?}
    C -- Always Correct --> D[LAS VEGAS TYPE]
    C -- Correct with Probability --> E[MONTE CARLO TYPE]
    D --> D1[Randomized QuickSort]
    D --> D2[Randomized Selection]
    D --> D3[Randomized Quickhull]
    E --> E1[Miller Rabin Primality]
    E --> E2[Karger Min Cut]
    E --> E3[Fingerprinting Strings]
    D1 --> F1[Expected Time Bound]
    D2 --> F1
    D3 --> F1
    E1 --> F2[Error Probability Bound]
    E2 --> F2
    E3 --> F2
```

## 4.2 Diagram — Asymptotic Notation Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[f of n] --> B{Asymptotic Analysis}
    B --> C[Big O Upper]
    B --> D[Big Omega Lower]
    B --> E[Big Theta Tight]
    B --> F[Little o Strict Upper]
    B --> G[Little omega Strict Lower]
    C --> H[worst case ceiling]
    D --> I[best case floor]
    E --> J[both directions]
    F --> K[strictly smaller growth]
    G --> L[strictly larger growth]
    E -.implies.-> C
    E -.implies.-> D
    F -.implies.-> C
    G -.implies.-> D
```

## 4.3 Diagram — Function Hierarchy Visual

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O1["O of 1 constant"] --> O2["O log n logarithmic"]
    O2 --> O3["O of n linear"]
    O3 --> O4["O n log n log linear"]
    O4 --> O5["O n squared quadratic"]
    O5 --> O6["O n cubed cubic"]
    O6 --> O7["O of 2 to n exponential"]
    O7 --> O8["O n factorial"]
    style O1 fill:#e0ffe0

```

> Each arrow indicates a **strict** asymptotic increase: $A \rightarrow B$ means $A = o(B)$.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Randomized QuickSort Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start with array A of n elements] --> R{Pick pivot randomly}
    R --> P[Partition A around pivot]
    P --> L[Left subarray less than pivot]
    P --> RT[Right subarray greater than pivot]
    L --> R1{Recurse left}
    RT --> R2{Recurse right}
    R1 --> B[Base case size 1]
    R2 --> B
    B --> O[Combine sorted halves]
    O --> END[Sorted array output]
```

## 4.5 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Notation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Analyze function f of n] --> A1{Know exact behavior?}
    A1 -- Yes --> A2[Use Theta notation]
    A1 -- No --> A3{Know only worst case?}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Use Big O notation]
    A3 -- No --> A5{Know only best case?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Use Big Omega notation]
    A5 -- No --> A7{Need strict comparison?}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Use little o or little omega]
    A7 -- No --> A9[Use Master Theorem or limit method]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Modeled on KTU University Exam pattern — direct, definition-based.*

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Las Vegas and Monte Carlo randomized algorithms with one example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Las Vegas | Monte Carlo |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Output | Always correct | Correct with high probability |
| Running time | Random variable | Deterministic or bounded |
| Error type | No output error | Possible incorrect output |
| Example | Randomized QuickSort | Miller–Rabin Primality Test |
| Amplification | Repeat until done | Repeat and majority vote |

A **Las Vegas algorithm** such as Randomized QuickSort always produces a sorted array, but its running time is a random variable with expected bound $O(n \log n)$. A **Monte Carlo algorithm** such as Miller–Rabin returns "composite" or "prime" with error probability $\leq \frac{1}{4^c}$ after $c$ independent rounds.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Big-O notation? Define it formally and state its significance in algorithm analysis. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Big-O notation describes an **asymptotic upper bound** on the growth rate of a function.

**Formal Definition:** $f(n) = O(g(n))$ if there exist constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Significance:**
1. Provides a **machine-independent** measure of efficiency.
2. Abstracts away hardware-dependent constants.
3. Focuses on growth for **large inputs**.
4. Used to characterize **worst-case** running time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Provide full model solutions.*

---

### Question B1 — Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain different asymptotic notations used to analyze algorithms with mathematical definitions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five asymptotic notations are:

**1. Big-O — $O(g(n))$ — Upper Bound:**

$$f(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\, n_0 > 0 : 0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)\, \forall\, n \geq n_0$$

This says $f$ grows **no faster** than $g$ asymptotically. Example: $3n^2 + 5n = O(n^2)$.

**2. Big-Omega — $\Omega(g(n))$ — Lower Bound:**

$$f(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\, n_0 > 0 : 0 \leq c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n)\, \forall\, n \geq n_0$$

This says $f$ grows **no slower** than $g$. Example: $3n^2 + 5n = \Omega(n^2)$.

**3. Big-Theta — $\Theta(g(n))$ — Tight Bound:**

$$f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff f(n) = O(g(n)) \text{ and } f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$$

Equivalently: $\exists\, c_1, c_2 > 0, n_0 > 0$ such that $c_1 g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 g(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**4. Little-o — $o(g(n))$ — Strict Upper Bound:**

$$f(n) = o(g(n)) \iff \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0$$

**5. Little-omega — $\omega(g(n))$ — Strict Lower Bound:**

$$f(n) = \omega(g(n)) \iff \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$$

**Diagrammatic Relationship:**

$$\Theta(g) \Rightarrow O(g) \text{ and } \Omega(g)$$
$$o(g) \Rightarrow O(g) \text{ but } o(g) \not\Rightarrow \Theta(g)$$

**(b)** Prove that $f(n) = 2n^3 + 5n^2 - 3 = \Theta(n^3)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

To prove $\Theta$, we need to establish both $O$ and $\Omega$ bounds.

**Part 1: Upper Bound — Show $f(n) = O(n^3)$**

We need $c_2 > 0$ and $n_0$ such that $2n^3 + 5n^2 - 3 \leq c_2 n^3$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

For $n \geq 1$:
$$5n^2 \leq 5n^3 \quad \text{and} \quad -3 \leq 0 \leq n^3$$

Adding:
$$2n^3 + 5n^2 - 3 \leq 2n^3 + 5n^3 + n^3 = 8n^3$$

Choose $c_2 = 8$ and $n_0 = 1$. Hence $f(n) = O(n^3)$.

**Part 2: Lower Bound — Show $f(n) = \Omega(n^3)$**

We need $c_1 > 0$ such that $c_1 n^3 \leq 2n^3 + 5n^2 - 3$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

For $n \geq 1$, observe:
$$5n^2 - 3 \geq 0 \iff n^2 \geq \frac{3}{5} \iff n \geq 1$$

Hence:
$$2n^3 + 5n^2 - 3 \geq 2n^3$$

Choose $c_1 = 2$ and $n_0 = 1$. Hence $f(n) = \Omega(n^3)$.

**Part 3: Conclusion**

Since $f(n) = O(n^3)$ (with $c_2 = 8$) and $f(n) = \Omega(n^3)$ (with $c_1 = 2$):

$$\boxed{2n^3 + 5n^2 - 3 = \Theta(n^3)}$$

---

### Question B1 — Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain randomized algorithms. Differentiate between Las Vegas and Monte Carlo algorithms with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A randomized algorithm is one whose behavior is partly determined by **random coin flips**. Formally, $\mathcal{A}(x, r)$ takes input $x$ and random bits $r$ drawn uniformly at random.

**Why use randomness?**
1. **Avoid worst-case inputs** constructed by an adversary.
2. **Simpler** than deterministic algorithms for complex problems.
3. **Provably faster** expected performance.
4. **Distributed efficiency** — no coordination needed.

**Las Vegas Algorithm:**
- Output is **always correct**.
- Running time is a random variable.
- Expected running time is bounded.

**Example — Randomized QuickSort:**
```text
RandomizedQuickSort(A, p, r):
  if p < r:
    q = RandomizedPartition(A, p, r)
    RandomizedQuickSort(A, p, q-1)
    RandomizedQuickSort(A, q+1, r)

RandomizedPartition(A, p, r):
  i = Random(p, r)
  swap A[r] with A[i]
  return Partition(A, p, r)
```
Expected running time: $O(n \log n)$.

**Monte Carlo Algorithm:**
- Running time is deterministic or bounded.
- Output is correct with probability $\geq 1 - \epsilon$.

**Example — Miller–Rabin Primality Test:**
- Input: integer $n$, security parameter $k$.
- Output: "composite" (definitely correct) or "prime" (correct w.p. $\geq 1 - 4^{-k}$).
- Running time: $O(k \log^3 n)$.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Property | Las Vegas | Monte Carlo |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Correctness | 100% | Probabilistic |
| Time | Random | Bounded |
| Domain | Sorting, Selection | Decision problems |

**(b)** Analyze the expected running time of Randomized QuickSort. Show that $E[T(n)] = O(n \log n)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $T(n)$ = number of comparisons on $n$ elements. Assume all elements are distinct.

**Setup:** Let $z_1 < z_2 < \dots < z_n$ be the sorted elements. The first pivot chosen is equally likely to be any of the $n$ elements.

**Indicator Variable:**
$$X_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } z_i \text{ and } z_j \text{ are compared} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

for $1 \leq i < j \leq n$.

**Total Comparisons:**
$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} X_{ij}$$

**Probability Calculation:**
The elements $z_i, z_{i+1}, \dots, z_j$ are compared if and only if the first pivot from this set of $(j - i + 1)$ elements is either $z_i$ or $z_j$. Hence:

$$P[X_{ij} = 1] = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

**Expected Value (Linearity of Expectation):**
$$E[T(n)] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

**Substitution:** Let $k = j - i$ (ranges $1$ to $n-1$). Inner sum has $(n-k)$ terms:

$$E[T(n)] = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} (n-k) \cdot \frac{2}{k+1}$$

**Upper Bound:**
$$E[T(n)] \leq \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{2n}{k} = 2n \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k} = 2n \cdot H_n$$

Using $H_n \leq \ln n + 1$:

$$E[T(n)] \leq 2n(\ln n + 1) = 2n \ln n + 2n$$

$$\boxed{E[T(n)] = O(n \log n)}$$

This bound is **independent of the input ordering**, defeating the worst-case adversary that produces $O(n^2)$ for deterministic QuickSort.

---

### Question B2 — Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** State and explain the Master Theorem for solving recurrences. Apply it to find the asymptotic bound for $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Master Theorem Statement:**

For recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ asymptotically positive:

Let $n^{\log_b a}$ be the critical exponent.

**Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$:
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$$

**Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ for $k \geq 0$:
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$$

**Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ and regularity condition $a f(n/b) \leq c f(n)$ for some $c < 1$:
$$T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$$

**Application to $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$:**

Identify parameters:
$$a = 4, \quad b = 2, \quad f(n) = n^2$$

Critical exponent:
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$$

Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^2$:
$$f(n) = \Theta(n^2) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^0 n)$$

This matches **Case 2** with $k = 0$:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)}$$

**(b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ using Master's Theorem. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Parameters:**
$$a = 2, \quad b = 2, \quad f(n) = n \log n$$

Critical exponent:
$$n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$$

**Comparison:** $f(n) = n \log n$ vs $n^{\log_b a} = n$.

$$f(n) = n \log n = \Theta(n \cdot \log^1 n)$$

This matches **Case 2** with $k = 1$:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)}$$

**Verification (informal):** Each level of recursion tree has work proportional to $n \log n$, but the input size halves. Total depth is $\log n$. Summing: $n \log n + n \log n + \dots$ ($\log n$ levels) $\approx n \log^2 n$.

---

### Question B2 — Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the importance of randomized algorithms in modern computing. List three real-world applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Importance of Randomized Algorithms:**

1. **Adversarial Robustness:** Deterministic algorithms can have worst-case inputs constructed adversarially (e.g., $O(n^2)$ QuickSort). Randomization defeats this.

2. **Simplicity:** Often randomized algorithms are simpler to design than deterministic counterparts with worst-case guarantees (e.g., randomized incremental convex hull vs. $O(n \log n)$ deterministic hull).

3. **Parallelism and Distribution:** Randomization enables coordination without explicit communication (e.g., randomized consensus in distributed systems).

4. **Cryptographic Security:** Randomness is essential for generating unpredictable keys (e.g., RSA key generation, Diffie–Hellman).

5. **Approximation Algorithms:** Many NP-hard problems have efficient randomized approximation algorithms (e.g., MAX-CUT via randomized rounding).

**Three Real-World Applications:**

1. **Cryptographic Primality Testing:** Miller–Rabin algorithm is used in OpenSSL for generating RSA primes. It runs in $O(k \log^3 n)$ and has one-sided error probability $\leq 4^{-k}$.

2. **Network Load Balancing:** Randomized Power-of-Two-Choice algorithm in data center switches reduces queue lengths from $O(\log n)$ to $O(\log \log n)$ compared to random assignment.

3. **Machine Learning — SGD:** Stochastic Gradient Descent uses random sampling of training examples to optimize neural networks efficiently at scale.

**(b)** Using the limit method, show that $n \log n = o(n^2)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Goal:** Prove $\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \log n}{n^2} = 0$.

**Step 1:** Simplify the ratio:
$$\frac{n \log n}{n^2} = \frac{\log n}{n}$$

**Step 2:** Apply L'Hôpital's Rule. Consider the continuous version:

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\log n}{n}$$

Take derivatives of numerator and denominator:
- Numerator: $\frac{d}{dn}(\log n) = \frac{1}{n}$
- Denominator: $\frac{d}{dn}(n) = 1$

**Step 3:** Apply the limit:
$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1/n}{1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Alternative — Integral Bound Argument:

$$\log n = \int_1^n \frac{1}{x} dx \leq \int_1^n 1 \cdot dx = n - 1 < n$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{\log n}{n} \leq \frac{n}{n} = 1$$

This gives only $O(n)$ bound. To get $o$, use a sharper argument:

$$\log n = \int_1^n \frac{1}{x} dx \leq \int_1^n \frac{2}{x+1} dx \leq 2 \log(n+1) - 2\log 2$$

$$\frac{\log n}{n} \leq \frac{2 \log(n+1)}{n}$$

Since $\log n = o(n)$, this ratio $\to 0$.

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \log n}{n^2} = 0 \implies n \log n = o(n^2)}$$

Hence $n \log n = O(n^2)$ but $n \log n \neq \Theta(n^2)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 4 (Asymptotic Notations)**
>
> 1. **Wrong direction in $\Omega$ proof:** Students often prove upper bound when $\Omega$ is asked. **Always verify:** $\Omega$ needs $c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n)$, NOT $f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$.
>
> 2. **Using $= $ instead of $\in$ or relational notation:** Strictly, $f(n) \in O(g(n))$. Writing "$f(n) = O(g(n))$" is loose but accepted; using "$\leq$" in place of "$O$" loses marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the constants:** Must specify $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ explicitly. Writing only the inequality without constants = **−2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing Little-o with Big-O:** $f(n) = o(g(n))$ means **strict** upper bound. Mistaking this for Big-O costs marks in KTU ESE.
>
> 5. **Randomized algorithm proofs:** Forgetting linearity of expectation, or not justifying independence of coin flips = **−2 to −3 marks**.
>
> 6. **No units in the answer:** Always state **which case** (best/worst/average) and the **bound type** ($O$, $\Omega$, $\Theta$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

### Randomized Algorithms
- [ ] Definition: uses randomness (PRNG) as part of logic.
- [ ] **Las Vegas:** always correct output, random running time.
- [ ] **Monte Carlo:** bounded running time, probabilistic correctness.
- [ ] Randomized QuickSort achieves $O(n \log n)$ expected.
- [ ] Karger's Min-Cut: $O(n^2 \log n)$ with high probability.
- [ ] Amplification: repeat Monte Carlo $k$ times → error $\leq 2^{-k}$.
- [ ] Indicator random variables + linearity of expectation are the key analysis tool.

### Asymptotic Notations
- [ ] $O(g(n))$: upper bound — exists $c, n_0$ with $f(n) \leq c g(n)$.
- [ ] $\Omega(g(n))$: lower bound — exists $c, n_0$ with $c g(n) \leq f(n)$.
- [ ] $\Theta(g(n))$: tight bound — both $O$ and $\Omega$ hold simultaneously.
- [ ] $o(g(n))$: strict upper — $\lim_{n \to \infty} f(n)/g(n) = 0$.
- [ ] $\omega(g(n))$: strict lower — $\lim_{n \to \infty} f(n)/g(n) = \infty$.
- [ ] **Always specify $c$ and $n_0$** when proving definitions.
- [ ] **Use $\leq$ in inequality, not $=$** when applying definitions.
- [ ] Master Theorem cases 1, 2, 3 with regularity condition.

### Growth Hierarchy (fastest recall)
- [ ] $1 < \log n < n < n \log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n!$

### Transpose Symmetry
- [ ] $f = O(g) \Leftrightarrow g = \Omega(f)$ — frequently tested.

### Limit Method Quick Reference
- [ ] Ratio $\to 0$: $f = o(g)$.
- [ ] Ratio $\to \infty$: $f = \omega(g)$.
- [ ] Ratio $\to$ constant $c > 0$: $f = \Theta(g)$.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
