<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4: Recurrences — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Recurrence Relation** is an equation or inequality that describes a function (typically representing the running time, $T(n)$, of a recursive algorithm) in terms of its value on **smaller inputs of the same form**. It expresses the cost of a problem of size $n$ as the cost of solving one or more subproblems of strictly smaller size, plus the cost of the work done at the current level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A recurrence for $T(n)$ is a function defined on the non-negative integers (or natural numbers) that satisfies:
> $$T(n) = f(n) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i \cdot T(n - b_i)$$
> along with one or more **base cases** (boundary conditions) that terminate the recursion.

In the analysis of algorithms, recurrences arise naturally whenever an algorithm calls itself on smaller inputs. The three canonical components of any recurrence are:

1. **The recursive term** $T(\cdot)$ — the cost of subproblems.
2. **The driving / non-recursive function** $f(n)$ — the cost of work done outside the recursive calls (partitioning, combining, comparison work, etc.).
3. **The base case** $T(1)$ or $T(0)$ — the cost when the input is trivially small.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Matryoshka (Russian Nesting Doll) Model

Imagine you are handed a **large wooden Matryoshka doll** of size $n$. To "solve" it, you must:

- Open the outer shell — that costs you **$f(n)$ units of effort** (prying, unscrewing, polishing).
- Inside, you find **$a$ smaller dolls**, each of size roughly $n/b$.
- You recursively repeat this for **each** smaller doll until you reach a tiny solid doll (base case) that costs a constant $T(1) = \Theta(1)$.

The **total effort** to dismantle the entire nesting is exactly what a recurrence computes: $T(n) = a \cdot T(n/b) + f(n)$. The total time of an algorithm is the **sum of effort at every level of nesting**, which is the conceptual heart of recurrence solving.

## 1.3 Why Recurrences Matter in DSA

Every divide-and-conquer algorithm (Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen's Matrix Multiplication, Karatsuba's Integer Multiplication) has its efficiency captured **exactly** by a recurrence. Solving that recurrence yields the asymptotic $\Theta$, $O$, or $\Omega$ bound that defines the algorithm's complexity class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Asymptotic Families Encountered via Recurrences:**
> - **Linear** $\Rightarrow T(n) = T(n-1) + c \Rightarrow O(n)$
> - **Logarithmic** $\Rightarrow T(n) = T(n/2) + c \Rightarrow O(\log n)$
> - **Linearithmic** $\Rightarrow T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \Rightarrow O(n \log n)$
> - **Quadratic** $\Rightarrow T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \Rightarrow O(n^2)$ (if work dominates)
> - **Cubic / Polynomial** $\Rightarrow T(n) = 8T(n/2) + n^2 \Rightarrow O(n^3)$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Divide-and-Conquer Recurrence

The **general form** of a divide-and-conquer recurrence (the most-tested form in KTU ESE) is:

$$T(n) = a \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ being an asymptotically positive function. The boundary condition is $T(1) = \Theta(1)$.

| Symbol | Meaning | Algorithmic Interpretation |
|:------:|:--------|:---------------------------|
| $a$ | Number of subproblems | How many recursive calls the algorithm makes |
| $n/b$ | Size of each subproblem | How much smaller each subproblem is |
| $f(n)$ | Cost of divide + combine | Work done **outside** the recursive calls |
| $T(1)$ | Base case cost | Cost when $n$ is trivially small (constant) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of $a$ as the **A**rmy of recursive calls and $b$ as the **B**alance factor of how the input is **B**roken up.

## 2.2 The Four Standard Methods to Solve Recurrences

### Method 1 — Substitution Method (Guess & Verify)
1. **Guess** the form of the solution (e.g., $T(n) = O(n \log n)$).
2. **Verify** by mathematical induction, finding constants $c > 0$ and $n_0$ such that the inductive hypothesis holds.
3. Used when the solution is **hard to obtain directly** but easy to verify.

### Method 2 — Recursion Tree Method
1. **Expand** the recurrence into a tree where each node represents the cost of a single subproblem.
2. **Sum** the costs level-by-level.
3. **Bound** the total using a geometric series or integral.
4. Yields intuition and is the basis for the Master Theorem.

### Method 3 — Master Theorem (Cook's Theorem, Cormen Version)
A **direct cookbook** solution for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ by comparing $f(n)$ against the critical exponent $n^{\log_b a}$.

### Method 4 — Change of Variable
Substitute $n = 2^k$ (or $n = b^k$) to convert a divide-and-conquer recurrence into a simpler linear recurrence in $k$. Useful for floor/ceiling complications.

## 2.3 The Master Theorem — Three Cases (Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein)

Let $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$. Compare $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$, the cost of the **leaves** of the recursion tree.

| Case | Condition | Result | Intuition |
|:----:|:----------|:-------|:----------|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | Recursion (leaves) **dominates**; $f$ is polynomially smaller |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \geq 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$ | $f$ and recursion are **balanced** at every level |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, **and** $a f(n/b) \leq c f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ (regularity condition) | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ | Root work $f(n)$ **dominates**; recursion shrinks geometrically |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Regularity Condition in Case 3** is the most-skipped condition in KTU answers. Without proving $a f(n/b) \leq c f(n)$ for some constant $c < 1$, Case 3 is **incomplete** and will cost marks.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Recurrence Pattern | Closed-Form Solution | Example Algorithm |
|:-:|:-------------------|:---------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Linear Search |
| 2 | $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Binary Search |
| 3 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Insertion Sort (worst) |
| 4 | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + c$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Tree Traversal |
| 5 | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Merge Sort |
| 6 | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Preorder Traversal |
| 7 | $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Naive Matrix Multiply |
| 8 | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$ | Strassen-like |
| 9 | $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Selection by Median |
| 10 | $T(n) = \sqrt{n}\, T(\sqrt{n}) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log \log n)$ | van Emde Boas sub-routine |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recurrence analysis is the **lingua franca** of algorithm engineering. Every modern compiler optimization (loop tiling, parallelization), database query planner (cost-based join ordering), and distributed system scheduler (work-stealing, fork-join pools) **relies on asymptotic cost models built from solving recurrences**. Without mastery of this topic, you cannot reason about whether Quick Sort is faster than Merge Sort in practice, or why $O(n \log n)$ sorts cannot be beaten by comparison-based methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Adaptive Depth Mode: ENGAGED** — Recurrences is a *high-complexity* analytical topic; every algebraic transition is spelled out below. No steps skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort) by Recursion Tree

We want the closed-form solution of:

$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n, \qquad T(1) = \Theta(1).$$

**Step 1 — Expand the tree one level at a time.**

At the **root** (level 0), there is **1 node** doing work $n$. We recursively solve 2 subproblems of size $n/2$ each.

At **level 1**, there are **$2$ nodes**, each doing work $n/2$, so the level cost is $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$.

At **level 2**, there are **$4$ nodes**, each doing work $n/4$, so the level cost is $4 \cdot (n/4) = n$.

**Step 2 — Identify the pattern.** At every level $i$ (until leaves), the number of subproblems is $2^i$ and each does work $n/2^i$, giving a per-level cost of:

$$
\text{Cost at level } i \;=\; 2^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}} \;=\; n.
$$

**Step 3 — Count the levels.** The tree stops when the subproblem size is $1$, i.e., when $n/2^i = 1 \Rightarrow i = \log_2 n$. So there are $\log_2 n + 1$ levels total (including the root).

**Step 4 — Sum all levels.** Total cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= (\text{levels of internal work}) + (\text{leaves cost}) \\
     &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n \;+\; \underbrace{2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1)}_{\text{leaves}} \\
     &= n \cdot \log_2 n \;+\; n \cdot \Theta(1) \\
     &= \Theta(n \log n).
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Closed Form:** $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ — confirms Merge Sort.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solving $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ via Master Theorem

Compare $f(n) = n^2$ against $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_4 3}$.

Since $\log_4 3 \approx 0.792$, we have $n^{\log_4 3} \ll n^2$. So $f(n)$ is **polynomially larger**.

$$
n^2 = \Omega\!\left(n^{\log_4 3 + \epsilon}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad \epsilon = 1.
$$

**Verify the regularity condition:**

$$
a \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) = 3 \cdot \left(\frac{n}{4}\right)^{2} = 3 \cdot \frac{n^{2}}{16} = \frac{3}{16} n^{2}.
$$

We need $\frac{3}{16} n^{2} \leq c \cdot n^{2}$ for some constant $c < 1$. Choosing $c = 3/16 < 1$ satisfies this.

**Therefore, by Master Theorem Case 3:**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n^{2}).
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solving $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ (Binary Search) by Change of Variable

Let $n = 2^k$, so $k = \log_2 n$. Define $S(k) = T(2^k)$.

**Step 1 — Substitute.** The recurrence becomes:

$$
S(k) = S(k-1) + 1.
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the linear recurrence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(k) &= S(k-1) + 1 \\
     &= [S(k-2) + 1] + 1 = S(k-2) + 2 \\
     &= [S(k-3) + 1] + 2 = S(k-3) + 3 \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= S(0) + k.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply base case.** $S(0) = T(2^0) = T(1) = \Theta(1) = c$.

$$
S(k) = c + k.
$$

**Step 4 — Back-substitute.** Since $k = \log_2 n$:

$$
T(n) = c + \log_2 n = \Theta(\log n).
$$

✅ **Closed Form:** $T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$ — confirms Binary Search.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Solving $T(n) = 2T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$ by Double Change of Variable

This is a non-standard recurrence and is a **favourite KTU trick question**.

**Step 1 — First substitution:** Let $n = 2^k$, so $\sqrt{n} = 2^{k/2}$. Define $S(k) = T(2^k)$:

$$
S(k) = 2 \, S\!\left(\frac{k}{2}\right) + 1.
$$

**Step 2 — Second substitution:** Let $k = 2^m$. Define $R(m) = S(2^m)$:

$$
R(m) = 2 \, R(m-1) + 1.
$$

**Step 3 — Solve by expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(m) &= 2R(m-1) + 1 \\
     &= 2[2R(m-2) + 1] + 1 = 4R(m-2) + 3 \\
     &= 2^{m} R(0) + (2^{m} - 1).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Back-substitute step by step.**

$R(0) = S(1) = T(2) = \Theta(1)$.

$R(m) = \Theta(2^m)$. Since $k = 2^m$, we have $m = \log_2 k$, hence $R(m) = \Theta(2^{\log_2 k}) = \Theta(k)$.

So $S(k) = \Theta(k)$, and $T(n) = S(\log_2 n) = \Theta(\log n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> Many students erroneously jump to $T(n) = \Theta(\log \log n)$ — that would be correct only if the original recurrence were $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$. Always back-substitute carefully.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Solving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n\log n$ — Master Theorem Gap Case

Here $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n \log n$. Compare with $n^{\log_2 2} = n^{1} = n$.

We ask: is $n \log n = \Theta(n \cdot \log^{k} n)$ for some $k \geq 0$?

Yes — with $k = 1$. So this fits **Case 2** with $k = 1$.

Therefore:

$$
T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_2 2} \cdot \log^{1+1} n\right) = \Theta(n \log^{2} n).
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Aid for Master Theorem

| Recurrence | $n^{\log_b a}$ | $f(n)$ | Compare | Case | Result |
|:-----------|:---------------|:-------|:--------|:----:|:-------|
| $4T(n/2) + n$ | $n^2$ | $n$ | $f \ll n^{\log_b a}$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $T(n/2) + n^2$ | $n^{0}=1$ | $n^2$ | $f \gg n^{\log_b a}$ | 3 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $8T(n/2) + n^2$ | $n^3$ | $n^2$ | $f \ll n^{\log_b a}$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^3)$ |
| $2T(n/2) + n/\log n$ | $n$ | $n/\log n$ | $f$ smaller, but **not polynomially** | — | Master Theorem **inapplies**; use recursion tree |
| $2T(n/2) + n\log n$ | $n$ | $n\log n$ | $f = \Theta(n \log^1 n)$ | 2 ($k=1$) | $\Theta(n \log^2 n)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> The Master Theorem **cannot be applied** when the gap between $f(n)$ and $n^{\log_b a}$ is **polylogarithmic** (e.g., $f(n) = n / \log n$, or $f(n) = n \log \log n$). KTU frequently tests this boundary.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["LEVEL 0: ROOT n = 1 subproblem of size n, cost n"]
    node2["LEVEL 1: 2 subproblems of size n/2, cost 2 times n/2 = n"]
    node3["LEVEL 2: 4 subproblems of size n/4, cost 4 times n/4 = n"]
    node4["LEVEL 3: 8 subproblems of size n/8, cost 8 times n/8 = n"]
    node5["LEAVES: n subproblems of size 1, cost n times 1 = n"]
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each level contributes cost $n$. There are $\log_2 n$ internal levels plus a leaf level. Total = $n \log_2 n + n = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Substitution Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["STEP A: GUESS the asymptotic form T of n"]
    stepB["STEP B: WRITE inductive hypothesis T of k <= c times g of k"]
    stepC["STEP C: SUBSTITUTE the hypothesis into the recurrence"]
    stepD["STEP D: SIMPLIFY the right-hand side algebraically"]
    stepE["STEP E: VERIFY inequality holds for large enough n"]
    stepF{"STEP F: DOES the base case hold?"}
    stepG["STEP G: ADJUST constant c and threshold n0 if needed"]
    stepH["RESULT: T of n equals Theta of g of n"]
    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF -- YES --> stepH
    stepF -- NO --> stepG
    stepG --> stepB
```

---

## 4.3 Master Theorem Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["INPUT: T of n = a T of n slash b + f of n"]
    q1{"Is f of n = O of n power log base b of a minus epsilon?"}
    c1["CASE 1: T of n = Theta of n power log base b of a"]
    q2{"Is f of n = Theta of n power log base b of a times log to the k of n?"}
    c2["CASE 2: T of n = Theta of n power log base b of a times log to the k plus 1 of n"]
    q3{"Is f of n = Omega of n power log base b of a plus epsilon AND regularity holds?"}
    c3["CASE 3: T of n = Theta of f of n"]
    gap["GAP: Master Theorem does not apply; use recursion tree or Akra-Bazzi"]
    start --> q1
    q1 -- YES --> c1
    q1 -- NO --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> c2
    q2 -- NO --> q3
    q3 -- YES --> c3
    q3 -- NO --> gap
```

---

## 4.4 Generic Recursion Tree Skeleton (for any $a$, $b$, $f(n)$)

```mermaid
graph TD
    lvl0["LEVEL 0: 1 NODE cost f of n"]
    lvl1["LEVEL 1: a NODES each cost f of n slash b, total a f of n slash b"]
    lvl2["LEVEL 2: a SQUARED NODES each cost f of n slash b SQUARED, total a SQUARED f of n slash b SQUARED"]
    lvlL["LEVEL L: a TO THE L NODES each cost f of n slash b TO THE L, total a TO THE L f of n slash b TO THE L"]
    leaves["LEAVES: n TO THE LOG BASE b OF a NODES cost Theta of 1, total Theta of n TO THE LOG BASE b OF a"]
    lvl0 --> lvl1
    lvl1 --> lvl2
    lvl2 --> lvlL
    lvlL --> leaves
```

---

# 🎓 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a recurrence relation. Give one example each from linear search and merge sort.

**Model Answer:**

A **recurrence relation** is an equation that expresses the value of a function $T(n)$ for a given input size $n$ in terms of its values on smaller inputs, plus the cost of work done outside the recursive calls. It is the natural mathematical description of the running time of recursive algorithms.

**Examples:**
- **Linear Search (worst case):** $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$, with $T(1) = c$. Solving gives $T(n) = \Theta(n)$.
- **Merge Sort:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$. Solving gives $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (3):** Definition (1) + Example 1 (1) + Example 2 (1).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** State the Master Theorem and identify which case applies to $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^3$.

**Model Answer:**

**Master Theorem Statement:** Given $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ asymptotically positive, let $n^{\log_b a}$ be the critical exponent.

- **Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k} n)$ for some $k \geq 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
- **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ and $af(n/b) \leq cf(n)$ for some $c < 1$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

**Application:** $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^3$. Compute $n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$.

Compare $f(n) = n^3$ vs. $n^2$: $n^3 = \Omega(n^{2 + 1})$, so with $\epsilon = 1$, **Case 3** applies. Regularity: $4 \cdot (n/2)^3 = 4 \cdot n^3/8 = n^3/2 \leq c \cdot n^3$ for $c = 1/2 < 1$. ✓

**Result:** $T(n) = \Theta(n^3)$.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Choice Rule:** Students must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n \log n$ using an appropriate method. Justify every step.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Master Theorem Application**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:** $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $f(n) = n \log n$.

**Step 2 — Compute critical exponent:**

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_4 3}.
$$

Numerically, $\log_4 3 = \frac{\log 3}{\log 4} \approx \frac{1.0986}{1.3863} \approx 0.7925$.

So the critical exponent is $n^{0.7925}$.

**Step 3 — Compare:** Is $n \log n = O(n^{0.7925 - \epsilon})$? **No**, because $n \log n$ grows faster than $n^{0.7925}$.

Is $n \log n = \Theta(n^{0.7925} \log^{k} n)$ for some $k$? **No**, because $n \log n = n^{0.7925} \cdot n^{0.2075} \log n$, which is not of the form $n^{0.7925} \cdot \log^{k} n$.

**Step 4 — Try Case 3:** Is $n \log n = \Omega(n^{0.7925 + \epsilon})$? Yes, with $\epsilon = 0.1$, since $n^{0.8925} \leq n \log n$ for large $n$.

**Step 5 — Verify regularity condition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a f\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) &= 3 \cdot \frac{n}{4} \log\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) \\
                              &= 3 \cdot \frac{n}{4} (\log n - \log 4) \\
                              &= \frac{3}{4} n \log n - \frac{3 \log 4}{4} n.
\end{aligned}
$$

For large $n$, the dominant term is $\frac{3}{4} n \log n$. We need:

$$
\frac{3}{4} n \log n \leq c \cdot n \log n \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \frac{3}{4} < 1. \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** **Case 3 applies** $\Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Verification by Recursion Tree**

Tree level $i$: $3^i$ subproblems each of size $n/4^i$, each doing work $(n/4^i) \log(n/4^i)$.

$$
\text{Level cost} = 3^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{4^{i}} \log\!\left(\frac{n}{4^{i}}\right) = n \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{i} (\log n - i \log 4).
$$

Tree height: $i = \log_4 n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} n \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{i} (\log n - i \log 4) + \text{leaf cost} \\
     &= n \log n \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{i} - n \log 4 \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} i \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{i} + \Theta(n^{\log_4 3}).
\end{aligned}
$$

The geometric series $\sum (3/4)^i = 4$ and $\sum i (3/4)^i$ converges to a constant. So:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n). \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Solve the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ using the recursion tree method. State the closed-form solution.**

> [!NOTE]
> This is the classic **Selection by Median of Medians** recurrence — irregular subproblem sizes make Master Theorem inapplicable, so recursion tree is mandatory.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Constructing the Recursion Tree**

The recurrence has **two unequal** subproblems: one of size $n/3$ and one of size $2n/3$. The tree is **unbalanced**.

**Level 0:** Root cost = $n$.

**Level 1:** Two children — one of size $n/3$, one of size $2n/3$. Total work = $n/3 + 2n/3 = n$.

**Level 2:** The short branch (size $n/3$) splits into $n/9$ and $2n/9$. The long branch (size $2n/3$) splits into $2n/9$ and $4n/9$. Total work = $(n/9 + 2n/9) + (2n/9 + 4n/9) = 9n/9 = n$.

**Inductive pattern:** At **every level**, the sum of the sizes of the children equals the parent's size. Hence, the **work per level is always $n$**.

**Tree Height:** The **longest path** corresponds to repeatedly picking the $2n/3$ branch: $n \to (2/3)n \to (4/9)n \to \cdots$ The recursion bottoms out when $(2/3)^k n = 1$, i.e., $k = \log_{3/2} n$.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Summation and Closed Form**

Total cost = (work per level) × (number of levels):

$$
T(n) = n \cdot \log_{3/2} n + \Theta(n).
$$

Using change of base:

$$
\log_{3/2} n = \frac{\log n}{\log(3/2)} = \frac{1}{\log(3/2)} \log n = \Theta(\log n).
$$

Therefore:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n). \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the regularity condition** in Master Theorem Case 3 — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\log^k n$ notation**: $\log^2 n$ means $(\log n)^2$, **NOT** $\log(\log n)$. This single mistake has lost students 7 marks.
> 3. **Off-by-one in recursion tree height**: $\log_b n$ vs. $\log_b n - 1$. The asymptotic answer is unaffected, but step-by-step marks are deducted if levels are miscounted.
> 4. **Applying Master Theorem to recurrences with unequal subproblems** like $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$. Master Theorem requires equal subproblems $n/b$.
> 5. **Dropping constants too early**: writing $T(n) \approx n$ instead of $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ at intermediate steps.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔑 A **recurrence relation** describes $T(n)$ in terms of $T$ at smaller arguments.
- 🔑 Every divide-and-conquer recurrence has the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.
- 🔑 **Master Theorem** is the fastest KTU method but requires **equal subproblems**.
- 🔑 **Three cases** of Master Theorem pivot on comparing $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$.
- 🔑 **Case 2** has a $\log^{k+1} n$ multiplier — never $\log^{k-1} n$ or $\log^{k} n$.
- 🔑 **Case 3** requires the **regularity condition** $af(n/b) \leq cf(n)$.
- 🔑 When Master Theorem fails (gap case), use the **recursion tree** or **Akra–Bazzi**.
- 🔑 **Recursion tree** works universally: total cost = sum of per-level costs.
- 🔑 **Substitution method** is verification-based: guess → substitute → adjust constants.
- 🔑 **Change of variable** converts $T(\sqrt{n})$ or $T(n/2)$ to linear in $k = \log n$.
- 🔑 Classic recurrences: Binary Search $\Theta(\log n)$, Merge Sort $\Theta(n \log n)$, Quick Sort average $\Theta(n \log n)$, Strassen $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$, Karatsuba $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$.
- 🔑 **Logarithmic identities**: $\log_b a = 1/\log_a b$; $(3/4)^i$ forms a convergent geometric series summing to $4$.
- 🔑 For KTU answers: **always state the parameters $a$, $b$, $f(n)$, $n^{\log_b a}$** before applying Master Theorem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NP-Complete Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT751 - DATA STRUCTURES & ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** NP-Complete Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NP-Complete (Non-deterministic Polynomial Complete)** is the class of decision problems in computational complexity theory that are **(1)** in **NP** (verifiable in polynomial time by a non-deterministic Turing machine) and **(2)** **NP-Hard** (every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time). If any NP-Complete problem can be solved in polynomial time, then **P = NP**.

The study of NP-Completeness is rooted in the formal theory of **Computational Complexity Classes**. The three foundational classes relevant to Module 4 are:

| Class | Formal Definition | Intuitive Meaning |
|:------|:------------------|:------------------|
| **P** | Problems solvable in **polynomial time** $O(n^k)$ by a deterministic Turing machine. | "Easy" — we can compute the answer quickly. |
| **NP** | Problems whose proposed solutions can be **verified** in polynomial time. | "Hard to solve, easy to check." |
| **NPC** | Problems in NP to which every NP problem **polynomial-time reduces**. | "The hardest problems inside NP." |
| **NP-Hard** | At least as hard as every NP problem (may not be in NP). | "Harder than or equal to NPC" — super-set of NPC. |

A **decision problem** is a problem whose answer is strictly **YES** or **NO**. Most NP-Completeness theory is built on decision problems (e.g., "Does graph $G$ contain a Hamiltonian Cycle?") rather than optimization problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Treasure Map Puzzle**
> Imagine you are given a massive 1000×1000 jigsaw puzzle (10 lakh pieces) and asked: *"Can these pieces be arranged to form a recognizable picture?"*
> - **Solving it from scratch (P vs NP):** Brute force would take an astronomical number of years (exponential time).
> - **Verifying a solution (the "N" in NP):** If a friend *claims* they have solved it, you can check their completed picture in minutes — that is polynomial-time verification.
> - **NP-Complete:** The puzzle is one of a special "elite" group of puzzles. If you could solve *any one* of them quickly (say, this jigsaw), then by clever transformations, you could solve *every* other elite puzzle just as fast.
> - **Reduction:** Converting one puzzle into another so that a solution to puzzle B also solves puzzle A. If transformation is fast (polynomial), the puzzles are equivalent in difficulty.

**Key Takeaway:** NP-Complete problems are the "**tipping point**" of tractability. They are the hardest problems *within* NP, and no polynomial algorithm is currently known for any of them. Yet, no proof exists that such an algorithm is impossible.

### 1.3 Why This Matters in DSA

In **Data Structures & Algorithms**, NP-Completeness tells engineers when to **stop searching for an efficient exact algorithm** and instead use:
- **Approximation Algorithms** (e.g., greedy TSP gives 2× approximation)
- **Heuristics / Metaheuristics** (e.g., simulated annealing, genetic algorithms)
- **Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT)** algorithms
- **Pseudo-polynomial dynamic programming** (e.g., subset-sum DP with $O(nW)$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A student is expected to (a) define P, NP, NP-Hard, NP-Complete, (b) prove NP-Completeness by reduction, (c) apply the **Cook-Levin Theorem**, and (d) state famous NP-Complete problems (SAT, 3-SAT, Hamiltonian Cycle, TSP, Vertex Cover, Knapsack, Subset-Sum, Graph Coloring).

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Decision Problem Framework

Every NP-Complete problem is formalized as a **language** $L \subseteq \{0,1\}^*$, where each string $x \in L$ represents a YES-instance. A Turing machine $M$ *decides* $L$ if for every input $x$, $M$ halts and outputs 1 if $x \in L$, else 0.

The complexity classes are then defined as:

$$P = \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{DTIME}(n^k)$$

$$NP = \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{NTIME}(n^k)$$

### 2.2 Polynomial-Time Reductions ($\leq_p$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Polynomial Reduction):** A language $L_1$ is polynomial-time reducible to $L_2$, written $L_1 \leq_p L_2$, if there exists a polynomial-time computable function $f : \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^*$ such that $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$.

**Logic of Reduction (Step-by-step):**
1. Suppose we have an algorithm $A_2$ that solves $L_2$ in polynomial time.
2. To solve $L_1$, transform any instance $x$ of $L_1$ into instance $f(x)$ of $L_2$.
3. Run $A_2$ on $f(x)$.
4. Output whatever $A_2$ outputs.

The total time is the transformation time (polynomial) plus the time of $A_2$ on a polynomial-size input (still polynomial). Therefore:

$$L_1 \leq_p L_2 \;\land\; L_2 \in P \;\Longrightarrow\; L_1 \in P$$

### 2.3 The Cook-Levin Theorem (The "First" NP-Complete Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-Complete.

**Proof Idea (high-level):**
1. SAT $\in$ NP — a satisfying assignment is the certificate, verifiable in $O(n)$ time.
2. SAT is NP-Hard — given any non-deterministic Turing machine $M$ and input $x$, construct a Boolean formula $\phi_{M,x}$ that is satisfiable **iff** $M$ accepts $x$. This construction is *itself* polynomial in the size of the computation table.

This is the cornerstone of the entire NP-Completeness theory. Once SAT is shown NP-Complete, every other NP-Complete problem is proved by a **chain of polynomial reductions** starting from SAT (or 3-SAT).

### 2.4 Strategy to Prove a Problem is NP-Complete

To prove a decision problem $\Pi$ is NP-Complete, a student must establish **two things**:

1. **Membership in NP:** Provide a polynomial-time verifier (certificate + checker algorithm). State the time complexity of verification.

2. **NP-Hardness:** Choose a known NP-Complete problem $\Pi'$ and construct a polynomial-time reduction $\Pi' \leq_p \Pi$. Demonstrate both directions:
   - If $x \in \Pi'$, then $f(x) \in \Pi$.
   - If $f(x) \in \Pi$, then $x \in \Pi'$.

### 2.5 The Famous NP-Complete Problems (KTU High-Yield List)

| # | Problem | Decision Form | Reduction Source |
|:--|:--------|:--------------|:-----------------|
| 1 | **SAT** | Is the Boolean formula satisfiable? | Cook-Levin |
| 2 | **3-SAT** | Is a 3-CNF formula satisfiable? | SAT $\leq_p$ 3-SAT |
| 3 | **Vertex Cover** | Is there a VC of size $\leq k$? | 3-SAT $\leq_p$ VC |
| 4 | **Clique** | Is there a clique of size $\geq k$? | 3-SAT $\leq_p$ Clique |
| 5 | **Independent Set** | Is there an IS of size $\geq k$? | Clique complement |
| 6 | **Hamiltonian Cycle** | Does $G$ contain a HC? | 3-SAT $\leq_p$ HC |
| 7 | **TSP** | Is there a tour of length $\leq k$? | HC $\leq_p$ TSP |
| 8 | **Subset Sum** | Is there a subset summing to $S$? | 3-SAT $\leq_p$ SS |
| 9 | **Knapsack (0/1)** | Is there a subset of value $\geq V$ with weight $\leq W$? | Subset Sum $\leq_p$ KP |
| 10 | **Graph Coloring** | Is $G$ $k$-colorable? | 3-SAT $\leq_p$ GC |
| 11 | **Partition** | Can the set be partitioned into equal halves? | Subset Sum $\leq_p$ Part |
| 12 | **Bin Packing** | Can items fit in $k$ bins of capacity $C$? | Partition $\leq_p$ BP |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of NP-Completeness

- **Operations Research:** Vehicle routing, scheduling, warehouse optimization — solved via heuristics because exact solutions are intractable.
- **Bioinformatics:** DNA sequence alignment (small instances NP-hard), protein folding prediction.
- **Cryptography:** The security of **RSA** rests on the assumed hardness of **Integer Factorization** (not proven NP-Complete, but in NP $\cap$ co-NP).
- **VLSI Design:** Circuit layout, routing, fault diagnosis.
- **AI/ML:** Satisfiability solvers power **SAT solvers** (MiniSAT, Glucose) used in software verification, model checking, and automated theorem proving.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reduction of 3-SAT $\leq_p$ Independent Set

This is the most frequently asked reduction in KTU exams. We show it in full detail.

**Setup (3-SAT):** Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ in 3-CNF with $n$ variables and $m$ clauses:

$$\phi = (x_1 \vee \neg x_2 \vee x_3) \wedge (\neg x_1 \vee x_2 \vee x_4) \wedge \ldots \wedge C_m$$

**Goal (Independent Set):** Construct a graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$ such that $\phi$ is satisfiable $\iff$ $G$ has an independent set of size $\geq k$.

**Step 1: Construct the vertices.** For each clause $C_j = (l_{j,1} \vee l_{j,2} \vee l_{j,3})$, create **3 vertices**, one for each literal.

$$V = \bigcup_{j=1}^{m} \{ v_{j,1}, v_{j,2}, v_{j,3} \}$$

So $|V| = 3m$.

**Step 2: Construct the edges (two types).**

*Type 1 — Intra-clause edges (triangle):* Connect the 3 vertices within each clause so they form a **triangle**.

$$E_1 = \big\{ (v_{j,a}, v_{j,b}) \;\big|\; j \in [1,m],\; a \neq b,\; a,b \in \{1,2,3\} \big\}$$

*Type 2 — Inter-clause edges (conflicts):* Add an edge between two literals that are **negations of each other** (in different clauses).

$$E_2 = \big\{ (v_{i,a}, v_{j,b}) \;\big|\; l_{i,a} = \neg l_{j,b},\; i \neq j \big\}$$

**Step 3: Set the target $k = m$.**

**Step 4: Prove equivalence ($\Rightarrow$).**

*Suppose $\phi$ is satisfiable.* Let $\alpha$ be a satisfying assignment. In each clause $C_j$, at least one literal is true under $\alpha$. Select **exactly one** true literal from each clause. Call this set $S \subseteq V$ with $|S| = m$.

- **Independence from Type 1:** No two selected vertices come from the same clause (we picked one per clause), so no triangle-edge is in $S$.
- **Independence from Type 2:** If $(v_{i,a}, v_{j,b}) \in E_2$, then $l_{i,a} = \neg l_{j,b}$. They cannot both be true under any consistent assignment, so they cannot both be in $S$.

Thus $S$ is an independent set of size $m$. $\;\checkmark$

**Step 5: Prove equivalence ($\Leftarrow$).**

*Suppose $G$ has an independent set $S$ with $|S| = m$.* Since $S$ cannot contain two vertices from the same triangle (Type 1 edges), $S$ contains **at most one vertex per clause**. With $|S| = m$ and $m$ triangles, $S$ contains **exactly one vertex per clause**.

- For each selected literal, set its underlying variable to make that literal **true** (e.g., if $l_{j,1} = x_5$, set $x_5 = 1$; if $l_{j,1} = \neg x_5$, set $x_5 = 0$).
- Since no edge in $E_2$ is inside $S$, no two selected literals conflict. So a consistent assignment $\alpha$ exists that makes all $m$ selected literals true, satisfying $\phi$. $\;\checkmark$

**Step 6: Polynomial time check.** Construction uses $3m$ vertices and at most $3m + 9m^2$ edges. Polynomial in input size.

**Conclusion:** Independent Set is NP-Complete.

$$\boxed{\;3\text{-SAT} \leq_p \text{Independent Set}\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Reduction of Hamiltonian Cycle $\leq_p$ TSP (Decision Version)

**Setup:** Given a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = n$, does $G$ have a Hamiltonian Cycle?

**Goal:** Construct an instance of TSP such that the answer is YES iff $G$ has a HC.

**Step 1:** Create a complete graph $G' = (V, E')$ where $V$ is the same vertex set, and assign distances:

$$d(u, v) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (u, v) \in E \\ 2 & \text{if } (u, v) \notin E \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** Set the bound $B = n$ (number of vertices).

**Step 3: Equivalence.**

*If $G$ has a HC:* A cycle of length $n$ using only edges of $G$, i.e., edges of distance 1. Total distance $= n \leq B = n$. TSP says YES.

*If TSP says YES:* A tour of total length $\leq n$ using $n$ edges (must be a cycle visiting each vertex once). If any edge had distance 2, total would be $\geq (n-1) \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = n+1 > n$. So all edges used are in $E$. Hence $G$ has a HC. $\;\checkmark$

**Step 4:** Construction takes $O(n^2)$ time — polynomial.

$$\boxed{\;\text{Hamiltonian Cycle} \leq_p \text{TSP (Decision)}\;}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Time Complexity Comparison

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{polynomial}}(n) &= n^k \quad \text{(e.g., } k=2,3,5\text{)} \\
T_{\text{exponential}}(n) &= 2^n \quad \text{(worst-case backtracking)} \\
T_{\text{factorial}}(n) &= n! \quad \text{(e.g., brute force TSP)} \\
\text{Growth ratio:} \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^k}{2^n} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms that **exponential algorithms are intractable** for $n > 50$ or so, justifying the search for polynomial algorithms — and the despair when none exists (NP-Complete).

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complexity Class Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the assumed relationship between the major complexity classes (under the unproven assumption $P \neq NP$):

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef hardClass fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#fef3c7
    classDef npcClass fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff1f2
    classDef pClass fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#dcfce7
    classDef npClass fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#93c5fd,stroke-width:2px,color:#dbeafe

    EXPTIME["EXPTIME\n(Exponential Time)"]:::hardClass
    NPHARD["NP-HARD\nHardest of all classes\nE.g., Halting Problem, QBF"]:::hardClass
    NP["NP\nVerifiable in Poly Time\nE.g., SAT, HC, TSP, VC"]:::npClass
    NPC["NP-COMPLETE\nHardest problems inside NP\nE.g., SAT, 3-SAT, HC, TSP"]:::npcClass
    P["P\nSolvable in Poly Time\nE.g., Sort, Shortest Path, MST"]:::pClass

    EXPTIME --> NPHARD
    NPHARD --> NPC
    NPHARD --> NP
    NP --> NPC
    NP --> P
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **P** $\subseteq$ **NP** $\subseteq$ **EXPTIME** (this is proven).
- **NP-Complete** = **NP** $\cap$ **NP-Hard** (definition).
- The equality $P = NP$ is the **Millennium Prize Problem** worth \$1,000,000 (Clay Mathematics Institute).

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**
*Define the classes P, NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete. State the open problem relating to them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **P:** Class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time $O(n^k)$.
> **NP:** Class of decision problems whose "yes" instances have certificates verifiable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
> **NP-Hard:** A problem $L$ such that every problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to $L$. (May or may not be in NP.)
> **NP-Complete:** A problem that is both in NP and NP-Hard. (Intersection of NP and NP-Hard.)
>
> **Open Problem:** Is $P = NP$? This is one of the seven **Millennium Prize Problems** of the Clay Mathematics Institute (prize money \$1,000,000). Most complexity theorists believe $P \neq NP$, but it remains unproven.

*(Award: 1 mark per definition × 3 classes + 1 mark for the open problem reference + 1 mark for clarity = 3 marks. Total = out of 3 marks; awarded 1+1+1+0.5+0.5 for the answer above.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*What is the Cook-Levin Theorem? Why is it important in the theory of NP-Completeness?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-Complete. That is, SAT $\in$ NP, and every problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to SAT.
>
> **Importance:**
> 1. It established the **first** NP-Complete problem, providing a foundation for the entire theory.
> 2. Before Cook, NP-Completeness was just a vague notion. Cook's proof gave it a concrete anchor.
> 3. It enabled a **chain of reductions** — once SAT was NP-Complete, Karp (1972) and others showed thousands of problems are NP-Complete by reducing SAT to them.
> 4. It crystallized the $P$ vs $NP$ question into a single concrete problem: *"Is SAT in P?"*

*(Award: 1.5 marks for the theorem statement + 1.5 marks for the importance. Total = 3 marks.)*

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**(a)** [7 marks] Prove that the **Independent Set** problem is NP-Complete.
**(b)** [7 marks] Show a polynomial-time reduction from **Vertex Cover** to **Independent Set** and explain the relationship.

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Proving Independent Set is NP-Complete**

*Step 1 — Show Independent Set is in NP (2 marks):*

> A **certificate** is a set $S \subseteq V$ of vertices. The verifier checks:
> 1. For every pair $(u, v) \in S \times S$, is $(u, v) \notin E$? (i.e., $S$ is independent)
> 2. Is $|S| \geq k$?
>
> Both checks take $O(|S|^2) = O(n^2)$ time, which is polynomial. Hence Independent Set $\in$ NP. $\;\checkmark$

*Step 2 — Show Independent Set is NP-Hard via 3-SAT $\leq_p$ Independent Set (5 marks):*

**Construction:** Given a 3-CNF formula $\phi$ with $m$ clauses, build a graph $G = (V, E)$ as follows:
- $V$: 3 vertices per clause, one per literal. $|V| = 3m$.
- Edges: 
  - **Triangle** within each clause (3 edges per clause).
  - **Conflict edge** between any two literals that are negations of each other.
- Set $k = m$.

**Correctness ($\Rightarrow$):** If $\phi$ is satisfiable, pick one true literal per clause. The set $S$ of size $m$ is independent:
- No edge in $S$ from triangles (one per clause).
- No edge in $S$ from conflicts (a true literal and its negation cannot both be true under any assignment).

**Correctness ($\Leftarrow$):** If $G$ has an IS of size $m$, since triangles forbid two from one clause, $S$ picks one per clause. Setting the underlying variables to make these literals true gives a satisfying assignment (no conflicts because $S$ is independent).

**Polynomial time:** Construction is $O(m^2)$ — polynomial.

**Hence Independent Set is NP-Hard + in NP = NP-Complete. $\blacksquare$**

#### Part (b) — Reduction from Vertex Cover to Independent Set

**Theorem:** A set $S$ is a **Vertex Cover** of $G$ $\iff$ $V \setminus S$ is an **Independent Set** of $G$.

*Proof (3 marks + 4 marks for application):*

> *Forward ($\Rightarrow$):* Let $S$ be a VC. For any edge $(u,v) \in E$, at least one endpoint is in $S$. Hence $V \setminus S$ contains **no edge**, i.e., it is an Independent Set.
>
> *Backward ($\Leftarrow$):* Let $T = V \setminus S$ be an IS. For any edge $(u,v) \in E$, not both $u$ and $v$ can be in $T$ (else $T$ would not be independent). Hence at least one of $u,v$ is in $S$. So $S$ covers every edge, i.e., $S$ is a VC. $\;\checkmark$
>
> **Application:** If we want to find the **minimum** Vertex Cover, we find the **maximum** Independent Set, then take the complement. Since they are complements, $|VC_{\min}| + |IS_{\max}| = n$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **Halting Problem** and show that it is **undecidable**.
**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss why the Halting Problem is **NP-Hard** but **not NP-Complete**.

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — The Halting Problem (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Given a program $P$ and input $x$, does $P(x)$ halt (terminate) or run forever?

**Proof of Undecidability (Turing, 1936) by Diagonalization (5 marks):**

Assume, for contradiction, a decider $H(P, x)$ exists where:
- $H(P, x)$ = 1 if $P(x)$ halts.
- $H(P, x)$ = 0 if $P(x)$ loops forever.

Construct a "diagonal" program $D$:

$$D(Y) = \begin{cases} \text{loop forever} & \text{if } H(Y, Y) = 1 \text{ (i.e., } Y(Y) \text{ halts)} \\ \text{halt} & \text{if } H(Y, Y) = 0 \text{ (i.e., } Y(Y) \text{ loops)} \end{cases}$$

Now consider $D(D)$:
- If $D(D)$ halts, then by definition $D$ should loop on input $D$ (contradiction).
- If $D(D)$ loops, then by definition $D$ should halt on input $D$ (contradiction).

**Conclusion:** No such decider $H$ can exist. The Halting Problem is **undecidable**. $\blacksquare$

*(Award: 1 mark problem statement, 5 marks for diagonalization construction, 1 mark conclusion = 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — Halting Problem is NP-Hard but not in NP (7 marks)**

> **Why NP-Hard (4 marks):**
> We can reduce any NP problem to the Halting Problem because:
> - For any problem $L \in$ NP with a non-deterministic Turing machine $N$, simulating $N$ on input $x$ is itself a computation that either halts (accepts) or loops (rejects).
> - More precisely, given any NP verifier $V$ and certificate $c$, the question "$\exists c$ such that $V(x, c)$ accepts" can be encoded as a halting question for a non-deterministic interpreter.
> - Hence every NP problem is "at most as hard" as Halting, meaning Halting is NP-Hard.
>
> **Why NOT NP-Complete (3 marks):**
> The Halting Problem is **NOT in NP** because:
> - NP requires a polynomial-time **verifier**. But the Halting Problem cannot be decided by *any* algorithm at all, let alone verified in polynomial time.
> - A "certificate" for halting would have to prove that a program halts in **all possible executions** — but by Rice's Theorem, all non-trivial semantic properties of programs are undecidable.
> - So Halting is NP-Hard but **lies outside NP entirely**, making it strictly *harder* than any NP-Complete problem.
>
> **Pictorially:** Halting $\in$ **NP-Hard** but $\notin$ **NP**, while SAT $\in$ **NP** $\cap$ **NP-Hard** = **NP-Complete**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Confusing NP-Hard with NP-Complete (LOSES 2-3 marks):** NP-Hard is a *superset* of NP-Complete. Halting Problem is NP-Hard but **not** NP-Complete. Always clarify the difference.
> 2. **Forgetting to show membership in NP (LOSES 1-2 marks):** When proving NP-Completeness, the student MUST show both: "**in NP**" + "**NP-Hard**". Only showing the reduction gives partial credit.
> 3. **Direction of reduction is wrong (LOSES 2 marks):** To show $\Pi$ is NP-Hard, reduce a **known** NPC problem TO $\Pi$ (not the other way). The direction is **FROM** known **TO** $\Pi$.
> 4. **Skipping the polynomial-time check (LOSES 1 mark):** A reduction is not valid unless you also argue that the construction runs in polynomial time in the input size.
> 5. **Confusing decision vs optimization versions (LOSES 1 mark):** TSP-decision (length $\leq k$) is NP-Complete, but TSP-optimization (minimum tour) is NP-Hard only.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for NP-Complete Problems (KTU Module 4)**

- **P**: Polynomial-time **solvable** decision problems.
- **NP**: Polynomial-time **verifiable** decision problems.
- **NP-Hard**: At least as hard as any NP problem; **may not be in NP**.
- **NP-Complete** = **NP** $\cap$ **NP-Hard**.
- **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971)**: SAT is the *first* NP-Complete problem.
- **Karp's 21 problems (1972)**: Showed 21 combinatorial problems are NP-Complete by reduction from SAT.
- **Polynomial reduction** $L_1 \leq_p L_2$ means "an algorithm for $L_2$ solves $L_1$ in poly-time."
- **To prove NP-Completeness of $\Pi$:** (1) Show $\Pi \in$ NP. (2) Show some known NPC $\Pi' \leq_p \Pi$.
- **Famous NPC problems**: SAT, 3-SAT, VC, Clique, IS, HC, TSP, Subset Sum, Knapsack, Graph Coloring, Partition, Bin Packing.
- **Independent Set $\equiv$ Vertex Cover** (complements in a graph).
- **Clique** in $G$ = **Independent Set** in $\overline{G}$.
- **Halting Problem**: undecidable, NP-Hard, **NOT** NP-Complete (not in NP).
- **P vs NP**: open problem. If any NPC $\in$ P, then P = NP.
- **Engineering response to NP-Completeness**: approximation algorithms, heuristics, pseudo-polynomial DP, FPT, randomized algorithms, parameterized complexity.
- **Polynomial time** = $O(n^k)$ for some constant $k$. **Exponential time** = $O(c^n)$, $c > 1$ — infeasible for $n > 50$.
- **KTU exam mantra**: Always state the certificate (for NP membership) and clearly write "this reduction runs in polynomial time" (for NP-Hardness).
- **Mnemonic** for reduction chain: **"S**at **3**-Sats **V**ery **C**old **I**nside, **H**aving **T**o **S**earch **G**raphs" → SAT, 3-SAT, VC, Clique, IS, HC, TSP, Subset Sum, Graph Coloring.
